                                                               <h1>Bionanotechnology – Principles and Applications [BE 303]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Nanotechnology and special focus on Bionanotechnology. <br><br>Nanotechnology with molecules of life: Proteins-Proteins Structures, Designs, Nanomachines, Origami designs and Applications; Carbohydrates-Glyconanotechnology, Applications in Glycobiology, vaccination, delivery and cell biology; Lipids-Structure, function, designs, membranes and applications in drug delivery formulations and cell biology; Combinatorial Bionanotechnology-Hybrid formulations, structures, functions and applications. <br><br>DNA Nanotechnology: Structure of DNA- Why DNA is genetic material of life?; Naturally occuring DNA nanomachines; Discovery of DNA nanotechnology - Initial designs and validations; Multi-arm DNA based 1/2/3D nanodevices; DNA Origami; Non-canonical Motifs and applications-Aptamers, i-motif, G-quadruplexes. <br><br>Applications of DNA Nanotechnology: Cell Engineering; Theranostics Diagnostics (Biosensing) and therapeutic applications; Biomedical Applications-Bioimaging including super-resolution imaging and single-particle tracking; DNA based computing and robotics.                                                                </p>

                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="BE">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/be.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Biomedical Engineering </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Biomedical Engineering [BE 304]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This course will provide an introductory overview of the multi-disciplinary field of
biomedical engineering. We will discuss applications of elementary engineering principles
to the analyses of physiological systems for addressing challenges in health care. The
course topics will include the following:

<br><br>
1. Biomedical engineers and modern health care systems: Addressing unmet clinical
needs, interfacing with clinicians, research, and development<br>
2. Overview of anatomy and physiology: Cellular organization, tissues, major organ
systems, communication systems, respiration, digestion, circulation, removal of
molecules, homeostasis<br>
3. Biomechanics: Basic mechanics (elasticity, viscosity, deformation and
viscoelasticity), mechanics of tissues (e.g., cartilage, ligaments, tendon, muscle),
physics and mechanics of locomotion (e.g., walking, running, swimming), design in
biomechanics and orthopedics<br>
4. Biomaterials: Materials in medicine, lessons from nature on biomaterial design and
selection, tissue-biomaterial interactions, safety testing<br>
5. Tissue engineering: Challenges in organ transplantation, cell culture, regulation of
healing processes, replacement of diseased tissues, tissue engineering in control
of drug delivery, artificial organs<br>
6. Biosignals: Acquiring and analyzing biosignals, electrocardiogram (ECG),
electroencephalography (EEG), electromyogram (EMG), thermography, blood flow
analysis<br>
7. Brief overview of imaging: Optical microscopy, X-Rays, computed tomography,
magnetic resonance, ultrasound, and nuclear medicine<br>
8. Medical devices: Classes of medical devices, considerations for design and
development, introduction to regulatory processes in Indian, U.S., and European
markets, case studies from India and abroad<br>
9. Bioethics: Animal and human experimentation, informed consent, euthanasia,
ethical issues in feasibility studies, ethical clearance                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#065A0B">BE 304</span>
												
						</div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Origins of Species </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                               <h1>Origins of Species [BE 305]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Hierarchical organization of life, the concept of species; Morphological, phenotypic and molecular bases of species; Concept of species in asexually and sexually reproducing organisms;  Genomes and species; Population sizes, genetic diversity within species; genetic intermixing of closely related species, positive and negative effects of hybrids between different species; Habitats, ecological niche separation, adaption and selection; Reproductive barriers and roles of sex chromosomes; Reproductive compatibilities and incompatibilities; Classical genetic techniques to study speciation; classical and modern molecular mechanisms of speciation.                                                                </p>

                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="BE">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/be.png" /></i>
						
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Methods in Biology </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>0 | 0 | 3 | 2							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Methods in Biology [BE 401]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>0 | 0 | 3 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Basic laboratory techniques that are an integral part of a Biological Engineering program will be
explored in detail via laboratory experiments. This is a laboratory course in which we will conduct
experiments in Molecular biology including PCR, restriction enzyme digestion, gel electrophoresis,
cloning, ligation, transformation, Protein expression &amp; purification in prokaryotic systems (e.g.
affinity chromatography, size exclusion chromatography) and characterization of proteins by SDS-
PAGE, Western blotting.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>

												<span class="label" style="background:#065A0B">BE 401</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="BE">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/be.png" /></i>
						
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Stem Cells: Science and Applications </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Stem Cells: Science and Applications [BE 403]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   1. Introduction to Stem Cells: Basic overview of stem cells--what they are, the history
of stem cell research, and the potential for stem cell therapies. Stem cells are found
throughout the tree of life.
2. How do Stem Cells work: How to induce stem cells to differentiate into particular
cell types? New stem cell-assisted technologies such as mitochondrial replacement
therapy and generating chimeras for organ transplantation. Background information
on the science and ethics of cloning, gene editing methods with special focus on gene
editing in stem cells. Combining genome editing and stem cell engineering as link
that explores the use of stem cells to understand disease and the use of stem cells as
therapeutics.
3. Using Stem Cells to study Diseases: How cultured cells can be used to understand
the cause of diseases and to look for drugs that will potentially cure them. We will
also explore how stem cells have the potential to help with the study of diseases like
hematological disorders, diabetes and Alzheimer&#39;s.
4. Using Stem Cells to Treat Diseases: Explore the potential for using stem cells in our
own bodies (adult stem cells) to treat hematological disorders, age-related macular
degeneration, neuronal-degeneration and like diseases. Some tools for evaluating
potential stem cell treatments will be covered in details.
5. The Bioethics of Stem Cell Research and Therapy: Discuss some of the controversies 
surrounding human embryonic stem cells and the legal, policy, and ethical challenges
associated with the use of stem cells in basic and clinical research. Discussion about
the future of reproductive technology and stem cell research.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#065A0B">BE 403</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="BE">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/be.png" /></i>
						
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Genetic Engineering – Principles and Applications </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Genetic Engineering – Principles and Applications [BE 404]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <strong>Module a. </strong>Introduction to genetic engineering + Fundamentals of molecular and cell biology <br>
Brief Introduction to the course and area of genetic engineering, Transcription, Translation, DNA replication and Recombination.<br>
<strong>Module b.</strong> Introduction to Recombinant DNA technology<br>
Polymerase Chain Reaction, Fundamental techniques of gene manipulation, Basic principles of gene cloning, Vectors for gene cloning, Purification of DNA from living cells, Isolation of desired Gene, Manipulation of purified DNA.<br>
<strong>Module c. </strong>Introduction of DNA into living cells <br>
Transgenics, chimeras, somatic and germ-line gene delivery, site directed mutagenesis, therapeutic reverse mutations, production of recombinant proteins in E.coli and other organisms.<br>
<strong>Module d:</strong> Principles of Gene editing<br>
CRISPR-Cas9 technology, genetic manipulation of microbes, plants and animals. <br>
<strong>Module e:</strong> Applications of gene cloning and DNA analysis in Biotechnology<br>
Gene cloning and DNA analysis in Medicine, Gene cloning and DNA analysis in Agriculture, Gene Cloning and DNA analysis in Forensic science and Archaeology, Ethical issues and concerns related to Genetically modified organisms.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Plant Biotechnology </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Plant Biotechnology [BE 405]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <strong>Module a.</strong> Introduction to Origin of plant biotechnology<br>
History and development of plant biotechnology<br>
<strong>Module b.</strong> Introduction to laboratory organization and techniques in tissue culture<br>
Laboratory organization methods and criteria; introduction to plant tissue culture; plasticity and totipotency; nutrition medium, sterilization techniques; cytodifferentiation; organogenic differentiation; types of cultures: seed culture, embryo culture, callus culture, organ culture, cell culture, protoplast culture; Micropropogation, generation of virus free plants; plant regeneration (somatic embryogenesis); cell suspension and secondary metabolites; invitro production of haploids (anther culture, microspore culture<br>
<strong>Module c.</strong> Special techniques in plant biotechnology<br>
Protoplast isolation; somatic hybridization; embryo rescue; somaclonal variation; germplasm storage and cryopreservation<br>
<strong>Module d:</strong> Transgenic technology for crop improvement<br>
Recombinant DNA technology; strategies for cloning genes from plants; gene transfer in plants; transgenics in crop improvement; current state of transgenic crops and GM crop regulation<br>
<strong>Module e.</strong> Beyond genetically modified crops <br>
Genome editing; genetic mapping; insertional mutagenesis and tilling; functional genomics.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Systems Biology </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                                               <h1>Systems Biology [BE 406]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to mathematical modeling of biological systems-<br>
Networks in biological systems- Reconstruction of networks from experimental data, Network motifs and their role in autoregulation, feed forward loops. Specific biological networks and their modeling- Transcription networks- Activators, Repressors, Input functions and Dynamics; Metabolic networks- Rate laws, Flux Analysis, Signal transduction networks- Static models and dynamic models. <br>
Robustness in biological circuits- Principle of robustness with examples of robustness in biological systems, detailed analysis of bacterial chemotaxis as robust biochemical system, Stochasticity in biological processes- introduction to stochastic modeling.<br>
Design of biological systems- Metabolic Engineering, Case Studies- synthetic biology, Emerging topics in systems biology- precision medicine, biomarker discovery using systems biology                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Cell Biology </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Cell Biology [BE 407]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Module a. Origin of Cell Biology<br>
History and development of Cell Biology; Invention of microscopes; Discovery of cells; Cell theory; Membrane and bulk phase theories; Steady-state membrane pump concept<br>
Module b. Cell structure and organization<br>
Cell as a unit of life, prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, difference between plant and animal cells, bio-membranes, Structure and functions of various cell organelles i.e., nucleus, ribosome, ER, golgi, lysosomes, peroxisome, exosomes, vacuoles, chloroplasts, cytoskeleton; Organization of tissues: Cell-cell and cell matrix interactions, cell adhesion molecules, composition of extracellular matrix, cellular junctions and role; Cellular movement: types of movement, cilia and flagella, muscle contraction, role of cytoskeletal proteins in movement<br>
Module c. How cells obtain energy from food<br>
Glycolysis, Citric acid cycle, Energy generation in mitochondria and chloroplasts, oxidative phosphorylation, electron transport, photosynthesis<br>
Module d: Cell cycle, Control of Cell Numbers and Cell Size <br>
G1, G2, S and M phase of the cell cycle, Cell cycle analysis and its applications, Programed cell death apoptosis vs necrosis, Mitogens stimulating cell division, growth factors<br>
Module e: Transport systems in cells <br>
Osmosis, active and passive transport, protein transporters, ion channels, protein sorting, vesicular transport, secretory pathways, endocytic pathways, exocytosis, micropinocytosis, role of cytoskeletal proteins in transport, molecular motors<br>
Module f: Cell signaling<br>
Principles of cell signaling, Concept or receptor-ligand, Signalling molecules, Receptor types: ion-channel-coupled receptor, G-protein Receptor, Enzyme coupled receptors<br>
Module g: Techniques used in cell biology and relevance of cell biology<br>
Histology, staining, microscopy, TEM and SEM, live cell imaging, Fluorescent dyes and genetically encoded fluorescent tags, cell culture, FACS; Understanding Cancer, Stem cells and application, Tissue engineering, Infectious diseases, manipulating cells for therapeutic purposes                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Applied Biological Engineering </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Applied Biological Engineering [BE 601]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Advanced themes that are an integral part of a modern Biological Engineering
graduate program will be explored in detail. A representative list of topics is given below:
<li> Nucleic acid in disease diagnosis: Detection of TB and Malaria by PCR, diagnosis of HIV by
qPCR, monitoring flu evolution and hereditary genetic tests.
<li> Antibiotic resistance: Prevalence and development of resistance in microbes. New
approaches to identification and management of resistance.
<li> Recombinant DNA technologies: Cloning vectors, mutagenesis methods, manipulation of
gene expression in prokaryotes, introduction to eukaryotic gene expression systems.
<li> DNA forensics: Sample collection, DNA extraction methods, DNA database searches, role of
bioinformatics, low level and degraded DNA detection.
<li> Waste water treatment and microbes: Aerobic and anaerobic oxidation, role of activated
sludge, microbial ecology of a water treatment plant, genetically engineered microbes.
<li> Biotechnology and clean energy: Renewable sources of energy, biofuel production by
microbes, hydrogen producing biological systems and challenges.
<li> Protein Engineering: Protein folding and recognition for protein design principles, Structure-
Based Combinatorial Protein Engineering (SCOPE), proteomics.
<li> Cancer Diagnostics: Diagnostics Imaging; X-rays, CAT-scan (computerized axial
tomography), Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), ultrasound. Tumor Markers Microarrays
in Cancer Diagnostics, Mass Spectrometry in Cancer Diagnostics.                                                                </p>

							<hr>

                                                               <h1>Biophysical Techniques [BE 603]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 1 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Molecular structure determination: Part A: X-ray crystallography, the single most important
technique used in determining the 3-D structure of macromolecules: Structural elucidation of
proteins, Nucleic acids (DNA). Crystallographic theory, exercises on crystallization, data
processing and model building (Docking, related software). Part B: Biophysical techniques are
covered that supplement the 3-D characterization of biological macromolecules: NMR (Amino
Acids, Mononucleotides, Proteins and Nucleic Acids) and mass spectroscopy (Fragmentation
studies: A tool for structural elucidation of proteins).                                                                </p>

mutagenesis, Sanger sequencing, heterologous expression systems.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Molecular Basis of Neurodegenerative Diseases </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Molecular Basis of Neurodegenerative Diseases [BE 608]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Overview of neurodegenerative diseases (NDs) including Alzheimer’s Disease (AD), Parkinson’s
Disease (PD), Huntington’s Disease (HD), Progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP) and prion disease;
Pathological events in human neurodegeneration; Genetic variation in inherited and sporadic disease;
Role of pathogenic proteins and their aggregation, Inter and intracellular transmission of toxic species
including Prion mechanism; Other prevalent disease mechanism hypotheses: Abnormal post-
translational modifications, ubiquitin proteasome system (UPS) impairment, autophagy dysfunction,
,oxidative stress and inflammation, microbial infection; Development of therapeutic approaches
including genome editing methods: latest trends and future perspective                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#065A0B">BE 608</span>

blocks, Energy currencies; Cellular processes at rest: Mechanical and Chemical Equilibrium of
biomolecules, Entropy considerations, Random walks and the structure of Macromolecules,
Electrostatics of intra and extracellular salty solutions, Biological membranes; Dynamics of cellular
processes: A statistical view of biological dynamics, Water and diffusion, Rate equations, dynamics of
molecular motors, Light and Electricity in biological systems; Organization of Biological Networks;
Biological Patterns in space and time.                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Biochemistry [BE 613]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Biomolecules: Chemical bonding, structure and function of carbohydrates, proteins, lipids
and nucleic acids, water and its properties.
<br>
Synthesis, separation and purification: biochemistry of cell membrane, isomerism,
subcellular fractions and their identification, protein separation and purification from
different host system, disorders of carbohydrate and lipid metabolism, peptide synthesis,
protein synthesis and post-translation modifications and it importance, protein
engineering, protein lithography and its applications.
<br>
Enzymes and their kinetics: enzymes, enzyme kinetics and enzyme regulation, enzyme
engineering and its applications.
<br>
Metabolism disorders: Inborn errors of metabolism, digestive diseases, disorders of liver
and kidney, principles of thermodynamics, bioenergetics and oxidative phosphorylation.
Blood clotting, electrolytes and acid-base balance, biological oxidation and muscle
contraction.
<br>
Nucleic acids: Structure and function of nucleotides, primary, secondary and tertiary
structure of nucleic acids, DNA and RNA forms and conformations and their biological
significance, denaturation and renaturation of DNA and RNA, in vivo and in vitro synthesis
of DNA and RNA and their applications.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Biostatistics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Biostatistics [BE 614]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The course is aimed at providing an understanding of statistical techniques used to
analyze biological and clinical data. The following topics will be covered:
<br>
1. Making sense of and comparing biological datasets (Probability distributions,
description of a population, measures of central tendency and dispersion,
hypothesis testing, analysis of variance, nonparametric methods, correlation, and
regression)
2. Rates and standardization (Rates, Methods for standardization of rates, cross-
sectional vs longitudinal studies, person-time observations, Inference on person-
time data)
3. Life tables (Computation of life tables, applications of life tables, years of potential
life lost)
4. Survival Analysis (life table method, product-limit method, log-rank test)
5. Clinical Trials (randomization, sample size calculation for clinical trials, statistical
power, inference on proportions)
6. Genetic Epidemiology (Twin studies, association studies, genome-wide
association studies, Manhattan plots, rare variant studies)
7. Risk (risk prediction, additive value of biomarkers, net reclassification index,
integrated discrimination improvement)
8. Scale construction and measurement (reliability, validity, responsiveness)
9. Measures of performance of diagnostic tests in clinical practice (Receiver
operating characteristic curves, sensitivity, specificity, positive and negative
predictive values, positive and negative likelihood ratios)
10. Common pitfalls and ethics in biostatistics reporting
11. Ethics in biological research (dealing with biological/human samples, research
integrity, informed consent, safety, privacy and data monitoring, rigor,
reproducibility)                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>

                                                               <h1>Bioinformatics and Computational Biology [BE 615]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Biological Data- origin (experimental, such as genome sequencing, macromolecular structure determination; and theoretical, such as structure prediction, mathematical modeling), type, representation, analysis and storage.<br>
Biological sequence analysis- databases- querying and retrieval, alignment- pairwise and multiple, Hidden Markov models, Phylogenetic trees- Bayesian and Maximum likelihood methods, RNA sequence and structure. Genome sequencing, Introduction to proteomics, transcriptomics and metagenomics<br>
Going 3D- Protein structure, function and evolution, databases (query and retrieval), alignment, protein structure prediction (secondary and tertiary)- conventional and recent deep learning methods, coevolution, protein folding, Introduction to in-silico drug discovery.<br>
Introduction to systems biology- biological networks- protein interaction networks, regulatory networks.<br>
Basics of biological computing- Python, Biopython and R- specific only to biological data analysis.<br>                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Human Physiology [BE 616]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>environment, cellular homeostasis, Transmembrane transport, active and passive.</p>
<p>Structure of a neuron, membrane potential, excitation and inhibition of nerve cells and signal propagation/conduction across axons, signal transmission between neurons or neurons and target cells.</p>
<p>Skeletal muscle and neuromuscular junction organization, Skeletal Muscle contraction, Excitation-Contraction Coupling, Functioning of smooth and cardiac muscle cells.</p>
<p>The Heart as a Pump, cardiac input and output volumes, cardiac blood pressure and its regulation, SA node function and its regulation, signal conduction from SA node to cardiac muscles, PQRS waves and basics of a normal electrocardiogram.</p>
<p>Architecture of the pulmonary and systemic circulations, arteries, veins, portal systems, peripheral blood pressure and its regulation. Lung-alveolar structural organization, Pulmonary Ventilation and regulation of Gas Exchange, Oxygen and CO2 transport, haemoglobin and RBCs, Chloride shift.</p>
<p>Capillaries, lymphatic system, lymphatic flow and functions. Body fluid compartments, Oedema, formation of urine, regulation of ion balance and pH. Cellular and molecular bases of blood fluidity and clotting. Basics of innate, acquired immunity, cellular and humoral immunity.</p>
<p>Neuroendocrine system (growth hormone, adrenal glands, thyroid hormones and reproduction-associated hormones), Hypothalamus-pituitary axis.</p>
<p>Structural organization of the digestive tract, digestion (carbohydrates, proteins and fats), absorption and transport, utilisation for energy or storage, insulin, glucagon, liver-muscle-adipose crosstalk in energy metabolism and lipoprotein-cholesterol transport, regulation of body core temperature.<br>
</p>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Novel Drug Delivery Technologies: Fundamental Principles and Engineering </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>

                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Novel Drug Delivery Technologies: Fundamental Principles and Engineering [BE 617]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <strong>Overview of drug delivery:</strong> Current landscape of therapeutics and medicinal products. Challenges in the development of new therapeutics. Processes of conventional drug delivery, dosage forms of drugs, delivery routes, biological barriers (Skin, systemic, and local) and toxicity. <br>
<strong>Novel Drug Delivery Technologies:</strong> Comprehensive overview of novel drug delivery technologies. Evolution of drug delivery technologies. Mechanisms of drug release (Drug release kinetics: Diffusion, degradation, bioerosion, swelling). Types of novel drug delivery technologies (Controlled drug delivery technologies, targeted drug delivery technologies (Passive and active, bio-responsive drug delivery technologies (pH, enzyme, and light), progress (Intra-articular drug delivery systems, local depots, and local drug therapies for bacterial infections), and promises. <br>
<strong>Wrap-up:</strong> Translational and clinical aspects of drug delivery technologies. Present and future advancements of drug delivery technologies. Development process of novel drug delivery technologies from concept to market. Regulatory, intellectual property consideration, industry and start-up opportunities.                                                                </p>

Case studies highlighting the applications of biotechnology in medicine, agriculture, forensic science, Archaeology and Evolutionary Biology; Genetic Engineering: Molecular cloning, generation of recombinant DNA, DNA transfer into living cells, Protein expression systems, purification of recombinant proteins, transgenics; Genetic manipulation: mutagenesis methods, targeted genome engineering, genome-scale screens.

Some core components include PCR, electrophoresis, restriction digestion, cloning by ligation, bacterial transformation and recombinant protein expression                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Membrane Transport: Active Transport, pumps, ion channels; Intracellular Transport: Transport of molecules between nucleus and cytosol, into mitochondria and chloroplasts, Peroxisomes, Endoplasmic Reticulum, Protein sorting; Vesicular Traffic: Transport from ER through Golgi, from Trans Golgi Network to Lysosomes, Endocytosis, Exocytosis; Cellular communication: signal transduction cascades(GPCR, Ras-MAPK, nuclear receptors etc.)  important for development,  differentiation, immune and hormonal response, cell proliferation and  disease (cancer, cardiovascular disorders etc)                                                                </p>

                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="BE">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/be.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Physical Biology of the Cell </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                                   Review of fundamentals of transport phenomena in biological systems: Mass conservation in microscopic & macroscopic systems, equation of continuity, shell balance approach, Momentum flux, fluid flow types, rheology in biological fluids, shear stress and shear rate, Newton’s law of viscosity, non-Newtonian fluids, boundary layers, properties of biological fluids, Steady state diffusion, unsteady state diffusion, pseudo steady state approximation, Thermal energy flux and equation of energy, temperature profile in a tissue, unsteady-state heat conduction; Transvascular Transport: Pathways for transendothelial transport: Continuous, fenestrated, and discontinuous capillaries, Rates of transvascular transport: Osmotic pressure, rate of fluid flow, and solute transport; Transport of gases between blood and tissues, Oxygen-hemoglobin equilibria and binding kinetics, dynamics of oxygenation of blood in lungs, Krogh cylinder model of oxygen transport in tissues, Nitric Oxide production and transport in tissues, Discrete capillary models and continuum porous approximations; Drug transport in solid tumors, Drug delivery in cancer, routes of drug administration, Quantitative analysis of transvascular transport, interstitial fluid transport, and interstitial transport of solutes; Mass and Heat Transport for Cell and Organ Preservation: Temperature dependence of diffusion and permeability, concentration changes during freezing                                                                </p>

Chemistry:Synthesis of Aspirin, a popular OTC drug, Removal of organic pollutant from water using UV light, Synthesis of a commercial polymer, Detection of pollutant gasses, Fingerprint detection, Recycling of solid waste, Organic photocatalysis, Water splitting experiment; Physics:Fraunhofer diffraction, Spectrometer, Operational amplifier, LCR circuit, Study of emission lines of hydrogen, Hall effect, Normal modes of a coupled pendulum,  Magnetic susceptibility measurements.                                                                </p>

                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Nanoscale Science [BS 401]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Surfaces at the Nanoscale – Particle shape and the surface (exterior surface area, interior surface
area); Surface and volume (specific surface area, spherical cluster approximation); Structural
magic numbers; Particle orientation (effect of size on surface polarization, quasi-static limit, effect
of orientation of nanoscale objects); Surface energy (fraction of surface atoms, energy of surface
atoms; lowering of surface energy); Chemical interactions at nanoscale; Surface reactivity and
catalysis; Surface stabilization (electrical double layer, surfactants and microemulsions, Ostwald
ripening, sintering); Synthesis and Assembly at the Nanoscale – Gibb’s energy at nanoscale;
Bottom-up approaches – gaseous phase (chemical vapor deposition, atomic layer deposition,

epitaxy, ion implantation, combustion, thermal decomposition), liquid phase (molecular self-
assembly, supramolecular chemistry, sol gel synthesis, electrolytic and electroless deposition),

and template synthesis(microporous template materials, mesoporous template materials); Top-
down approaches – mechanical , thermal, high energy, chemical, and lithographic; Self-assembly

for synthesizing hierarchical nanomaterials (assembling nanosized components); Societal
Implications of Using Nanomaterials – Overview of how nanomaterials are being applied (select
examples from photonics, plasmonics, energy harvesting and storage, environmental remediation,
devices); Ethical implications; Legal implications; Environmental implications (nanotoxicology, risk
assessment); Public perception; Future trends.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>

method; column analogy method; Introduction to matrix method. Introduction to using
structural analysis software for the analysis of simple structures/structural components.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">

                                                                   Background on stones, bricks, tiles, cement, steel, concrete, paints and polymers with
relevant discussions of IS code provisions; concrete mix design; durability of concrete.
<br>
Standard consistency, initial and final setting time of cement sample using Vicat’s
apparatus; Soundness of given sample of cement and lime by (Le‐Chatelier test, autoclave
test); Compressive strength of cement sample; Fineness of cement using (dry blank sieving,
Blaine’s air permeability method) + Specific gravity and water absorption of coarse
aggregate; Fineness modulus and particle size distribution, shape test and abrasion test of
coarse, fine, and all in aggregates; Consistency &amp; workability of freshly mixed concrete
(slump &amp; compaction test); Cube strength and cylinder strength of concrete of given
proportion and given water cement ratio; Tensile strength of steel; Compressive strength
and water absorption of burnt clay bricks and stone samples. Bitumen tests.                                                                </p>

																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 1 | 0 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Basic structural behavior and design of low-rise bearing wall buildings; Basic material
properties; Strength design of unreinforced masonry elements; Allowable stress design of
unreinforced masonry elements; Introduction to reinforced masonry; Introduction to confined
masonry.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:blue">CE 307</span>
												
						</div>

                                                               <h1>Hydrology and Hydraulics [CE 310]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 1.5 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Fundamentals: control volume approach, continuity, energy, momentum,
velocity distribution; Hydrologic processes: introduction to hydrology, precipitation,
evaporation, and infiltration; Surface runoff: drainage basins and storm hydrograph,
hydrologic losses, unit hydrograph; Streamflow routing: hydrologic reservoir routing,
hydrologic river routing, hydraulic routing; Extreme Value Statistics: Generalized Extreme
Value Theory for return levels; Open channel flow: steady uniform flow, specific energy and
specific forces, steady and gradually varied flow, rapidly varied flow, discharge measurement;
Groundwater flow: groundwater concepts, saturated flow, steady state one dimensional
flow, steady state well hydraulics, transient well hydraulics, and boundary effects                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Design of Reinforced Concrete Structures </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 1.5 | 5							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Design of Reinforced Concrete Structures [CE 311]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 1.5 | 5<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Reinforced Concrete Structures and Design Philosophy: Working stress (WS) and limit state (LS) design philosophy; Factor of safety vs partial safety factor; and Characteristic strength vs characteristic load; Design of Typical Structural Components: Beams (WS and LS methods; Singly vs doubly reinforced; and Flexure and shear actions); Slabs (One-way and Two-way; Deflection check; and Yield line theory); Columns (Columns under axial load and bi-axial moment; P-M-M interaction; and Slender columns and secondary effects); Foundation (Isolated footing with axial and uniaxial / biaxial moment; and Combined footing); Staircase (Dog-legged; and Three flights); and Shear walls (per IS 13920); Introduction to Seismic Design: Seismic load as per IS 1893; Capacity Design per IS 13920; and Design of beam-column joints; Introduction to Design of Flat-Slab and Raft Foundation (Structural Design): Equivalent frame method; and Analysis using FE software; Introduction to Prestressed Concrete Design: Philosophy; Losses in prestressing; and Design of prestressed concrete beam; Testing of Cement: Standard consistency, Initial and final setting time and Soundness. Fineness (Dry blank sieving, Blain's Air permeability) and Specific gravity; Testing of Aggregate: Bulking of sand, Water absorption, Bulk density of course and fine aggregate; Sieve analysis and fineness modulus of course and fine aggregate; Abrasion resistance, Impact toughness, flakiness and elongation test; Testing of Fresh and Hardened Concrete: Air content, Slump cone test, Compaction factor test and Flow table test; Compressive strength of concrete cube and cylinder and mortar cube; Concrete mix-design; Non-destructive Testing of Concrete: Ultrasonic pulse velocity test and rebound hammer tests.<br>
Active Learning Components: Architectural planning, design and detailing of a low-rise RCC building in a group-project; Survey of a construction site and preparing a brief report describing the observations on i) formworks, ii) construction sequence, iii) reinforcement detailing etc. This report will map onto the background knowledge imparted during the lecture classes; Laboratory testing of concrete in the fresh and hardened states and the mix-design of concrete for a targeted strength                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Design of Steel Structures [CE 312]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of Design Philosophy: Working stress (WS), Ultimate load (UL), Load resistant factor design (LRFD), and Limit state (LS); characteristics load vs strength; Load combinations; and Partial safety factors; Elementary Plastic Analysis: Moment-curvature relationship; Plastic hinges; and Collapse load and Mechanism; Design of Pure Tension and Pure Compression Members: Bolted vs welded members; Theory of elastic buckling with and without imperfection; Long and short column effects; and P-Delta effect; Design of Beams and Plate Girders: Kirchhoff’s plate bending theory; Plate buckling; Torsional and Lateral-torsional buckling; Design of laterally supported and unsupported beams; and Rolled steel vs built-up sections ; Design of Beam-Columns: Combined effect of axial and flexure (P-M interaction), Design of columns subjected to axial force and uniaxial / biaxial moment; and Rolled steel vs built-up sections; Design of Beam-Column Joints: Strong vs weaker axis connections; Moment-shear connection, Torsion-shear connection; and Bolted vs welded connections; Design of Column-Base Plate Connections: Bolted vs welded connections; Axial force and uniaxial / biaxial moment; and Uplift and Anchor bolts.  <br>   
Active Learning Components: Design and detailing of one of the followings in a group project. Design of an Industrial Shed, Design of a Moment-Resisting Frame Building, Design of a Gantry-Girder system                                                                </p>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                               <h1>Environmental Science and Engineering [CE 313]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of fundamental concepts: absorption, adsorption, reaction kinetics, dispersion, diffusion, electrochemistry, and biochemistry; fundamental of combustion and pollutant formation. Types of environmental pollution: air, water, soil, and noise pollution along with solid and hazardous waste such as nuclear, chemical, and bio; causes/sources of pollution, impacts, and extent; standards and regulations for each type of pollution; in case of water pollution, treatment technologies (source to end-user and end-user to discharge); in case of air pollution, transformation, transport, and the fate of air pollutants. indoor vs outdoor air pollution, and mitigation technologies and strategies. Environmental Management: Introduction to environmental impact assessment, environmental audit, environmental monitoring Hands-on component: Laboratory tours, instrument orientations, basic tests such as water characterization (alkalinity, pH, TDS, etc.), basic air quality instrumentation and measurements, and modeling of pollutant transport. Filed visit and course project: A field visit to a water or waste treatment facility, thermal power plant, or any other relevant site. A hands-on group project involving but not limited to data collection, data analysis, or sample analysis using the in-campus resources accessible to undergraduate students.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Geotechnical Engineering </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>

							<br>1.5 | 1.5 | 3 | 4							</h4>

                                                               <h1>Geotechnical Engineering [CE 314]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 1.5 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Geotechnical investigations, reconnaissance and investigation plan, drilling, sampling, field-tests, groundwater level, laboratory tests, etc.; Stress-strain-strength behavior of soils, Triaxial tests – UU, CU and CD tests, stress paths, Skempton’s pore-pressure parameters, sample disturbance; Stability of slopes, limit equilibrium methods, ordinary methods of slices and simplified Bishop method, factors of safety; Seepage and stability of earth embankments, Types of foundations - shallow/deep, isolated, combined, mat, etc., contact pressure distributions, soil - foundation interactions, basics of structural design; Design of shallow foundation, bearing capacity, stress distribution in soils, total  and differential settlement, plate-load test, structural forces and design; Retaining structures, gravity, cantilever, counterfort, reinforced earth, etc., design and checks for stability; Design of deep foundations, piles, pile groups, well foundations, shaft and base resistances, downdrag, pile load tests. Introduction to Geosynthetics and reinforced earth structures.                                                                </p>

                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Civil Engineering Materials </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">

                                                               <h1>Civil Engineering Materials [CE 315]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   1. Introduction: selection of materials, engineering properties, nature of materials.<br>
2.Iron and Steel: production process, microstructure, phase change diagrams, properties and test methods, corrosion.<br>
3.Cement: production process; types of cements; composition; hydration reactions; chemical and physical properties. <br>
4.Aggregates: types of aggregates; experimental methods to determine physical properties &ndash; water absorption, density.<br>
5.Chemical Admixtures: Types of admixtures; working mechanisms; applications.<br>
6.Concrete: Mixture proportioning, types of concrete, fresh properties, hardened properties, durability. <br>
7.Bituminous materials: production process, asphalt grades and properties, asphalt concrete<br>
8.Wood and wood products: properties, shrinkage and seasoning, treatment and durability<br>
9.Masonry: masonry units; masonry construction: types of bonds and joints; mortar, grout, and plaster; properties. <br>
10.Plastics and polymers: types, properties, and applications<br>
11.Composites 12.Sustainability                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Comprehensive Project - 1 </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>0 | 0 | 3 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">

                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Comprehensive Project - 1 [CE 401]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>0 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   A big construction project will be considered and will be sub-divided into several components.
Teams of students will act as separate ‘consulting agencies’ and will carry out complete
planning, analysis, design and construction planning for these components. The teams will be
required to merge their designs of individual components of the entire project towards the end
and present a consolidated plan, design and construction plan of the entire project.                                                                </p>
