							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Structural Design for Fire </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Structural Design for Fire [CE 615]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of performance-based design concepts, LRFD cold design of RCC and steel structures;
codified models for fire, standard and natural fire; material behavior of steel and concrete
constituents in fire; estimation of time-temperature curves for structural members in different fire
and exposure/ventilation scenarios; design considerations for individual members exposed to
fire: structural members in tension, compression, bending; provisions in Eurocodes and IS 456,
800, 1641, 1642 and 1643 to ensure fire safety; fire proofing materials used to enhance fire
resistance of structural members; behavior of structural systems under fire;
preventive/protective systems for structures.                                                                </p>

                                                    </div>

                                                               <h1>Structural Dynamics [CE 622]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Single Degree Freedom System (equation of motion; free and force vibrations; seismic excitation;
time history analysis; response spectrum; approximate methods), Multiple Degrees Freedom
System (eigenvalue problem; shear buildings; mode superposition method; modal combination
rules; time history analysis), One story system (lateral-torsional coupling; non-orthogonal lateral
coupling; directional combination rule) and introduction to continuum systems (flexural beam, its
natural properties, response due to seismic excitation)                                                                </p>

overflow,levee, river bed lining,bridge pier protection due to river water flow;Design of bridges, dams,
spillways; Irrigation control structures; Indian standards related to river engineering; Flow measurements
in rivers; Physical modeling of river flows;Protection of river water from pollution;Introduction to various
Indian agencies (CWC, NIH, CWPRS etc.); Field trip to a river;Project work .                                                                </p>

characterization of seismic hazard, seismic analysis and design of bridges, retaining walls,
liquid-storage tanks, dams etc., design of non-structural components, passive structural
control                                                                </p>

                                                                   Historical background, Types, Manufacturing Methods, Functions, Typical Applications; Geosynthetic
Testing, Physical, Mechanical, Construction survivability and durability testing of Geotextiles and

(creep etc.), Elements of Design + NCMA Method, Fill Material, Detailed Design of Reinforced Soil Walls
(BS 8006), Reinforced Slopes (BS 8006), Facia and Construction Methodology, Specifications, Bearing
capacity Improvement, Embankments on soft soils, Basal Reinforcement – Design, Geocell – Design,

Principles, Typical applications, Dams, French Drains, Testing, Design Principles, Specifications;
Pavements and Airports; Influence of cyclic loading, Design for soft soils (unpaved) and new construction –
Granular layers, GS in Bituminous Pavements – new construction and rehabilitation, Construction and
specifications, Rural Road applications, Railway Tracks, Indian experiences;
Natural fibre Materials, Jute and Coir Geotextiles - Products, Processes; Environmental Control, Erosion
Control, Mechanics of Erosion, Methods of Control, Products, Design for various conditions, Silt Control,
Land slides – Occurrence and methods of mitigation, Liners for Pounds and Canals; Engineered Landfills,
Municipal Solid Waste, Hazardous Waste Landfills, Site location –MOEF Guidelines, Covers and liners,
GCLs, Material aspects and, Design, construction and maintenance, the Indian Scenario                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Advanced Concrete Design </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Advanced Concrete Design [CE 632]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of Design Philosophy: Characteristic load and strength, WSM / ULM /
LRFD / LSM, Limit States: ULS / SLS, Probabilistic framework; Essential
Structural Components: Estimation of wind and earthquake load; Design of
beam and column; Design of beam-column-joint with ductile detailing; Design of
Shear wall--Solid with boundary elements--Degree of coupling--Shear lag--
Flanged and Shear wall core; Strut-and-Tie Model based Design: Conceptual
design; Application to the design of--Deep Beam--Shear Wall--Corbel--Beam-
column joint--Secondary beam and beam-to-beam connection--Diaphragm-to-
beam connection and flanged action; Yield Line Theory in Regular Slab;
Design of Flat plate and Flat Slab; Design of Foundation Systems: Isolated
and Strip; Combined and Raft; Pile; Overall Structural Design (One of the
three modules in a project based mode)— Module-1: Design of Liquid
Retaining Structures--Water tank at ground--Elevated water tank with staging--
-Module-2: Design of Mid-to-High rise Buildings---Understanding through
different structural configurations--Shear wall core--Tube system and Tube-in-
Tube system---Module-3: Design of Bridges---Highway or Railway---Loading,
load combination and different limit states--Bridge deck---Piers---
Foundation—pre-stressed concrete bridges.
It is proposed to cover one module in special topics depending on the instructor.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Infrastructure Systems: Planning and Management </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">

                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Infrastructure Systems: Planning and Management [CE 633]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   1. Introduction and System modeling: System Components, Planning and Management, Case study: modeling of water resource systems, Supply and Demand Curves for Infrastructure systems.<br>
2. Introduction to optimization: Modeling Constraints, Linear Programming Solution Strategies: Graphical and simplex methods, Dynamic Programming and stochastic programming, Python for Water Resource Management: Introduction to GEKKO, and PULP.<br>
3. Engineering cost-benefit analysis: Premise of cost-benefit analysis; Time value of Money; Comparison of alternatives including uncertainty analysis; Decision trees (Deterministic and probabilistic); Life cycle cost analysis<br>
4. Risk assessment: Probabilistic Risk and Reliability Assessment; Risk Aversion; Non-stationary risk analysis; Fragility curves for Infrastructure systems<br>
5. Network modeling for Infrastructure systems: Critical Infrastructure Fundamentals; Network Analysis; Robustness and tipping point analysis; Network of Network modeling; Recovery Modeling of networked systems,<br>
6. Future of Critical Infrastructure Systems: Integrated risk and resilience management policy, resilient design: best practices and opportunities.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Air Pollution Control Engineering </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Air Pollution Control Engineering [CE 634]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Overview of air pollution: Types of air pollutants (classified into two categories): (1) Particulate matter (PM) and (2) gaseous pollutants (volatile organic compounds, sulfur oxides, nitrogen oxides, ozone, CO2); common sources; health and environmental impacts of air pollutants; the global and national context of air pollution: evolution of policies and regulations and the current status</p>
<p>Particulate Matter (PM): Particle size distribution and size-dependent transport properties such as settling, impaction, interception, and diffusion/Brownian motion; cyclones and impactors: operating principle, design, and sizing; electrostatic precipitator (ESP): operating principle, drift velocity, collection efficiency, and design and sizing of ESPs (including area, number of plates, and sections); fabric filters: operating principle, surface velocity, collection efficiency, and its temporal variation; baghouse filters: types of baghouse filters, design, and sizing, including size and number of bags and operating schedule; face mask and introduction to airborne transmission of coronavirus (an example of bio-particulate matter); scrubbers: operating principle and design; applications of the same principles for COVID and other airborne disease mitigation</p>
<p>Sulfur oxides and nitrogen oxides: Elementary oxidation-reduction chemistry; fuel desulfurizations; limestone scrubbing; thermal and fuel NOx; combustion modification as a control strategy for NOx</p>
<p>Volatile organic compounds (VOCs): Review of fundamentals of vapor pressure, multi-phase equilibrium, and diffusivity; oxidation chemistry and kinetics of VOC incineration; design and sizing of thermal oxidizers; VOC prevention (substitution, process modification, and leakage control or fugitive emissions); VOC capture via adsorption: physical and chemical adsorption and isotherms; fixed-bed adsorption system: design and sizing and breakthrough curves (operating schedule); VOC capture via absorption: design and sizing of absorption tower</p>
<p>CO2 and ozone (O3): Global warming effects of CO2; CO2 capture technologies; Overview of CO2 sequestration; atmospheric chemistry of ozone and interplay with other pollutants; photochemical smog</p>
<p>Air pollution and meteorology: Wind circulations; lapse rates and inversions; atmospheric dispersion modeling; Gaussian plume model (downwind concentration and ground-level concentration from a point source emissions); plume rise and stack height determination</p>
<p>Other topics to be covered: Mercury control; mobile sources (motor vehicles): IC engine and diesel engine operation, air-fuel ration, formation of pollutants, catalytic converter; indoor air quality: differences from outdoor air, sources, material balance </p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>

                                                                   <p>Cement: Production process; types of cements; composition; hydration reactions; chemical and physical properties; characterization techniques – XRF, XRD, particle size analysis;  Aggregates: Types of aggregates; experimental methods to determine physical properties – water absorption, density, abrasion and impact resistance, particle size distribution; Supplementary Cementitious Materials (SCMs): Types of SCMs; chemical and physical properties; hydraulic and pozzolanic reactions; SCM reactivity tests – strength activity index, lime reactivity, R3 test; Chemical Admixtures: Types of admixtures; working mechanisms; applications; Concrete Mixture Proportioning – high performance concrete; performance-based mixture proportioning; Fresh Properties: Rheology; advanced tests to determine fresh properties of special concretes such as self-compacting concrete; pumping of concrete – high-rise buildings, 3D concrete printing; Hardened Properties: Mechanical properties – compression, tensile, flexural strength tests; creep; shrinkage – chemical, plastic, and drying; Transport Properties: Pore structure; diffusion; permeability ; Concrete Durability: Corrosion; carbonation; sulfate attack; alkali-aggregate reactions; freezing and thawing damage. The deterioration mechanisms and advanced tests to evaluate them. </p>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">AI for Civil Engineering </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>AI for Civil Engineering [CE 639]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Mathematical foundations of machine learning: linear algebra, probability, optimization, and vector calculus; supervised and unsupervised learning methods: regression, classification, clustering, and dimensionality reduction; neural networks and deep learning: feedforward, convolutional, recurrent, and attention-based architectures; transfer learning: fine-tuning for small and heterogeneous datasets; integration of physics and domain knowledge: physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) and hybrid loss formulations; operator learning: DeepONet, Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs), Graph Neural Operators (GNOs), and transformer-based operator networks for spatio-temporal modeling; fully differentiable architectures: differentiable physics simulators and end-to-end learnable pipelines for governing processes; gray-box and hybrid modeling frameworks: combining mechanistic equations with learnable residuals; uncertainty quantification and explainable AI: SHAP, LIME, and feature-attribution methods for model interpretability; applications in hydrology and water resources: rainfall–runoff modeling, flood forecasting, groundwater flow and recharge estimation; environmental systems: air quality prediction, pollutant dispersion, and coupled hydro-climatic modeling; transportation systems: traffic flow and travel-time prediction using graph and transformer networks, pavement performance prediction models; applications in geotechnical engineering: slope stability and landslide prediction using convolutional networks, liquefaction potential prediction models, soil classification models; structural and material systems: constitutive relations, crack detection, and damage localization through physics-guided neural networks, surrogate models for design; integration with urban digital twins and resilience analytics: linking data-driven insights with decision-making for sustainable and climate-adaptive infrastructure; ethical, interpretable, and trustworthy deployment: responsible use of AI/ML in civil and environmental domains                                                                </p>

                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Hydrodynamics of Sediment Transport </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Hydrodynamics of Sediment Transport [CE 640]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Sediment properties: Physical properties of sediment, sediment sizes, sediment mixture, suspended sediment mixture, terminal fall velocity of sediment in fluid; Turbulent flow characteristics: Introduction to turbulence, decomposition and averaging procedure, Reynolds-averaged Navier–Stokes (RANS) equations, Reynolds shear stress in a zero-pressure gradient flow, the law of the wall, the log-wake law and the velocity defect law, secondary currents and dip phenomenon; Sediment threshold: Definition of sediment threshold, threshold velocity concept, Yang’s threshold velocity model, lift force concept, threshold bed shear stress concept, Shields diagram, probabilistic concept of sediment entrainment, turbulence-induced concept of sediment entrainment, stable channel design, straight trapezoidal channels, stable-ideal section of a threshold channel; Bedload transport: Definition of bedload transport, bed shear stress concept of bedload transport, du Boys’ approach, du Boys-type equations, discharge concept of bedload transport, velocity concept of bedload transport, bedform concept of bedload transport, probabilistic concept of bedload transport, Einstein’s approach, Engelund and Fredsøe’s approach, deterministic concept of bedload transport, Bagnold’s approach, Yalin’s approach, saltation, saltation characteristics, particle trajectory and characteristic parameters, streambed armoring and water-worked bed; Suspended-load transport: Diffusion concept of suspended-load transport, generalized advection–diffusion equation for suspended sediment motion, governing equation for sediment concentration distribution, various approaches to determine sediment concentration distribution, reference level and reference concentration, computation of suspended load by diffusion approach, energy concept of suspended-load transport, wash load, turbidity current; Total-load transport: Indirect approach, direct approach, total-load transport of nonuniform sediment; River processes: Bed forms, bars, prediction of bedforms, mathematical developments on sand waves, bed features in gravel-bed streams, resistance to flow due to bedforms, meandering rivers, braided rivers; Scour: Scour within channel contractions, scour downstream of structures, scour below horizontal pipelines, scour at bridge piers, scour at bridge abutments, scour countermeasures                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Computation and Cognition </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Computation and Cognition [CG 501]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Mechanistic/algorithmic approaches and issues addressed by these approaches: parallel versus serial processing, flow of information, timing effects.
Rational/probabilistic approaches and issues addressed by these approaches: adaptation to the environment, behaviour under uncertainty, learning, timing effects.
General issues: top-down versus bottom-up processing, online processing, integration of multiple sources of information.
Methodology and issues in the development and evaluation of cognitive models: Which psychological data are relevant? What predictions are made by a model? How could these be tested?
Modelling techniques: in the assignments, students will experiment with both symbolic (rule based) and subsymbolic (probabilistic) cognitive models.
Connectionist models | Bayesian models |Dynamic system models |Logic/symbol-based models | Classical cognitive architectures/production models | Unified Theories of Cognition, Cognitive Architectures and Frameworks. COGENT as a modelling framework
SOAR and ACT-R as cognitive architectures | Models of specific cognitive functions                                                                </p>

                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Foundations of Cognitive Science </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Foundations of Cognitive Science [CG 502]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The course aims to give an overview of different component disciplines of cognitive science and
how they come together to study brain and cognition. The topics will include:
Philosophy: Theories of mind (Indian &amp; Western), Consciousness, Ontological and
epistemological approaches to Mind
Artificial Intelligence: cybernetics, Information theories, Turing machine, knowledge
representation, neural networks
Linguistics: Ancient India, structuralism, formalism, generative grammar, Chomsky’s arguments
against behaviorism, syntax and semantics
Anthropology: Evolutionary and alternative theories, diffusion, functional school, culture and
language, personality research, structural, symbolic anthropology
Psychology: associationism, psychophysics, psychometrics, cognitivism, metacognition,
constructivism, evolutionary psychology
Neuroscience: neuron, reflex arc, brain structure and function, disorders (deficit), stimulation,
Hebbian theory, experimental methods (electrophysiology, imaging), language &amp; lateralization                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>

                                                               <h1>Research Methods in Cognitive Science [CG 504]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 1.5 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Research methods: Null Hypothesis formulation &amp; testing, dependent &amp; independent variables,
qualitative, quantitative and categorical data, measurement scales, descriptive and inferential
statistics (measures of central tendency, significance testing, ANOVA, repeated measures,
posthoc testing, correlations, chi-square tests); Experimental design:single subject, between-
group and within-group designs, randomization, factorial, parametric, subtractive, conjunction
type designs; Research Tools: Review of basic mathematics, experimental psychology software
(psychtoolbox, eprimeetc), Statistics using PSPP/Matlab/R; Research ethics and scientific
writing with reference to APA guidelines.                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Experimental Techniques in Cognitive Science [CG 506]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 1.5 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to experimental design – Hypothesis; Independent and dependent variables;
principles of Reaction Time studies.
Applying statistical techniques – lab based module primarily intended to create a practical
awareness of statistical testing. This will include many example data datasets and deciding what
an effective test is and how to do it.
Data interpretation – what does significant statistical results mean. How does these numbers
inform about mental processes.
Writing – Including effective writing techniques; how to report statistics.
Hands on experience in different behavioural and imaging methodology including eyetracking,
EEG etc.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Evolutionary Neuropsychology </h4>
							<hr>

                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Evolutionary Neuropsychology [CG 507]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Paleoneuropsychology is the study the evolution of the structures and functions of the
human brain. It is an emerging multidisciplinary science that spans the fields of
anthropology, archaeology, cognitive neuroscience, and psychology. This course is
designed as introduction to the specific structures and functions of the human brain with
particular attention to empirical brain research advances since 2000. It is also designed as
an introduction to the evolution of the human brain (size, shape, neurons, chemical
neurotransmitters, etc.) and the evolution of human cognitive processes such as memory,
language, dreaming, and creativity.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Behavioral Economics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Behavioral Economics [CG 513]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction: Historical development of the field, interdisciplinary perspectives linking psychology,
economics and neuroscience, Overview of the field with real life examples.
Modes of thought, Heuristics and biases: Modes of thought (intuitive and deliberative), heuristics
(anchoring and availability, representativeness), Biases (Framing effects, mental accounting), taming
intuitive heuristics, self-regulation in the brain, The positive side of heuristic decision making.
Choice under certainty - concept of preference in rational choice theory, revealed preference,
decision making under certainty.
Choice under uncertainty – The concept of value and utility under uncertainty, psychological
weighting of probabilities, deciding about prospects, gains and losses, Neural representations of
subjective value and choices in the brain.
Thinking over time - Discounted utility, hyperbolic discounting, affective forecasting biases,
retrospective experiential biases for value computation.
Applying behavioral economics - Marketing examples from prospect theory, choice architecture,
Real examples of nudges for better health, wealth and happiness.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Cognitive Linguistics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Cognitive Linguistics [CG 517]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Basics of Cognitive Linguistics: This module introduces theoretical assumptions and commitments of Cognitive Linguistics, a functional approach to language use contrasted by formal approaches. In that sense, a fundamental question is asked: “What does it mean to know a language?” Also, two main aspects of Cognitive Linguistics—Language and Cognition—are interpreted; Linguistic Relativity: Beginning with the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis, this module taps into uniformity and diversity across languages in regard to the associated processing and behavior. The main discussion is on cross-linguistic variations in conceptualizations of time, space, and color; Categorization: Projection of how mental lexicon and concepts operate in the human mind.   To understand the hierarchy of concepts, the association of lexical items as well as lexicon-internal polysemy are explored. Theories including the prototype theory are discussed and compared. It leads to the semantic basis of grammatical category; Mental Spaces: Cases of metaphor, metonymy, meronomy, and blends are analyzed with cross-linguistic points of view. The discussion components include the Conceptual Metaphor Theory and the Mental Space Theory; Cognitive Approaches to Grammar: This module discusses the structural consideration of human language processing. It covers idioms, Construction grammar, and Cognitive grammar.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#8924EC">CG 517</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="CG">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/cg.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Motor Learning and Memory </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Motor Learning and Memory [CG 601]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Motor systems in the brain – motor and premotor cortices, parietal and frontal cortices, basal ganglia and
cerebellum; Approaches to studying motor learning – defining and measuring motor learning, designing
experiments on learning, alternative methods for measuring learning, issues about “amount” of learning,
understanding differences between learning and changes in performance; Learning of motor sequences –
implicit versus explicit learning, chunking, role of sleep, neural basis, disruption in different brain disorders;
Adaptation learning – adaptation to novel dynamical and visual perturbations, sensory signals responsible for
adaptation, generalization and transfer of learning, elementary computational models, neural basis; Learning
under unstable and unpredictable conditions – motor noise, impedance control, feedforward and feedback
components of impedance control; Motor skill learning – use dependent learning, “model-free”
reinforcement learning, neural basis; Vocal learning in songbirds – role of variability and use of exploration,
critical period, neural circuits, application to human speech learning; Application to neurorehabilitation and
robotics – motor learning principles in rehab, robot-assisted rehab, error augmentation strategies, model of
motor recovery after stroke, concurrent force and impedance adaptation in robots, robotic implementation.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#8924EC">CG 601</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="CG">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/cg.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Classics in Brain Science </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 2							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Classics in Brain Science [CG 602]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Half semester course examining, at an advanced level, classic research that has shaped current understanding
of brain function. Following topics will be covered:
1. Generation and transmission of neuronal signals (
2. Intercellular communication
3. Vision
4. Proprioception (Processing of somatosensory information and organization of somatosensory cortex.
Classic experiments by Vallbo, Matthews and Willis)
5. Spinal Reflexes (Classic experiments on reflex circuit organization by Sherrington, which won him the
Nobel prize; work by Matthews, Eccles and Lundberg on reflex physiology).
6. Control of voluntary movement (organization of motor cortex, descending projections from motor
cortical areas, movement planning and control all examined via studies of Evarts, Georgopoulos, Strick
and Kalaska)
7. Learning and Memory (Cortical basis of learning and memory, two learning/memory systems, retention
and recall of memories as explained by the definitive work of Squire, Poldrack and Nobel prize winner
Kandel)
Modern techniques used in research in the above areas will be introduced alongside the discussion on each
topic.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#8924EC">CG 602</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="CG">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/cg.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->

                                                               <h1>Perception and Attention [CG 603]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Information processing view and ecological approach to perception; The visual anatomy and physiology;
Psychophysical methods; Perception of color, motion and depth; Theories of attention – FIT, CODE, TVA
etc; Mechanisms of selection; locus of control; Inhibitory processes in selection etc; Experimental
paradigms (cueing, visual search, and phenomena like in attentional blindness and attentional blink)                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Phenomenology, Embodiment, and Consciousness </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Phenomenology, Embodiment, and Consciousness [CG 604]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This course introduces Phenomenology, the various forms that it takes (Transcendental,
Genitive, Existential, Feminist, Post-, Neuro), its emphasis on description (particularly first-
person experience), and the role of the body in consciousness. Phenomenology as a
method is oriented around what is called the ‘époché’ or bracketing (phenomenological
reduction), wherein the subjective concerns of an individual are placed ‘in brackets’ in order
to move toward an accurate description of the things themselves. We will discuss whether
or not such a bracketing is possible, the role of Phenomenology in description and pay
particularly close attention to concepts such as sedimentation, constitution, institution,
intentionality, incorporation, extension, habit, bodily motility, spatiality, intersubjectivity, and
body/mind dualism as they are presented through the work of founding figure of
Phenomenology, Edmund Husserl, the Existential Phenomenology variation and focus on
the body disclosed in the work of Maurice Merleau-Ponty, and the potential for intertwining
Phenomenology and Cognitive Science in the more recent disclosure of
Neurophenomenology.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Psychological theories of learning, behavioral and cognitive approach to learning. Classical
conditioning, reinforcement learning and Motor learning. Types of Associations, Biological
constraints on classical conditioning. Procedures of shaping behavior, Role of reinforcer.
Theories of memory, Storage, Encoding and retrieval, Types of Memory, techniques of testing
memory. Localization of memory, Mechanisms of Memory, memory disorders
Neurobiology of learning and memory, functional network of brain areas involved in learning
and memory                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Philosophy of Mind </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Philosophy of Mind [CG 606]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Cognitive Science and philosophy of mind: Epistemic and metaphysical issues of mind in
Cognitive science
Western theories of mind: Dualism, behaviorism, materialism, eliminativism functionalism,
physicalism, phenomenology, representational theory of mind, modularity of mind and identity
theory
Neurobiological approaches to mind: Patricia Churchland arguments, the binding problem, the
problem of Mary&#39;s Knowledge, Connectionism
Computational approaches to mind: Searle&#39;s Chinese room argument, intentionality, the
problem of intelligence and the representational nature of mind
Indian theories of mind: Heterodox: Jainism and Buddhism; Orthodox: Samkhya, Nyaya and
Vedanata                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Emotion and Cognition </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 1 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Emotion and Cognition [CG 607]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 1 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Historically, cognition and emotion were considered two independent processes that did not interact
much with each other. Moreover, the study of emotions was considered subjective and therefore
unscientific in a scientific world dominated by behaviourism. However, recent developments in cognitive
neuro-sciences have enabled the study of emotions in a scientific manner and have increasingly shown
evidence to its interconnectedness to cognition. What are the implications of such inter-connectedness?
How do our feelings affect our thoughts, memory, decision making etc. and vice-versa?
Topics will include: nature and measurement of emotions, categorical and dimensional approaches to
affect, Affect cognition relations in specific cognitive processes like perception, attention, memory and
judgement.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Neural Plasticity </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Neural Plasticity [CG 612]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The emergence of plasticity: experience-dependent plasticity in cats (monocular deprivation), Hubel & Weisel’s experiments, the concept of ‘critical period’, developmental plasticity and correlation with cognitive development<br><br>

Adult plasticity: plasticity following peripheral deafferentation in adults, Merzenich & Kaas’ experiments, limits and phases of adult plasticity, phantom limb phenomenon, peripheral vs CNS plasticity (stroke), motor plasticity and performance - expertise and time course of plasticity.
<br><br>
Cross-modal plasticity: somatosensory plasticity following blindness, Molyneux question, plasticity in the newly sighted, visual cortex of deaf, rewiring of cortex in animal models (Pallas and Sur experiments)
<br><br>
Neural mechanisms of plasticity: axonal sprouting, dendritic spine dynamics, synaptic plasticity ( LTD, LTP), Hebbian and homeostatic plasticity, the molecular and genetic basis of plasticity mechanisms
<br><br>
Plasticity for cognitive enhancement: mechanisms for reintroducing critical period plasticity in animals, FastForWord - Merzenich and Paula Talal, brain training apps, stereopsis training for adult plasticity                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Analysis and Modelling of Locomotion </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 2							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Analysis and Modelling of Locomotion [CG 613]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to the basics of anatomy and physiology of locomotion, evaluations methods in clinics, and gait disorder; Spatio-temporal gait analysis. Walking phase detection, measurement of stride length, stride velocity, cadence, and other spatio-temporal parameters. Gait symmetry, gait variability, and gait coordination measurement. Clinical gait analysis. Practical examples to clinics, dual-task paradigm, and modelling relevant analogies of equivalents of locomotion; Kinematics approach for gait analysis, kinematics measurement, and motion capture. Stereo-photogrammetry, ultrasound, and magnetic motion capture. Accelerometers, gyroscopes, magnetometers and inertial-based motion capture systems, Lab vs. daily life monitoring, anatomical and functional calibration, joint kinematics, and application in orthopedics; Kinetics of locomotion. Forces and moment measurements- Forces transducers and force plates, pressure measuring systems and pressure insoles, combining kinetics with kinematics, energy, and power of body segment inverse dynamics, muscular activity. Application to knee adduction moment measurement; Neural basis of locomotion. Spinal circuitry underlying locomotion, the role of sensory information, modulation through descending systems, and cortical circuitry contributing to locomotion. Implication for the design of gait neuroprosthesis and exoskeletons; Models of the mechanics of biped locomotion. Inverted pendulum models. Spring-loaded inverse pendulum models. Links to robotics such as passive and dynamic walkers.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#8924EC">CG 613</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="CG">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/cg.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Human Brain Mapping </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Human Brain Mapping [CG 614]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Principles of Brain Mapping: Localization of mental functions including perception, memory, attention and executive processes in the human brain and the study of their neural mechanisms; Mapping methods: Fundamentals and principles of mapping methods like functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI), and Electroencephalography (EEG). fMRI – Basics of MR physics, image acquisition, acquisition protocols, basics of T1w/T2w/T2* imaging, understanding BOLD data and sources of noise, design of experiments suitable for BOLD imaging, analysis of BOLD task and rest data, visualization of BOLD images, creation of individualized and averaged brain atlases; EEG – basics of EEG, experiment design, properties of data, sources of noise, Event Related Potentials, signal processing of brain state, microstates analysis, principles behind concurrent EEG-fMRI. Brain connectivity: Understanding how brain function and connectivity are interrelated, brain connectome, diffusion weight imaging (DWI), fMRI connectivity, changes with various disorders, connectivity based machine learning models, graph-based network properties.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>

                                                               <h1>General Chemistry [CH 201]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Coordination Chemistry: Werner’s work, EAN Rule, V. B. Theory, Crystal field theory, Ligand
Field Theory, Jahn–Teller distortion, Square planar and Tetrahedral complexes, Chelates,
Nomenclature and Isomerism of coordination compounds.
Structure and bonding in rare gas compounds; HSAB theory; Chemical and biological buffers.
Bioinorganic chemistry: Occurrence and availability &amp; biological functions of inorganic elements
in organisms, Uptake, transport and storage of Oxygen, Hemoglobin and Myoglobin,
Photodynamic therapy.
Organic Chemistry and Stereochemistry: Resonance and Inductive effects; Aromaticity and
Huckel’s rule; Optical activity, Polarimeter, Chirality, Enantiomerism, Configuration, R-S
Nomenclature, Reactions of stereoisomers, Resolution, Conformations of Alkanes and
cycloalkanes, Introduction to UV-visible and IR spectroscopy and their applications.                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Electrochemical Science and Engineering [CH 302]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Basic principles- Electrochemical cells, characteristics of electrochemical equations, Faraday&rsquo;s law and efficiency, potentials and Ohm&rsquo;s law; Cell potential and thermodynamics: Electrochemical reactions, Standard potentials, activity correction, equilibrium electrochemistry, Pourbaix diagram, liquid junction potential; Electrochemical kinetics: Double layer capacitance, use of Butler-Volmer (BV) kinetic expression and its simplified forms, Fitting of BV, Ion transport in electrochemical systems; Electrode structure and configuration: Ideally polarized electrode, Micro electrodes, porous electrodes, gas liquid interface in porous electrodes; Electrochemical techniques: cyclic voltammetry, static techniques, electrodeposition; Battery fundamentals: Battery science fundamentals, cell chemistry, various types of batteries and their chemistry, Materials aspect in the battery; Corrosion: Fundamentals and thermodynamics of corrosion, types of corrosion, corrosion protections; In class demonstration: Analytical techniques, double layer charging, capacitor charging and discharging cycle etc.                                                                </p>
