							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Food Chemistry </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Food Chemistry [CH 401]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   1. Description of Core Constituents of Foods: Understanding of biomolecular composition of raw fruits, vegetables and meats. Alteration in chemical constituents during standard food preparation (cooking/processing) approaches. Changes in bonding, composition and activity of water during food preparation.<br>
2. Food Flavor, Color and Aroma: Molecular basis of flavor, aroma and color of foods; changes during standard food processing methods.<br>
3. Biochemical transformations underlying food processing; implications on flavor, color and aroma. Production of food products with corresponding enzymes such as: high-fructose corn syrup (a-amylase, glucose isomerase), Cheese and dairy products (chymosin, b-galactosidase), Bread making/baking (groups of enzymes), Malt brewing (a-amylase, protease), fruit juices (pectic enzyme). Immobilized enzymes for analytical processes, starch to fructose conversion. Pasteurization of milk.<br>
4. Additives, Contaminants and Adulterants: Molecular basis of additional ingredients that are intentional, incidental and/or hazardous additives. Introduction to chemical basis of preservative action. Established chemical measures of food safety.<br>
5. Analytical Methods and Approaches: for study of composition, color, texture, flavor and aroma. Detection and quantification of hazardous substances. Introduction to global regulatory framework of food safety. Detection of chemical adulterants and detect antibiotic residues in foods (IR spectrometer), Colorimetric detection of pesticides and food additives (UV-vis spectrometer), pesticide residue and veterinary drugs in foods (LC-Mass analysis).<br>
6. Hands-on Experiments: Adulteration in edible oil, added food colors/dyes and sugars in the food items and soft drinks can be tested /detected by simple demonstration based experiments. Students would be able to test the samples of food items/soft drink by simple instruments such as: polarimeter, chromameter and UV-visible spectrometer. The details are provided in Annexure II.                                                                </p>

																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Structure and Bonding, Dipole moment, polarisability, Hyperconjugation; Tautomerism,
Aromaticity in benzene and nonbenzenoid compounds; Acidity and Basicity,Bronsted/Lewis
acids and bases, hard/soft acids and bases, nucleophilicity and basicity; pKa of organic
compounds, structural effects of acidity and basicity; Addition, Substitution and Elimination
Reactions,Nucleophilic addition to the carbonyl group, electrophilic and radical addition to
alkenes, electrophilic and nucleophilic aromatic substitutions, S N 1 , S N 2 and S N i reactions,
eliminations E1, E2 and E1cB reactions; Reactive Intermediates,Introduction to structure,
formation, stability and reactions of carbocations, carbanaions, free radicals, radical anions,
radical cations, arynes, carbenes and nitrenes,methods to determine the reaction
intermediates; effect of structure on reactivity; Protecting groups, Introduction to the
importance of protecting groups in organic synthesis.                                                                </p>

                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Quantum Chemistry [CH 511]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of mathematical methods. Vectors, matrices, determinants, eigenvalues and ordinary
differential equations; Solution of the Schrodinger equation for exactly solvable problems like
particle in a box, particle in a ring, harmonic oscillator, one dimensional potential step and wells.
Angular momentum; Hydrogen atom; Variation Method and Perturbation Theory; Valence bond and
molecular orbital theories; Hückel approximation                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>

						</div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Reactions and Mechanisms in Organic Chemistry </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Reactions and Mechanisms in Organic Chemistry [CH 512]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Substitution, Elimination and Addition Reactions: Nucleophilic aliphatic substitutions, Elimination
reactions, Structural and solvation effect on reactivity, and Electrophilic, nucleophilic and free radical addition
reactions. Electrophilic, nucleophilic, and Transition metal-catalyzed aromatic substitution reactions, and
Aromatic radical substitution.
Oxidation and Reduction: oxidation of alcohols, ketones and aldehydes (transition metal oxidants, peroxides
and peracids etc.), allylic oxidation, oxidation of C-C double bonds (epoxides formation, ozone, KMnO 4 , OsO 4
etc.). Catalytic hydrogenation of functional groups, Group III hydride donor reagents (LAH, NABH4, DIBALH,
B2H6), Groupd IV hydride donors (Silicon hydride and hydride transfer from Carbon).
Functional Group Interconversion: Conversion of alcohols to alkylating agents, installation and removal of
common protecting groups, Interconversion of carboxylic acid groups.
Stereochemistry: Stereoisomers, Optical isomerism and Chirality, Resolution,
Stereoselective/Setreospecific/Enantionselective reactions.                                                                </p>

                                                    </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Advance Organic Chemistry </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content

                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Advance Organic Chemistry [CH 513]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Rearrangement reactions: Electrophilic (Beckman, Hofmann, Lossen, Curtius, Wolff, Schimidt, Baeyer-
Villiger, Pinacol-pinacolone, Wagner-Meerwein etc.), Nucleophilic (benzilic acid, Favorski), radical
rearrangement (Wittig, aza-Wittig).
Pericyclic reactions: Cycloaddition reactions, stereochemical aspect and applications of Diels-Alder reaction,
1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition reactions, [2+2] cycloaddition reactions, electrocyclic reactions, overview of
sigmatropic reactions, [1,3]-, [1,5]-, [1,7]- Sigmatropic shifts of hydrogen and alkyl groups, Sigmatropic
rearrangements (Cope, aza-Cope, Oxy-Cope, Claisen, aza-Claisen, Eschenmoser-Claisen, vinyl-
cyclopropane-cyclopentene).
Photochemistry: General principles, cis-trans-Isomerazation, photoisomerization of 1,3-butadiene,
photochemistry of carbonyl compounds, photochemistry of aromatic compounds, cycloaddition reactions,
photochemical reactions of 1,4-butdienes.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Bioinorganic Chemistry </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Bioinorganic Chemistry [CH 517]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Biogeochemical cycles: Energy transduction between biotic and abiotic world (Emphasis on the carbon,
hydrogen, and nitrogen cycles); Transport and storage of metals in biology: Transferrin and Ferritin will be
discussed in details by using iron as a model system; Metals in medicine: Development and current usage of
various metal based anti-cancer agents and MRI contrast agents; Hydrolytic chemistry by metalloenzymes:
Role of acid-base chemistry in biology. Examples of Carbonic anhydrase, Urease, and Acotinase will be
discussed in details; Electron transport in biology: The basic concepts of electron transport through proteins.
Roles of [Fe-S] clusters and Copper cofactors. Fundamentals of Photosynthesis, and Respiration; Detailed
study of complex metalloenzymes: [NiFe]- and [FeFe]-Hydrogenase, and Nitrogenase.                                                                </p>

                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="CH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/chem.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Inorganic Chemistry Laboratory </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>0 | 0 | 3 | 2							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Inorganic Chemistry Laboratory [CH 522]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>0 | 0 | 3 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The Inorganic Chemistry Lab will have the following list of experiments:
Synthesis of transition metal complexes: Objective is to learn synthetic skills to prepare
coordination complexes of 3d metals [e.g. Ni(II) and Co(II)] from their metal salts. The
experiment will also demonstrate the use of few topics “spectrochemical series” and
“chelate effect” covered in theory course.
Polarimetry: Experiment involves breakdown of disaccharide sugar (sucrose) into its
components (glucose and fructose) and kinetic study of this process with the help of
Polarimeter instrument. Optical rotation of organic substances (sugars) can be measured
and in-turn the rate constant.
Gravimetric analysis: Purpose is to learn basic principles of Gravimetry, an analytical
method used for the quantitative estimation of analytes (metal present in the solution) by
treating it with the reagents to give sparingly soluble products which can be precipitated
and weighed.
Complexometric titration: Basic principles of titrimetric method will be demonstrated and
hardness of water can be calculated by the solutions of of Zn(II), Mg(II) salts and titrating
them with EDTA (ethylenediamine tetraaceticacid), a chelating reagent.
Recycling of aluminium: This “Green experiment” is used to demonstrate, how
aluminium foils can be recycled by converting it to potash alum in the chemical laboratory.
Estimation of phosphoric acid: Phosphoric acid is used as acidifier in “Cola drinks” and
its amount can be determined by interesting chemical method using molybdenum complex
and “Absorption spectrophotometer”.
Back Titration: Direct titration of the aluminium ions in the solution is challenging, due to
the slow formation of the metal complex with the chelating reagents and non-availability of
suitable metal indicator. Thus, back titration of solution containing mixture of ions (Zn 2+ and
Al 3+ ) with EDTA can be used effectively to determine the aluminium present in the solution.
UV-visible Studies: Experiment includes measurement of optical density (OD) of the
given set of metal complexes in solution, which can be correlated with their Crystal Field
Stabilization Energy (CFSE in Octahedral geometry).
Fluorescence Studies: This involves solution based emission studies of organic dyes
prepared in the “Organic Chemistry Lab” and calculate the emission quantum yield with
the help of a fluorescence standard.
IR Spectral analysis of compounds: Experiment includes hands-on training of IR
spectrometer and learn basic interpretation of IR spectra of metal complexes and selected
organic compounds; which is a simple analytical tool to find out the functional groups
present in the sample.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Organic Chemistry Laboratory </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit

							<br>0 | 0 | 3 | 2							</h4>

                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content

                                </a>

                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Organic Chemistry Laboratory [CH 523]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>0 | 0 | 3 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Synthesis of dibenzyledeneacetone- Experimental demonstration of aldol condensation
for the carbon-carbon bond forming reaction.
Synthesis of aspirin from salicylic acid- Acetylation of phenol using acetic anhydride.

Methyl salicylate from aspirin- Esterification reaction of the synthesized salicylic acid
using methanol and conc. sulfuric acid.

Syntheis of Fluorescein-, Preparation of a classic fluorescent dye using phthalic
anhydride and resorcinol.
Reduction of ketone- NaBH 4 reduction of benzophenone /acetophenone in methanol.

Synthesis of Chalcone- A solid state chemical reaction using the mechanical grinding
method

Diels-Alder reaction- One of the important pericyclic reaction, [4+2] cycloaddition of
anthracene and maleic anhydride.
Solvent extraction- Separation of an Acidic, a Basic and a Neutral Substance using
solvent extraction will be performed.
Interpretation of IR spectra of organic molecules- IR spectrum of the synthesized
organic molecules will be recorded and the interpretation of the spectrum will be carried
out to identify the organic molecules.
Interpretation of NMR spectra of organic molecules- NMR spectrum of the
synthesized organic molecules will be recorded and the interpretation of the spectrum will
be carried out to identify the organic molecules.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Analytical and Computational Chemistry Laboratory </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>0 | 0 | 3 | 2							</h4>

                                                               <h1>Analytical and Computational Chemistry Laboratory [CH 524]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>0 | 0 | 3 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Analytical Chemistry
<li> Spectrophotometric Determination of Iron: The experiment will teach
Complexometry, UV absorbance measurements of standards and unknowns.
<li> Identification of amino-acids using pH titrations:
<li> Estimation of Solubility Products of Silver Salts through potentiometric methods.
<li> Determination of Formation constants of Iron(III)-Glycine using spectrophotometry.
<li> Chromatographic separations: Students will learn the following techniques; Thin
Layer, Paper chromatography, High Pressure Liquid Chromatography and Gas
Chromatographic separation methods.
<li> UV and Fluorescence spectroscopy: Solvatochromic studies of dyes in various
solvents.
Theoretical Chemistry
<li> Density functional theory calculations: Identification of transitions states.
<li> Molecular Dynamics: Introduction to force fields and molecular dynamics simulation
methodology.
<li> Molecular Docking: Identification of lead compounds.                                                                </p>

                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="CH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/chem.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Physical Chemistry Laboratory </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>0 | 0 | 3 | 2							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Physical Chemistry Laboratory [CH 525]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>0 | 0 | 3 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Acetic acid adsorption over charcoal-Testing the physisorption models.
Thermodynamics of charge transfer complexes- Estimating parameters such as
enthalpy and entropy from an equilibrium reaction.
Elevation in boiling point to calculate the boiling point elevation constant- Verifying
this physical phenomenon by using a number of solute and solvent.
Calculating the distribution constant and/or equilibrium constant of I 2 in aqueous
medium- An equilibrium reaction between potassium iodide and iodine will be used to
estimate the parameters.
Electron transfer studies in Fe 3+ /Fe 2+ system using electrochemical techniques-
Several techniques such as cyclic voltammetry will be used to understand the electron
transfer process.
Recording and interpretation of powder XRD pattern of an oxide materials- Specific
oxide sample will be used to record the x-ray diffraction and its interpretation will be taught.
Estimating the density of a crystalline powder using x-ray diffraction method-
Powder x-ray diffraction pattern will be recorded and the lattice parameter estimation will
reveal the density.
Sol-gel synthesis of SiO 2 aerogels- Sol-gel synthesis will be utilized to make few
amorphous and crystalline material and characterize
Temperature dependent adsorption of CO 2 over solid materials- A detector based
technique will be used to quantify the adsorption of gases such as CO 2 on adsorbent
materials.

Biodiesel synthesis using soybean oil and its characterization using TGA- Base
catalyzed reaction will be used to synthesize the biodiesel. Due to specific combustion
behavior, biodiesel can be easily characterized by TGA.
Thermal decomposition behavior of carbonates, nitrates and hydroxides using TGA-
Thermal behavior of the mentioned materials will be used to understand their
decomposition behavior by TGA technique.                                                                </p>

                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#0085FE">CH 525</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="CH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/chem.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Group Theory & Spectroscopy </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Group Theory & Spectroscopy [CH 526]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Symmetry and group theory: Introduction to group theory, properties of a group, Symmetry elements and symmetry operations, Symmetry point groups; Matrix representation of groups, properties of matrix representations, reducible and irreducible representations, character tables, Orthogonality relationships in group theory; Application of group theory in spectroscopy: Basic description of light-matter interactions using time-dependent perturbation theory approach, transition probability and selection rules; Molecular vibrations, Simple harmonic oscillator model and quantum mechanical solution, application of group theory to molecular motions, IR and Raman spectroscopy; Electronic spectroscopy, Molecular orbitals, selection rules, Emission of light: spontaneous and stimulated emission. Various applications in lasers and in imaging.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to organometallic complexes: 18-electron "rule" and its exceptions; oxidation states, coordination number and geometry.
-bonded & -bonded ligands: Metal alkyls, aryls and hydrides, alkene, alkyne and allyl complexes, cyclopentadienyl complexes. Fischer and Schrock carbenes, Carbonyls and Phosphine complexes
Organometallic reagents:  examples of Grubbs catalysts and Pd-catalysts and their applications in organic synthesis, olefin metathesis, CH activation.
Role of metals in biology: metals in medicines, introduction to organometallic complexes and their role in biology /medicine.                                                                </p>

                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Theories of Bonding in Coordination Chemistry (VBT, CFT), Metal d-orbital splitting in different geometries (using CFT) Jahn-Teller distortion with examples in different geometries. The Isomerism with examples in coordination complexes. Electronic Spectroscopy: Term symbols, Laporte and Spin transition rules. Orgel diagrams: Absorption spectra of transition metal complexes (3d series)
Tanabe-Sugano Diagrams, Lanthanides and Actinides, Molecular magnetism Calculation of magnetic moments (μs, μJ): (in d-block, Lanthanides and Actinides elements), Effects of the structural geometry on magnetic moments.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Fundamentals of machine learning for chemistry: supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning with examples from molecular and reaction modeling; Chemical data representation and feature engineering: SMILES and molecular graphs, atomic and bond features, molecular descriptors and fingerprints using RDKit; Chemical informatics tools and databases: overview of key datasets (QM9, ZINC, PubChem) and open-source packages (scikit-learn, Chemprop, DeepChem); Machine learning for chemical property prediction: regression and classification models for thermochemical, electronic, and solubility data; model validation and benchmarking; Deep learning for molecular modeling: neural networks and graph neural networks for structure–property relationships; introduction to ML potentials; Generative and inverse design approaches in chemistry: overview of variational autoencoders, GANs, and diffusion models for molecular design                                                                </p>

products.                                                                </p>

inspired by biomacromolecules, bio-inspired molecules in imaging, Case studies of commercially

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Interpretative Organic Spectroscopy </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Interpretative Organic Spectroscopy [CH 622]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   UV–Visible Spectroscopy: Electromagnetic spectrum, basic principles, concept of chromophore,
electronic transitions in organic compounds, Structure elucidation by UV-Vis and Woodward –
Fisher rules; IR – Spectroscopy: Basic principles, IR as a tool for functional groups
determination in organic compounds; NMR Spectroscopy: Basic principles. Introduction to NMR
techniques – CW and FT NMR techniques, 1H NMR Spectral parameters – intensity, chemical shift,
multiplicity, coupling constant; Analysis of first and second order spectra. Multinuclear NMR (with
specific emphasis on 13C NMR): Proton coupled, off–resonance decoupled 13C NMR spectra.
Structure determination by NMR: Assignment of chemical shifts, additively effect, characteristic
chemical shifts of common organic compounds and functional groups, DEPT spectra.
Introduction to multinuclear NMR of common hetero atoms present in organic compounds (N, F,
O, P, D). 2 D NMR techniques Homo- &amp; Hetero-COSY and NOESY spectra; Mass
Spectroscopy: Basic principles, techniques of ion production and ion and daughter ions,
molecular ion and isotope abundance, Meta-stable ions, Fragmentation pathways: Mc-Lafferty
Rearrangement and fragmentation pattern of common chemical classes. Application of MASS for
organic structure elucidation: determination of molecular formula and MASS spectra-structure
correlation.                                                                </p>

                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Solid state Chemistry- Introduction to crystal structure, Bonding and electronic
properties of solids, Defects and non-stoichiometry, Brag’s law and application,
Descriptive solid state chemistry, Introduction to phase diagram, electrical and
magnetic properties, symmetry elements and point groups.
Molecular spectroscopy- Theories of spectroscopic; vibrational and rotational
spectroscopy, photo electron spectroscopy, magnetic resonance spectroscopy.
Surface chemistry- Heterogeneous catalysis, adsorption isotherms.
Introduction to electrochemistry- Redox reactions, Electrode processes, Electric
double layer, Electron transfer and kinetics; Nernst Equation, Butler-Volmer equation,
Tafel equation, Instrumental methods in electrochemistry; cyclic voltammetry and
steady state techniques; Electrode reactions with coupled chemical reactions, Special
Topics; electrocatalysis, batteries, and fuel cells.                                                                </p>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">

                                                               <h1>Asymmetric Synthesis and Catalysis [CH 626]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to asymmetric synthesis; chiral auxiliaries and chiral pool approach; Chiral resolution processes
such as Kinetic Resolution (KR); Parallel Kinetic Resolution (PKR); and Dynamic Kinetic Resolution (DKR);
Asymmetric catalysis like Biocatalysis; Metal catalysis and Organocatalysis. Application to the asymmetric
synthesis of bioactive drug molecules.                                                                </p>

                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="CH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Metabolism & Biosynthesis </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                       Course Content

                                </a>

                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Metabolism & Biosynthesis [CH 627]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Chemistry of Digestion and Absorption Processes:
Digestion and absorption of carbohydrates, lipids and proteins; Solute transport; Solubility of substrates
and products; Molecular chaperones for absorption
Metabolic Pathways and Control:
Location of carbohydrate, lipid and protein metabolism pathways with respect to one another; common
metabolic sources and fates; compartmentalization of metabolic pathways; regulation of carbohydrate,
lipid and protein metabolism; synthesis, storage and utilization of energy sources; thermodynamic
relationships among pathways
Biosynthesis:
Synthesis of biomolecules (carbohydrates, lipids and proteins) from common building blocks; transport
and regulation of synthetic precursors; common intermediates in synthesis of other biomolecules;
synthesis of nucleic acid components
Clinical Relevance of Metabolism &amp; Biosynthesis:
Health implications of abnormal metabolism and biosynthesis; energy metabolism and diet; selected
diseases of metabolism; drug development for countering abnormal metabolism and biosynthesis.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction
Basic modes of catalytic action, Classification and key concepts; Practical applications and Economic
impact; Auto catalysis; Bio catalysis; Electro catalysis and Photo catalysis,
Materials Perspective
Desired characteristics, nature of the active site, metal support interaction, lattice oxygen and defect;
Adsorption, adsorption mechanisms and models; Oxides, noble metals, and other materials as catalysts;
Synthesis and Characterization of catalysts; Analytical techniques; Supports, Dopes, Alloys, Core-shell,
Nanoparticles.
Screening of catalysts
Scientific and Engineering considerations
Kinetics of Catalytic Reactions
Strategies and designs for laboratory studies; Development of basic forms of rate equations.
Deactivation and poisoning
Mechanisms, models and design strategies                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Solid State Chemistry and Applications [CH 632]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Structure and chemistry of solids: Diffraction techniques and analysis of diffraction data; Crystal
defects, nonstoichimetry and solid solutions; Structure of solid electrolytes, conducting polymers and
surfaces; Solid state transformations and reactions. Electronic and magnetic Properties: Band theory
of metals, insulators and semiconductors, k space and Brillouin zones. Photodiodes, Avalanche
photodiodes, piezoelectric effect; Magnetic moment, Curie law, Curie-Weiss law, Mechanism of
magnetic ordering, Exchange Interaction, Domain theory, Hysteresis, Anisotropy, Ferromagnetism,
Ferrimagnetism, Antiferromagnetism. Optical properties of solid: Polarizability, origin of refractive
index, optical properties of dielectrics and metals, Surface plasmon resonance. Selected examples of
materials, structures, properties and applications with respect to structure/property relations.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Single Molecule Spectroscopy </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4							</h4>

                                                               <h1>Single Molecule Spectroscopy [CH 633]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of interaction of matter and light; Transition dipole moment; Frank Codon principle;
Photophysics of molecules; Introduction to microscopy, diffraction limit, numerical aperture.
Various microscopy methods: confocal, wide-field, and wide-field TIRF; Excitation and
detection of single molecules in solution and immobilized on surface. Systematic study of
signals, noise, and signal-to-noise. Fluorescence correlation spectroscopy; Fluorescence
resonance energy transfer; Detection of single molecules in a solid matrix a low temperature.
Zero-phonon line, natural linewidth; saturation, spectral diffusion, Zeemn effect; Introduction to
super-resolution imaging. PALM, STORM, and STED method for super-resolution localization;
Application of single molecule imaging in biology, chemistry, soft matter; Application as single
photon source in quantum optics.                                                                </p>

                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="CH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/chem.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Molecular Dynamics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Molecular Dynamics [CH 634]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Provides an introduction to molecular modelling. Course covers the following topics. Empirical force field models: Molecular mechanics; Molecular Dynamics simulation method; Energy minimization methods for exploring the energy surface; Density Functional Theory. The course will introduce students to bio-molecular simulation programs CHARMM and NAMD. The Gaussian 09 package will be introduced to perform DFT calculations. Tutorial sessions will be included into the curriculum. The course requires a project to be completed for credits.</p>
<p>Guided introduction to force field development and molecular dynamics simulations using a project heavy module to enable students to setup simulations and execute the same.<br>
  1) Empirical force fields <br>
  &bull; The energy components of the force field<br>
  &bull; Understanding the bonded and non-bonded terms and their contributions.<br>
  &bull; How to choose a force field<br>
  &bull; Additive vs Polarizable force fields.<br>
  2) Parametrization protocol to develop a force field <br>
  &bull; How to develop consistent parameters<br>
  &bull; Parameter transfer vs Parameter development<br>
  &bull; What to target for parametrization<br>
  &bull; Density Functional Theory calculations<br>
  &bull; Crystallographic databases<br>
  &bull; Thermodynamic data<br>
  &bull; NMR observables.<br>
  3) Energy minimization methods<br>
  &bull; Steepest descent method, Newton Raphson method and Adopted basis Newton Raphson method.<br>
  4) Molecular Dynamics simulation methodology and protocol <br>
  &bull; Periodic boundary conditions &ndash; Implementation and the crystal cell types, cubic vs truncated octahedron. <br>
  &bull; 2D boundary simulations - Slab Boundaries<br>
  &bull; Ewald Summation<br>
  &bull; Non-bonded cutoff schemes and their implementations&ndash; Switch vs Shift <br>
  &bull; Temperature schemes &ndash; Langevin dynamics vs Lowe-Andersen dynamics<br>
  &bull; Pressure schemes &ndash; Berendsen vs Nose-Hoover Langevin piston<br>
  5) Which ensembles to use NVT vs NPT vs NVE &ndash; Parameters defining the choice of the ensembles. </p>
<p>6) Long simulations vs enhanced sampling simulations <br>
  &bull; Micro-second simulations<br>
  &bull; Replica exchange simulations <br>
  &bull; Free energy perturbations schemes. </p>
<p>Research Tools: <br>
  &bull; CHARMM (Force Field Development) <br>
  &bull; NAMD (Production Simulations on CPUs)<br>
  &bull; GROMACS (Production Simulations on GPUs) <br>
  &bull; Gaussian 09 (Density Functional Theory Calculations) </p>
<p>The course contents described above will be taught using a research project. Topics will be discussed by implementing the same in the project. Research projects will be devised which to target a very specific system setup. The projects will be picked up with emphasis on bio-molecular and nanoscale simulations. </p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Chemical Crystallography </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>2 | 0 | 1 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Chemical Crystallography [CH 635]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 0 | 1 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Introduction to chemical crystallography; Database approach for structure identification; Single crystal X-ray Diffraction Techniques; Small molecule crystallization; Crystal symmetry and lattice space groups; Data collection strategy and data integration; Data reduction, space group determination and structure identification; Oak Ridge Thermal Ellipsoid Plot (ORTEP) and Mercury GUI software; Crystallographic information file (CIF); Check CIF report for publication; Mercury software applications; Polymorphism and cocrystals in small molecules; </p>
<p>Practical sessions: Crystallization of small molecules; Crystal picking and mounting; Unit cell or Matrix determination: Data collection; Data Reduction; Identification of structural geometry; Initial structure determination or starting model; Structure solution; Structure refinement; Reporting crystal structures; Analysis of Crystal structures; Different structural aspects.</p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->

												<span class="label" style="background:#0085FE">CH 635</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="CH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/chem.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Statistical Thermodynamics and its Applications in Chemistry </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Statistical Thermodynamics and its Applications in Chemistry [CH 636]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>
<b>Module I: Statistical Viewpoint</b><br>
Thermodynamics postulates; conditions of equilibrium; reversible processes and maximum work postulate; Maxwell relation; review of probability theory; phase space; microstates and configurations; Boltzmann distribution law; significant configurations; physical interpretation of the distribution law; variation of W with E; approach to thermal equilibrium; the free particle and the ideal gas; concept of entropy
<br><br>
<b>Module II: The Partition Function</b><br>
Definition of partition function; chemical equilibrium and partition function; formulation of partition function – multiple degrees of freedom, degeneracy, indistinguishable and multiple units; thermodynamic functions from partition function – energy, entropy, factorability and separability; Fermi-Dirac and Bose-Einstein distributions and their classical limit
<br><br>
<b>Module III: Evaluations of Partition Functions</b><br>
Translational partition function – ideal monoatomic gas, Sackur-Tetrode equation; multitude of translational states, Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution; internal degrees of freedom – rotation, vibration, electronic excitation, potential energy function for a diatomic molecule; rigid-rotor harmonic-oscillator approximation; polyatomic gases; monoatomic crystals; molecular symmetry and relationship with partition function
<br><br>
<b>Module IV: Statistical Mechanical Ensembles</b><br>
Ensemble concept – basic postulates, types; microcanonical ensemble; canonical ensemble; grand canonical ensemble; analytical descriptions of the ensembles; equivalence of ensembles; fluctuations of thermodynamic properties
<br><br>
<b>Module V: Applications</b><br>
Intermolecular potential, Virial coefficients; heat capacities – Einstein solid, diatomic gases; the equilibrium state – dissociation of iodine, determinants of equilibrium; transition state theory – potential energy surface; distribution functions in classical monoatomic liquids
</p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#0085FE">CH 636</span>
												
						</div>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>

                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Organic Electronics [CH 637]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Molecular Crystals; Amorphous Molecular/Polymer Films; Electronic States of a Molecule; Atomic
Orbitals to Molecular Orbitals; Orbitals to States; Transitions between States; Potential Energy
Curve; Radiative Transitions: Absorption and Emission; Electronic Factor; Vibrational Factor; Spin
Factor; Franck-Condon Factor and Energy Gap Law; Electronic Coupling; Accepting Modes,
Promoting Modes, and the Isotope Rule; Basic Photophysical Parameters: Lifetimes and
Quantum Yields; Molecular Excitations – Effects Due to Polarization, Statistical Averaging,
Environment, and Couplings; Dimers and Excimers; Electronic Interaction in the Ground and
Excited States; Förster and Dexter Energy Transfer; Creation of Charged Molecules by Injection,
Absorption, and Doping; Charged Molecules in Disordered Films and Molecular Crystals;
Electrical and Optical Gap; Amorphous Solids; Comparison between Inorganic and Organic
Semiconductors; Charge Carriers and Excitons; Frenkel and Wannier Exciton; Injection and
Extraction of Charge Carriers; Charge Transport in Organic Solids; Band Transport; Hopping
Transport; Polaronic Transport; Disorder Controlled Transport; Trapping Effect; Impact of Charge
Carrier Density and Morphology on Transport; Charge Carrier Recombination; Generation and
Dissociation of Excitations; Diffusion of Excitons; Basics of Organic Light-Emitting Diodes; Device Structures; Fluorescent and Phosphorescent Emitters; Organic Solar Cells Based; Electron-Donors and Electron-Acceptor Materials; Bulk-Heterojunction Structure; Role of Morphology of the Active Layer; Operational Principle of Organic Field-Effect Transistors; Improving Charge
Transport; Polarity; Ambipolar Transport.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Electronic Structure Theory </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                                               <h1>Electronic Structure Theory [CH 638]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Overview: Quantum theory and origin of electronic structure; many-electron problem; Born-Oppenheimer approximation; periodic solids and bands; uniform electron gas and simple metals. Basis sets, linear algebra, and secular equation; Slater and Gaussian type orbitals; Wave-function based approaches: Hartree, Hartree-Fock, self-consistent field method; correlation corrections; configuration interaction theory; coupled cluster methods; many-body perturbation theory; Density functional theory: DFT foundations; Thomas-Fermi-Dirac approximations; Hohenberg- Kohn Theorems; intricacies of DFT; Kohn Sham variational equations; time dependent DFT; local spin density approximation; GGA; LDA; solving Kohn-Sham equations; Numerical implementations: basis sets; atomic pseudopotentials; Brillouin zone sampling; iterative diagonalization methods; software packages; Ground state properties of materials: Hellmann-Feynman theorem; stress tensor; geometry optimizations; electronic band structures; Fermi surfaces; Electronic excitations: time-dependent density functional theory; Bethe-Salpeter equation; optical spectroscopy; Hands-on component: Predicting properties of matter from electronic structure.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Biogeochemical cycles: Energy transduction between the biotic and abiotic world (Emphasis on the carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen cycles); Transport and storage of metals in biology: Transferrin and Ferritin will be discussed in detail by using iron as a model system; Metals in medicine: Development and current usage of various metal-based anti-cancer agents and MRI contrast agents; Hydrolytic chemistry by metalloenzymes: Role of acid-base chemistry in biology. Examples of Carbonic anhydrase, Urease, and Aconitase will be discussed in detail; Electron transport in biology: The basic concepts of electron transport through proteins. Roles of [Fe-S] clusters and Copper cofactors. Fundamentals of Photosynthesis, and Respiration; Detailed study of complex metalloenzymes: [NiFe]- and [FeFe]-Hydrogenase, and Nitrogenase; Small molecule activation and conversion by metalloenzymes: Diatomic and monoatomic oxygen incorporation into substrates: cytochromes, methane monooxygenase, dioxygenases, and spectroscopic characterization of their reaction intermediates. Nitrate reductase, Tungsten containing formate dehydrogenase; Artificial metalloenzymes in catalysis. (Newly added topics are in italics)                                                                </p>

Global energy demand, Renewable vs Non-renewable energy, Role of chemistry in energy conversion and storage<br>
Materials of interest: Layered structures (MX&#8322;, Ruddlesden-Popper, Dion&ndash;Jacobson (DJ), Layered double hydroxides (LDHs), Homologous Series), Tetrahedral frameworks (Zeolites, MOFs, Phosphates), Pyrochlore &amp; Garnet, Intermetallic, Topological Materials, and others<br>

Solid-state reaction, Sol-gel, Single-source precursors, Hydrothermal, Microwave, and Vapor-phase synthesis, Intercalation/deintercalation, Thin film deposition (CVD, ALD, PLD), High-pressure synthesis and Crystal growth, Nanomaterials <br>
3.	Selective Topics on Energy Conversion and Storage Technologies (18 lectures)<br>
(Working principles, state of art materials and their structure-property correlations)<br>
&bull;	Thermoelectric materials for waste heat recovery (4)<br>
&bull;	Hydrogen Energy (production, storage, catalysts) (4)<br>
&bull;	CO&#8322; storage and conversion technologies (3)<br>
&bull;	Topological materials for sustainable energy applications (2)<br>
&bull;	Solid-state batteries (Li-ion, Na-ion) (3)<br>
&bull;	Supercapacitors and next-gen energy storage devices (2)                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   &bull;	Time-dependent quantum mechanics (8 lectures): Schr&ouml;dinger equation, time evolution Hamiltonian and operator, light-matter interaction, two-level system, time-dependent perturbation theory, Fermi&rsquo;s Golden rule, Schr&ouml;dinger and Heisenberg representations, Interaction picture.<br>
&bull;	Condensed-phase dynamics (6 lectures): mixed states and density matrix, fluctuations, time-correlation function, linear response theory, line broadening, spectral diffusions.<br>
&bull;	Applications and techniques (8 lectures): energy and charge transfer, quantum relaxation processes, linear spectroscopy, pump-probe spectroscopy, time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC), fluorescence up-conversion, two-dimensional spectroscopy (2D NMR, 2D infrared spectroscopy, 2D electronic spectroscopy and 2D electronic-vibrational spectroscopy).<br>
&bull;	Hand-on learning (4 classes): UV-Vis, fluorescence, FTIR spectroscopy, time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC).a                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">

																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 1 | 0 | 3<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of Basics of Fluid Flow: Fluid Statics, pressure measurement; Basic equations of fluid
flow, viscosity, newtonian and non-newtonian fluids, laminar and turbulent flows, Boundary layer
theories; Bernoulli theorem and applications; Flow of incompressible fluids, friction factor, piping
systems; Flow of compressible fluids, adiabatic and isothermal flows, sedimentation and
flotation, centrifugal separation, packed beds and fluidized beds; Transportation and metering
of fluids, pump types, pump curves, blowers and compressors, direct flow measurement (pitot
tube, rotameter, orifice meter etc., indirect methods and commercial flow meters; Mixing and
Agitation, power consumption, impeller types and flow patterns, mixing times.                                                                </p>

Phase equilibrium criteria, non-ideal solutions, residual and Excess properties; Fugacity and Activity
Coefficient models; vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) at low to moderate pressures; Raoult’s Law, Henry’s
law High-Pressure VLE, Triangular phase diagrams. Chemical Reaction Equilibrium: Homogeneous
and Heterogeneous reactions; Multi- reaction Equilibria; Combined Phase and Reactions Equilibria.                                                                </p>

																<br>2 | 1 | 0 | 3<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Unsteady state ideal reactors (isothermal and non-isothermal), Non-ideal Reactors and Reactor

models to treat External diffusion, Internal Diffusion & Reaction control reactions. Catalysis: Catalytic
rate equation and catalytic reactor design, catalyst deactivation, Various fluid-particle reactors and its
designs. Gas-Liquid Reactions: Rate equation for mass transfer and reaction limited reactions, Factors

important to design Gas-liquid reactors and various gas-liquid contactors and its design aspects. Solid-
Solid Reactions: Kinetic models and reactor design for solid processing units. Special Reactors:

Bioreactions and Bioreactors, - Enzyme Fermentation and Microbial Fermentation, photoreaction and
photoreactors, Vacuum and aerosol systems for material synthesis.                                                                </p>

								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Chemical reaction engineering<br><br> [CL 322]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Motivation; fundamentals of batch and flow reactors; general mole balance equation; rate laws and
stoichiometry; conversion and reactor sizing; reactors in series; algorithm for data analysis from batch
reactor and flow reactor data; experimental planning for data analysis; Isothermal reactor design: mole
balance in terms of conversion, mole balance in terms of concentration and molar flow rates, effect of
pressure drop in conversion, micro reactors, membrane reactors; Multiple reactions: selectivity and yields
in series, parallel and complex reactions. selection and ordering of reactors for parallel, series and
complex reactions; Steady state non isothermal reactor design: energy balance equation to flow and
batch reactor systems, design of adiabatic and isothermal reactors, equilibrium conversions, multiple
steady states; Catalytic reactors: catalytic reaction mechanisms, rate law for catalytic reactions, external
diffusion, internal diffusion, design of packed bed reactor; Residence Time Distribution (RTD):
fundamentals of non-ideal reactors, measurement and characterization of RTD, RTD for ideal reactor,
non ideal reactor modeling using RTD                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>

                                </div>
