Macromolecules in solutions, Equilibrium thermodynamics, Extension to polymer blends and gels,
Kinetics of phase separation, Dynamical systems involving polymer gels; Historical perspective and
classification of polymer processing operations, Manufacturing processes, Orientation and structure
development during processing, Models for processing techniques.                                                                </p>

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Chemical Engineering Practice in Industry [CL 328]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 0 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The Chemical Engineering Practice course aims to bridge the gap between academic learning and industrial application, focusing on the practical aspects of Chemical Engineering concepts. The course will consist of lectures delivered by invited instructors who are experienced professionals from the Chemical Industry. The lectures will cover case studies and current industry standards on:<br>
&bull;	Synthesis and production of organic and inorganic chemicals<br>
&bull;	Optimization of separation processes in industrial settings <br>
&bull;	Challenges in scaling up reactions and optimizing reaction conditions for yield/selectivity<br>
&bull;	Safety as well as design of industrial reactors <br>
&bull;	Process optimization and troubleshooting using control systems<br>
&bull;	Application of Chemical Engineering concepts in food industry<br>
&bull;	Production of specialty chemicals, catalysts, high-value products (essential oils, perfumes, etc.)<br>
&bull;	Production methods, processing techniques, and quality control in polymer industry<br>
&bull;	Process development, scaling up, technology transfer, and compliance with regulatory standards in pharmaceutical industry<br>
&bull;	Case studies on application of Chemical Engineering beyond the Chemical Industry (for example, semiconductors, biomedical devices, automobiles, aerospace, defense, etc.) and related technical challenges.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Process Safety </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>1.5 | 0 | 0 | 2							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Process Safety [CL 353]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 0 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Process Safety: What is process safety? Difference between process safety and occupational safety, Importance in the chemical industry, Accident and loss statistics; learning from history, Brief on inherently safer design.<br>
Safety Regulations and Management Basics: OSHA Process Safety Management (overview), Key national and international standards (OSHA, ISO, OISD), Role of safety audits and compliance.<br>
Basics of Reactive Chemicals &amp; Hazard Identification: Hazardous and reactive chemicals, reactor safety, Hazard surveys and simple hazard analysis, Introduction to HAZOP (basic concepts only), Real-world examples of hazard identification. Basics of risk analysis<br>
Fires and Explosions: Fire triangle and ignition sources, Flammability concepts (limits, diagrams), Types of explosions (VCE, BLEVE, dust explosions &ndash; basics only), Simple prevention measures (inerting, ventilation, explosion-proof equipment).<br>
Toxicology and Industrial Hygiene: Basic toxicological concepts: dose-response, TLVs, PPE basics and industrial hygiene principles, Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS).<br>
Accident Investigation &amp; Case Studies: Simple accident investigation process, Root cause thinking, Brief review of major industrial accidents (e.g., Bhopal, Texas City, Flixborough) and key lessons.<br>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Process Control<br><br> </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Process Control<br><br> [CL 422]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   First Principles model development; Process dynamics for first, second and higher order systems:
linearisation, transfer function models, effect of poles, zeros and time delays on system response;
Empirical models from data; control system instrumentation; introduction to feedback control: objectives,
PID control; analysis of closed loop systems: stability, root locus, frequency response using Bode and
Nyquist plots; control design techniques: design criteria, time and frequency domain techniques, model
based design, tuning; advanced control strategies: cascade and feed forward, introduction to
multivariable control; controller implementation through discretisation.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Biochemical Engineering </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Biochemical Engineering [CL 426]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Biochemical Engineering<br>
Who is a Biochemical Engineer – What do Biologists and Engineers have in common, Success Stories from the World of Biochemical Engineering (Penicillin to COVID vaccine)<br>
An Engineer’s Perspective on Biology – Microbial Diversity, Cell Structure and Construction, Cell Nutrients <br>
Overview of Enzymes – What are Enzymes and how do they function, <br>
Metabolic Pathways – Glucose metabolism, Respiration, Nitrogen Fixation <br>
Cell Kinetics – Cell growth in batch and continuous cultures, Cell growth phases, Factors affecting cell growth, Chemostats and its deviations<br>
Engineering Principles for Biochemical Engineering<br>
Operating Consideration for Bioreactors – Choosing the cultivation method, Chemostat with recycle, multistage chemostat, Fed-batch operation, Perfusion systems<br>
Immobilized Cultures - Cell immobilization, diffusional limitations <br>
Bioreactors – Mechanically stirred tanks, Bubble columns, Packed bed reactors, Airlift reactors, Microreactors, Scale-up, Scale-down<br>
Unit Operations in Biochemical Engineering<br>
Separation Processes in Biochemical Engineering - Filtration, Centrifugation, Coagulation, Flocculation <br>
Cell disruption – Ultrasonication, Osmotic shock, Freezing, Enzymatic digestion, Chemical solubilization <br>
Purification and Quantitation of Biochemical Products - Precipitation, Chromatographic, Electrophoretic, Aqueous Two-Phase Systems <br>
Product Storage – Stabilization and storage of proteins, Concentration, Ultrafiltration, Lyophilization, Dialysis <br>
Overview of Unconventional Approaches and Applications of Biochemical Engineering<br>
Circumventing Traditional Biochemical Processing - Extracellular protein production; Cell-free protein synthesis<br>
Applications of Biochemical Engineering – Medical Devices (artificial kidney), Gene therapy, Biological wastewater treatment, Biomaterials, Biofuels, Biosensors, Hybrid Materials<br>
Biochemical Engineering and Society - Bioethics, Regulating the use of Genetically Modified Organisms, Safety and Concerns <br>
Future – Where do we go from here? Moving towards a circular economy and a sustainable future involving synthetic meat                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Formulation Science and Engineering </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Formulation Science and Engineering [CL 427]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The course is focused on liquid, semisolid (paste-like) formulations where one or more components are dispersed in a solvent.  The course will be taught in the form of modules, each module focusing on a formulation with a specific application.<br>
Module-I:    Introduction to formulations design.  Examples of formulations. <br>
Module-II:    Suspensions: Formulations of solid/liquid dispersions.  Understanding of inter-particle interaction potential, stability, and potential stabilizer.  Discussion on properties of dilute, semi-dilute, and concentrated suspensions. <br>
Module-III:   Emulsions: Formulations of liquid/liquid dispersions.  Synthesis and thermodynamics of emulsification.  Introduction to nanoemulsions, microemulsions, Pickering, and multiple emulsions.  Stabilization and destabilization of emulsions.  Discussion on properties of dilute, semi-dilute, and concentrated emulsions. <br>
Module-IV:  Foams: Formulations of air/liquid dispersants.  Surface force theory.  Surface viscosity and elasticity.  Foam films, foam drainage, foam collapse <br>
Module-V:   Evaluation techniques for formulations after dilution <br>
Module-VI:  Evaluating formulations without dilution.  Discussion on toxicity evaluation of formulations                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->

                                </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Advance Transport Phenomena<br><br> </h4>
							<hr>

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Advance Transport Phenomena<br><br> [CL 601]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Vector and tensor algebra, Calculus. Kinematics: Motion, streamlines, pathlines and
streaklines.Governing equations of fluid mechanics: Equation of continuity, Momentum balance,
Stress tensor, constitutive equations, Boundary conditions. Unidirectional flows: steady and
unsteady; Two-dimensional flows: Stream function. Limiting cases: Creeping flow, Inviscid flow,
Boundary layer theory, Turbulent flow: Transition to turbulence, Turbulence models. Macroscopic
balances. Governing equations of heat transfer: Energy balance, Constitutive equations,
Boundary conditions, Conduction, Forced convection, Natural convection. Radiation, Macroscopic
balances. Concentrations, velocities and mass fluxes: Governing equations of mass transfer:
Species mass balance, Constitutive equations, Boundary conditions. Complete solutions,
Diffusion in gases, Diffusion and reaction, Forced convection, Simultaneous heat and mass
transfer.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Advanced Thermodynamics<br><br> </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Advanced Thermodynamics<br><br> [CL 602]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Classical Thermodynamics: Introduction, Review of Basic Postulates, Conditions of Equilibrium,
Legendre Transformation and Maxwell’s relations, Stability of Thermodynamic systems, First
Order phase transitions and Critical Phenomenon (second order phase transitions) Phase Rule,
Single component phase diagrams, Introduction to Multicomponent Multiphase equilibrium
mixtures, partial molar properties, fugacity and activity coefficients, Ideal and Non-ideal solutions,
VLE/SLE/LLE/VLLE triangular plots, Chemical Equilibrium and Combined phase and reaction
equilibria.
<br>
Statistical Thermodynamics: Mathematics-probability distribution, stirling approximations etc
Canonical Ensemble, Microcanonical Ensemble, Grand Canonical Ensemble: Expressions for
partition functions and their relation to thermodynamics variables Boltzmann, Monoatomic,
Diatomic and Polyatomic Gases: Calculation of Partition functions and thermodynamics
properties, Introduction to Classical Statistical Mechanics: Calculation of partition function, phase
space, Liouville equation Crystals (Solids): Partition functions, the Einstein and Debye theory
Intermolecular forces and potential energy functions, Imperfect Monoatomic Gases: Virial
Equation of State, Second Virial Coefficient, Law of Corresponding states, Perturbation theory:
Distribution functions.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="CL">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/cl.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Advanced Reaction Engineering<br><br> </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">

                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Advanced Reaction Engineering<br><br> [CL 604]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of fundamentals in chemical reaction engineering, Numerical solutions of reaction
engineering problems, membrane reactors, micro-reactors, reactive distillation, unsteady state
non-isothermal batch-semibatch-CSTR-PFR reactor design, chemical reaction with mass transfer,
fluidized bed reactor, multiphase reactors, design of non-ideal reactor using residence time
distribution data.                                                                </p>

                                                    </div>

                                                               <h1>Chemical Microscopy<br><br> [CL 625]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Fundamentals of optics (reflection, refraction, dispersion, objectives,
aberrations, illumination, image formation, resolving power, and image contrast);Polarized light
microscopy (In-situ imaging, measuring anisotropy, melting point determination, contaminant
identification, phase transformations);Phase contrast and interference contrast microscopy;
Confocal microscopy coupled with fluorescence spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy for
chemical mapping; Advanced techniques in fluorescence microscopy (Fluorescence
Resonance Energy transfer, Fluorescence lifetime imaging, fluorescence recovery after photo
bleaching, fluorescence correlation spectroscopy); Scanning probe microscopy (Near field
optical microscopy, chemical force microscopy, scanning thermal and tunneling microscopy);
Electron microscopy (field emission, combination with x-ray and auger electron spectroscopy
;Ultraviolet and Infrared microscopy for chemical imaging; Overview of microscopic practices                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Particulate Solids: Processing & Surface Engineering<br><br> </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Particulate Solids: Processing & Surface Engineering<br><br> [CL 627]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Dry synthesis of particles, Particle and bulk powder characterization: Particle size, shape,
hardness, surface properties, nano-scale surface roughness, Surface energetics, Powder flow
and packing properties, Dispersion properties of fine particles, Mechanical properties of powder
bed; Powder processing: Storage and flow of powders through hoppers – hopper designing,
Separation of particulate system from gas – gas cyclone, Pneumatic transport of powder
materials, Mixing and segregation of particulate solids, Flow through packed bed of particulate
solids – fluidization, Reactor with suspended solid particulates – the bubbling fluidized bed (BFB),
the K-L model for BFB, Circulating fluidized bed, Heat transfer in fluidized bed, Granulation,
Powder sintering; Inter-particle forces and surface engineering of particulate solids; Particle
engineering through surface coating, plasma processing, size reduction, design and formation of
composite particles for advanced applications in pharmaceuticals, food, propellant, aerosols,
cements, fertilizers, cosmetics and fine chemicals etc.; Hazards of fine particle handling:
Explosion hazards of fine powders, Health effects of fine particles.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Liquid State Theory </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Liquid State Theory [CL 628]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Recap of statistical mechanics: Microstates and macrostates, canonical ensemble, isothermal-isobaric ensemble, grand canonical ensemble, introduction to the types of simulations. Functionals: Introduction to functionals and their differentiation, functional derivatives of the grand potential. Distribution functions: Introduction to Distribution functions, direct correlation functions, density response functions, Classical density functional theory. Diagrammatic methods: Diagrammatic expansions of direct correlation functions, Introduction to virial expansion of the equation of state. Integral equation theory: Yvon-Born-Green hierarchy, Ornstein-Zernike equation, hypernetted chain approximation, Percus-Yevick approximation, mean spherical approximation. Time-dependent correlations: Properties of time correlation functions, Langevin equation, linear response theory, time-dependent fluctuations, estimation of the macroscopic transport coefficients. Molecular liquids: Molecular pair distribution function, Site-site distribution functions, Reference Interaction Site Model (RISM) equations. Inhomogeneous liquids: Free energy functionals for liquids at interfaces, application to liquid-vapor interface, calculation of surface tension, density functional theory of wetting.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Fundamentals of Aerosol Science </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Fundamentals of Aerosol Science [CL 629]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>The proposed course encompasses aerosols' properties, transport, and transformation that one would need a firm hold on to mitigate undesired aerosols and engineer desired aerosols or nanoparticles via the aerosol pathway. </p>
<p>The size range of particles for this course will be from a few nanometers to &gt;20 microns. </p>
<p>The course content is divided into the following modules for ease of reference:</p>
<p>Physical and chemical characterization: size and shape, particle morphology, size distribution functions, moments of distribution function, fractals, self-preserving size distribution, common instrumentation</p>
<p>Transport and deposition: diffusion, convection, force migrations, thermophoresis, inertial Transport and deposition, impactors, gravitational settling, design and operating principle of aerosol instruments based on particle transport</p>
<p>Optical properties: light scattering by a single particle, Mie theory, Rayleigh scattering, aerosols impact on visibility, design and operating principle of aerosol instruments based on optical properties</p>
<p>Aerosol dynamics: coagulation (Brownian and force-field), collision frequency function, thermodynamic properties, Kelvin Relation, nucleation, gas to particle conversion, evaporation, condensation, general dynamic equation</p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Catalyst Design for Heterogeneous Reactions </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Catalyst Design for Heterogeneous Reactions [CL 630]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   1. Introduction to heterogeneous catalysts: prominent industrial thermal catalysts; unconventional catalyst synthesis techniques &ndash; vacuum synthesis, autoclave methods, thermal methods<br>
2. Fundamentals of heterogeneous catalysis: turnover-rate; tools (mass spectrometry, gas chromatography, titration) and methods of catalytic measurements; transient and steady-state measurements; mass transfer limitations and catalyst loss<br>
3. Basics of chemical bonding and reaction barriers<br>
&bull; Free energy/ scaling relationship <br>
4. Understanding elementary catalytic steps: pressure dependence; kinetic isotope measurements; steady-state isotopic transient kinetic analysis<br>
5. Catalyst design: <br>
&bull; Can the ligand effects and ensemble effects be decoupled? <br>
&bull; Ex situ tools <br>
&bull; Composition, importance of particle and crystallite sizes, morphology, acidity/basicity (NH3 adsorption), heats of adsorption (TPD, isothermal calorimetry, differential scanning calorimetry)<br>
&bull; Surface science studies of crystalline catalysts; Measure of reaction barriers<br>
&bull; In situ tools <br>
&bull; Importance and challenges, current status; time-resolved measurements and effective spectroscopic cell design <br>
&bull; Spectroscopic technique selection rules; X-ray techniques, electron beam techniques, light techniques (IR, Raman, UV-vis) and other techniques<br>
&bull; Operando measurements <br>
&bull; Set up and limitations, structure-function relationships, compatibility with in situ spectroscopic techniques<br>
&bull;  Case-studies of in situ and operando measurements &ndash; strength and pitfalls                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Pharmaceutical Crystallization </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Pharmaceutical Crystallization [CL 631]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Basics of crystallization: Solubility, solid-liquid equilibrium, supersaturation, metastability, nucleation, crystal growth, crystal structure, crystal habit, population balance, control of particle size and morphology, application of crystallization in pharmaceuticals and foods</p>
<p>Processing and characterization of pharmaceutical crystals: Continuous crystallization, antisolvent crystallization, evaporative crystallization, reactive crsyatllization, filtration and washing, drying, morphology/habit control, milling of organic crystals, blending and granulation of pharmaceutical powders, Solid-state characterization of pharmaceutical crystals using X-ray diffraction, Differential Scanning Calorimetry, Thermogravimetric Analysis, Infrared and Raman Spectroscopy, microscopic techniques</p>
<p>Intermolecular interactions and mechanical properties of pharmaceutical crystals: Types of intermolecular interactions, impact of intermolecular interactions on crystal packing and crystal properties, importance of mechanical properties in pharmaceutical manufacturing, basic concepts related to mechanical properties, characterization of mechanical properties of pharmaceutical crystals, structure-property relationship</p>
<p>Polymorphs and cocrystals: Concept of polymorphism, types of polymorphs, Monotropy and Enaniotropy, Phase stability rules, Ostwald rule of stages for crystallization of polymorphs, polymorphic interconversions of polymorphs, impact of polymorphs on bioavailability, cocrystal formation, thermodynamics of cocrystal formation, molecular interaction affecting cocrystallization, structural and thermodynamic properties of cocrystals, corystal solubility, applications of cocrystals</p>
<p>Drug nanoparticles: Bottom-up and top-down techniques of nanoparticle production, milling, high pressure homogenization, antisolvent precipitation, sonoprecipitation, supercritical fluid processing,  nanoparticle stabilization, non-classical crystallization, application of pharmaceutical nanoparticles</p>                                                                </p>

                                                    </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Advanced Separation Processes </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Advanced Separation Processes [CL 632]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Fundamentals of Separation Processes: An overview of mass transfer fundamentals such as driving forces, and mass transfer coefficients, overview of basic separation processes (gas absorption, distillation, extraction etc.) and their limitations; Membrane Separation Processes: Membrane types, materials, synthesis, and characterization. Transport mechanisms (e.g., solution-diffusion model) and membrane modules, Pressure-driven processes such as microfiltration (MF), ultrafiltration (UF), nanofiltration (NF), and reverse osmosis (RO), Concentration polarization and membrane fouling, Other membrane techniques like dialysis, electrodialysis, gas separation, and pervaporation; Adsorption and Chromatography: Adsorption equilibria (e.g., Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms), Adsorbents and ion exchangers, Chromatographic techniques, retention theory, and design, Advanced methods like pressure swing adsorption (PSA), temperature swing adsorption (TSA), and simulated moving bed (SMB) chromatography; Supercritical Fluid Processes (SFE): Properties of supercritical fluids (typically supercritical CO2) and phase equilibria, Working principle, advantages over conventional solvents, and industrial applications (e.g., extraction of essential oils from seeds, spices and flowers); Advanced and Hybrid Distillation Techniques: Multicomponent distillation, Enhanced distillation methods, including azeotropic and extractive distillation. Reactive and catalytic distillation, combining reaction and separation in a single unit for process intensification, Molecular distillation and short-path distillation for processing heat-sensitive materials. Pressure swing distillation; Advanced Crystallization: Basics of crystallization, principles of melt crystallization and fractional crystallization, Techniques like zone melting and freeze crystallization, Supercritical Fluid Crystallization processes                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Applications of AI in ChE </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Applications of AI in ChE [CL 633]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Foundations of AI and Chemical Engineering (Overview and AIs transformative role in chemical engineering, Understanding AI paradigms: machine learning, deep learning, and hybrid approaches, Practical applications including process control, optimization, and materials discovery); Data Handling and Preparation (Nature of chemical engineering data: process measurements, molecular data, and time series, Techniques for data preprocessing—cleaning, normalization, and feature extraction, Exploratory data analysis and visualization tools to reveal underlying patterns); Classical Machine Learning Methods (Key supervised learning algorithms: linear and nonlinear regression, classification (Support Vector Machines (SVM), decision trees, ensembles), Model selection strategies, validation techniques, and performance measurement, Applications: Prediction of thermophysical properties of solutions); Unsupervised Learning and Dimensionality Reduction (Clustering techniques—k-means, hierarchical, density-based clustering of applications with noise (DBSCAN)—and their uses in process monitoring, Dimensionality reduction methods such as principal component analysis (PCA) and t-distributed stochastic neighbour embedding (t-SNE) for handling complex chemical data, Applications in anomaly detection and development of soft sensors for chemical reactors); Deep Learning in Chemical Engineering (Introduction to neural network architectures including Multilayer Perceptrons (MLPs), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), and Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), Tasks such as reaction prediction, fault detection, and image analysis in chemical processes, Addressing overfitting, generalization, and interpreting deep learning models); Integrated Modelling, Physics-Informed Machine Learning, and Optimization (Hybrid Modelling: Combining mechanistic first-principles models with data-driven AI to create robust, efficient process simulators and surrogate models, Physics-Informed Machine Learning (PIML): Embedding physical laws, constraints, governing differential equations directly into machine learning models, physics-based feature engineering, custom loss functions incorporating PDE residuals, neural network architectures constrained by process physics, Applications focus on modelling complex multi-physics phenomena in chemical reactors, including momentum, heat, and mass transfer, nonlinear chemical kinetics, Optimization and Control: Implementing AI for real-time process optimization, predictive maintenance, anomaly detection, and adaptive control strategies. Use of model predictive control (MPC) frameworks and reinforcement learning for scheduling and decision-making.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Statistical Mechanics of Liquids* </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit

								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                    <div class="modal-content">

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Statistical Mechanics of Liquids* [CL 634]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Recap of statistical mechanics: Microstates and macrostates, canonical ensemble, isothermal-isobaric ensemble, grand canonical ensemble, introduction to the types of simulations; Functionals: Introduction to functionals and their differentiation, functional derivatives of the grand potential; Distribution functions: Introduction to Distribution functions, direct correlation functions, density response functions, Classical density functional theory; Diagrammatic methods: Diagrammatic expansions of direct correlation functions, Introduction to virial expansion of the equation of state; Integral equation theory: Yvon-Born-Green hierarchy, Ornstein-Zernike equation, hypernetted chain approximation, Percus-Yevick approximation, mean spherical approximation; Time-dependent correlations: Properties of time correlation functions, Langevin equation, linear response theory, time-dependent fluctuations, estimation of the macroscopic transport coefficients; Experimental estimation of distribution functions; Applications of machine learning.                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Software Tools and Techniques for AI (STT-AI) [CS 203]</h1>

																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 1.5 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   1. Data collection and labeling:
a. collecting data: tools for logging/instrumenting sources (e.g. website)
to collect usage data;
b. validating data
c. labeling data: tools such as label studio to annotate datasets,
methods such as Cohen Kappa inter-annotator agreement, snowball
sampling, active learning for cost-effective data annotation,
d. data augmentation
<br><br>
2. Model and code reproducibility and versioning:
a. architecture and hyperparameter search: tools such as weights and
biases for architecture and hyperparameter search and various
AutoML tools such as AutoGluon
b. model checkpointing: tools such as MLFlow, Tensorboard/Weights,
and Biases for model/weights checkpointing
c. data versioning;
<br><br>
3. Self-contained environments: tools such as Docker for managing released
packages; cloud platforms such as GCP/AWS/Azure;
<br><br>
4. Run-time profiling and optimization (e.g., memory footprint, measuring GPU
utilization and optimizing it).
<br><br>
5. Continuous Integration/Co: A/B tests; Distribution, Covariate shift— data
version management and retraining to account for shifts; Redeployment
<br><br>
6. Deployment on constrained devices: tools such as TF-lite, topics like
quantization, etc.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 1.5 | 5<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Operating System basics: OS Evolution, History and Design Principles.
<br>
Processes and Threads: Kernel/User Level Operations; Thread/Process Synchronization.
System Calls and APIs; Interrupt Handling; CPU Scheduling.
<br>
Memory Management: Virtual Memory; Address Spaces and Translations; Segmentation and
Paging; role of TLBs in Virtual Memory; Other caching techniques.
<br>
Concurrency and Synchronization: Race Conditions; Locks; Semaphores; Concurrent Data
Structures.
<br>
I/O Systems and Storage Devices: Hard Disk Drives(HDDs); Solid State Devices(SSDs); File
Systems Design Principles and Implementation; Maintaining Data Integrity.
<br>
Miscellaneous: Virtualization; Introduction to Distributed Systems.
<br>
Case Studies: Linux/Unix.                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Mathematical Foundations for AI [CS 303]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>1. Refresher Module<br>
  &middot; Data representation (floating point arithmetic, underflow, overflow)<br>
  &middot; Linear Algebra<br>
  &middot; Calculus (univariate calculus)<br>
  &middot; basic probability<br>
  2. Multivariate Calculus and Automatic Differentiation<br>
  &middot; Gradient, Jacobian, Hessian<br>
  &middot; Finite difference methods and limitations<br>
  &middot; Automatic gradient computation<br>
  &middot; Continuous Multivariate Probability distributions<br>
  &middot; Maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) and Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)<br>
  &middot; Conjugate Priors<br>
  3. Optimization<br>
  &middot; Continuous optimization:<br>
  i. Taylor&rsquo;s series and connections to optimization methods<br>
  ii. Unconstrained Optimization:<br>
  1. Gradient descent.<br>
  2. Gauss-Newton methods and variants.</p>
<p>iii. Constrained Optimization: Lagrange multipliers, primal and dual forms, KKT<br>
  conditions.<br>
  iv. Linear and Quadratic Programming<br>
  4. Unification of Information Theory and Machine Learning<br>
  &middot; Information Theoretic concepts: self-information, entropy, KL divergence<br>
  &middot; Perfect communication over a noisy imperfect communication channel: Data<br>
  compression problem: Optimal code, Huffman encoding</p>
<p>5. Markov chains :sequential modeling, joint probability of a sequence, representation of a<br>
  chain, learning the parameters given a sequence.</p>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Data Science </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Data Science [CS 328]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Overview of data representation (as vectors, sets, graphs), basics of feature creation (e.g.
document to sets/vectors); probability basics, central limit theorem, probabilistic inequalities
and concentration bounds; Singular Value Decomposition/PCA and best-fit subspaces, low-
rank approximation, eigenvectors and power method; mixture models (gaussians, document-
topic models, graph models); applications of matrix factorizations to mixture model learning;
Modeling and estimation; maximum likelihood, Bayesian estimators, regression, sampling
methods (e.g. unbiasing, bootstrapping, confidence intervals); hypothesis testing (basics and
augmentations e.g. non-parametric, multiple hypothesis testing, Bayes Factor);
Unsupervised methods; clustering (k-means/median, kmeans++); graph analytics: ranking
methods (pagerank, HITS) and connection to random walks, algorithms for community
detection and connection to eigenvalues, triangle counting;
Introduction to memory efficient approximate computation, Bloom filters, approximate counting
(Morris’ algorithm, Count Sketch, Count-Min sketch, AMS sampling);
Distributed paradigms for big data e.g. MapReduce, introduction to Hadoop and Spark
(examples e.g. pagerank and kmeans);
Ethical and privacy issues in data science, introduction to privacy models; introduction to causality
and counterfactuals, bucket testing;                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Foundations of AI: Multiagent Systems </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Foundations of AI: Multiagent Systems [CS 329]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to AI. Flavors of AI: strong and weak, neat and scruffy, symbolic and subsymbolic, knowledge-based and data-driven. The computational metaphor. What is computation? Church-Turing thesis. The Turing test. Searle's Chinese room argument;  Representing Knowledge. Production rules, monotonic and non-monotonic logics, semantic nets, frames and scripts, description logics; Algorithmic, game-theoretic and logical foundations of multi-agent systems. Distributed optimization and problem solving, non-cooperative game theory, learning and teaching, communication, social choice, mechanism design, auctions, negotiation, coalitional game theory, logics of knowledge and belief, collaborative plans and social systems; Fairness, Ethics, and Bias. Algorithmic fairness: Individual fairness, Group fairness. Applications of fairness in AI: Fairness in natural language processing, Fairness in computer vision, Deepfakes, Federated learning.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 1.5 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Operating systems basics: Evolution, history and design principles; Processes and threads: Kernel/user level operation, thread/process synchronization, system calls and APIs, interrupt handling, CPU scheduling; Memory management: virtual memory, address spaces and translations, segmentation and paging, TLBs and other caching techniques; Concurrency and synchronization: Race conditions, locks, semaphores, concurrent data structures; I/O systems and storage devices: Hard disk drives, solid state devices, file systems design principles and implementation, maintaining data integrity; Advanced topics: Virtualization, distributed systems.                                                                </p>

							<hr>

							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                       Course Content

                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">

                                                               <h1>Computer Networks [CS 331]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Computer Networks: Layered Network Protocol Architectures: OSI and TCP/IP reference models. Overview of Telecommunications, Cellular networks & Internet Architecture; Physical and Link Layers: Basics of communications; Delay, Loss and Throughput. Physical media types and their important bandwidth and bit-error-rate characteristics; Wired and Wireless media including copper cables, optical fiber and wireless. Framing; Error control: Bit-parity, CRC and Hamming Codes; Medium Access Control (MAC): Shared media systems; Bus, Star and Ring topologies; TDMA, FDMA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, Ethernet and IEEE 802.3; IEEE 802.11 including CSMA/CA protocols; Shared and Switched Ethernet; Introduce protocols such as ARP, RARP and ICMP; Network Layer: Design Principles: Protocols, Addressing and Routing. Study of Internet Protocol (IP) suite; Hierarchical network architectures; IPv4 and IPv6 addressing and headers; Routing protocols: distance-vector and link-state approaches; Interior and Exterior Gateway Protocol concepts; Routing Algorithms including Dijkstra's algorithm and distributed Bellman-Ford algorithm; Example protocols: OSPF, RIP, BGP; Transport Layer: Connection oriented and Connectionless protocols: TCP and UDP; Details of TCP: Buffer Management, Flow Control and Congestion control; Study of TCP variants such as Reno, Tahoe, Vegas and CUBIC. Reliable transmission and Automatic Repeat Request (ARQ) protocols: Stop-and-Wait, Go-back-N, Selective Repeat; Application Layer: Study of Protocols and Applications: DNS, FTP, HTTP, and SMTP.  Socket Interface and Socket programming; Advanced topics and recent trends in Computer Networks and Future Internet: Information Centric networking, Software Defined Networking (SDN), Network Function Virtualization (NFV), Internet of Things (IoT), Docker and Container Networking (CNIs), Programmable switches, OVS, and SmartNICs; Miscellaneous: Introduction to Datacenter Networks, Different RFCs; Case Studies: Linux/Unix TCP IP Stack; Network Simulators: NS-3, and Mininet; Optional: NS-2, OMNet,                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Ethics of AI </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Ethics of AI [CS 333]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <strong>Introduction to AI Ethics and Accountability</strong><br>
- Discuss the critical role ethics play in AI research and deployment, emphasizing the potential societal      impacts and the necessity for responsible innovation to prevent harm and promote trust.<br>
-Identify stakeholders responsible for AI outcomes, including developers, organizations, and policymakers, and discuss the allocation of accountability.<br>
<strong>Algorithmic Bias and Fairness</strong><br>
- Introduction: Case studies on biased AI systems and explore sources of bias.<br>
- Fairness Definitions: Sufficiency, Separation and Calibration and their relationship with each other and accuracy<br>
- Fairness Approaches: Explore one in-processing, pre-processing and post-processing approach with<br>
application to real-world dataset for mitigating bias<br>
<strong>Privacy</strong><br>
- Case studies on Data Privacy<br>
- Reconstruction and Membership Inference Attacks<br>
-  Introduction to Differential Privacy<br>
-  Differentially Private - Stochastic Gradient Descent<br>
<strong>Interpretability</strong><br>
- Motivation and Goals for Interpretability<br>
- Overview of Model Agnostic Methods<br>
- Overview of Model Specific Methods.<br>
- Neural network interpretation<br>
<strong>Global Perspectives and Regulations</strong><br>
- International policies and guidelines on AI ethics.<br>
- Comparative analysis of AI regulations across countries.<br>
- The role of organizations in setting ethical standards.                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Computer Networks [CS 433]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Computer Networks: Layered Network Protocol Architectures:
OSI and TCP/IP reference models. Overview of Telecommunications, Cellular
networks &amp; Internet Architecture.
Physical and Link Layers: Basics of communications; Delay, Loss and
Throughput. Physical media types and their important bandwidth and bit-error-rate
characteristics; Wired and Wireless media including copper cables, optical fiber
and wireless. Framing; Error control: Bit-parity, CRC and Hamming Codes.
Medium Access Control (MAC): Shared media systems; Bus, Star and Ring
topologies; TDMA, FDMA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, Ethernet and IEEE 802.3; IEEE
802.11 including CSMA/CA protocols; Shared and Switched Ethernet; Introduce
protocols such as ARP, RARP and ICMP.
Network Layer: Design Principles: Protocols, Addressing and Routing. Study of
Internet Protocol (IP) suite; Hierarchical network architectures; IPv4 and IPv6
addressing and headers; Routing protocols: distance-vector and link-state
approaches; Interior and Exterior Gateway Protocol concepts; Routing Algorithms
including Dijkstra&#39;s algorithm and distributed Bellman-Ford algorithm; Example
protocols: OSPF, RIP, BGP.
Transport Layer: Connection oriented and Connectionless protocols: TCP and
UDP; Details of TCP: Buffer Management, Flow Control and Congestion control;
Study of TCP variants such as Reno, Tahoe, Vegas and CUBIC. Reliable
transmission and Automatic Repeat Request (ARQ) protocols: Stop-and-Wait, Go-
back-N, Selective Repeat.
Application Layer: Study of Protocols and Applications: DNS, FTP, HTTP, and
SMTP. Socket Interface and Socket programming.
Advanced topics and recent trends in Computer Networks and Future Internet:
Information Centric networking, Software Defined Networking (SDN), Network
Function Virtualization (NFV), Internet of Things (IoT), Docker and Container
Networking (CNIs), Programmable switches, OVS, and SmartNICs.
Tutorials will also include hands-on programming and problem solving.
Miscellaneous: Introduction to Datacenter Networks, Different RFCs.
Case Studies: Linux/Unix TCP IP Stack.
Network Simulators: NS-3, and Mininet.
Optional: NS-2, OMNet,                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Software Engineering and Testing </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Basics of software engineering: software versus product; why software engineering; development styles; Software development life cycle: models; classic; waterfall; agile; scrum; devOps; Phases: requirement analysis; feasibility study; software design; software architecture; coding; testing; release; maintenance; Types of testing: unit; system; regression; white box; grey box; black box; Software maintenance: evolution; change analysis; test-case selection; prioritization; reduction; debugging; fault localization, version control; Software reverse engineering: decompilation; binary lifting; Advanced topics: differential testing; domain specific testing, flaky tests, test parallelization, test dependency detection.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Human-Computer Interaction </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Human-Computer Interaction [CS 435]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction: Human-Computer Interaction (HCI); the role of an effective design
(example of good and bad design) in the interaction design process; Concepts:
describes major design principles associated with HCI e.g., visibility, flow,
affordance, metaphors, feedback, consistency etc.; Understanding users and
data gathering: Overview of how to plan and run successful data gathering
sessions including interviews, design questionnaires and run observations;
Requirements formulation: describes how to interpret results from data
gathering sessions into requirements for design, explains the process and
purpose of literature reviews and goes through how to develop scenarios and
use cases; User interfaces and modalities: introduction to the many different
types of user interface and modalities, and how they are used; Design,
prototyping and construction: explain why to prototype and how to construct
prototypes (low and high-fidelity), and how to begin construction based on
designs; Evaluation: why is evaluation important, goals, methods, case study;
Data analysis: discuss the qualitative and quantitative data and describes how
best to analyze the results from data gathering sessions; Group course project
(low/high-fidelity prototype) submission and presentation; Future directions:
what is next for HCI and interaction design?                                                                </p>

																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction: A Historical Perspective of Computer Systems - Hardware, Firmware and Software. Key inventions and Proliferations.
Development of computer networks, Internet and computing platforms - the philosophy of computers, necessity and innovations, including
the history and evolution of Indian ICT.<br>
Advancements: Proliferation of Computers in various domains of science and engineering, business, finance, healthcare, medicine,
finance and governance. Evolution of Utility computing, Clusters, Grids, Data Centers, Cloud. Cybercrimes and Cybersecurity, Policies,
Computer Ethics and Societal Impact.<br>
Future Prospects: Quantum Computing, Quantum Communication. Reflections on the lessons learnt by the History and evolution of
computing.                                                                </p>

Theory: Ramseyan Theorems for Numbers, Hales–Jewett Theorem.                                                                </p>
