                                                               <h1>Advanced Algorithms [CS 614]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   ● Approximation Algorithms: Greedy and combinatorial methods; Local search; Dynamic
programming and approximation schemes; Linear programming rounding methods
(randomized, primal-dual, dual-fitting, iterated rounding); Semidefinite program based
rounding; Metric methods; Hardness of Approximation; Unique Games
● Randomized Algorithms: Game-theoretic techniques; Moments and deviations; Tail
inequalities; The probabilistic method; Markov chains and random walks; Algebraic
techniques.
● Parameterized Algorithms: Bounded-Depth Search Trees; Kernelization; Iterative
Compression; Color Coding; Divide and Color; Treewidth; Introduction to W-hardness and
the hardness of kernelization.
● Exact Algorithms: Branching; Dynamic programming over subsets; Inclusion-Exclusion;
Algebraic techniques; Measure and Conquer; Local Search; Hardness results conditioned on
SETH and ETH.
● Suggested Problems: Vertex Cover; Set Cover; Feedback Vertex Set; Sparsest Cut;
k-Cut; Multiway Cut; Facility Location; Satisfiability and variants; Constraint
Satisfaction                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Distributed Systems and Cloud Computing </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 1.5 | 5							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Distributed Systems and Cloud Computing [CS 616]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 1.5 | 5<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Distributed Systems: Fundamental concepts of processes and synchronization; locks and barriers; Semaphores and Monitors, communication concurrency control. Logical Clocks, Vector Clocks and Clock Synchronization. Lab exercises based on synchronization using Go programming language.<br>
Distributed Consensus: Transactions and Leader election Algorithms. Fault tolerance: ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) /BASE (Basically Available, Soft-state, Eventual consistency) properties, (Consistency, Availability, Partitioning) CAP theorem, Replication and Redundancy models. Lab exercises based on fault tolerance using Go programming language.<br>
Interaction: Message passing interface (MPI), remote procedure call (RPC) and rendezvous; (manager/workers, heartbeat, and pipeline), probe/echo, broadcast, token passing, and replicated servers. Interconnection topologies, load balancing, memory consistency model, scalability and performance studies, scheduling, storage systems. Process/VM Migration. Security of distributed systems and data sharing, authentication and authorization, protocol security.<br>
Cloud Computing: Characteristics and components of Cloud, service and deployment models, hybrid &amp; multi-cloud architecture, virtual machines, containers and serverless computing. Among recent advances in Distributed Stream Processors, Google Cluster Architecture, Amazon Lambda, Kubernetes. Lab exercises on Kubeless/Kubernetes/Serverless Platforms. <br>
Labs will include hands-on programming in the Go programming language and working with different distributed tools and platforms.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->

																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   ● Fundamentals: Definition of Turing Machines, Time and Space, Diagonalization, Hierarchy Theorems.<br>
● Poly-time (P), Nondeterministic poly-time (NP), and completeness: Definitions of P and NP, Turing reductions and many-one reductions, NP-completeness of Satisfiability (SAT).<br>
● Space complexity: Logspace (L) and Nondeterministic Logspace (NL), Savitch’s Theorem, logspace reductions, NL-completeness of directed graph reachability, NL=coNL (coNL indicating complement of NL), PSPACE (polynomial space).<br>
● Randomness and Interaction: Bounded-error probabilistic polynomial-time (BPP) and randomized-polynomial time (RP), Polynomial Identity Testing (PIT), Valiant-Vazirani Theorem, Polynomial hierarchy (PH) and its characterizations, PSPACE (polynomial space), PSPACE-completeness of Quantified Boolean Formulas (QBF), interactive proofs (IP), Arthur-Merlin classes, IP = PSPACE.<br>
● Non-uniformity: Boolean circuits, Nick’s class (NC), Matching in Randomized NC (RNC), Karp-Lipton Theorem.<br>
● Cryptography and Complexity: One-way functions and Pseudorandom generators.<br>
● Counting: #P, Toda’s theorem, The permanent<br>
● Circuits: Alternating circuits of constant depth (AC0), Alternating circuits with counting (ACC0), Threshold circuits (TC0), Nick’s class level 1 (NC1) etc., AC0 lower bounds.<br>
● Branching programs: Barrington’s Theorem, Monoid programs.<br>
● Exact complexity: Exponential-time hypothesis (ETH) and Strong exponential-time hypothesis (SETH), sparsification lemma.<br>
● Randomness vs Hardness: Derandomization implies circuit lower bounds.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#000000">CS 617</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="CS">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/cse.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Theoretical Foundations of Machine Learning </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Theoretical Foundations of Machine Learning [CS 618]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Concentration of measure – various concentration inequalities; PAC learning, VC dimension, Rademacher complexity, generalization bounds, sample complexity. Generalization in deep learning setting – double descent; Optimization – random projection, randomized numerical linear algebra and its applications in efficient optimization (e.g. regression, matrix factorization, second order descent); Beyond the worst-case analysis of machine learning algorithms – algorithms for non-negative matrix factorization, tensor decomposition, sparse recovery, dictionary learning; Kernels and their applications in ML --- kernel trick, Representer theorem, Support vector machine with kernels, Green’s theorem, Mercer’s theorem, Bochner’s theorem; learning kernels; Subset selection and its variants --- using submodularity, using importance sampling (coresets). Applications in data valuation, continual learning, and in optimization; Introduction to Gaussian Process (GP), neural network-GP equivalence, neural tangent kernel; Learning in stochastic settings -- Introduction to online learning and multi-armed bandits; Gittin’s index, Thomson sampling, adversarial bandits, contextual bandits.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>

						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="CS">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/cse.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">CS Theory Toolkit </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>CS Theory Toolkit [CS 619]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p><strong>Logic:</strong> Propositions, connectives, quantifiers, rules of inference,<br>
  induction, strong induction, structural induction.</p>
<p><strong>Combinatorics:</strong> Definition of graphs and various structures in graphs. Koenig, Hall, and<br>
  Menger theorems. The TONCAS (The obvious necessary conditions are also sufficient)<br>
  principle, Common proof techniques (structural induction, attacking minimal counterexamples etc.) for graph theory, algebraic techniques, algorithmic proofs (Havel-Hakimi<br>
  theorem, Eulerian circuits, Pruefer codes). Advanced Topic: Parameterized algorithms:<br>
  Branching algorithms and proof by cases, refining cases to increase efficiency, tree<br>
  decompositions and dynamic programming (Independent set), algebraic techniques for<br>
  exact algorithms (inclusion-exclusion)</p>
<p>  <strong>Probability theory:</strong> Review of basics, Expectation and variance, linearity of expectation,<br>
  concentration bounds, derandomization, probabilistic methods in proofs. Advanced topic: A<br>
  randomized algorithm for perfect matching.\</p>
<p>  <strong>Computational models:</strong> What is computation?, motivation and definition of RAM (Random<br>
  access machine) model, Algorithmic correctness and worst-case complexity. lower bounds<br>
  vs upper bounds, reductions, Satisfiability and reductions to Satisfiability (Hamiltonian Cycle<br>
  to Satisfiability, Vertex Cover to Satisfiability), hardness and completeness via reductions.<br>
  identifying the right reductions (many-one, Turing, logspace etc.), gadget constructions (one<br>
  or two advanced examples). </p>
<p><strong>Advanced topic:</strong> Boolean circuits, constant-depth circuit<br>
  constructions, circuit reductions</p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->

                                </div>
                            </div>

                                                               <h1>Incentives and Machine Learning [CS 620]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p><strong>1.	Introduction to Incentives </strong><br>
  a.	Multi-agent Interactions and Equilibrium<br>
  b.	Mechanism Design - VCG<br>
  c.	Optimal Auctions<br>
  d.	Ad Auctions</p>
<p>  <strong>2.	Machine Learning in Incentive Design</strong> <br>
  a.	Automated Mechanism Design (AMD) &ndash; Optimal Auctions through Deep Learning (DL)<br>
  b.	AMD &ndash; Redistribution Mechanisms<br>
  c.	Iterative Combinatorial Auctions using DL and ML<br>
  d.	Multi-Armed Bandit-based Mechanism Design</p>
<p><strong>3.	Incentive Aware Machine Learning </strong><br>
  a.	Strategic Classification<br>
  b.	Incentive Aware PAC-Learning<br>
  c.	Incentivizing Differential Privacy in ML</p>
<p><strong>4.	Collaborative Machine Learning </strong><br>
  a.	Federated Learning<br>
  b.	Incentives to Collaborate in Federated Learning</p>
<p><strong>5.	Data Quality</strong><br>
  a.	Data Valuation and Crowdsourcing<br>
  b.	Peer Prediction for Responsible Data Governance</p>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Ethics of AI </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Ethics of AI [CS 621]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Algorithmic Fairness and Accountability, where students study operationalized fairness criteria, constrained optimization for fair models, and the socio-technical dimensions of bias through case studies in vision and NLP; the Interpretability and Explainability module introduces inherently interpretable models, post-hoc explanations (LIME, SHAP, Integrated Gradients, SmoothGrad), counterfactual and concept-based explanations (TCAV, Network Dissection), and connects interpretability with fairness; Mechanistic Interpretability and Representation Engineering delves into circuits, compiled transformers, and emergent reasoning in large models (e.g., Chain-of-Thought prompting, interpretability in diffusion and vision-language models); Robustness and Adversarial ML explores vulnerabilities in AI systems, the history and theory of adversarial examples, adversarial training, robustness–accuracy trade-offs, and the security of large models under adversarial or distributional shifts; Privacy and Security in AI explores data protection, membership inference, reconstruction attacks, and the theoretical and practical aspects of Differential Privacy and DP-SGD; Ethics of Large-Scale AI Systems examines the societal, legal, and governance implications of LLMs and generative models, including transparency, accountability, and global regulatory trends.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Technopreneurship: Product Proposition </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>2 | 0 | 3 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Technopreneurship: Product Proposition [DES 201]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The course intends to promote contextual innovation that is feasible and marketable. The students will get an opportunity to apply the theoretical knowledge of engineering to design a solution that works. The course structure and pedagogy will require creation of functional teams within students, simulating professional practice<br>
This course also aims to integrate entrepreneurial activity with the academic curriculum and promote innovations that are technologically strong.<br>
Design process to be central to the stage wise progress; cyclic iterative development will be integral to development of the product solution. Various tools and methods to find a focused issue and lead towards an effective solution will be employed suitably.<br>
Topics covered<br>
&#9635; <b>Introduction to business innovations</b><br>
- Drivers of successful innovations (usability, desirability and feasibility)<br>
- Opportunities for business innovations (organization structure, product offerings, customer experience) and their intersection with product innovation<br>
&#9635; <b>Define the project brief: opportunities for product innovation</b><br>
- Defining a need statement that leads to the investigation in target market segmentation - User profile: Investigate and define demographics of the target user as applicable.<br>
- Environment/ context: establishing the seasonal, geographical, social and environmental needs etc. as applicable<br>
- Prior art and/or existing solutions/trend analysis in the market and potential gap areas<br>
&#9635; <b>Concept development: iterative, cyclic design process</b><br>
- Application of design thinking tools such as mind maps, fishbone diagrams to be used for data mapping and analysis<br>
- Ideation (multiple ideas based on the opportunities): use of design methods such as Brainstorming/ SCAMPER / Synectics for ideation.<br>
- Consolidation of concepts using progressive sketches (schematic &gt;&gt; product concept) and low fidelity mock-ups using materials available around <br>
&#9635; <b>Product proposition</b><br>
- Consolidated proposition with user, context and design opportunities<br>
- Medium fidelity prototype(s) as a proof of concept: the prototype(s) should explain the working of the product and interaction with the user (combined or separate as required)                                                                </p>

                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

                                            </div>

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Creativity, Design and Doing [DES 302]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Design as a phenomenon: The meaning of design as perceived by an ‘informed’ user, lay person, designer, artist and the one who
sells a designed product; Craftsmanship: Design in relation to Craftsmanship / the object culture / the built environment. Design as
a social, political and environmental act; Why design? Doing more with less energy, resources and time. Design for comfort,
convenience, pleasure, productivity, ease of operation, safety, equity; How does one design? The process from conceptualization to
concretization; What is creativity? The importance of design thinking and creativity; Design and skills: Be informed, imagine,
visualize, sketch, draw, simulate and make.                                                                </p>

						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="DES">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/design.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Technopreneurship: Product Development + Business Strategy </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>2 | 0 | 3 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Technopreneurship: Product Development + Business Strategy [DES 303]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>The course will involve a major project; where the students work parallely on multiple fronts to make the product solution ready for business incubation. Introductory sessions for each topic and mentoring will be provided to the students. The project is driven by student teams; seeking mentorship as they face roadblocks. Timely evaluations and feedback from a curated panel of members from the industry and business experts are integrated in the structure. </p>
<p>They will make extensive use of design as a strategic problem solving process that leads to better quality of life through innovative products, systems, services and experiences. <br>
  Topics/activities covered:</p>
<p>&bull; <strong>Developing high fidelity prototype (using in-house IITGN facilities and/or vendors and external mentorship)</strong><br>
  - Understanding criticalities of design detailing for materials and processes, assembly<br>
  - Learning to plan for fabrication and assembly, optimizing the resources (material, time, human)<br>
  - Material procurement, communication with vendor and logistics </p>
<p>&bull; <strong>Protecting the intellectual property (as and if applicable)</strong><br>
  - Understanding the need and process of protecting intellectual property (Copyright / Patent/ Registration etc)<br>
  - Scrutinizing which method and/or process will be most appropriate for the proposed concept</p>
<p><strong>&bull; Chalking out a business strategy: keeping the product solution as the focus</strong><br>
  - Vision for the near future such as identifying the target market size<br>
- Understanding the basics of production management, decisions on volume of production, projecting the resources required</p>
<p>  <strong>&bull; Customer feedback, validation from subject experts (adapted from user experience design)</strong><br>
  - Testing how the intended users interact with the product; modifications / demonstrations required for a smooth barrier free interaction.<br>
- Subject experts in fields such as medical experts, manufacturers,policy makers or NGO workers etc. as applicable for implementation of product solution.</p>
<p><strong>&bull; Understanding the basics of production and management</strong><br>
  - Making the participants ready for pilot production;decisions on volume of production, projecting the material resources and infrastructure required.<br>
- Inventory management and logistics required in the near future (as applicable to the product solution only)</p>
<p><strong>&bull; Registration of the business venture</strong><br>
  - Understanding business structures like Proprietorship, Partnership, Company (Private company or Public company), etc.<br>
  - Legal and financial aspects of each of these structures<br>
- A brief introduction to statutory requirements </p>
<p><strong>&bull; Financial plan and resource management: for first two years of product venture management</strong><br>
  - Introduction to return on investment, break even point<br>
  - How to come with a financial equation for a financial quarter or a fiscal<br>
- An understanding for capital vs operational funds required for the venture</p>
<p><strong>&bull; Presentation and representation of the project:</strong><br>
  - How to pitch the solution; various formats suitable for particular audiences<br>
  - Media for verbal and nonverbal communication<br>
  - Representation of technical data/ product details<br>
  - Packaging the solution in a marketable format: usability, product handling, introduction to branding and packaging<br>
</p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#4B0082">DES 303</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="DES">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/design.png" /></i>

                                                               <h1>Visual Design for Academia [DES 601]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Overview of various types of visual presentation artifacts required by academia; Planning the
visuals; Types of content and corresponding types of graphics; Visual representations of
information: Mind maps and concept maps; Principles of the gestalt theory of visual perception,
which include: similarity, continuation, closure, proximity, figure/ground, and symmetry;
Typography: types and categories of fonts, weights, kerning, leading, spacing, using appropriate
typography for the respective graphic; Color theory: meaning, associations, color schemes,
contrast, etc; Copyright information for graphics available online: Licenses and their scope;
Visualization and representation of research data: Principles of data visualization, types of data
representation, and applying visual design principles while presenting; Student independent
projects for designing academic artifacts: Diagrams, Posters, Presentations.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Fundamentals of 3D Modeling and Animation </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Fundamentals of 3D Modeling and Animation [DES 603]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The course follows a spiral learning structure where core concepts&mdash;form, light, composition, and motion&mdash;are introduced through simple analog<br>
experiences and revisited in digital form with increasing complexity. Each spiral loop integrates both tactile and digital modes of learning, allowing<br>
students to first explore ideas through drawing, paper modeling, origami, or shadow play, and then translate those understandings into 3D modeling<br>
and animation using latest tools such as Maya, 3DS Max, and Blender.<br>
Introduction to form and space through sketching and observation of basic 3D shapes.<br>
Exploration of structure by constructing geometric forms using folds and surfaces.<br>
Understanding 3D environments, navigation, and creating simple shapes in a virtual space.<br>
Developing complex forms using transformations, editing techniques, and modifiers.<br>
Studying the behavior of light and shadow through observation and model interaction.<br>
Applying lighting techniques to create depth, focus, and contrast in modeled scenes.<br>
Composing visual scenes by arranging elements with attention to balance and hierarchy.<br>
Using viewpoint and framing tools to guide visual storytelling within the scene.<br>
Exploring surface qualities through materials, colors, and textures.<br>
Investigating the principles of motion through simple sequencing and visual timing.<br>
Animating objects with transformations to convey movement and rhythm.<br>
Final project development, rendering, and presentation of animated scenes.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>

                                                                   Properties of Semiconductors: Introduction to semiconductors; Energy bands, semiconductor in equilibrium, charge carrier in semiconductors, intrinsic, extrinsic semiconductors, Fermi-Dirac statistics, charge neutrality, carrier transport, drift, diffusion, excess carriers in semiconductors; Fundamental semiconductor devices: Semiconductor diodes - Basic structure, operation, I-V characteristics, ideal and non-ideal characteristics; Metal oxide semiconductor field effect transistor - Basic structure, operation, I-V characteristics, ideal and non-idealities characteristics; Bipolar junction transistor - Basic structure, operation, I-V characteristics, ideal and non-ideal characteristics; Special semiconductor devices: Optoelectronic devices and modern semiconductor devices (for example: light emitting diodes and lasers; solar cells, multi-gate devices: FinFETs and nanosheet FETs, power mosfets, HEMTs, other advanced electronic devices, etc.).                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Electrical Machines [EE 223]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 1 | 1.5 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Transformers: Role of transformers in power system, working principle, single phase
transformer, equivalent circuit, voltage regulation, losses and efficiency; three phase
transformers; autotransformer; instrument transformers; Linear and rotating machines:
Principles of electromechanical energy conversion – forces and torques in magnetic field
system, field energy and co-energy; DC Machines: Applications, constructional details,
generating and motoring modes, classification of machines, terminal characteristics,
losses and efficiency, starting and speed control of DC motors; Synchronous machines:
Generator – Three phase EMF generation, circuit model, terminal characteristics, voltage
regulation, parallel operation and load sharing, Motor – creation of travelling magnetic
field, starting methods, speed control; Induction Machines: Induction motor – Workhorse
of industries, constructional details, working principle, circuit model, terminal
characteristics, starting methods, speed control; Special machines and applications:
Single-phase induction motor, permanent magnet motors (BLDC and PMSM).                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Power Systems </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Power Systems [EE 224]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to power systems: Electricity supply value chain - generation, transmission,
and distribution; Key stakeholders involved in electricity business; Power and energy
outlook in India; Modeling of power system components; Circuit fundamentals, Active and
reactive power calculations, Three-phase circuits, Single line diagram and per unit (PU)
system representation; Transmission line parameters and their calculations; Transmission
line modelling and performance analysis – short, medium and long transmission lines;
voltage regulation and transmission line efficiency; Power flow analysis – network
modeling, power flow solution methods: Gauss seidel (GS), Newton-Raphson (NR)
method and DC power flow ; Economic operation of power system – optimal dispatch of
generation, automatic generation control (AGC), and frequency control; Short circuit
analysis – symmetrical faults, symmetrical components and sequence networks,
unsymmetrical fault analysis; Power system stability – swing equation and equal area
criterion of stability; Introduction to electricity market operation and framework, smart grid
and renewable energy integration, electricity tariff and billing.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

in Semiconductor, Temperature dependence, Carrier Transport (Drift and Diffusion), Basic Semiconductor Equations; PN Junction Diode:
Physics and Operation, Zener Diode, Optoelectronic Devices like Photodiodes and Light Emitting Diodes; Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT):
Physics and Operation, Heterojunction Bipolar Transistors (HBTs); Metal Oxide Semiconductor Field Effect Transistor (MOSFET): Physics
and Operation, Modern MOSFETs like FinFET, GAAFETs; Memory Devices: Volatile and Non-volatile Memories, Flash Memory Devices,
Introduction to Emerging Non-Volatile Memories.                                                                </p>

																<br>2 | 1 | 1.5 | 4<br>

                                                                   <p>Overview of CMOS technology and applications, Semiconductor ecosystem: Design to tapeout, MOSFET I-V characteristics, Threshold voltage, regions of operation, Capacitances in MOSFETs, CMOS inverter introduction, DC transfer characteristics (VTC), Noise margins; Circuit simulation basics, introduction to SPICE models and simulation of inverter and logic gates; &lambda;-based layout design rules, Stick diagrams, Inverter layout &amp; simulation, Rise/Fall Time, Dynamic Power, Energy-Delay Product; logical effort introduction, Delay and power analysis, Logical effort method for delay estimation, fan-out and sizing, NAND, NOR, XOR, transmission gates, Logic gate sizing, Layout and delay trade-offs, Latch, D-FF design using transmission gates and CMOS logic, AOI/OAI gates, Basics of Interconnects, Overview of CMOS Common-source amplifier, gain and frequency response, Current mirrors, Differential Amplifiers, OTA, 6T SRAM bitcell sizing, Read/write path, stability, Sense amplifier basics, memory subsystem.</p>
<p>Labs will be based on topics covered in theory and will involve circuit simulations and analysis.</p>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Electromagnetic Waves<br><br> </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Electromagnetic Waves<br><br> [EE 311]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of static electric and magnetic fields; electromagnetic (EM) waves and applications;
Transmission lines: concept of distributed elements, transmission line equations, phase and
attenuation constants, propagation constant and characteristic impedance, lossless, low-loss and
distortion-less lines, travelling and standing waves; reflection coefficient and SWR, input impedance,
impedance matching – quarter and half-wave lines, equivalent reactive elements, load impedance
measurement, analysis of open-circuited and short-circuited lines, stub matching, power flow in a
transmission line, maximum power transfer condition, graphical representation of a transmission line,
Smith chart, transmission line calculations using the Smith chart, pulse propagation, various types of
transmission lines; Maxwell’s equations, displacement current, time-varying potentials, Lorentz
conditions, boundary conditions at media interface; EM wave propagation - in lossy dielectrics, in
lossless dielectrics, in free-space and in conductors, skin effect and skin depth, intrinsic impedance,
complex permittivity and loss tangent, power flow and the Poynting vector, phase and group velocity,
reflection of EM waves; Waveguides: parallel plate waveguide, rectangular and cylindrical
waveguides, cut-off frequency, TE, TM and TEM modes; EM radiation and antennas: retarded
potentials, Hertzian dipole, short loop antenna, antenna characteristics and radiation parameters,
Friis equation, standard antennas – dipole, array, aperture and horn.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

                                                               <h1>Engineering Electromagnetics [EE 312]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The EM spectrum, technological importance of each of the spectral bands, material
properties and responses to frequencies; Freespace and guided waves; Maxwell’s
equations as generalization of circuit laws, lumped and distributed circuit representations,
power and energy in waves and relation to power and energy in circuits; Linearity and
reciprocity, boundary conditions; The transmission line and its distributed circuit model,
reflection and standing waves, impedance matching and impedance transformation,
Smith chart, microwave circuits and networks. Relevance of TL concept to power system
transients, IC interconnects and RF ICs; The waveguiding of EM energy, metallic and
dielectric waveguide, modes, cutoff and evanescence. Phase and group velocity,
rectangular and circular waveguides. Relevance of waveguide concept to optical fiber
links and silicon photonics. Basics of EM radiation, performance metrics of antennas
including directivity, gain and bandwidth, common antennas and applications, Dipole and
aperture antennas, antenna arrays.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">

																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 1 | 0 | 3<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Components of a communication link, channel signal to noise ratio, channel capacity;
Modulation and demodulation; Analog and digital communication links; Review of signals
and spectra, signal vector space; Review of random processes and power spectral
density; Analog communication Baseband vs carrier communications, Amplitude
modulation, QAM, Angle modulation (PM, FM); Narrowband and wide-band FM.
Transmission bandwidth analysis, signal generation and detection; Frequency division
multiplexing; Pulse code modulation, sampling theorem, aliasing, analog-to-digital
conversion and quantisation noise; Uniform and non uniform quantisation, time division
multiplexing; delta modulation, DPCM; Digital communications basics, Baseband and
carrier-modulated digital, various line codes and their PSD, pulse shaping, inter-symbol
interference, Nyquist ISI criterion, Duo-binary signalling and differential encoding; Noise
analysis - Gaussian, Rayleigh and Rician distributions; Eye diagrams, timing
extraction, Detection - non-coherent and coherent, BER analysis of PCM; Digital
bandpass modulation, ASK, PSK, FSK, BER analysis.                                                                </p>

                                                                   Review of BJT and MOS transistor operation; BJT and MOS transistor biasing schemes, small signal
models; CE/CS, CB/CG and CC/CD amplifiers; BJT and MOSFET high frequency models, SPICE
models and simulation; frequency response of CS amplifiers; Miller’s theorem; current mirrors; active
loads; cascode amplifier; differential amplifiers; feedback topologies and properties, stability, Barkhausen
criterion, effect of feedback on amplifier poles, Bode plots, gain and phase margins; positive feedback
and sinusoidal oscillators, Wien bridge oscillator, other op-amp based RC oscillators; multivibrators,
square and triangle waveform generation; precision rectifier circuits; filter types and specifications,
Butterworth and Chebychev filters first and second order filters, second order LCR resonators, active
filters based on inductor replacement; data conversion, D/A converter circuits, A/D converter circuits.                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Analog & Mixed Signal Circuits [EE 322]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 1 | 1.5 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Analysis of Linear Systems and Non-linearity in Analog Electronics: Review of Electronic Components, Two-port Matrix Analysis, Sources of Noise, Non-linearity in Amplifiers, Dynamic Range, Signal-to-noise Ratio, Fundamental Relationships between Power, Speed and Accuracy; Operational Amplifier Circuits (Analog Filters): Introduction to Operational Amplifiers; Transfer Function and Frequency Response; Poles, Zeros and Stability Analysis; Low Pass, High Pass, Band Pass, Band Stop and All Pass Filters; Continuous Time and Discrete Time Filters; Oscillators and Waveform Generators: Positive and Negative Feedback; Barkhausen’s Criterion; Sinusoidal Oscillators; RC Phase Shift Oscillator; Wien Bridge Oscillator; Other OpAmp based RC Oscillators; Relaxation Oscillators; Monostable and Astable Multivibrators; Phase-Locked Loops: Principles and Properties (Acquisition/lock, Phase Noise); Basic architecture and Building blocks; Applications of PLL: Up/Down Conversion, Frequency Synthesizers, FM Demodulation, Clock and Data Recovery; Analog-to-Digital and Digital-to-Analog Conversion: Principles and Properties of Data Conversion (Resolution, Linearity, Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)); Sampling and Quantization; Building Blocks: Sample & Hold/Track & Hold; ADC Architectures: Nyquist and Oversampling A to D converters; DAC Architectures                                                                </p>

						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="EE">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/ee.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Digital Signal Processing </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Digital Signal Processing [EE 323]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p><strong>Module 1: Discrete Time Signals and Systems</strong><br>
Introduction To Discrete-Time Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) Signals and Systems, Linear Phase</p>
<p>Systems, Brief Review of Fourier Representations; Sampling And Reconstruction of Continuous-<br>
  Time Signals, Analysis of LTI Systems Using Z Transform, Applications.</p>
<p><strong>Module 2: Discrete Fourier Transform</strong><br>
  The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT), Properties, Efficient DFT Computation by FFT, Effects of<br>
  Finite Word Length, Linear and Circular Convolutions, Block Convolutions for Long Sequences,<br>
  Signal Analysis by DFT, Spectral Analysis by Periodogram and Autocorrelation Estimates,<br>
Applications.</p>
<p>  <strong>Module 3: Digital Filter Design</strong><br>
  Analog Filter Design, Digital Filters - Infinite and Finite Impulse Response (IIR And FIR), IIR Filters<br>
  - Stability, Design By Impulse Invariance, Bilinear Transformations, Frequency Transformations of<br>
  Low Pass IIR Filters, FIR Filter Design by Windowing Method and Parks-McClellan Algorithm, Finite<br>
  Precision Numerical Effects; Decimation and Interpolation of Signals, Quadrature Mirror Filters and<br>
  Perfect Reconstruction, Subband Decomposition, Applications.</p>
<p>  <strong>Module 4: Real-World Applications</strong><br>
  Real-world applications will be drawn from the research areas, such as Communication Systems,<br>
  Antennas, Image Processing, Audio Processing, Finance, and Biomedical Engineering.</p>                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Electrical Machines [EE 331]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Transformers – working principle, single phase transformer, equivalent circuit, voltage
regulation, losses and efficiency; three phase transformer; autotransformer; instrument
transformers.
Principles of electromechanical energy conversion – forces and torques in magnetic field
system, field energy and coenergy; DC machines – constructional details, generating and
motoring modes, classification of machines, terminal characteristics, losses and efficiency,
starting and speed control of DC motors; Alternator – generation of three phase emf,
circuit model, terminal characteristics, voltage regulation, parallel operation of alternators
and load sharing; Synchronous motor – creation of travelling magnetic field, starting
methods, speed control; Induction motor – constructional details, working principle,
circuit model, terminal characteristics, starting methods, speed control; Special machines
– universal motor, single phase induction motor, stepper motor, servo motor, permanent
magnet motors, switched reluctance motors; Selection of motor for specific application;
Engineering aspects of electric machine performance and operation.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Energy sources;Structure of power system; Basic concepts of 3-Øelectrical systems;
Modeling of power system components - transmission lines,synchronous machine,
loads, transformers, etc.; Per unit system; Line parameters and their calculations;
Transmission line performance and analysis, Power flow – formulation and solution
methods like Gauss seideland Newton-Raphson method; Economic operation of power
systems – economic dispatch of generation, unit commitment, automatic generation
control, and frequency control; Fault analysis–symmetrical faults, symmetrical
components, sequence models, unsymmetrical faults; Power system stability–swing
equation and equal area criterion of stability; Introduction to switchgears and
protection.                                                                </p>

zero-current switching; Applications of power electronics – power supplies, motor drives,
industrial applications, power system; Harmonics and mitigation.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of signals and spectra, band-limited signals, analysis of signals, distortion in
transmission; linear CW modulation, methods of generation, bandwidth efficiency, synchronous
and asynchronous detection, frequency division multiplexing; exponential modulation,
narrowband PM and FM, transmission bandwidth, generation and detection, de-emphasis and
pre-emphasis filtering; pulse modulation, sampling theorem, aliasing, PAM, PWM, PPM, time
division multiplexing; pulse code modulation, delta modulation, DPCM; review of random
processes and power spectral density, signal space; Noise analysis; Digital communications
basic, line codes and their spectra, pulse shaping, inter-symbol interference, Nyquist criterion for
distortionless transmission, equalization; Basics of digital bandpass modulation, ASK, PSK,
FSK.                                                                </p>

																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to discrete-time signals and systems; linear time invariant (LTI) systems
and properties, linear phase systems; brief review of Fourier representations; sampling
and reconstruction of continuous-time signals; the z-transform, properties and
applications to LTI systems; the discrete Fourier transform (DFT), properties, efficient
DFT computation by FFT, effects of finite word length; linear and circular convolutions,
block convolutions for long sequences; Signal analysis by DFT, spectral analysis by
periodogram and autocorrelation estimates; brief review of analog filter design; IIR
filters, stability, design by impulse invariance, bilinear transformations, frequency
transformations of low pass IIR filters; FIR filter design by windowing method and
Parks-McClellan algorithm, finite precision numerical effects; decimation and
interpolation of signals, quadrature mirror filters and perfect reconstruction, subband
decomposition; introduction to discrete wavelet transforms.                                                                </p>

engine/electric hybrid vehicle; Fuelled electric vehicles; Electric vehicles using
supply lines; Support systems; Efficiency and Pollution aspects.
<li> Basics of Vehicle dynamics: Power train components of EV system; Dynamic
axle loads; Aerodynamics; Acceleration and braking Performance; Force – Velocity
Profiles; Approach to EV modelling.

Safety issues; Future trends on battery management systems; Alternative energy
sources.

characteristics of the motor and load; Motor Cooling, Efﬁciency, Size, Mass and
Rating. Power Electronic Converters for drives. Energy recovery through
regenerative Braking; Safety and performance monitoring;
<li> Ancillary Systems: Battery charging stations; Data Acquisition, safety and
performance monitoring systems; Vehicle supervisory control and communication
systems.                                                                </p>

                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="EE">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/ee.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Analysis and Synthesis of Linear Circuits </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Analysis and Synthesis of Linear Circuits [EE 432]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <b>Module 1: Introduction to circuit analysis techniques:</b><br>
Motivation and Basics; Importance of circuit theory, practical applications in Electrical/Electronic systems, review of circuit elements, circuit models and network theorems, Laplace transforms, transfer functions, Topological description of network; Network variables; Source transformation technique; node-voltage analysis; mesh-current Analysis.<br><br>
<b>Module 2: m-port Network</b><br>
Tellegen's Theorem; Passive and Lossless Network; Z-parameter, Y-parameter, Transmission matrix and h parameters; network Interconnection; Characteristic impedance and propagation function.
<br><br>
<b>Module 3: Transient and steady state analysis</b><br>
Transient response in RL, RC and RLC circuits; network equations and solutions using Laplace transform, initial conditions. Fourier analysis for periodic signals; Fourier transform, Steady- state response - amplitude and phase spectra, resonance.<br><br>
<b>Module 4: Network Functions</b><br>
Driving point function, transfer function, concepts of poles and zeros, Bode plots; Impulse response and convolution. <br><br>
<b>Module 5: Graph theory</b><br>
Fundamental theorem of graph theory; loop analysis; two basic facts of loop analysis, loop analysis for linear time-invariant networks, properties of the loop impedance matrix, cut-set analysis; cut-set analysis for linear time-invariant networks, properties of the cut-set admittance matrix.<br><br>
<b>Module 6: Passive Network Synthesis</b><br>
Elements of realizability theory; causality and stability, Hurwitz polynomials, positive real functions, elementary synthesis procedures, properties of L-C immittance functions, synthesis of L-C driving point immittances, synthesis of R-C impedances or R-L admittances, properties of R-L Impedance and R-C admittances. Poles and zeros, Foster‘s reactance theorem, Foster and Cauer forms of LC Networks, Foster and Cauer Canonic forms. Synthesis of RLC networks<br><br>
<b>Module 7: Filters</b><br>
Maximally flat low-pass filter approximation, transient response of low pass filter, synthesis of low pass filters, magnitude and frequency normalization, Frequency Transformations.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#708090">EE 432</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="EE">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/ee.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Principles of Digital Communications </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Principles of Digital Communications [EE 433]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Optimum detection and matched filtering in AWGN, Inter-symbol interference (ISI),  Nyquist criterion, and pulse shaping, Wireless channel models: flat and frequency-selective fading, Modulation performance in AWGN and fading channels; Motivation and coding gain, Linear block codes: Hamming codes and cyclic redundancy checks (CRC), Convolutional codes: encoding, trellis representation, and Viterbi decoding (hard decision); Turbo coding principles: concatenation, interleaving, puncturing, constraint length, and free distance, Iterative and soft-decision decoding (BCJR, log-MAP), LDPC code construction, sparse parity-check matrices, and belief-propagation decoding, Coding gain–complexity trade-offs and examples from modern standards (LTE, Wi-Fi, 5G); Symbol-timing recovery: early–late gate and Gardner algorithms, Carrier-frequency offset and phase recovery (PLL concepts, differential detection), Sensitivity of synchronization to SNR, multipath, and Doppler effects; Impact of multipath and ISI on detection, Pilot-aided channel estimation: correlation, least-squares (LS), and minimum mean-square-error (MMSE) estimators, Linear equalizers: Zero-Forcing (ZF) and MMSE, Nonlinear equalization: decision-feedback equalizer (DFE); Effects of imperfect synchronization and channel estimation, Error propagation in equalizers; performance–complexity trade-offs, System-level integration of coding, synchronization, channel estimation, and equalization in a complete single-carrier link, Case studies from LTE, NR, and Wi-Fi receivers.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>

																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction: an overview of software-defined radios (SDRs) and PlutoSDR, I/Q sampling, frequency allocations, antennas, and regulatory disclaimers regarding transmissions; Analog receivers: implementations of analog receivers using SDRs, introduction to the filter design for audio signals, recording UHF-based analog wireless microphones and/or AM/FM radio; Digital receivers: the burst transmissions by the car key fobs, the energy detection-based synchronization, demodulating amplitude shift keying (ASK) signals and bit decoding; Synchronization and data decoding: Introduction to packet synchronization using correlation, revision of the pulse position modulation, building aircraft trackers using ADS-B-based broadcasts at 1090 MHz; Wireless Protocols: introduction to various packet formats such as RFID and AlectoV1 for ISM-band weather stations and perform full-stream data recovery to get live weather updates.                                                                </p>

lasers, quantum cascade lasers; Modulation of lasers; electro-optic, acousto-optic and direct current
modulation; Frequency multiplication of laser beams, introduction to nonlinear optical effects.                                                                </p>
