                                                               <h1>Solar Photovoltaics: Physics, Technologies and Applications [EE 643]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction: Photovoltaic (PV) history, PV economics, Solar energy conversion; Physics: Crystal
structures, atomic bonding, types of semiconductors, energy band diagram, p-type and n-type
semiconductors, doping and carrier concentration, diffusion and drift of carriers, continuity equation, P-N
junction and its properties, dark I-V equation of P-N junction, junction under illumination, solar cell
parameters, measurements of solar cell parameters, short circuit current, open circuit voltage, fill factor,
cell efficiency, optical losses, electrical losses, surface recombination velocity, quantum efficiency, I-V
characteristics; Technologies: Crystalline/Poly-Crystalline silicon solar cell technologies, Amorphous
silicon solar cell technology, Thin film solar cell technologies, Characterization of solar cell materials and
devices, Nanostructured solar cells; Applications: System components; Cells, modules and arrays;
Interfacing PV modules to loads; Power Conditioning and Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)
Algorithms; Inverter control topologies for stand-alone and grid-connected operation; Stand-alone PV
systems, Grid-connected (utility interactive) PV systems.                                                                </p>

geometry, estimation of fundamental matrix, image rectification, stereo correspondence, shape
from stereo; Three view geometry – trifocal tensors; Motion – optical flow field, Estimation of
dense and accurate optical flow field; Multi view geometry – structure from motion,
triangulation, factorization, bundle adjustment; Internet vision – mining community photo
collections (Flickr, Facebook, etc.).                                                                </p>

synchronous systems; Multiple clock domains and interfacing between independent clock
domains; Case studies: some recent (commercial) asynchronous IC&#39;s; Current research trends.                                                                </p>

                                                                   Familiarization with Cadence design tools; Extraction of Metal Oxide Semiconductor (MOS) model parameters; Design and Simulation of Complementary MOS based amplifiers (Single stage, Cascade, Simple Cascode and Gain-boosted Cascode); Design and Simulation of Current mirrors, Current sources and Current amplifiers; Design and Simulation of Voltage and Current references; Design and Simulation of Operational Amplifiers (single stage and multi stage); Pole-zero compensation and effect of feedback; Simulation of Operational Amplifiers with double ended output; Common Mode Feedback (CMFB) circuits; Design of fully-differential amplifier; Simulation of switched capacitor based circuits; Analog and Mixed-signal layout techniques                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#708090">EE 653</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="EE">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/ee.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Carrier Transport in Advanced Semiconductor Devices </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Carrier Transport in Advanced Semiconductor Devices [EE 654]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction: Advancements in MOSFET scaling: bulk MOSFETs, thin-body SOI MOSFETs, multi-gate FETs, nanosheet FETs, 2D material FETs; Review of classical carrier transport: drift-diffusion and continuity equations, Einstein relation, effective mass, density-of-states, Fermi-Dirac statistics, classical size effects; Limitations of drift-diffusion approach in describing modern MOSFETs: quantum confinement effects, Review of Schr<br>
odinger equation and its solutions.<br>
Band-structure modeling beyond effective-mass approximation: Review of electronic band-structures (E-k dispersion); Full band-structure from Density-Functional-Theory (DFT); Characterization of band structures using effective mass, density-of-states, and electronic bandgap; Band-structure modeling approaches: effective-mass based Hamiltonian, k.p approach, tight-binding Hamiltonian, and Wannier function approach.<br>
Semi-classical carrier transport in semiconductors: Introduction to the probability current, Fermi’s golden rule, Carrier scattering mechanisms in semiconductors, Electron-phonon interactions, The relaxation time approximation, The Boltzmann Transport Equation (BTE), Validity of the relaxation time approximation, Limitations of semi-classical transport.<br>
Carrier transport in nanostructures: Atomic-size conduction channels; Landauer formula; Conductance quantization; General Landauer–Büttiker approach: transmission function, scattering matrix, recursive Green’s function; Open and closed system description using Non-Equilibrium Green’s Function (NEGF): channel Hamiltonian, electrode self-energies, retarded, advanced, lesser and greater Green’s functions, spectral function, charge density matrix; Transmission function and current.<br>
Applications of quantum transport in advanced nanoelectronics devices: High mobility and short-channel MOSFETs; Tunneling field-effect transistors (TFETs); Transport in low-dimensional materials, e.g., two-dimensional materials; Quantum computing devices; Molecular devices.<br>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Heterostructure Devices </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Heterostructure Devices [EE 655]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Module 1: Physics of Heterojunctions<br>
  Submodules: Overview of Metal-Semiconductor (MS) junctions: Ideal MS contacts, Schottky diode, the origin of interface/surface states, practical considerations; Overview of Heterojunctions: Materials consideration, semiconductor alloys, lattice-matched systems, energy band-diagrams,  types of heterojunctions (type-I, type-II, type-III); Effects of quantum confinement; Hot electrons and Ballistic electrons in heterostructures; Formation of two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG); Quantum wells, Superlattices.</p>
<p>Module 2: Heterojunction Bipolar Transistors (HBTs)<br>
  Submodules: Review of homojunction bipolar junction transistors (BJTs): overview of BJTs, IV characteristics, advantages and disadvantages; Advantages of heterojunction over BJT device performance, High-frequency performance; Abrupt junction HBTs; Graded junction HBTs; Graded base HBTs; Double heterojunction HBTs, GaAs/InP/Si HBTs, Physical characterization of HBTs, Thermal effects, Performance metrics: transition frequency, maximum frequency, Electrical characterization of HBTs.</p>
<p>Module 3: High electron mobility transistors (HEMTs)<br>
  Submodules: Modulation-doped field-effect transistor (MODFET): device structure, device physics, electrical characteristics, the use of III-V materials; Charge control of 2DEG; Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) based HEMTs; Indium Phosphide (InP) based HEMTs; Gallium nitride (GaN) based HEMTs: polarization in GaN material, AlGaN/GaN HEMTs, Polarization Effects in AlGaN/GaN HEMTs, current collapse, fabrication, and challenges.<br>
  <br>
  Module 4: Optical sources and detectors<br>
  Submodules: Basics of sources/detectors; Device structure and materials involved; Figure of merits; Quantum dots based infrared photodetector (QDIP): III-V material based QDIPs, Effect of variabilities on performance parameters; QD based Lasers: working principle, basic characteristics of lasers, different types of lasers; Market demand; Technology trends.</p>
<p>Module 5: Van der Waals (vdW) heterostructures<br>
  Submodules: Two-dimensional materials and vdW heterostructures: Properties, growth methods, physical characterization, advantages, and applications; Graphene-based vdW heterostructures: Atomic and electronic structure, device applications such as vertical tunnelling based devices; Advantages of transition metal dichalcogenides (TMD) vdW heterostructures based on MoS2, WS2, MoSe2, WSe2, etc., Electrical and optoelectronic properties, TMD heterostructures with twist angle.<br>
</p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Memory Device Technologies and Applications [EE 658]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p><strong>Module 1: Introduction and Overview</strong><br>
  Review of a computer system; Memory hierarchy; Semiconductor memory overview; Memory<br>
  comparison, tradeoffs, and challenges; volatile &amp; non-volatile memories</p>
<p>  <strong>Module 2: Conventional Volatile and Non-volatile Memories</strong><br>
  Volatile memories - SRAM: Review of Device basic structure; memory unit cell operation; Array<br>
  Operation; Reliability issues and Current Advances;Volatile memories - DRAM: Review of Device</p>
<p>basic structure; memory unit cell operation; Array Operation; Reliability; Current Advances;Non-<br>
  volatile memory - FLASH: Floating Gate (FG) &amp; Charge Trap (CT) flash; Flash memory physics;</p>
<p>NAND vs NOR flash; Multi-bit capability (MLC); Reliability and scaling issues; Current Advances:<br>
  Nanocrystal; 3D NAND;</p>
<p><strong>  Module 3: Emerging Non-volatile Memories (eNVM)</strong></p>
<p>Resistive RAM (RRAM): Unipolar and Bipolar; Filamentary &amp; non-filamentary; Ion transport; multi-<br>
  bit operation; reliability; Selector devices for memories;Phase change memory (PCM); Conductive</p>
<p>bridge RAM (CBRAM); Ferroelectric RAM (FeRAM); Spin-transfer Torque Magnetic RAM (STT-<br>
  MRAM) - Device physics and operation; eNVM performance comparison; eNVM challenges and</p>
<p>opportunities to replace existing memory; eNVM modeling</p>
<p><strong>  Module 4: Memory Architecture and applications</strong><br>
  Conventional Memory architecture; Cross-point array architecture; Peripheral/control circuit;<br>
  Sense amplifier; Memories for Neuromorphic computing; In-memory computing applications;<br>
  Security applications; System-level implementation and challenges</p>                                                                </p>

																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">

Smart grid architecture and building blocks of the grid transformation: electric infrastructure, industry structure, control structure, digital infrastructure and regulatory structure; network instrumentation - IT/OT solutions, IEDs, sensors, RTU, PMU, AMI, etc, grid analytics and data-driven grid, advanced asset management
Smart grid operation and management: optimal power flow and security constrained economic dispatch (SCED), transmission line power transfer capability calculations, grid congestion management, renewable energy integration and grid balancing; electricity market optimization - electricity trading (day-ahead and real-time), green-term ahead market, renewable energy certificate (REC) market; AI/ML assisted forecasting tools and data analytics to handle grid uncertainties and improved grid operation 
Smart grid flexibility and transactive energy market: ancillary services - frequency regulation, contingency reserve, reactive power and voltage control, flexibility reserves; energy storage optimization, distributed energy resources optimization and management; demand side energy management – peer-to-peer energy trading and demand response (DR) programs                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Fundamentals of Nanoelectronic Devices </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Fundamentals of Nanoelectronic Devices [EE 661]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Introduction: Advancements in MOSFET scaling: bulk MOSFETs, thin-body SOI MOSFETs, multi-gate FETs, nanosheet FETs, 2D material FETs; Review of classical carrier transport: drift-diffusion and continuity equations, Einstein relation, effective mass, density-of-states, Fermi-Dirac statistics, classical size effects; Limitations of drift-diffusion approach in describing modern MOSFETs.</p>
<p>  Review of quantum mechanics: Wave-particle duality, Wavefunction, Real and k-space representation, Electron as a wavepacket, Schro &#776;dinger&rsquo;s wave equation, Time-independent Schro &#776;dinger equation, Solution for a free particle, reiterating concept of effective mass, Solution for bound electrons in 1D, introduction to quantum confinement and tunneling, Solutions for different electron barrier shapes commonly found in MOSFETs.</p>
<p>Electron density in nanoscale MOSFETs: Dispersion (E-k) relation and charge density in k-space: 1D (quantum wire), 2D (quantum well) and 3D (bulk MOSFET); Sub-bands in nanosheet FETs, Mobile charge below and above threshold voltage with Quantum Mechanical (QM) effects, Surface and volume inversion, 2D-MOSFET electrostatics and charge modeling.</p>
<p>Electron transport in nanoscale MOSFETs: Current increase with channel length scaling: Limit of conductance, Diffusive to Ballistic conduction, Landauer formula, Ballistic limit for nanoscale transistor; Quasi-Ballistic Transport: Ballistic to diffusive transition, Mean Free Path, Carrier scattering mechanisms in semiconductors, Mobility modelling; Current-voltage characteristics of nanoscale MOSFETs.</p>
<p>Connecting nanoscale Device Physics to Circuit Design: Overview of a PDK: Core Device Model, Example of modeling QM effects in BSIM-CMG, Layout dependent parasitic effects; Impact of QM effects on basic analog and digital circuit figure-of-merits.</p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

                                                               <h1>Mixed Signal IC Design [EE 662]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Motivation for mixed signal design, Review of signal Analysis techniques -- Fourier, Laplace and Z transforms.<br>
Quantization: Quantization noise, Signal to quantization noise ratio (SQNR)<br>
Sampling: Nyquist criterion, Sampling by an impulse train, Sample and hold, Follow and hold.<br>
Data Converters -- (Nyquist rate):<br>
DACs: Resistor chain, R-2R, Current mode, Charge redistribution DACs. Concatenating DACs of different types.<br>
ADCs: Single, double and multiple slope ADCs, Successive Approximation, flash, semi-flash and Pipeline ADCs, Self calibration<br>
and Digital correction in ADCs.<br>
Data Converters &ndash; (Oversampled):<br>
ADCs: Resolution gain with oversampling, Noise shaping, first, second and higher order Sigma Delta modulation, MASH<br>
architecture, Digital filters and decimation for Sigma Delta ADCs. Sinc filters, Half Band FIR filters.<br>
DACs: Sigma Delta DACs, Up-sampling by zero insertion and filtering.<br>
Mixed Signal circuits for Digital Communication: Phase Locked Loops (PLL), Frequency synthesis and Clock recovery circuits.                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Advanced Wireless Communications [EE 663]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Introduction &ndash; motivation, types of wireless networks, applications and design challenges in modern wireless systems;</p>
<p>  Wireless propagation &ndash; Free-space path loss, empirical pathloss models, shadowing, multipath fading, Doppler shift, coherence time and 
  bandwidth, WSSUS model, stochastic fading models;</p>
<p>  MIMO wireless channel modeling &ndash; i.i.d. and Kronecker-based spatial channel models, spatial correlation, geometry-based models, 
  narrowband and block-fading assumptions;</p>
<p> Multi-antenna techniques &ndash; Diversity (Alamouti, STBC generalizations), Spatial Multiplexing, linear precoding (SVD, ZF, MMSE), ergodic
and outage capacity, AoA estimation (MUSIC, ESPRIT), beamforming for LoS and mmWave settings;</p>
<p>  Basics of OFDM &ndash; Transmitter and receiver block diagrams, cyclic prefix, subcarrier orthogonality, FFT/IFFT operations.</p>
<p>  MIMO-OFDM TX/RX signal processing &ndash; Resource mapping, pilot structure, synchronization signals, channel estimation (Schmidl-Cox, 
LS/MMSE), frequency-domain equalization (ZF/MMSE), iterative equalization, precoding, demodulation;</p>
<p>  OFDM impairments and Mitigation Techniques &ndash; Peak-to-Average Power Ratio (PAPR), Inter-Carrier Interference (ICI), Carrier 
Frequency Offset (CFO), timing mismatch, spectral leakage.</p>
<p>  Applications and Emerging Topics &ndash; MIMO-OFDM link-level simulation, Massive MIMO, mmWave communication, Integrated Sensing 
and Communications (ISAC).</p>                                                                </p>

Demand flexibility and relevant regulation and obligation;  DSM targets and its assessment, DSM cell in distribution companies; Renewable consumption obligation (RCO), Energy efficient technologies, Bureau of energy efficiency (BEE) and its functionalities; Energy efficiency in distribution system and potential use cases, relevant Indian electricity grid code and regulatory framework; Digital Technologies in Distribution System: AI/ML applications in distribution systems – forecasting, grid optimization and control, state estimation and anomaly detection, data-driven predictive asset management, Emerging technologies - digital twins, blockchain platform, P2P energy trading, and edge intelligence for smart distribution grid operation and management.                                                                </p>

                                                                   Advanced MOSFET Scaling and Limits: Basic MOSFET operation, I-V characteristics; Breakdown, transport mechanism; Moore's Law and scaling principles; Short Channel Effects (SCEs); Moore Devices: Non-Planar MOSFETs: FinFETs (Fin Field-Effect Transistors) and their advantages; Gate-All-Around FETs (GAAFETs/Nanosheet FETs; Tunnel FETs, Negative Capacitance FETs; More-than-Moore Devices: Heterostructure devices for RF and High-Power Applications                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#708090">EE 669</span>
												
						</div>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                               <h1>Wireless System Design and Implementation [EE 671]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction: an overview of software-defined radios (SDRs) and PlutoSDR, I/Q sampling, frequency allocations, antennas, and regulatory disclaimers regarding transmissions; Analog receivers: implementations of analog receivers using SDRs, introduction to the filter design for audio signals, recording UHF-based analog wireless microphones and/or AM/FM radio; Digital receivers: the burst transmissions by the car key fobs, the energy detection-based synchronization, demodulating amplitude shift keying (ASK) signals and bit decoding; Synchronization and data decoding: Introduction to packet synchronization using correlation, revision of the pulse position modulation, building aircraft trackers using ADS-B-based broadcasts at 1090 MHz; Wireless Protocols: introduction to various packet formats such as RFID and AlectoV1 for ISM-band weather stations and perform full-stream data recovery to get live weather updates.                                                                </p>

                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="EH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Field Practices in Earth System Sciences </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>0 | 0 | 0 | 2							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Field Practices in Earth System Sciences [EH 301]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>0 | 0 | 0 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <b>Goals/ tasks</b><br><br>

1.	Field geology (rock, feature, structure identification on different spatial and temporal scales): Training for developing skills as an independent field geologist. Focus is to learn what to look for in the field and how to identify and scientifically explain the feature you observed.<br>
2.	Learning a few easy mobile devices (compass, brunton, clinometer) and topo sheet etc.<br>
3.	Mapping and /or sample collection techniques: Rock/ sand/soil/water sample collection for various analytical purposes. Collection of oriented rock samples and learning how to procure samples for various geochronological measurements.<br>
4.	Reading/analyzing features and documentation.<br>
5.	Report writing: A detailed field report has to be submitted within 10 days of completion of the fieldwork and students will be evaluated on the basis of the report and performance in the field.<br><br>

<b>Skills that can be achieved</b><br><br>

1.	Identifying geological and geomorphic features, sample collection and process interpretation.<br>
2.	Bridging field data with model simulations. <br>
3.	Marine Structures, Life and Pollution.<br>
4.	Features of the biodiversity/national forest: Components, Conservation and Survey 
<br><br>
<b>Prospect areas</b><br><br>

1.	Mountain belt/ hills: Understanding the geology of the hills and rock formations, finding structural features showing active tectonics and surface features, erosion etc. These areas need extensive geological mapping. It will be a mapping intensive course. (Kutch-Bhuj area, Aravalli range near Gujarat-Rajasthan border, Deccan traps in northern Maharastra, Northwestern Himalaya etc.)<br>
2.	Beach / coastal areas: Study of sedimentary features and the role of sea. Understanding the vegetation. Study of nearby hills and their geology. Their role in sediment transport and above all, how sea level change occurs and how does it affect the landscape and surface processes (e.g.: Cambay area, Karnataka coast, south-Maharastra coast etc.)<br>
3.	National parks and Sanctuaries: Geology, vegetation and fauna of the national park areas. Understanding the behaviour of the fauna. Mapping will not be possible in the national parks, but in nearby areas. (e.g. Gir National park, Ranthambore national park, Bandhavgarh National Park, Pench Tiger reserve etc.).<br>
4.	River systems and water bodies: Understanding the river systems and their evolution. Role of tides in river confluences near sea and related deposits. How vegetation and fauna depend on the dynamic nature of the delta. (e.g.: Narmada delta).<br>
5.	Mining Areas: Mine visits are essential for understanding how geology and engineering gets coupled. The focus is to learn the geology and genesis of the ore mineral, understanding the mining techniques, safety protocols and reserve estimation. (e.g.: Coal mines in central India, manganese mines in Maharashtra, lignite mines in Gujarat).<br>
6.	Landslides and landfill: Understanding the processes related to landslides, different type of landslides, methodology for slope stability analysis.<br><br>

<b>For each year one/two prospect areas will be selected by the instructors.</b>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Elements of Earth System Science<br><br> [EH 302]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Dynamical Earth’s System: Defining the Earth system an its properties; Earth system
components: Lithosphere, atmosphere, hydrosphere, biosphere and sociosphere;
Processes and characteristics of different earth components, Interaction between Earth’s
components: mass and energy exchanges,
Biogeochemical cycles: Water, Oxygen, carbon and nutrients cycles, Regulation of
major biogeochemical elements.
Coevolution of life and Earth’s planet: Geological time scale, Origin of Earth, Origin of
life, Origin of cycles, Changes in Earth’s system components through time.
Anthropocene: Coupled human-earth system, Anthropogenic mass transport and energy
generation, Changes in mass energy balances and question of sustainability.
Models of earth system: Growth, decay, oscillation, spreading, and reactions; Carbon,
water and nutrient cycle modes, Diffusion of mass and energy models.
Prediction and uncertainties: Sensitivities, Uncertainties in earth system, risk,
management, sustainability, Environmental management in high risk and high uncertain
systems                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Origin of Earth<br>
  Overview of planet formation with emphasis on Earth, meteorites, layers of the earth, Earth&rsquo;s magnetic field.</p>
<p>Origin and Evolution of Life<br>
  Fossils, Formation and evolution of life, Geological time scale.</p>
<p>Minerals and Rocks<br>
  Major Earth forming minerals, Introduction to Petrology: Igneous, metamorphic and sedimentary, fossils.</p>
<p>Energy and Natural Resources<br>
  Mechanical, thermal, chemical, mineral deposits, oil and natural gas resources, coal reserves.</p>
<p>Material and energy cycles: Rocks/ plates/heat/hydrological/carbon.<br>
  Plate tectonics, mantle convection and geochemical signatures, water and carbon cycle.</p>
<p>Natural Hazards and Sustainability<br>
  Types and causes of Natural Hazards, risk assessment, climate change and sea-level rise, mitigation and policy making.</p>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Archaeology: Theory and Methods </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Archaeology: Theory and Methods [EH 401]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Overview and Introduction: Why study or practice Archaeology? Defining the discipline: its nature, scope, and key concepts, its place among other disciplines (Anthropology, History, Prehistory), and in the contemporary world; Theoretical development: Antiquarianism to culture-Historical approach, paradigms of Processual and Post-Processual Archaeology, controversies, current and future trends, and Archaeology in India; Archaeological methods through case studies: Pre-excavation strategy, excavation and post-excavation techniques and analyses, data and contexts, contribution of sciences (chemistry, physics, geophysics, earth sciences, biological sciences and palaeoclimatology) in Archaeology, contribution of technology (mapping, remote sensing, viewing/imaging and computer science) in Archaeology; Cultures and Chronologies: mapping human origins and evolution to cultures, cultures (Palaeolithic, Mesolithic, Neolithic, Chalcolithic, Iron age, Early Historical, Medieval and Modern periods) vs material culture (artifacts and ecofacts), simple to complex societies to civilizations, explaining cultural change/adaptation through case studies, cultural heritage (tangible and intangible), cultural resource management, laws and legislations; Branches/Types of Archaeology: Social archaeology, Environmental archaeology, Public archaeology, Underwater archaeology etc.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Human Evolution </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Human Evolution [EH 402]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction: subjects, theories, methods and concepts employed in the study of human evolution, history of evolutionary thought, concepts of adaptation, natural selection, speciation, mutation, heredity, variation, micro and macro evolution, Linnaean system of classification, geological time scale, prehistoric timeline, context and material culture in Archaeology, dating techniques, palaeoenvironmental settings, process of fossilization and taphonomy; Assessment of the human fossil record during the Miocene- Pliocene- Early Pleistocene epochs (between 23 and 5 million years ago to 1.2 million years ago), primate evolution, Hominoidea superfamily; divergences, the earliest hominins, Genus Australopithecus, Kenyanthropus and Paranthropus, key fossil finds (origins, description and comparative assessment of their biological, behavioural and adaptive characteristics); Assessment of the human fossil record during the Pleistocene (2.5 million years ago to about 20,000 years before present) epoch, earliest Homo and other Homo species before the emergence of Modern Homo sapiens, recent fossil discoveries from Africa and Eurasia (origins, description and comparative assessment of their biological, behavioural and adaptive characteristics), dispersal theories and the extant human diversity, using DNA to trace the most recent common ancestor; One-day field visit to nearby prehistoric sites and museums; Laboratory: human osteology and morphometrics                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->

												<span class="label" style="background:#5D5B5B ">EH 402</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="EH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/ee.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Earth Surface Processes in the Anthropocene </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 1.5 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">

                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Earth Surface Processes in the Anthropocene [EH 601]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 1.5 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction: Introduction to earth surface processes and historical development in concepts, Source of
energy, Mass conservation and geomorphic transport laws, Process interaction in shaping the earth surface,
Nonlinear and complex behavior of earth systems. Specific Earth Surface Processes: Weathering and
formation of soils, karst, slope and catchment erosion processes, fluvial, eolian, glacial, periglacial and
coastal processes, and resultant landforms, Water and sediment flux in river systems. An introduction to the
earth surface of India.
Process Dynamics and controls: Rates and changes in surface processes; Techniques for process
measurement, Geochronology of surface and its sediments. Earth system response to external controls i.e.
tectonics, sea level/base level change. Geomorphic connectivity, time lag and complexity in signal
propagation, Landscape evolution models, Evolutionary trajectory of the landscapes in response to external
forcing. Predictability of Earth’s surface processes.
Anthropocene: Anthropocene in the context of formal geological classification, Signature for the
Anthropocene, Human as an external forcing on Earth’s surface processes, Natural hazards and resource
management in Anthropocene; Nonlinear behavior of earth systems and challenges in natural resource
managements,                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>River Morphology and Ecology [EH 602]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to River Science, Introduction to geomorphic concepts: threshold, sensitivity, connectivity, hierarchy
and complexity. River basin approach: Sediment source and catchment erosion processes, Transition between
hillslope and fluvial processes, Longitudinal river profiles, Sediment load and sediment yield, Sediment and
nutrient transport process in rivers, Erosion and sedimentation processes in channel, Geochemical proxies to study
sediment dynamics in a river basin. Drainage Network: Quantitative analysis, Role of drainage network in flux
transfer, 3-dimensional connectivity in a river basin, Hydrological response of a river basin, Processes in confluence
zones, Evolution of drainage network. River processes and morphology: River fluxes, energy distribution and
patterns of alluvial rivers - braided, meandering and anabranching channels; Hydrological, sedimentological and
ecological characteristics and their interrelationship in different channel patterns; Dynamics of alluvial rivers;
Different classification approaches in fluvial geomorphology and its applications. Glacio-fluvial interaction:
Sources of water in river system, Hydrological budgeting in the glaciated mountainous region, Spatial variability of
glacial melt component in the Himalaya. Bedrock channels: Stream Power law and Bedrock incision process; River
response to climate, tectonics and human disturbance; Quantitative analysis of bedrock channel processes and
evolution of fluvial landscapes. Stream Management: Fluvial hazards and their causes, Humans and rivers,
Ecosystem based approach to stream management, Concept of river health, Environmental Flow (e-flow) –
definition, data requirement, different approaches for e-flow estimation.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Open system framework and introduction to Critical Zone, Components of Critical Zones: Introduction to
climate, landforms, soil, water and biota components; Multiscale Geophysical imaging of the Critical Zone:
Magnetic, Electrical, Electromagnetic, GPR, NMR and Seismic methods for subsurface imaging.
Geochemistry of the Critical Zone: Chemical analysis of soil, surface water and groundwater. Process
models in Critical Zone: Radiative energy and mass balance on the Earth’s surface, Thermodynamics of
solutions and mixtures, Rate of entropy production, Geochemical kinetics: rate models, Chemical reactions
in soil-water interaction, Conservation laws and constitutive relationships for porous media, Richard’s and
Boussinesq equations for unsaturated and saturated media. Heat conduction and advection in sub-surface,
Mass and energy transport in river and landform evolution; Ecosystem processes: Growth and decay and
Advection-diffusion-reaction models; Process - structure integration in the Critical zone: Geological and
climatic drivers of critical zone processes, Modifications by anthropogenic forcing.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Engineering Seismology and Seismic Hazard Analysis </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Engineering Seismology and Seismic Hazard Analysis [EH 610]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction of earthquake hazard, historical perspective for effects of earthquakes,
tsunamis, liquefaction and landslides, major earthquakes in India and in the world, overview
of seismic zonation and codes, national and global seismic hazard mapping programs.
Overview of plate tectonics, types of faults, earthquake source, source models.
Quantification of earthquake size: earthquake energy, magnitude scales, magnitude
relations, intensity scale. Wave propagation and attenuation: body waves, surface waves,
geometrical spreading and inelastic attenuation, scattering.
Earthquake recordings and ground motion parameters: displacement, velocity and
acceleration motions, response spectra, spectral acceleration, Fourier spectra. Earthquake
location and magnitude determination using seismic records.
Brief overview of probability theory: Basic principles, subjective probability, conditional
probability theorem, random variables and distributions.
Seismic hazard analysis methods: deterministic and probabilistic methods, types of sources
and source zonation, earthquake catalogues and seismicity analysis, ground motion models,
ground motion simulations, hazard integral and hazard computation, types of uncertainties
and their treatment in typical seismic hazard studies, UHS (Uniform Hazard Spectrum).
Examples of Seismic hazard studies, e.g., GSHAP (Global Seismic Hazard Assessment
Program), and other international programs.
Overview of site-specific seismic hazard analysis and micro-zonation, local site-effects, site
response analysis methods and site-classifications. State of the practice of seismic hazard
analysis post Fukushima incident and examples of some site-specific seismic hazard studies
for NPPs (Nuclear Power Plants) around the world.
Overview of seismic risk and vulnerability, difference between hazard and risk.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>

													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/ee.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Near Surface Geophysics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Near Surface Geophysics [EH 611]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <li> Physical fields of the Earth: Gravitational field, Geomagnetic field;
electromagnetic field, seismic wave-field, thermal field.
<li> Properties of the Earth and processes: Mechanical properties, density,
elasticity, seismic waves; Electrical properties (conductivity and resistivity) of
the rocks; Magnetic properties of rocks, soils, Remanent magnetization; Gravity
anomalies, Earth’s heat, temperature inside the Earth; Heat transport in the
Earth.
<li> Measurements of physical fields and rock properties: Gravimeter and
gravity measurements, Satellite based gravity measurements, working principle
of magnetometer, seismometer and GPR (Ground Penetrating Radar).
<li> Methods of geophysical interpretation: Direct and inverse methods, gravity,
magnetic electrical surveying and electromagnetic surveying; Seismic reflection
and refraction data processing and interpretation; GPR data processing and
interpretation.
<li> Applications: Applications of geophysical methods in the field of groundwater,
geotechnical, civil infrastructure, mining, hydrocarbon exploration using existing
data.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Ocean and Global Change </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Ocean and Global Change [EH 612]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Module 1: Physio-Chemical Oceanography, Ocean Circulation and Dynamics<br>
Oceans, their divisions, distribution, characteristics (temperature, salinity, and density), and masses. Ocean observation concepts and techniques; Chemical properties and biology in the ocean; Photosynthetic Primary production and light; Primay production and Nutrients; Geographic variations in primary production; Ocean Circulation and Dynamics I: Ocean Circulation (Wind-driven currents; Open ocean surface currents; Equatorial surface currents; High Latitude surface currents; Upwelling and Downwelling; Coastal currents;  Eddies; Thermohaline Circulation; The conveyor Belt); Ocean Circulation and Dynamics II:  Ocean-atmosphere Interactions (water and heat budget; Interannual climate variations El Nino and other Oscillations; climate and ocean surface water properties; Ocean Circulation and Dynamics III: Waves and Tides; Summarizing Module 1 with the global fluxes and the deep circulation<br>
Module 2: Introduction to Palaeoceanography<br>
Introduction to Ocean and its evolution through time and its influence on Climate; K/T boundary (Chixulub + Deccan Basalts) and Early Paleogene (carbon addition and global warming); Antarctica Glaciation and influence on Ocean and Global Change (Eocene-Oligocene Boundary); Messinian Salinity Crises (Miocene); The last glacial maxima and deglaciation, and Younger Dryas.; OAE’s (Ocean Anoxic Events)<br>
Module 3: Anthropogenic effects on Ocean and Global Change <br>
Sea-level fluctuations and Coastlines, Coral reefs and Ocean Acidification; Plastic Pollution; Future Predictions on Ocean and Global Change<br>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

																	Credit

																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 1.5 | 4<br>

                                                                   <b>Carbonate Sedimentology Overview:</b> Introduction to carbonates, historical overview, Carbonate chemistry and mineralogy, components, texture, fabric – rock classification. The carbonate factory: microbes and algae, single cells, shells, echinoderms and colonial invertebrates.<br><br>
<b>Carbonate Depositional Systems:</b> Warm water neritic realms; Muddy Peritidal carbonates; Carbonate Lagoons; Sand Bodies; Evaporites, lacustrine, springs; Modern and Ancient Reefs; Carbonate Slopes; Deepwater Pelagic Carbonates; Coolwater neritic realms; Basics elements of carbonate stratigraphy; Sequence stratigraphy overview; Carbonate Ramps vs Platforms .<br><br>
<b>Porosity, Permeability, and Diagenesis:</b> Carbonate diagenesis overview & chemistry; Diagenetic environments, Paragenetic sequence, Burial graphs; Porosity types and classification.                                                                </p>

<br><br>
<b>Energetic of Lithosphere:</b> Heat transport and production in Earth’s Interior; Cooling of the oceanic lithosphere; Temperature distribution in subduction zones; Continental geotherms; Plate tectonics through ages and The supercontinent cycle and evolution of life
<br><br>
<b>Dynamics of Lithosphere:</b> Driving Mechanism of Plate Tectonics: Slab Pull and Ridge Push; Rift and Subduction initiation; Accretionary prisms formation; Isostasy and Flexure; Sedimentary, metamorphic, and ore generation
<br><br>
<b>The Indian Lithosphere:</b> Indian plate motion through ages; Geodiversity of Indian plate; The Indian continental geotherm; Distribution of earthquakes                                                                </p>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                                               <h1>Statistical Seismology [EH 615]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p> Earthquake Physics<br>
  Stick slip with blockslider model and its stability, Single slip dynamics: various friction models, Friction laws, Regularized rate and state-dependent friction and its invariant formulation</p>
<p>Earthquake phenomenology<br>
  Earthquakes and different magnitude measurement scales, Earthquake catalogs, Handling modern public-access data for earthquake catalogs, Statistical distributions of earthquake numbers, Earthquake size distribution, Methods of Magnitude of completness Computation, a and b value estimation using maximum likelihood method, Temporal earthquake distribution, Earthquake location distribution, Focal mechanism orientation and source complexity</p>
<p> Earthquake Forecasting<br>
ETAS Model, Phenomenological branching models and earthquake occurrence estimation, Long-term rate density estimates, Short-term forecasts, Using Expectation Maximization approach for ETAS parameter estimation; Machine learning approaches of Earthquake forecasting, Forecast results and their discussion, Earthquake rate estimation, Collaboratory for the Study of Earthquake Predictability (CSEP): Global and regional forecast testing; Industrial Earthquake forecasting RichterX and Quantectum</p>
<p> The Indian Earthquakes:<br>
Distribution of Earthquake in and around the Indian plate, Objective estimation of magnitude of completeness, Declustering long term models of earthquakes for Indian earthquakes, Making a long-term probabilistic model for Indian earthquakes</p>                                                                </p>

							<hr>

                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Materials of Earth’s Deep Interior [EH 616]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <strong>Introduction to the Earth&rsquo;s interior:</strong><br>
Origin of the Solar System: Types of planets, Proto-Sun, planetesimals.<br>
&bull; Chemical and isotopic composition of meteorites: Types of Meteorites, difference and similarities with Earth composition.<br>
&bull; Planetary models<br>
&bull; Hadean Earth: Magma Ocean, Core formation, Atmosphere formation.<br>
&bull; Internal structure of the Earth and its significance: Overview to Layers of the Earth.<br>
&bull; Major Seismic discontinuities: Gutenberg, Moho etc.<br>
<strong>Crystal Structure:</strong><br>
&bull; Crystal Systems and Space Groups: Introduction to Space groups and fundamental crystallography.<br>
&bull; Symmetry: Crystal symmetry with applications to deep earth.<br>
&bull; Major minerals in the earth&rsquo;s interior: Olivines, Pyroxenes, Spinel, Perovskite etc.<br>
&bull; Phase transitions.<br>
&bull; Phase diagrams.<br>
&bull; Experimental techniques in mineral physics: X-ray diffraction and Spectroscopy.<br>
&bull; Introduction to synchrotron sources<br>
&bull; Theoretical techniques and their applications: Density functional theory and its applications to Earth sciences.<br>
&bull; Equations of State: Birch Murnaghan, Vinet, Mie-Gr&uuml;neisen.<br>
&bull; Thermodynamics and its applications in geophysics: Laws of thermodynamics, Gr&uuml;neisen parameter and its importance.<br>
<strong>Mantle:</strong><br>
&bull; Composition of the upper and lower mantle: Composition of the Early Mantle and its evolution.<br>
&bull; Heat Flow: Fundamentals of heat-transfer.<br>
&bull; Geochemical signatures of the mantle.<br>
&bull; Seismological signatures of the mantle: Heterogeneities, Anisotropy of Mantle.<br>
&bull; Elastic constants, Hooke&rsquo;s Law: Fundamentals of Elasticity; Elastic tensor.<br>
&bull; Elastic moduli and their applications: Calculation and experimental techniques for determination of elastic constants.<br>
&bull; Elastic anisotropy<br>
&bull; Pressure and temperature profile of the mantle: Geothermal gradient, Adiabats, Isentropes.<br>
&bull; Constraints and uncertainties on the mantle temperatures.<br>
&bull; Rheology: Determination of viscosity; rheological Models.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>0 | 0 | 3 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Quantitative Topographic Analysis: Principles of topographic mapping, methods of topographic
profile construction, interpretation of natural factors controlling topography, topographic control
over earth surface processes (including natural hazards); Natural Processes of geomaterials:
Igneous, sedimentary and metamorphic processes and their products, characterization of Earth
forming materials including economic resources, primary and secondary features in rocks;
Geometrical analysis of structural features: Measurements of orientation of various 1D and 2D
structural elements (e.g. tectonic fabrics), techniques of high-resolution structural mapping, large
scale structural mapping of geological terrains and construction of geological sections,
applications of stereographic projections in field analysis; Identification and mechanics of tectonic
structures: Characterization of tectonic features: Fabrics, folds and fractures, interpretation of
structural mechanics from geometrical data, reconstruction of deformation history from structural
records; Post-processing of field data and geological reconstruction: Preparation and
interpretation of geological maps, graphical presentation of geological data, reconstruction of
geological evolution of the study area; Field report preparation.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Geobiology </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">

                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content

                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Geobiology [EH 619]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   What is Geobiology?  Basic introduction to the course. What is Life? Life as a geologic agent.  Origin of Life, it’s Theories and Evolving concepts, Building blocks of life, Amino Acids, Biomolecules (Miller-Urey Experiment), Major transitions in origin of life. 
Biogeochemical cycles: Introduction to basic concepts in biogeochemistry: Isotopic nomenclature, isotopic calculations including mass balance, processes that cause isotopic fractionation in organic matter, pyrites and carbonates including C, H, N, O and S. Carbon, Hydrogen, Oxygen, Sulfur, Nitrogen Cycles. 
Origin of Life and Precambrian Era: Concept of chemical equilibrium, alkalinity, weathering of carbonate rocks, Dissolved Inorganic Carbon, Redox reactions, photosynthesis, Biogeochemical redox reactions, Precambrian Banded Iron Formation, Stromatolites and microbial processes, sources and sinks for Carbon, Precambrian life. Neoproterozoic glaciations (Snowball earth).
Biomineralization: Concept of biomineralization, Mineral-organic-microbe interface, Geobiological agents, Geobiology of Archaen, Proterozoic, Phanerozoic, and Anthropocene Era
Hands on Data Analysis: Trace element data analysis, interpretation in Foraminifera (Biomineralization Concepts).                                                                </p>

                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Sequence Stratigraphy </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Sequence Stratigraphy [EH 620]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   History of sequence stratigraphy, basic terminology, definitions and sequence stratigraphic method applied to cores and well logs. Vertical stacking, surfaces and systems tracts, the accommodation succession method, and the sequence stratigraphy hierarchy. Fundamentals of seismic stratigraphy, seismic response of stratigraphic surfaces, seismic reflection termination mapping and seismic facies analyses. Sequence stratigraphic method applied to non-marine, shallow-marine, and deep-marine depositional settings utilizing well logs, cores, outcrops, and seismic. Wheeler diagram, hydrocarbon play element recognition and mapping, reservoir architecture, interpretation and mapping techniques from exploration to production business scales.
Practicals: 1) Lithostratigraphy vs chronostratigraphy, 2) Beds, bedsets, and parasequences, 3) Incised Valleys in outcrop and cores, 4) Well-logs to cores, 5) Idealized depositional sequence, 6) Tank experiment, 7) Seismic forward models and idealized depositional sequence, 8) Terminations interpretation in seismic, 9) Sequence, sequence sets, and composite sequence, 10) Well-log seismic tie, 11) Chronostratigraphy cycle charts, 12) Wheeler diagram                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#5D5B5B ">EH 620</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="EH">

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Climate of the Past </h4>
