																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Climate and Climatic Variation, Introduction to Paleoclimate Reconstruction, Base Maps for Paleoclimatology, Refining Paleolatitudes, Proxies: Elemental and Isotopic Proxies (&delta;18O for changes in salinity, &delta;13C for carbon cycle, Mg/Ca for paleotemperature, Boron isotopes for paleo-CO2 reconstruction respectively), Biological proxy datasets would be used.<br>
  Proterozoic and Phanerozoic Case Studies:  Neo-Proterozoic (Snowball Earth), Phanerozoic (Permian-Triassic, Paleocene Eocene Thermal Maximum, Eocene Oligocene Transition, Pliocene-Pleistocene Transition, Holocene Climatic Optimum etc). </p>
<p>  Hands on exercises/ class assignments: Formulating a hypothesis, Data collection (from case studies), analysis of data (microfossils), simple models for data using proxy records (paleo-CO2 reconstruction), general inferences about climate change in the geologic past and implications for future.</p>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Fluid Flow and Reactions in Reservoir Rocks </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Fluid Flow and Reactions in Reservoir Rocks [EH 622]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to rock properties governing fluid flow: porosity, permeability, permeability tensor, capillary pressure, relative permeability, Darcy&rsquo;s law for single- and two-phase systems, drainage and imbibition, saturation functions, pressure build-up.<br>
Groundwater flow: Vadose and phreatic zones, groundwater table, aquifer-aquitard, fluid potential, groundwater flow direction, hydrostatic equilibrium, conductivity-storativity-transmissivity, impact of heterogeneity and anisotropy, Darcy&rsquo;s law for groundwater systems, transient groundwater equation, tracer tests, diffusion and dispersion, 2 phase groundwater contamination.<br>
Groundwater geochemistry: Major and minor groundwater species, TDS-conductivity-alkalinity-redox potential, activity and fugacity, activity variation with concentration, phase solubility, delta-delta plots, Stiff and Piper plots, reactive contaminants, contaminant retardation, acidic contaminants and reaction with rocks, contamination remediation.<br>
Geological CO2 storage: CO2 density variation with depth, gas migration under different force balances, viscous-capillary-gravity equilibrium, structural-capillary-solubility-mineral trapping, lithological heterogeneity, Leverett-J scaling, snap-off and capillary heterogeneity mechanisms, impact of salinity on solubility, mineral dissolution and precipitation, impact of flow rate on reaction kinetics.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

Stratigraphy of the Petroliferous basins of India <br>
Introduction to Anthropocene: Should it be included in Geological Time Scale? An ongoing debate <br>
Hands on exercises/ class assignments: Paleogeographic Maps (Precambrian to Recent), correlating them with stratigraphic evolution and paleoclimate. <br>
Data from Landmark research articles within Indian sedimentary basins will be used for hands on exercises (covering stratigraphic important events from Precambian and Phanerozoic)<br>
Case study: Paleoclimate and Sedimentation in the Indo-Gangetic basins <br>
Case study: BioStratigraphy of Petroliferous Basins of Bombay offshore, Kutch, Kerala Konkan regions, focus on paleoclimate studies of Paleogene and Neogene in Indian subcontinent <br>
Case study: Permian Triassic (Guruyur Ravine, Spiti) K/Pg (Deccan, Meghalaya)                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">

																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Introduction: Geological time scale, Evolution of Anthropocene concept, Human as a geological agent, Climate change and CO2 dynamics from the geological time scale to present. Boundary of the Anthropocene.</p>
<p>Earth Systems, its resilience and sensitivity to change: Planetary boundaries and Tipping point concept &ndash; Climate change, Atmosphere aerosol / Ozone, freshwater, land-system, Biosphere integrity, Ocean acidification and sea-level rise, Novel entities, Biogeochemical cycles and flows. Rivers, glaciers, river hazards, sediment movement and landscape change, sinking of deltas. Resilience systems. Limits to growth.</p>
<p>Sustainability in the Anthropocene: and its significance. Natural resources and natural hazards management in the Anthropocene, Coupled socio-ecological systems and perspective from archaeology, Socio-economic diversity and environment degradation;  Introduction to Sustainability Development Goal (SDG). </p>                                                                </p>

																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Module 1: Introduction to hominin evolution, Fossil and genetic evidence for anatomically modern humans, Competing models of human<br>
origins: &ldquo;Out of Africa&rdquo; vs. Multiregional<br>
Module 2: Dispersal and Global Migrations. Migration routes out of Africa: inland and coastal, Archaeological and genetic evidence of<br>
dispersals, Early migrations into Asia, Europe, and Sahul, Colonization of the Americas<br>
Module 3: Encounters, Adaptations, and Innovations. Interactions with Neanderthals, Denisovans, and other hominins, Genomic<br>
insights into admixture and adaptation, Responses to environmental and climatic challenges<br>
Module 4: Regional Diversity and Ecological Impact. Development of regional lithic and cultural traditions, Emergence of symbolic and<br>
ritual practices, Human role in Pleistocene megafaunal extinctions                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Ceramics in Archaeology </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Ceramics in Archaeology [EH 629]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Overview of ceramics, their origin, types (pottery, stoneware, faience), and artistic significance (figurines). Includes the earliest examples globally and in India. Theoretical framework on the importance of studying ceramics; Covers typology, seriation, classification, and documentation. Explores ceramic production processes, how pots were made, open-pit and updraft kilns. Discusses decoration types and cultural significance, scientific approaches to ceramic analysis including provenance, petrology, microstructural, macrostructural and manufacturing technology. Includes functional analysis (residue Analysis) with examples, and the importance of ethnographic surveys and studies; The importance of stoneware and faience technology in ancient times. Various production methods and scientific investigations. Comparable faience technology in Egyptian and Mesopotamian civilizations. Experimental research conducted by scholars and different methods of analysis; Examination of figurines within archaeological contexts, focusing on their cultural and social importance; technology of production. Analysis of bricks, including their production processes, architectural applications, and evolution over time; Ethnographic surveys to the potter’s workshop to understand the manufacturing processes of pottery; Hands-on handling and better understanding of pottery from various cultural stages, spanning from the Harappan period to the Historical period.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#5D5B5B ">EH 629</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="EH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/ee.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Harappan Civilization </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                                               <h1>Harappan Civilization [EH 630]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   "Introduction to Harappan Civilization, discovery, terminology, chronology, geographical distribution, contemporary cultures, important sites and their role in defining the civilization; Understanding the sedentary lifestyles of humans around 10000 years before present, emergence of villages, towns and cities; regional chalcolithic cultures; other contemporary Bronze Age Cultures, i.e. Egyptian, Mesopotamian and Chinese; Integration of regional chalcolithic cultures around 2600 BCE into Harappan civilization; characteristics that define the Harappan culture; Architecture, Arts, Burial Practices / Disposal of dead, Society and Religion; Technologies (bead, shell, metal, stoneware bangle, steatite, faience, seals, etc.), Writing systems, issues related to non-decipherment; authors of the Harappans; trade
relations, both internal and external intermingled with scientific methodologies to study various aspects; Causes of decline, transformation of Harappans into rural settlements and way of life; continuity; Visit to a nearby Harappan archaeological site; Hands-on handling artefacts and ceramics from Harappan context "                                                                </p>

                                                                   Concept of Stress & Strain:  Traction & Stress, normal and shear stress, stress in two-dimension, Strain in rocks, Longitudinal and shear strain, principal axes of strain, strain ellipse concept, Planar & Linear Structures: Morphological features of foliations and lineations. Brief idea of origin of foliation and lineation and its relationship with tectonic stress. Ductile behaviour of Rocks: Fold and its morphology; Geometric and morphological classification of folds; mechanism of folding. Brittle behaviour of Rocks: Classification of fractures, Faults and Joints; Relation of Joints to Folds; Fault zone terminology; Geometric classification of faults; Anderson analysis of faulting; Shear zone and shear sense indicators. Interpretation of topographic maps; Stereographic projections of planes and lines; Orientation of planar and linear features; fold and fault problems through graphical and stereographic projection methods. Interpretation of geological maps with unconformity, fault, fold and igneous bodies                                                                </p>

Topics: China: millet and rice domestication, Southeast Asia: root crops, early rice cultivation, maritime adaptations, South Asia: zebu cattle, mung bean, urad, horse gram, regional innovation and integration of agriculture into cultural traditions; Mesoamerican domesticates: maize, beans, squash. Andean domesticates: potato, quinoa, llama, North American domesticates sunflower, goosefoot, Environmental and cultural diversity in agricultural origins                                                                </p>

                                                                   Overview and Introduction: Why study or practice Archaeology? Defining the discipline: its nature, scope, and key concepts, its place among other disciplines (Anthropology, History, Prehistory), and in the contemporary world; Theoretical development: Antiquarianism to culture-Historical approach, paradigms of Processual and Post-Processual Archaeology, controversies, current and future trends, and Archaeology in India; Archaeological methods through case studies: Pre-excavation strategy, excavation and post-excavation techniques and analyses, data and contexts, contribution of sciences (chemistry, physics, geophysics, earth sciences, biological sciences and palaeoclimatology) in Archaeology, contribution of technology (mapping, remote sensing, viewing/imaging and computer science) in Archaeology; Cultures and Chronologies: mapping human origins and evolution to cultures, cultures (Palaeolithic, Mesolithic, Neolithic, Chalcolithic, Iron age, Early Historical, Medieval and Modern periods) vs material culture (artifacts and ecofacts), simple to complex societies to civilizations, explaining cultural change/adaptation through case studies, cultural heritage (tangible and intangible), cultural resource management, laws and legislations; Branches/Types of Archaeology: Social archaeology, Environmental archaeology, Public archaeology, Underwater archaeology etc.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Electrical Systems </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Electrical Systems [ES 103]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Circuit elements – active, passive, time-variant, time-invariant, linear, non-linear,
unilateral, bilateral; Sources – independent and dependent; Electric circuit and analysis
– Ohm’s law, Kirchhoff’s laws, loop and node analyses, limitations of lumped circuit
analysis; Network theorems – Superposition, Thevenin, Norton, Maximum power
transfer; Natural and forced responses to DC excitation – RL, RC and RLC circuits;
Sinusoidal steady state analysis; Polyphase circuits. Magnetic field – Biot-Savart law,
Ampere’s circuital law, Faraday’s laws, Lenz law; Magnetic materials, characteristics,
losses, coupled circuits. Transformers – single phase and three phase transformers,
auto-transformers. Electro-mechanical energy conversion systems – DC generator and
DC motor; AC Machines – synchronous generator and motor, three phase and single
phase induction motors; Stepper motor. Power system - generation, transmission,
distribution, costing of electricity.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Analog and Digital Electronics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Analog and Digital Electronics [ES 104]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to signals and spectra, analog and digital signals, basic amplifier characterization,
frequency characteristics and Bode plots; Ideal operational amplifiers, inverting and no-inverting
amplifier circuits, instrumentation amplifier, integrators, differentiators; effects of finite
(frequency dependent) gain, DC imperfections, and slew rate on performance; terminal
characteristics of ideal and practical diodes, rectifiers, limiters and clampers, voltage doublers,
Zener diodes; terminal characteristics of MOSFETs and BJTs; biasing, small signal analysis,
simple amplifier circuits; basic feedback theory, simple oscillators; number systems; Boolean
algebra and logic gates, minimization with Karnaugh maps; adders, comparators, decoders,
encoders, multiplexers; sequential circuits – basic flip-flops, asynchronous and synchronous
counters, registers; programmable devices – PLA, PAL and ROM; Memories.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Computing </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 3 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Computing [ES 112]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p><strong>1.&nbsp;</strong><strong>Elementary Programming Techniques</strong></p>
<ul>
  <li>Building familiarity with instruction driven task completion using Scratch or CS Unplugged.</li>
  <li>Von Neumann architecture, the translation process for a program (Source code -&gt; Interpreter/Compiler -&gt;&nbsp; Machine code)</li>
  <li>Expressions: values or literals, combining values using operators, basic types (int, float, str, bool); type conversion; lazy evaluation for Boolean operators.</li>
  <li>Functions (scope), examples from math and random; writing your own simple functions that do not require control flow; (e.g, &ldquo;celsius_to_fahrenheit&rdquo;)</li>
  <li>Program development lifecycle (edit, test, debug; libraries such as pytest, simple_benchmark; and tools such as flake8)</li>
  <li>Control flow: conditionals (if-else, match); loops (while, for).</li>
</ul>
<p><strong>2.&nbsp;</strong><strong>Advanced Data Types</strong></p>
<ul>
  <li>Tuples: construction, unpacking.</li>
  <li>Lists: construction (+ comprehensions), indexing, slicing.</li>
  <li>Sets: construction, selection, set operations.</li>
  <li>Dictionaries: construction, indexing, update, merging.</li>
  <li>Complex data (dictionaries of lists to lists etc.); frozenset, frozendict.</li>
  <li>Objects and Classes</li>
  <li>Exceptions: try-catch-finally, writing your own exceptions.</li>
</ul>
<p><strong>3.&nbsp;</strong><strong>Advanced Programming Techniques</strong></p>
<ul>
  <li>Recursion (basic examples such as Fibonacci and Towers of Hanoi)</li>
  <li>Functions as values (functools)</li>
  <li>Iterators and generators (itertools, more-itertools)</li>
</ul>
<p><strong>4.&nbsp;</strong><strong>Programming in the Large</strong></p>
<ul>
  <li>Modules: code organization</li>
  <li>Selection of python modules (e.g, re, numpy, scipy, beautifulsoup, flask, etc).</li>
</ul>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Data-Centric Computing </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>1.5 | 0 | 1.5 | 3							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Data-Centric Computing [ES 113]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 1.5 | 3<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Algorithms for processing data: Understanding time complexity – Asymptotics (big-O notation and examples); Storing sequential data: arrays and linked lists, data structures for storing tabular data; Organizing data and finding items – Searching (linear/binary search), Sorting – sorting almost sorted data (bubble sort, insertion sort), fast sorting in the worst-case (heapsort), using randomization to sort faster (quicksort), sorting without random-access ex. linked-lists (mergesort); Data Analysis: Tools - Spreadsheets (Excel/Google Sheet/Calc); data manipulation specific Python libraries e.g. Pandas; introduction to SQL; Answering queries over tabular data: map-filter-reduce, joins and unions. Using vectorized operations for efficiency. Techniques: Data structuring (tidy data) and cleaning (dealing with multiple labels for the same data, missing data) Summarizing data (averages, variance, moments), functions of tables (loading, cleaning, normalizing); Visualizing data introduction to matplotlib; plotting various statistics; categorical distributions, numerical distributions, overlaid graphs; Deriving Conclusions: Anomaly detection techniques in data: tests e.g. GRIM test, Benford’s law; Statistical traps: Correlation vs causation, Base-rate fallacy / prosecutor's fallacy. Simpson's paradox. Data censoring; Will Rogers effect, lead-time bias, and length time bias. Means versus medians. Importance of higher moments. Misleading visualizations; Making Predictions: Linear regression; basics of classification (train, test, validation); creating features; naive Bayes classification, logistic regression and its interpretation; introduction to clustering (k-means). Confidence in predictions: prediction intervals, confidence intervals.</p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

                                            </div>

                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Probability, Statistics, and Data Visualization </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit

								L | T | P | C
							<br>1.5 | 0 | 1.5 | 3							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Probability, Statistics, and Data Visualization [ES 114]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 1.5 | 3<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Computational Tools: Statistical Data Analysis using Python/R, Introduction to Relevant Libraries ; Basic Probability: Set Theory and Probability Spaces, Axioms, Probability Assignment for Discrete and Continuous Spaces, Combinatorics, Conditional Probability, Independence, Mutual Exclusiveness, Law of Total Probability, Bayes Rule, Examples ; Random Variables: Discrete and Continuous Random Variables, Probability Mass Function (PMF), Probability Density Function (PDF), Transformation (Function) of Random Variable, Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF), Moments, Characteristic Function, Examples of Random Variables, Applications; Two Random Variables and Random Vectors: 
Joint PMF and PDF, Conditional PMF and PDF, Joint and Conditional Moments, Joint Characteristic Function, Covariance and Correlation Coefficient, Covariance Matrix, Dependence and Correlation, Transformation, Examples and Applications ;  Statistical Inference: Confidence Interval, Hypothesis Testing - Critical Value test, p-Value test, Z-test, and t-test, Neyman-Pearson Test, Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) and Precision-Recall (P-R) curves, Applications ;  From Data to Visualization: Data - Numbers, Strings, Sequences, Tables, Functions on Tables, Visualization - Categorical Distributions, Numerical Distributions, Overlaid Graphs. Statistical Visualization - Line Charts, Bar Charts, Contour Plots Spatial data visualization, network visualizations using Python libraries, visualizing probability distributions. Interactive Visualization - Curve Fitting, Regression, Clustering, Linear and Non-Linear Classification. Dimensionality Reduction (e.g. n-D to 2-D) - Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE). Examples and Applications in various engineering disciplines.

</p>                                                                </p>

                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="ES">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/esc.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Design, Innovation, and Prototyping </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 5							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Design, Innovation, and Prototyping [ES 115]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 5<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Design: Introduction design process as a strategic problem-solving process, Drivers of innovation, Industrial design; Methods of data collection, Synthesizing, analyzing, and representing the data; Making a good brief; Craftsmanship/ techno-aesthetic detailing; Human factors, Applied ergonomics; Nature inspired design, Responsible design, Nuances of design; Innovation: Theory  of  inventive  problem  solving:  Double  diamond  process,  lateral thinking; Creativity  models  and  tools  used  to  push  the  boundaries:  mind  maps/ fishbone diagrams, brainstorming techniques, double diamond process; Prototyping: Freehand sketch, Overview of 3D solid modeling (CAD), 3D scanning using laser scanners; 3D Printing: Fused deposition modeling, stereolithography; Familiarization with machining processes: Centering, drilling, and milling using tabletop reconfigurable CNC machines; Casting, molding, and vacuum forming; Wood crafting: wood turning and joint making, laser cutting; Electronics prototyping: PCB milling and soldering; Familiarization with joining processes: adhesives, riveting, and fasteners; Familiarization with finishing processes: abrasive grinding, sand blasting, painting; Power tools in fabrication.
</p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#663399">ES 115</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="ES">

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Principles and Applications of Electrical Engineering </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 1.5 | 5							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Principles and Applications of Electrical Engineering [ES 116]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 1.5 | 5<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p><strong>Introduction:</strong> </p>
<p>relationship between electrical engineering and the physical world; Signals and Sources: concept of a signal; physical origin of signals, types of signals (temporal, spatial, slow, rapid, periodic, aperiodic); independent and dependent sources; voltage sources, current sources, impedance matching; Measures of a signal: amplitude, peak value, average value, rms value, repetition rate, duty cycle; phasor representation; Spectral analysis of signals: elementary concepts of the Fourier series and the Fourier transform Types of systems: linear and nonlinear systems; active and passive systems; Signal gathering: basic properties of sensors, linearity, dynamic range, time response, cross-sensitivity Energy storage: the role of reactive circuit elements; effects of capacitance and inductance, time-domain properties of systems, transient behaviour of system described by first-order differential equations; Frequency selectivity: frequency response of systems, low-pass, high-pass and resonant behaviour described by second-order differential equations; Signal conditioning: concept of amplification, operational amplifiers as amplifiers; Signal conditioning: filtering, use of operational amplifiers with reactive circuit elements Analog-to-digital conversion: sampling and quantization; Noise: elementary ideas about types and sources of noise, signal-to-noise ratio; Nonlinear circuit elements: the diode and simple diode circuits, the MOSFET as an amplifier and as a switch Combinational Logic: brief of logic gates, minimization of logic expressions; Sequential Logic: Latch, Flip-flops (SR, JK, D), flip-flop applications (frequency counter, registers, decoders, multiplexers, demultiplexers, comparators); Overview of applications of EE in important areas: renewable energy, electric vehicles, communication and signal processing.</p>                                                                </p>

decoders, encoders, multiplexers, demultiplexers; Circuit implementation on
Programmable array logic (PAL) and on field programmable gate array (FPGA), MOS
implementation of circuits; Shannon’s decomposition theorem, datapath circuits including
comparator design, multiplier design, etc., static and dynamic hazards digital circuits;
Sequential logic: Timing in flip-flops and registers, metastability; Finite state machine:
Design and analysis of synchronous Mealy and Moore FSMs; Basics of memory
organization; Basics of computer organization through a simple processor design
including instruction set architecture, mnemonics, assembly programming concepts,
basics of processor organization, Introduction to microcontrollers; Introduction to Verilog
and VHDL, various HDL programming constructs, FPGA programming.                                                                </p>

						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="ES">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/esc.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Thermodynamics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>2 | 1 | 0 | 3							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Thermodynamics [ES 211]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 1 | 0 | 3<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction and basic concepts <br>
Thermodynamics & energy; applications of thermodynamics; importance of dimensions and units; systems and control volumes; properties of a system; state and equilibrium; processes and cycles; temperature and the zeroth of thermodynamics; generic problem-solving technique in thermodynamics<br><br>
Energy, Energy transfer and general energy analysis<br>
Forms of energy; energy transfer due to heat, work and matter; mechanical forms of work; the first law of thermodynamics; energy conversion efficiencies; energy and the environment<br><br>
Properties of pure substances<br>
Phases of a pure substance; phase-change processes; property diagrams and tables; link between classical and statistical properties; equations of state<br><br>
Energy analysis of closed systems<br>
Moving boundary work; energy balance for closed systems; specific heats; internal energy, enthalpy and specific heats for ideal gases<br><br>
Mass and energy analysis of control volumes<br>
Conservation of mass; flow work and the energy of a flowing fluid; energy analysis of flow systems; steady flow systems; unsteady flow processes<br><br>
The second law of thermodynamics<br>
Thermal energy reservoirs; heat engines, refrigerators and heat pumps; reversible and irreversible processes; internally reversible processes; Carnot cycle and principles; Carnot heat engine and refrigerator<br><br>
Entropy<br>
Clausius inequality; increase of entropy principle; entropy change of pure substances; property diagrams; isentropic processes; reversible steady-flow work; isentropic efficiency for steady flow devices; entropy balance<br><br>
Introduction to thermodynamic cycles<br>
Introduction to Rankine, Diesel and Brayton cycles for power production, mobility and aviation; introduction to vapor compression refrigeration cycle<br><br>
Exergy<br>
Work potential of energy; reversible work and irreversibility; second-law efficiency; exergy change of a system; exergy balance for closed systems and control volumes<br><br>
Thermodynamics of radiation<br>
Planck’s law for black bodies; Wien’s displacement law; absorption and emission of radiation; black bodies; Stefan-Boltzmann law<br><br>
Fundamentals of photovoltaic energy conversion<br>
Properties of sunlight; solar cell operation                                                                </p>

                                                    </div>

of NS equations, Importance of Reynolds number (Re), High Re flow: Prandtl’s approximation, basic

integral equation. Displacement and Momentum Thickness. Laminar and Turbulent Boundary Layers.
Power Laws. Skin friction coefficient and drag estimation. Skin friction lines on surfaces. Flow through
packed beds and fluidized beds; Transportation and metering of fluids, pump types, pump curves,
blowers and compressors; Mixing and Agitation, power consumption, impeller types and flow patterns,
mixing times.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">

																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Free body diagram, Modeling of supports, Conditions for Equilibrium, Friction Force- deformation
relationship and geometric compatibility (for small deformations) with illustrations through simple
problems on axially loaded members and thin walled pressure vessels, Axial force, shear force, bending
moment, and twisting moment diagrams of slender members, Concept of stress and strain at a point,
Transformation of stresses and strain at a point, Principal stresses and strains, Mohr’s circle (only for
plane stress and strain case), Displacement field, Strain Rosette, Modeling of problem as a plane stress
or plane strain problem, Discussion of experimental results on 1-D material behavior. Concepts of
elasticity, plasticity, strain-hardening, failure (fracture/yielding), idealization of 1-D stress-strain curve,
Concepts of isotropy, orthotropy, anisotropy, Generalized Hooke’s law, (without and with thermal strains),
Notions of elasticity, Torsion of circular shafts and thin-walled tubes, Bending of beams with symmetric
cross-section (normal and shear stresses),Combined stresses, Yield criteria, Deflection due to bending,
Integration of the moment-curvature relationship for simple boundary conditions, Super position principle,
Concepts of strain energy and complementary strain energy for simple structural elements (those under
axial load, shear force, bending moment, and torsion), Castigliano’s theorems for deflection analysis and
indeterminate problems, Concept of elastic instability and a brief introduction to column buckling and
Euler’s formula.                                                                </p>

- Autocorrelation and Power Spectral Density, Gaussian Processes, White Gaussian
Noise, Brownian Motion, Auto Regressive, Moving Average, Poisson Processes,
Applications to Networks, Detection Theory, and Communication Channels.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Control Systems </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">

                                                               <h1>Control Systems [ES 245]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of basic concepts: Notion of feedback; open- and closed-loop systems; Review of
Laplace transforms, transfer functions, conversion of models between time domain and
frequency domain; Modelling and representations of control systems: Ordinary differential
equations, state-space representations, transfer function and block diagrams; Linearity,
time-invariance versus time-variance; Linearization; Canonical forms; Dynamic response
and stability of systems; Time-domain analysis, first order systems, second-order
systems, standard responses – Impulse, step, ramp; Poles and zeros and their effects on
responses; Dynamic response from the state equations, definition of stability,
characteristic-equation, and roots; BIBO Stability, Routh-Hurwitz criteria; Design of
controllers for SISO systems: Feedback control systems: Basic idea of feedback control
systems, stabilizing control, regulation and tracking; Steady state error analysis; P, PI,
PD, PID controllers, Ziegler–Nichols Tuning of the PID Controller; the root-locus
technique; Design of controllers using root-locus; Feedforward control by plant model
Inversion; Design of controllers for MIMO systems: Control-Law design for full-state
feedback, pole placement with state feedback, controllability; Pole placement with output
feedback, observability, Luenberger observer; LQR control; Frequency domain analysis
and design: Frequency responses; Bode-plots; Gain-margin and phase-margin; Nyquist
plots; Stability analysis in frequency domain, design via frequency response: Lead-lag
compensators.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

mass transfer coefficients, correlations; analogy between momentum, heat and mass transfer;                                                                </p>

mean, variance, correlation; Markov, Chebychev and Chernoff bounds; sequences of random variables,
strong and weak law of large numbers, central limit theorem; linear mean square estimation and
orthogonality principle; maximum likelihood and parameter estimation. Random processes, strict and
wide sense stationary processes; ergodic processes; bandlimited and periodic processes; random
processes and linear systems; power spectral density; noise processes; Wiener filtering; Kalman filtering;
examples of random processes, Poisson process, Markov process.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Interface Design </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Interface Design [ES 334]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   · Introduction: Evolution of interfaces, Nuances of interfaces, Man machine interaction diagram
· Effective Communication: Intent of communication, Modes of
presentation(language.gesture.signs), Mental processes, Communication gaps, Emotional and
Cultural aspects of communication
· Elements of interfaces: Types of controls, Types of displays, Gesture control, Role of sensors,
Light and sound, emergency and warning signs
· Applied ergonomics: Physical (hand reach, RSIs), Cognitive aspects, Organizational
· Principles of visual ergonomics: Visual hierarchy, Grouping, Color coding, Placement and
orientation
· Methodology: User persona, Scenario building, Opportunity mapping, Information management,
prototyping and testing of interfaces                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Process Data Analytics, Optimization, and Control </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Process Data Analytics, Optimization, and Control [ES 338]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   1. Basics of optimization: variables, constraints, objectives, linear, nonlinear, mixed-integer, convex, nonconvex, formulation and solution using computer solver<br>
2. Basics of process control: proportional, proportional-integral, and proportional-integral-derivative controllers, dynamic &amp; multi-variable process modeling using computer software, and closed-loop simulation under different controllers<br>
3. Data analytics: outlier removal, dimensionality reduction, principle component analysis, feature selection, k-means clustering, hierarchical clustering, support vector machines, random forest classification<br>
4. Data-driven modeling and optimization: design of experiments, sampling, response surface model, neural network model, validation, surrogate-based optimization<br>
5.   Model predictive control (MPC): introduction to MPC, data-driven state-space model development, MPC controller development using state-space model, closed loop simulation under MPC.<br>
The course will use datasets familiar to engineers, such as industrial process data, energy &amp; environmental systems data, and semiconductor manufacturing data.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>0 | 0 | 3 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Overview of cleanroom environment and classifications, Cleanroom utilities and infrastructure requirements, Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), Safety protocols and best practices, Gowning procedures, Laboratory documentation and in-lab record keeping; Microfabrication process experiments: Wafer preparation (cleaning steps and native oxide removal), Mask preparation using laser writer (Mask design), Photolithography (Spin coating, Pre-bake, UV exposure, Post-bake,  Development), Physical vapor deposition (sputtering, evaporation) and process parameter optimization, Chemical vapor deposition and process parameter tuning, Wet etching (etch rate determination and testing etching profiles on various orientations of Silicon), determination of over etching, under etching and end point parameters, thermal oxidation (validation of Deal-Grove model and diffusion) and doping (pre-deposition and subsequent drive-in of impurities to form electrical junctions). Fabrication and testing of basic semiconductor devices: resistor, capacitor, diode and MOS capacitor (MOSCAP).                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Networks and Complex Systems </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Networks and Complex Systems [ES 404]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to networks, empirical study of real world networks: Social networks, Technological
Networks, Biological networks, Neural Networks, and Information networks;
Basic concepts in graph theory, Network representation, Adjacency matrix and edge lists, weighted
networks, directed networks, bipartite networks, planar networks, degree, paths and connectivity,
graph Laplacian; Characterization of and measures on networks, degree centrality, degree distribution,
Katz centrality, hubs and authorities, between-ness centrality, clustering coefficient, Modularity,
homophily and assortative mixing. Analytical and computational tools in networks; Representation of
network with gephi software package.
Random networks, properties of random networks; Small world networks: Watts-Stogartz model,
properties and real world examples; Scale free networks: Barabasi-Albert model, theoretical
approaches, characteristics of power law behavior in scale free networks. Examples from citation
network, cellular network, internet etc.
Dynamical processes on networks, microscopic approaches to dynamical phenomena, master
equation, mean field solutions; Disease spreading on networks, Basic compartmental models like:
SIS, SIR and SEIR. Epidemics spreading on static and adaptive networks, with examples from
SARS and H1N1; Sexual contact networks and HIV.
Resilience and robustness of networks, Percolation phenomena and phase transitions,
percolation on complex networks; damage and resilience in networks, coupled networks and
targeted attacks, cascading failures in network.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Elements of Mechatronics systems: overview of Instrumentation, Electromechanical, and
Information Systems.
Actuators: Mechanical, Electromechanical, Electrical, Fluid power and Thermal systems;
Characteristics, study and analysis in time and frequency domains.
Sensors and Transducers: characteristics, design and applications; Signal conditioning, A/D, D/A
conversions.
Miniaturization: Micromachining and fabrication processes, Introduction to MEMS and MOEMS,
Technology and Applications.
Industrial Data Networks: Inter-device communications and Data logging.
Systems and Controllers: Process Controllers, Programmable Logic Controllers, DCS, SCADA,
and CNC Systems; Introduction to Computer Integrated Manufacturing.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Inverse Modelling in Physical Sciences </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Inverse Modelling in Physical Sciences [ES 410]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review: Mathematical prerequisites; Nature of mathematical modeling in physical sciences; Classification of inverse problems: discrete (parameter estimation) and continuous inverse problems. Discretizing continuous inverse problems using basis functions. ; Nature of ill-posed problems. Examples of ill-posed problems in physical sciences ; Inverse solutions using least squares methods. Rank deficiency and ill-conditioning: Singular Value Decomposition and generalized inverse; covariance and resolution of the generalized inverse solution. Minimum norm inverse solutions. model resolution and information density matrices. ; Tikhonov regularization: resolution, bias and uncertainty in solution, discrepancy method. Higher order Tikhonov regularization-Other regularization techniques: Sparsity regularization, bounds constraints; Fourier techniques: regularization in Fourier space. ; Stochastic inverse theory, Bayesian inverse theory: multivariate normal case, Markov Chain Monte Carlo methods,
Analyzing MCMC outputs. Using stochastic methods for identifying a host of acceptable models. ; Nonlinear inverse modeling: Occam's inversion.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#663399">ES 410</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="ES">

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Overview of optical communications; Planar optical wave guides, modes and the eigenvalue equation,
single-mode and multimode fiber; Group  velocity and material dispersion, inter-modal and intra-modal
dispersion, attenuation, pulse dispersion, fiber bandwidth, dispersion management; wavelength division
multiplexing (WDM) concepts, Signal degradation, attenuation, dispersion and its compensation; Optical
sources, semiconductor lasers and light emitting diodes, structure, spectral and temporal properties,
modulation schemes; Photo-detectors, structure, operation, quantum efficiency, responsivity, spectral
and temporal response; Detection schemes, coherent and non-coherent detection, performance analysis;
WDM components, splitters, isolators, circulators, fiber Bragg gratings (FBG), Fabry-Perot and thin-film
filters; Optical amplifiers, erbium doped fiber amplifiers                                                                </p>

                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Deep Learning [ES 413]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <b>Fundamentals of Deep Learning:</b> Mathematics for Deep Learning - Calculus, Matrices, and Probability. Classification and Regression Problems. Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), Deep MLP and Backpropagation Algorithm. Implementation - Training, Validation, and Testing using Deep MLP. Optimization Techniques for Deep Networks - Gradient Descent, Stochastic Gradient Descent(SGD), Momentum, Adaptive Gradient (Adagrad), Adaptive Momentum (AdaM), Root Mean Square Propagation (RMSProp). Regularization Techniques for Deep Networks - L1/L2, Data Augmentation, Dropout, DropBlock, Batch Normalization.
<br>
<b>Deep Neural Networks:</b> Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Selected CNN Architectures and their Applications - LENet-5, VGG-16, AlexNet, GoogLENet, InceptionNet, ResNet. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN), Selected RNN Architectures and their Applications - Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) Networks and its variants, PixelRNN. Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN), Selected GAN architectures and their Applications - Conditional GAN, CycleGAN, WassersteinGAN, StyleGAN, StackGAN, BigGAN.
<br>
<b>Advanced Deep Learning Tools and Techniques:</b> Design and Implementation - 3D CNN, Transfer Learning, Domain Adaptation, Autoencoders, Attention Mechanism, and Transformers. Deep Learning on Graphs - Embeddings, Graph Neural Networks, and their Applications. Self Supervised Learning. Tips and Tricks to enhance the Performance of Deep Neural Network implementations. Recent Advances.
<br>
<b>Applications:</b>  Research domains - Sustainability, Computer Vision, Natural Language Processing, Web Mining, Signal Processing, Bioinformatics, Climate Modeling.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Basic properties of light and classical treatment of light-matter interaction; origin of refractive index, absorption, dispersion; basic optical phenomena - interference, diffraction, birefringence;<br>
Generation of light: basic operation of light-emitting diodes and lasers; Lab demonstrations of important temporal and spectral properties of these sources<br>
Controlling the properties of light to convey information; modulation of intensity, phase and polarization; electro-optic modulators, acousto-optic modulators, polarizers; splitters, combiners, multiplexers;<br>
Propagation of light through dielectric waveguide structures – conventional and unconventional optical fiber; rectangular waveguide structures; metal-dielectric waveguides;<br>
Detection of light: p-i-n photodiodes, avalanche photodiodes; quantum efficiency, responsivity, spectral and temporal response<br>
Applications in engineering and science such as optical fiber communication, optical sensing of physical quantities (displacement, speed, strain, vibration, temperature) and chemicals (high-resolution molecular spectroscopy to identify different gases), plasmonics, biophotonics, imaging, light detection and ranging (LIDAR) as used in distributed sensing and autonomous cars. Lab demonstrations of sensing displacement, vibration, temperature and gas leaks<br>                                                                </p>
