							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Nature-inspired Materials Design [ES 415]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p><strong>I. Preliminaries of biomimetic materials</strong><br>
  - Principles of biomimicry from the fields of biology, engineering and design<br>
  - Processing and characterization of Biomimetic materials<br>
  - Models and simulations of structure-property relationships in biomimetics</p>
<p><strong>II. Strong, tough and lightweight materials</strong><br>
  - Strengthening and toughening mechanisms inspired by spider silk, nacre and hard tissues<br>
- Applications in design of nature-inspired composites with superior mechanical properties </p>
<p><strong>III. Wear and impact-resistant materials</strong><br>
  - Structure and design inspiration from hard and wear resistant materials such as teeth, bones, and horns <br>
- Nano-scale mechanisms of wear and impact resistance </p>
<p><strong>IV. Self-shaping and self-healing materials</strong><br>
  - Reversible/switchable properties such as stiffness, strength and shape inspired by sea cucumber and squid beak<br>
  - Self-healing properties from plants and tree-bark inspiring vascular networks<br>
- Nanocomposites and concretes demonstrating self-shaping and self-healing properties</p>
<p><strong>V. Self-cleaning materials and surfaces</strong><br>
  - Fundamentals of wettability and self-cleaning<br>
- Superhydrophobicity (Lotus effect) and self-cleaning dry adhesives inspired by Gecko feet. </p>
<p><strong>VI. Materials for optical applications</strong><br>
  - Diffraction gratings, reflectors and photonic materials inspired by nature<br>
- Nature-inspired antireflective surfaces and lenses</p>
<p><strong>VII. Special manufacturing methods for advanced biomimetic materials </strong><br>
</p>                                                                </p>

and B5G networks: MEC Architecture, Mobility, service continuity, traffic routing; 5G Automation:
Network Slicing, Requirements for slice automation and 5G packet core services. End-2-End
network slicing and service Assurances. Cloud RAN Architecture, challenges and Non-public 5G
networks.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Numerical Methods for Engineers </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Numerical Methods for Engineers [ES 419]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Basic review of numerical methods and mathematical modeling in engineering through simple examples. Review of Taylor series and error estimates; Solutions of Linear and Nonlinear Systems: Review of LU decomposition, Singular value decomposition (SVD), Thomas algorithm, Review of Iterative methods, Successive Over-Relaxation (SOR); review of Newton-Raphson method and applications in engineering; Numerical differentiation: Review of curve fitting and regression, finite difference method - explicit and Implicit schemes, Crank-Nicolson and Alternating-direction implicit method; Numerical integration: Romberg integration, Newton-Cotes method and Gaussian quadrature; Numerical Solution of Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs), Initial value problems: Runge-Kutta methods (RK2, RK4, embedded and adaptive), multi-step predictor-corrector methods: Adams-Bashforth, Adams-Moulton, Stability and convergence analysis, Boundary value problems: finite difference and collocation methods; Numerical Solution of Governing Equations in Engineering: Diffusion equation (parabolic), Laplace and Poisson equations (elliptic), advection-diffusion equations, Wave equation (hyperbolic), Eigen value problems, Stiff equations, Introduction to finite element and finite volume methods, applications in heat transfer, fluid mechanics, and structural analysis; Computational Methods in Engineering: Preconditioning and subspace methods, model order reduction techniques (multi-physics applications) – proper orthogonal decomposition, reduced basis method. Accelerated time integration methods, multiple time stepping and method of multiple scales                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

SCADA: Remote terminal units, Master station, Communication architectures and Open SCADA protocols. Laboratory: Experiments based on the above contents.                                                                </p>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                               <h1>RFIC Design [ES 421]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 3 | 5<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to RF and Transmission Line Theory covering signal behavior, reflection coefficients,  VSWR, and impedance matching using the Smith Chart for lumped and distributed elements; RF Network Representation focusing on S-parameters for circuit characterization and shifting of reference planes; Wireless Architectures and CMOS Integration exploring transceiver blocks, IC design constraints, and the transition from discrete components to Silicon microelectronics; High-Frequency Transistor Modeling and Low Noise Amplifiers (LNA) focusing on f, T, fmax, and parasitic effects to implement inductively degenerated CMOS topologies with optimized noise figures; Mixer Design and Frequency Translation encompassing active Gilbert Cells and passive CMOS switching mixers while addressing conversion gain and port isolation; Oscillators and Frequency Synthesis covering negative resistance theory, LC and Ring Oscillator topologies, phase noise, and the basics of Phase Locked Loops (PLL); Amplifier and CMOS Power Amplifier (PA) Design focusing on stability, Class A-C operations, and efficiency (PAE) for large-signal performance; System Integration addressing transceiver challenges such as IQ imbalance, DC offsets, and the role of integrated filters in signal chains.                                                                </p>

							<hr>

                                                               <h1>Foundations of Fluid Dynamics [ES 607]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   vector calculus, Introduction to tensors.
Concept of continuum, Knudsen’s number
Kinematics of fluid motion: Eulerian and Langarangian viewpoint.
Basic laws: Continuity and momentum equations, Conservation Laws for Transport Processes
(Mass. Momentum and Energy), Analysis of Stress in Viscous Fluids and Strain for Fluid
Deformation, Constitutive Relation:, Navier-Stokes equations. , Examples of incompressible
flow patterns.Vorticity dynamics: vorticity diffusion, intensification by stretching, production of
vorticity, Helmholts law, Kelvins theorem. Motion of Vortex Lines, Vortex Filaments and Biot-
Savart Law. Potential flow: Streamlines and velocity potential. Boundary layers, concept of
boundary layers, displacement and momentum thickness, von Karman momentum integral,
approximate methods. Blasius and Falkner-Skan similarity solutions, flow separation,
axisymmetric boundary layers, free shear layers, jets.
Introduction to turbulent flows, types and characteristics of turbulent flows, energy cascade,
Kolmogorov scale, Reynolds decomposition, RANS equation, closure problem.                                                                </p>

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Modern Control Theory [ES 613]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to linear algebra, (Matrices, differential equations and states), linear time invariant
systems and its solutions, mulitivariate systems introduction, state space analysis of continuous
systems, controllability, observability, stability with respect to state space analysis, Conversion
between transfer function and state space models, Lyapnov theorem for stability, full state back
control design, pole placement and control, overview of important concepts in stochastic data
analysis, Kalman filters for state estimation, Discussion on Kalman filter as Best Linear Unbiased
Estimate (BLUE), Introduction to Model predictive control (MPC) for linear systems with emphasis on
linear quadratic control; Advanced topics based on Time criteria: Case studies with emphasis on use of
Kalman filters with MPC for state estimation and control.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Stress Analysis: Theory of stress, equilibrium equations, principal stresses and stress invariants.
Analysis of Deformation: Lagrangian and Eulerian description, finite strain, small deformation theory, principal strains and strain invariants, compatibility conditions.  
Constitutive Theory: Generalized Hook’s law, linear elasticity relations, constitutive relations for orthotropic and anisotropic materials yield criteria, plastic potential and flow-rule.
Two-dimensional problems: Plane stress and plane strain, Airy stress function, pressure vessels. Torsion: Non-prismatic sections, thick and thin-wall cylindrical shafts, shear centre and shear flow. Unsymmetrical bending. Thin Plates: Kirchhoff plate theory, rectangular plates. Buckling: rectangular plates. Class Project: Experimental / Numerical as decided by the Instructor.                                                                </p>

                                                                   Vector spaces, linear independence, basis, inner product spaces: Applications in Fourier analysis of

determinant: combinatorial approach; Solving Linear Diophantine equations; Expander graphs and
Eigenvalue, Raleigh quotient, Cheeger’s inequality; Factoring: Shor’s algorithm;SVD and it applications in
machine learning.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

                                        </section>
                                    </div>

Introduction to water quality parameters, standards, Fundamentals, optimization and design of

Ozone contactor design: Transport model for contactor design, Air stripping: Design and apply
equation for air stripping process, Membrane Processes: Membrane materials, module types,
High Pressure Membrane Process: Nanofiltration, Reverse Osmosis - models and their
application, Low Pressure Membrane Process: Microfiltration, Ultrafiltration - models and their
application, Activated Carbon Adsorption Process: Adsorption isotherms, required carbon
dose, Ion exchange: Types of ion exchange resin, ion exchange design equation, Design
Project: Design a water purification system for a sea-side town with the results of field tests of
several water supplies in the town given.                                                                </p>

unsupervised learning such as SVM, Logistics Regression, Linear Regression, Kernel Methods, Sparse
Modelling, Artificial Neural Networks; Smooth Convex Optimization: Convex Sets, Convex Functions,
Gradient Descent Methods, Stochastic Gradient Descent Methods, Nesterov’s Method, Projected
Gradient Methods; Non-smooth Convex Optimization: Subgradient and Proximal Methods, Shrinkage
Methods; Duality Theory: Lagrangian Dual, Wolfe Dual, Fenchel Dual; Lagrangian Relaxation: Dual
Decomposition, Augmented Lagrangian Methods; Large-Scale Learning: Alternating Direction Method of
Multipliers (ADMM), Parameter Mixing, Non-decomposable Loss Functions.                                                                </p>

																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Vibration of discrete systems: Variational approach to derive the equations of motion (EOM), equilibrium points and linearization about an equilibrium point, forced response due to arbitrary excitations (Duhamel/ convolution integral), modal analysis and decoupling of EOMs. Vibration of continuous systems: Vibration of strings, bars, beams (Rayleigh beams, EulerBernoulli beams and Timoshenko beams) and membranes. Variational approach to derive the EOM, modal analysis, Sturm-Liouville boundary value problem, orthogonal functions and orthogonality conditions, expansion theorem, initial and boundary value problems, forced response of systems, time dependent boundary conditions, D'Alambert's wave solution, solution by Green's function approach and by integral transforms, approximate methods (Ritz method, Rayleigh-Ritz method, Rayleigh quotient, Galerkin's method).                                                                </p>

								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">

                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to nonlinear systems with examples from mechanical, electrical, biological and chemical systems, theorems of existence and uniqueness of solutions for general class of nonlinear oscillators, dynamics in phase space, stability and classification of fixed points, general types of orbits; Basic asymptotic/perturbation methods for analyzing the free and forced responses of single- and multi-degree-of-freedom nonlinear oscillators; Forced (fundamental, sub-harmonic and super-harmonic) response, internal and combination resonances in nonlinear systems, and linearized stability analysis; Floquet theory for linear parametrically excited systems, and stability of periodic solutions; Introduction to discrete-time dynamical systems (nonlinear maps) and Poincare' maps; Theory of bifurcations in nonlinear systems.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

                                        </section>

                                </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Medical Imaging Systems </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Medical Imaging Systems [ES 655]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The goal of this course is to provide an overview of standard clinical imaging
modalities from the signals and systems perspective. Ultrasound, X-ray, Computer
Tomography (CT), and Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) will be covered. The
course will address image acquisition and reconstruction for respective imaging
modalities, choice of the appropriate modality for specific applications, modality-
specific issues on image quality, clinical applications, and safety- related
considerations for each approach. The students will be introduced to metrics for
assessing imaging performance and diagnostic value. Examples from classic and
current literature will be discussed. The following topics will be covered:
1. Review of linear systems, 2-dimensional Fourier Transform, electromagnetic
spectrum, acoustic spectrum, attenuation, scattering
2. Ultrasound imaging: Acoustic propagation, reflection, diffraction, attenuation,
Doppler effect, tissue properties, speckle, transducer probes, devices,
image artifacts, safety
3. Conventional X-ray: X-ray sources, tissue properties, artifacts,
noise/resolution
4. Computed tomography: Projection, central-slice theorem, image
reconstruction, backprojection, filtering, convolution, noise, artifacts, safety
5. 5. Magnetic Resonance Imaging: Larmor frequency, tissue properties,
rotating frame, gradients, excitation pulses, Fourier transform relations,
devices, artifacts, and safety                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                               <h1>Human-Robot Interaction [ES 656]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <li>Introduction: Human movements; Experimental tools to measure movements; Robots for human movement interaction
<li>Joint mechanics: Actuation in human motion; Single joint neuromechanics; Voluntary movement; Multi-joint kinematics; Robotic analogy
<li>Impedance in movements: Muscle stiffness and viscosity; Joint impedance; Impedance modeling in limbs; Learnings from robotic analogy 
<li>Redundancy in movements: Actuation redundancy; Kinematic redundancy; Redundancy resolution; Redundancy optimization in robots
<li>Movement dynamics: Forces at limbs and joints; Limb stability and motion; Dynamic redundancy; Learnings from robotic analogy
<li>Movement adaptation: Sensory feedback; Movement planning and neural control; Motor learning and memory; Learning models; Movement compensation 
<li>Physical human-robot interaction (pHRI): Interaction perception; Interaction measurement and modeling; Interaction control - motion and impedance
<li>Human-robot collaboration: Movement rehabilitation - assistance and restoration; Performance enhancement                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Biomedical Ultrasound </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                       Course Content

                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">

                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Biomedical Ultrasound [ES 657]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>The proposed course will provide the conceptual foundations and practical insights for the use of ultrasound in imaging and therapy, encompassing physics, instrumentation, signal generation/processing, and clinical applications. <br>
  Acoustic propagation and scattering: Wave equation and solution, plane wave reflection, refraction and  transmission, scattering from objects and inhomogeneous tissue<br>
  Ultrasound tissue interaction: Acoustic attenuation, absorption, cavitation<br>
  Ultrasound sources: Piezoelectric effect, radiation from ultrasound sources, imaging and therapy transducers <br>
  Ultrasound imaging systems: Hardware and Architecture, Software and signal processing incliuding beamforming, steering, image formation, speckle, artifacts<br>
  Flow imaging and contrast agents: Color Doppler, Power Doppler, Microbubble Contrast Agents<br>
  Therapeutic applications of ultrasound: Bioheat transfer, Hyperthermia, ablation, lithotripsy, histotripsy, ultrasound-enhanced drug delivery<br>
  Safety, regulatory and ethical issues: Thermal Index, Mechanical Index, Image Guidance and Therapeutic Monitoring, As Low as Reasonably Achievable (ALARA) principle<br>
  Recent advances in biomedical ultrasound: Discussion of Elasticity Imaging, Quantitative Ultrasound, Neuromodulation, and Ultrafast Imaging </p>
<p></p>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Molecular Simulations – Theory and Applications </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Molecular Simulations – Theory and Applications [ES 658]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to computer simulation-Model System Interaction Potentials-Construction of intermolecular potentials- Studying small systems- Periodic Boundary Conditions-Potential truncation<br>
Classical Statistical Mechanics- Ergodicity- Common Statistical Ensembles Canonical Ensemble, Isobaric Isothermal Ensemble, Grand Canonical Ensemble- Transforming Ensembles- Thermodynamic averages, Fluctuations, Structural Quantities, Pair correlation functions, Transport coefficients, Long range corrections<br>
Monte Carlo Simulations - Importance Sampling, Metropolis Method, Monte Carlo Algorithm, Detailed Balance, Trail Moves, Isobaric-Isothermal Monte Carlo, Grand Canonical Monte Carlo<br>
Molecular Dynamics Simulations- The Idea, Initialization, Equations of Motion for atomic systems, Different Integration Algorithms and their optimal applications ( Verlet, Velocity Verlet, Predictor- Corrector and Gear Predictor Corrector), Common Thermostats ( Anderson, Nose- Hoover), Common Barostats ( Berenden, Parinello , Liouville Formulation, Lyapunov Instability, Computer experiments<br>
Analysis of Results – Liquid Structure, Time Correlation Functions, estimating errors in averages, fluctuations, structural quantities and time correlations- Correcting Thermodynamic averages<br>
Introduction to Advanced Simulations – Free energy calculation-Chemical Potential, Widom Insertion, Multiple Histogram, Non-Boltzmann sampling, Gibbs Ensemble, Configurational Bias Monte Carlo, Parallel Tempering, Replica Exchange simulations<br>
Introduction to Molecular Dynamics packages: Large-scale Atomic/Molecular Massively Parallel Simulator (LAMMPS) for Coarse Grained Polymer, Material Modeling and Solid State Atomic Simulations, Nanoscale Molecular Dynamics (NAMD), GROningen MAchine for Chemical Simulations (GROMACS)- Simulations of Proteins, Lipids and Nucleic Acids<br>                                                                </p>

<p>Selected Applications: Shape Restoration, Shape Correspondence, Symmetry Detection, Shape Completion, Shape Segmentation, Shape Manipulation and Morphing, Cultural Heritage.</p>                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Variational Methods in Mechanics [ES 660]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Mathematical preliminaries: Basics of multivariable differential calculus, maxima and minima for a general function of n variables, constraints and Lagrange multipliers, examples; Mathematical preliminaries: Deﬁnition of a function, metrics and metric space, norm and vector spaces, function spaces relevant to engineering and applied sciences, definition of a ‘functional’ and its properties; Historical genesis of calculus of variations, classical problems beginning with the works of Fermat, Bernoulli, Euler, Lagrange, Jacobi, Weierstrass, Hamilton, Legendre and others, examples from the domain of applied mechanics, dynamics, thermal sciences, control theory, design optimization; Definition and physical significance of variational derivative, variation of a functional. Necessary and sufficiency condition for extremum or stationarity of a functional, the Euler’s equation, invariance of Euler’s equation. Canonical form and first integrals of the Euler equations, Legendre and canonical transformations, Noether's theorem, principle of least action, conservation laws; Variational methods in statics (principles of minimum potential energy and virtual work), extended-Hamilton’s principle, Hamilton-Jacobi differential equation, examples of variational method and its application in continuum mechanics, elastodynamics, evolution equations and boundary conditions for one- and two-dimensional structural members (bars, beams, membranes); Variational problems with fixed and movable boundaries, broken extremals, Weierstrass-Erdmann condition and general jump conditions (examples from continuum mechanics); Deﬁnition of the second variation and the strong/weak minimizer, necessary and suﬃcient condition for a minimum, Legendre and Jacobi conditions, Weierstrass E-function, field of extremals; Minimum characterization of eigenvalue problems, Sturm-Liouville problems, Rayleigh quotient for vibration of continuum, buckling and stability analysis of elastic columns; Direct methods of solving variational problems, Ritz method and the method of finite differences</p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Probabilistic Machine Learning </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Probabilistic Machine Learning [ES 661]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Probability refresher: Probability theory, discrete distributions, continuous distributions, joint probability distributions, sampling from different distributions (e.g. using Box-Muller transform), uncertainty modelling, information theoretic concepts: (KL-divergence, entropy); Bayesian concept learning: Likelihood, Prior, Posterior, Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE), Maximum A-Posteriori estimation (MAP), Full Bayesian Estimation with Conjugate Priors (Beta-Bernoulli model for the coin toss example, Normal-Normal model for estimating parameters of Normal distribution); Bayesian supervised methods: Regression: Bayesian linear regression, Robust linear regression via alternative likelihood (e.g. Laplace, Student-T); Classification: Bayesian Logistic regression, Bayesian Naive Bayes; Latent Variable modelling: Gaussian Mixture Model, Probabilistic principal component analysis (PPCA); Approximate Inference: Sampling based strategies: Rejection sampling, Importance sampling, Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), Metropolis Hastings, Gibbs sampling, No U-Turn sampler (NUTS); Variational inference: Mean field approach, Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO), Reparameterization trick, Stochastic Variational Inference, Automatic Differentiation Variational Inference (ADVI); Gaussian Processes (GP): Multivariate Normal distribution and its properties, Kernels, GP regression, GP classification, Approximate Inference and Sparse GPs; Bayesian Optimization (BO) and Active Learning (AL): AL - Query by committee, Uncertainty sampling, Expected model change, BO - Acquisition functions, GP based BO, Random Forests based BO; Probabilistic Deep Learning: MC Dropout, Deep Ensembles, Bayesian neural networks (BNNs), Deep GPs.</p>                                                                </p>

                                                                   <p>Overview of flexible electronics their Materials Systems: Background and history of flexible electronics, trends, emerging technologies, applications, research trends; Mechanics of active material and substrate: Stress and strain analysis, failure modes, mechanics of stretching, mechanical characterization; Materials for flexible devices- synthesis and processing: Organics and inorganics materials – polymeric semiconductors, silicone based material, transition metal oxides, paper-based material, textile based material, other materials; Patterning processes: Ink-jet printing, screen printing, gravure, flexographic printing, imprint lithography, spray pyrolysis and 3D printing. Design rule and considerations. Critical mechanisms involved in printing, practical examples, and solutions to challenges of formulating conductive ink; Flexible and Stretchable Sensors and Actuators: Design strategy and functional material, challenges and system integration. Flexible and stretchable temperature sensors, UV sensors, strain sensors, pressure sensors, gas sensor, Actuation principle, characteristic of an actuator, electroactive polymer and actuator; Optically, thermally, shape memory, pneumatic driven actuation; Wearable devices as human-machine interface: Sensor-actuator- microcontroller integration, design issues and consideration, Body interface, activity monitoring, speech – gesture –motion – emotion recognition.</p>                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p><strong>Design of smart renewable energy systems:</strong><br>
Centralized and distributed energy systems; Fundamentals of renewable energy system (RES) with storage: Photovoltaic (PV) cell characteristics, series and parallel interconnections, insolation based PV sizing; Grid connected wind energy conversion system: selection of generator; Battery backup design; RES penetration in power system and challenges; Role of power electronics in RES: electrical and thermal design of power converter for RES; Smart renewable energy system (SRES): application of artificial intelligence (AI) and internet of things (IoT) in RES.</p>
<p><strong>Data-driven modelling of SRES:</strong><br>
Physics-based system understanding; Data collection approach using IoT for RES including PV, wind and battery: choice of sensor, parameters, range, resolution and sampling rate; Use of physics-inspired artificial intelligence (PIAI) models for RES; Introduction to state-space modelling for average modelling of power electronic converters to interface RES.</p>
<p><strong>Optimization of SRES:</strong><br>
  Design of smart algorithms: real-time data-driven reward-based learning techniques; Local and global optimization techniques: maximum power point tracking; Scheduling of PV, wind, battery and grid power; Application specific optimization case studies: EV battery management system (BMS) and sustainable microgrid operation using RES; Life-cycle costing: initial cost, cost of energy, net present cost and cost-benefit analysis.<br>
</p>                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>

																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Mathematics for Deep Learning - Calculus, Matrices, and Probability. Classification and Regression Problems. </p>
<p>  Basics of Deep Learning: Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), Universal Approximation Theorem, Deep MLP and Backpropagation Algorithm. Implementation - Training, Validation, and Testing using MLP. Optimisation Techniques for Deep Networks - Gradient Descent, Stochastic Gradient Descent(SGD), Momentum, Adaptive Gradient (Adagrad), Adaptive Momentum (AdaM), Root Mean Square Propagation (RMSProp). Regularisation Techniques for Deep Networks - L1/L2, Data Augmentation, Dropout, DropBlock, Batch Normalization. </p>
<p>  Convolutional Neural Networks: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Selected CNN Architectures and their Applications - LENet-5, VGG-16, AlexNet, GoogLENet, InceptionNet, ResNet, and related CNNs. Recent Advances.</p>
<p>  Generative AI: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN), Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) Networks and variants. Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN), Selected GAN architectures and their Applications. Diffusion Models and their Applications. Variational Autoencoders. Attention Mechanism and Transformers with Applications. Normalising Flows. Recent Advances.</p>
<p>  Advanced Deep Learning Tools and Techniques: Design and Implementation - 3D CNN, Transfer Learning, Domain Adaptation, Neural Tangent Kernel. Deep Learning on Graphs - Embeddings, Graph Neural Networks, and their Applications. Self-Supervised Learning. Uncertainty Quantification in Deep Learning. Tips and Tricks to enhance the Performance of Deep Neural Network implementations. Recent Advances.</p>
<p>  Applications:  Research domains - Sustainability, Computer Vision, Natural Language Processing, Web Mining, Signal Processing, Bioinformatics, Climate Modeling, and Others.</p>                                                                </p>

							<hr>

Core, RAN and air interface, protocol stack and layers. 5G physical layer, air interface and RAN: Millimeter wave propagation characteristics, Modulation and demodulation. multiplexing and multiple access. OFDM, OFDMA. Multiple antenna techniques, Air interface protocol stack, frequency and time domain structures, numerologies. radio interface and access protocol. <br>
5G virtualization:  Software defined networks,  Architecture and Principles: Control and Data Planes, separation of control and data, SDN use cases. Network virtualization, NFV, service management and orchestration, Multi-access Edge computing in 5G and B5G networks: MEC Architecture, Mobility, service continuity, traffic routing. <br>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Advanced Computer Architecture and Systems </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Advanced Computer Architecture and Systems [ES 669]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Computer Architecture and Systems: Overview of computer architecture, historical context and evolution. Performance metrics and measurements, benchmarking techniques, Amdhal&rsquo;s law, Gustafson-Barsis law, Little&rsquo;s law and their implication. Instruction set Architecture, MIPS/RISC-V and CISC. <br>
Parallel Architecture: Instruction Level Parallelism: pipelined processor design, superscalar and out-of-order execution techniques. Dynamic scheduling, register renaming, scoreboarding, Tumasulo&rsquo;s algorithm, speculative execution. Multi-core and many core systems, Data level and thread level parallelism.<br>
Memory Hierarchy: Cache and Memory hierarchy, advanced techniques for cache optimization. Distributed Shared memory, Coherence protocols (MSI, MESI, MOSI). Memory Consistency models, Sequential Consistency and relaxed consistency models. Advanced cache and memory bandwidth control and monitoring using allocation technologies, RDT.<br>
GPU Architecture and Programming: Massively parallel processors, SIMT model, synchronous and asynchronous memory updates, unified virtual addressing and unified memory. Fundamentals of CUDA programming.<br>
Advanced topics on Heterogeneous and Emerging Architecture: Interconnection networks, Network-on-chip, Accelerators: near, in-memory and neuromorphic computing, NPU, DPU and TPUs. Approximate computing, intermittent and energy-harvesting computation.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Matrix Methods for Signal Processing, Data Science, and Machine Learning </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                                               <h1>Matrix Methods for Signal Processing, Data Science, and Machine Learning [ES 670]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Vectors and Matrices. Matrix Factorization: Eigendecomposition, QR, and Singular Value Decomposition (SVD). Subspaces, Rank, and Nearest-Subspace Classification. Norms and Procrustes Problems. </p>
<p>Least Squares Problems. Conditioning and Stability. Linear Least-Squares Regression and Binary Classification. Low-Rank Approximation and Multidimensional Scaling. Special Matrices, Markov Chains, and PageRank. </p>
<p>Optimization Problems. Logistic Regression and Other Supervised Learning Frameworks. </p>
<p>Matrix Completion. Neural Network Models. Random Matrix Theory, Signal + Noise Matrices, and Phase Transitions.</p>
<p>Applications (from): Audio Signal Processing, Image Processing, Computer Vision, Biomedical Engineering, Computer Graphics, Optics, Machine Learning, Deep Learning, and Data Science.</p>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Mechanics of Composite Materials </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Mechanics of Composite Materials [ES 671]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>1.	Review of solid mechanics, foundations of tensor analysis</p>
<p>  2.	Introduction to composites: classification and advantages </p>
<p>  3.	Micro-mechanics of a lamina: Strength of materials approach and brief introduction to elasticity approach</p>
<p>  4.	Macro-mechanics of a lamina<br>
&bull;	3-D constitutive laws for anisotropic, monoclinic, orthotropic, transversely isotropic and isotropic bodies<br>
&bull;	2-D constitutive law for specially orthotropic and generally orthotropic layers made up of unidirectional fiber composite<br>
&bull;	Experimental measurements of lamina / laminate material constants<br>
&bull;	Failure theories of an angle lamina<br>
&bull;	Hygrothermal stresses and strains in a lamina </p>
<p>  5.	Macro-mechanics of a laminate<br>
&bull;	Classical laminate theory <br>
&bull;	Laminate constitutive law for various layups<br>
&bull;	Hygrothermal stresses and strains in a laminate<br>
&bull;	Stretching problems of symmetric laminates<br>
&bull;	Bending problems: Navier-type solutions for symmetric and unsymmetric cross-ply layups and for antisymmetric angle-ply layups.<br>
&bull;	Brief introduction to free vibration and buckling analysis: Navier-type solutions</p>
<p>  6.	Comparison between different failure theories, progressive failure analysis of a composite laminate</p>
<p>  7.	Interlaminar stresses and delamination</p>
<p>  8.	Brief introduction to shear deformation theories</p>                                                                </p>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Semiconductor Packaging [ES 672]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p><strong>Module 1: Introduction to Semiconductor Packaging (8 hours)</strong><br>
  &bull;	Brief overview of ICs highlighting FEOL and BEOL to pad <br>
  &bull;	Scaling guidelines for Front-end (Moore&rsquo;s Law) and Back-end<br>
  &bull;	Anatomy and function of semiconductor packaging<br>
  &bull;	Various types of packages (difference in choice of materials and designs)<br>
&bull;	Surface mount and through hole packages</p>
<p><strong>Module 2: Semiconductor Packaging Manufacturing and Testing (12 hours)</strong><br>
  &bull;	Description of semiconductor assembly and test factory<br>
  &bull;	Learn about the various stages of semiconductor package manufacturing<br>
&bull;	Testing of Electronic Packages (Setup and Operation of Test Equipment, Wafer Level &amp; Component Level Testing Process Flows)</p>
<p><strong>Module 3: Fundamentals of Package design (8 hours)</strong><br>
  &bull;	Package design for required electrical, thermal and mechanical performance<br>
&bull;	Design flows for solving the electrical, thermal and mechanical challenges of a PCB</p>
<p><strong>Module 4: Reliability and Quality (6 hours)</strong><br>
  &bull;	Reliability testing &amp; Qualification &ndash; MST/MSL, TC/TS, HAST, HTSL, Lead Integrity, Solderability, Board level test<br>
  &bull;	Role of Process Control Systems in semiconductor manufacturing<br>
&bull;	Using control charts to monitor process performance</p>
<p><strong>Module 5: Advanced Semiconductor Packaging (6 hours)</strong><br>
  &bull;	Introduction to Pathway for Assembly and Packaging technologies <br>
&bull;	The evolution and impact of packaging on product performance and innovation. </p>                                                                </p>

                                                                   Foundations of NeuroAI: Introduction to Neuroscience for AI Researchers, Introduction to AI for Neuroscientists (Multilayer Perceptron, Gradient Descent, Backpropagation), Biological vs. Artificial Neural Networks; Neural Architectures Inspired by the Brain: Hierarchical and Modular Brain Networks, Cortical Columns and Their Role in AI, Recurrent and Attention-based Models Inspired by the Brain, Spiking Neural Networks and Neuromorphic Computing; Building AI Systems from Brain Principles: Implementing Brain-Inspired Learning Rules (Hebbian, STDP, etc.), Memory and Working Memory in AI Models, Predictive Coding and Energy-Efficient AI, Hands-on: Building a Novel Architecture Using Brain Principles; AI for Neural Data Analysis: EEG, fMRI, and Electrophysiology Data Processing, Machine Learning for Decoding Brain Activity, Functional Connectivity and Network Neuroscience, Generative Models for Neural Data Simulation; Cutting-Edge Research and Applications: AI for mental disorders and neurodegeneration prediction, The Future of Brain-Inspired AI                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Process Data Analytics, Optimization, and Control </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Process Data Analytics, Optimization, and Control [ES 674]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   1. Basics of optimization: variables, constraints, objectives, linear, nonlinear, mixed-integer, convex, nonconvex, formulation and solution using computer solver<br>
2. Basics of process control: proportional, proportional-integral, and proportional-integral-derivative controllers, dynamic &amp; multi-variable process modeling using computer software, and closed-loop simulation under different controllers<br>
3. Data analytics: outlier removal, dimensionality reduction, principle component analysis, feature selection, k-means clustering, hierarchical clustering, support vector machines, random forest classification<br>
4. Data-driven modeling and optimization: design of experiments, sampling, response surface model, neural network model, validation, surrogate-based optimization<br>
5.   Model predictive control (MPC): introduction to MPC, data-driven state-space model development, MPC controller development using state-space model, closed loop simulation under MPC.<br>
The course will use datasets familiar to engineers, such as industrial process data, energy &amp; environmental systems data, and semiconductor manufacturing data.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Photonics – Principles and Applications </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Photonics – Principles and Applications [ES 675]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Basic properties of light and classical treatment of light-matter interaction; origin of refractive index, absorption, dispersion; basic optical phenomena - interference, diffraction, birefringence; Generation of light: basic operation of light-emitting diodes and lasers; Lab demonstrations of important temporal and spectral properties of these sources; Controlling the properties of light to convey information; modulation of intensity, phase and polarization; electro-optic modulators, acousto-optic modulators, polarizers; splitters, combiners, multiplexers; Propagation of light through dielectric waveguide structures – conventional and unconventional optical fiber; rectangular waveguide structures; metal-dielectric waveguides; Detection of light: p-i-n photodiodes, avalanche photodiodes; quantum efficiency, responsivity, spectral and temporal response; Applications in engineering and science such as optical fiber communication, optical sensing of physical quantities (displacement, speed, strain, vibration, temperature) and chemicals (high-resolution molecular spectroscopy to identify different gases), plasmonics, biophotonics, imaging, light detection and ranging (LIDAR) as used in distributed sensing and autonomous cars. Lab demonstrations of sensing displacement, vibration, temperature and gas leaks.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Classical Information Theory </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Classical Information Theory [ES 676]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   1. Review of Real Analysis (rigorous and abstract) and probability theory;<br>
2. Information measures: entropy (joint, relative and condition);<br>
3. Asymptotic Equipartition Property (AEP) and typicality;<br>
4. Variable length lossless compression: prefix codes, the Kraft-McMillan inequality and Huffman code;<br>
5. Reliable communication and channel capacity – One shot and Asymptotic;<br>
6. Information measures for continuous random variables;<br>
7. AWGN channel and its capacity;<br>
8. Lossy compression and rate distortion – One shot and Asymptotic;<br>
9. Topics in Network Information Theory (MAC, Broadcast Channel Coding and Slepian Wolf Theorem);<br>
10. Basic Hypothesis Testing and Error Exponents (Time Permitting).                                                                </p>

                                                    </div>

						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="ES">

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Physics Inspired Machine Learning for Engineering Design </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">

																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to physics inspired machine learning and analytics for engineering design; embedding physical principles and constraints into data-driven models; physics-guided feature engineering and dimensional analysis; regression, classification, and clustering with physics priors; surrogate and reduced-order models for efficient design optimization; hybrid modeling combining machine learning architectures with governing equations; physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) and extensions; neural operator learning and applications in PDE. Uncertainty quantification, probabilistic modeling, and physics-informed optimization.  Multi-domain applications overview including EE (power systems, signal processing, communications), ME/CE/CH (thermal-fluid, structural health monitoring, materials discovery), Semiconductor manufacturing and process optimization.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#663399">ES 677</span>
												
						</div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Machine Learning </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Machine Learning [ES 678]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Learning Foundations: Introduction and applications, learning paradigms, bias-variance tradeoff, empirical risk minimization, generalization, overfitting; Supervised Learning: linear and logistic regression, SVMs, decision trees, ensemble methods; Optimization: gradient descent, stochastic gradient descent, convexity, constrained optimization; Unsupervised Learning: PCA, k-
Means, NMF, hierarchical clustering; Deep Learning: MLPs, CNNs, RNNs, autoencoders, autograd; Reinforcement Learning:
conceptual introduction, Q-learning, value/policy intuition; PAC learning, Inductive bias, Deriving loss functions via Maximum Likelihood Estimation, convergence analysis and adaptive optimization, Gaussian mixture models, self-supervised pretraining.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">RFIC Design </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 3 | 5							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>RFIC Design [ES 679]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 3 | 5<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to RF and Transmission Line Theory covering signal behavior, reflection coefficients,  VSWR, and impedance matching using the Smith Chart for lumped and distributed elements; RF Network Representation focusing on S-parameters for circuit characterization and shifting of reference planes; Wireless Architectures and CMOS Integration exploring transceiver blocks, IC design constraints, and the transition from discrete components to Silicon microelectronics; High-Frequency Transistor Modeling and Low Noise Amplifiers (LNA) focusing on f, T, fmax, and parasitic effects to implement inductively degenerated CMOS topologies with optimized noise figures; Mixer Design and Frequency Translation encompassing active Gilbert Cells and passive CMOS switching mixers while addressing conversion gain and port isolation; Oscillators and Frequency Synthesis covering negative resistance theory, LC and Ring Oscillator topologies, phase noise, and the basics of Phase Locked Loops (PLL); Amplifier and CMOS Power Amplifier (PA) Design focusing on stability, Class A-C operations, and efficiency (PAE) for large-signal performance; System Integration addressing transceiver challenges such as IQ imbalance, DC offsets, and the role of integrated filters in signal chains.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

                                                               <h1>Cultures of Communication [FP 601]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 0 | 2 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This course provides an induction for a graduate student to enter into fields of thought, action and
communication with other researchers from divergent disciplines as well as ideologies. Structured like
a foundation, the course will focus on specific themes each year to be handled by a group of faculty
members. Specific themes may include: listening ‘silences’; decoding the visual; the uncertainty
principle; peer group evaluation; conversation across disciplines; philosophical assumptions and
knowledge making; activism as communication; How to ‘read’ arguments in quantitative and qualitative
research.
The course will include activities such as the following: Panel discussions, field visits, invited speakers,
group discussions, self-analysis through diagnostic tests; cross-disciplinary perspectives through
observations studies.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">General Education 1 “Experiencing Hindustani Raga Music” </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>General Education 1 “Experiencing Hindustani Raga Music” [GE 101]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Survey of various fundamental aspects of Hindustani raga music including: (1) a general overview of classical Indian music history; (2) a delineation of basic theoretical concepts relating to raga and tala including arohi, avarohi, raga taxonomy, thāt theory, melakarta system, etc.; (3) a review of different raga music genres (dhrupad, khayal, thumri) along with a survey of influential artists of the twentieth century; and (4) an exploration of Indian aesthetics (rasa theory) and its relation to the idea of transcendence through art.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
