							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">English Studies </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>English Studies [HS 101]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This course is designed to provide students with the necessary tools to improve their practical
use of communicative English. To this end, the course will include the following four patterns of
learning. Listening: accurate, complete and coherent grasp of basic content in lectures as well
as dialogues/conversations; Speaking: development of confidence in the use of English
language for basic spoken expression at the interpersonal level; Reading: culturally familiar
semi-technical and non-technical readings for the purposes of basic comprehension of content,
as well as advanced comprehension of underlying compositional structures, development of
ideas, and modes of assimilation (understanding, retention, analytical/critical engagement);
Writing: vocabulary (scope and correctness/appropriateness), correct grammatical expression
(agreement, tenses, sentence structure), clear and coherent development of ideas in a
composition through an understanding of the process of writing from pre-writing through
revision.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

																 <hr class="short">

																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This course will introduce students to the nuances of English Studies. The following are the broad
objectives of the course: Develop critical, creative, and analytical thinking abilities appropriate for
responding to a variety of rhetorical situations and genres; Foster appreciation and sensitivity for
some of the world’s seminal writings and creative projects; Introduce the concept of using reading
and writing as a process of inquiry; Emphasize interactive and active learning.                                                                </p>

													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/hs.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">French Studies<br><br> </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                </a>

                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Foundational Sanskrit [HS 104]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Verb Basics: Sounds and Technical Terms used for different sets of sounds; Introduction to Verbs and their
classifications; Conjugation of verbs in Present Tense; Indeclinables; Svara (Vowel) and Visarga Sandhi.
Noun Basics: Forming declensions of nouns; Meanings of six Kārakas and cases used for them; Adjective
and Pronouns; Prefixes; Vyañjana (Consonant) Sandhi. Sentence, Tense and Voice: Active and Passive
voice; Imperfect Tense; Participles - Gerund, Infinitive and Past; Declensions of consonant ending nouns and
pronouns; Understanding Verb and Noun forms as the two basic components of a sentence. Functions:
Reading simple prose; Reading simple poetry; Writing short essays; Understanding some most common
Sanskrit chandas (metres); Reading selected verses from the classical Sanskrit literature.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This course introduces non-Gujarati students to the basics of Gujarati Language (largely spoken, to a
lesser extent, written), literature and culture. Starting with learning basic Gujarati language using the
manual A step by step course towards mastering Gujarati, it will attempt to draw some insights into
the psycho-Socio aspects of Gujarati linguistics. The course will also incorporate aspects of Gujarati
culture and ethos as revealed in the literature, grammar, idioms, proverbs and poetry.
<br><br>
The course will also introduce the students to the Basics Gujarati Literature and its thousand year old
history. Through short tour of surrounding tourist spots of cultural, literary and linguistic significance, we
also intend providing exposure to the Gujarati art and expression, beliefs and perceptions and if possible,
taste and cuisine.<br><br>
Through basic exposure of Language, Literature and culture, the course intends taking one closer to the
Gujarati world.                                                                </p>

																	<br>

																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction (How do economists view the world?  Importance of Economics in our daily lives); Individual decision making (Scarcity, choice, MRS, MRT); Strategic interactions (self-interest, social interest, altruism, game theory); Understanding the market (Demand-Supply, Competitive versus Monopoly cases, revenue, profit and cost (sunk/variable/fixed)); Growth and its measures: GDP, per capita income; Development and its measures: inequality, poverty; Money, its meaning, its functions, its forms, Prices in the context of inflation/deflation, The monetary system; Market failures (Externalities - positive and negative), Public goods; Trade (absolute advantage versus comparative advantage), International trade (import duty, export promotion, exchange rate, appreciation, depreciation); Economics of Environment (trade-off between environment and growth, Environmental externalities); Personal Finances (The power of compounding, Investment and instruments of investments); Economics of Innovation and Technology (unemployment, Creative Destruction, AI Economics)                                                                </p>

Grammar; Basic sentences referring to things, demonstrative nouns and ordinal numbers; Polite
Form of Japanese Verbs, Particles and Japanese Clock; motion verbs like to go, come and return
and related particles; Japanese Calendar including dates, months, days of the week etc;
Vocabulary referring to living & non living things; counting things (counters); Continued usage of
adjectives and related particles."                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

<br><br>
Fundamentals of Academic Writing: SMART goals, Elements of academic communication, Evaluating sources, Identifying “fake news”, or misinformation, IEEE citation style and practice.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Comprehensive overview of several important civilizational developments in the history of
the world. The course will focus on how multiple cultures from around the globe have
developed varying scientific, artistic and philosophical modes of knowledge in their pursuit
to understand the human condition, society and the world at large. Civilizations to be
covered in class include, but are not limited to, the Indus, Vedic, Mesopotamian, Egyptian,
Han, Mayan, Aztec, Greek and Roman. Emphasis will be placed on the advancement and
transmission of scientific knowledge, the implications for cross-cultural interactions, and
the plurality of global thought.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Sociology </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Sociology [HS 301]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Sociological perspectives: what is sociology?; answering and asking sociological questions;
“sociological imagination” (C.W Mills); Sociological theories: Emile Durkheim; Karl Marx; Max Weber;
Pierre Bourdieu; McDonaldization of society (George Ritzer); Inequality: social stratification, class and
caste; gender (education, employment and health inequalities with special focus on India); sexual
inequality (social construction of heterosexuality/ heteronormativity); Sociology of Education: Pierre
Bourdieu and cultural capital; sociology of science and technology (SCOT); sociology of professions-
the making of the Indian engineers; women and science/technology education; Culture; Media and
Representation: popular culture; mass media; media representation; cultural imperialism debates; role
of social media Social Movements: Theories of social movements; Social movements in India; ICTs,
social media and social movements; Sociology of Globalization:-what is globalization?; globalization
debates; consequences of globalization; globalization and development (Stiglitz); in defense of
globalization or making globalization work.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Classical Indian Literature in English Translation [HS 308]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Comprehensive overview of several important works of classical Indian literature in Sanskrit,
Prakrit, Pali, Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, Persian and other languages. All texts will be read and
analyzed in English translation with some references to the original texts. Works to be covered
will include excerpts from the following texts, authors and compendiums: Vedas, Upanishads,
Tripitika, Sattasai, Sangam, Puranas, Mahabharata (including the Bhagavad Gita), Ramayana,
Asvaghosha, Kalidasa, Bhavabhuti, Amir Khusrau, Basava and others. We will also explore the
greater history and evolution of literary cultures in India by contextualizing premodern literary
production within a framework of synchronic social, religious and political developments.
Students will also be encouraged to produce their own translations of classical literature.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Anthropology, Citizenship and Human Rights </h4>
							<hr>

                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content

                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Anthropology, Citizenship and Human Rights [HS 327]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The course aims at emphasizing the division between public and academic perceptions and constructions of citizenship and human rights grounded on an anthropological approach. These topics will be illustrated in different societies from the viewpoint of disadvantaged people and social groups such as migrants, refugees, exiles, homeless, dispossessed. India will be taken as a reference and the students will be stimulated to carry out fieldwork at the margins of the social structure. Euro and ethnocentrism will be criticized. Assessment includes class attendance and participation (10%) and the presentation and debate of one topic of the syllabus (30%).The final paper consists on a report of a fieldwork observation (60%).                                                                </p>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Politics [HS 414]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   State and institutions: Exploring the nature of the modern state; comparison between Parliamentary and
Presidential forms of government (India, UK and US); the judicial system (special focus on the role of the
Supreme Court in defining the “Basic Structure” of the Indian Constitution); Election Commission. Political
<br><br>

formations and representation: party politics: The ‘Congress system’ and multi-party rule in India; rise of
the Hindu nationalist BJP; caste reservations in India and (race related) affirmative action politics in South
Africa and United States. Social movements and civil society: role of civil society in deepening or
threatening democracy (Latin America and India); NGOs; women’s movements and farmer’s movements.
<br><br>

Identity and ethnic politics: meaning and history of secularism (unique features of Indian secularism
compared to western concept of secularism) and communalism in Indian politics (ethnic and religious conflict;
politics of multiculturalism (India, France and Canada). Political economy: neoliberalism and liberalization of
Indian economy; politics and redistribution (critical focus on Employment Guarantee and right to work
policies); the remaking of cities in the era of globalization (India, China and USA), rise of middle class politics.                                                                </p>

                                                    </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Global Political Economy </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Global Political Economy [HS 417]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Economics and politics have always been inextricably interlinked. Kingdoms and nation states have
survived and thrived on their ability to forge viable economies. Starting from Antoine de
Montchrétien’s coining of the term, économie politique has defined world history in modern times.
Developments such as industrialization, colonization, division of labor, development of infrastructure
and evolution of institutions have transformed the world beyond recognition. In an era of
globalization, political economy assumes more importance to understand the past, present and
future.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Linguistics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Linguistics [HS 421]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Study of sounds in language: Vocal articulation; International Phonetic Alphabet;
Distinct sound units in language, rule-based Phonology<br><br>
Study of meaning in language: Rule-based morphology, Computational Morphology,
Semantic roles<br><br>
Sentence structure: Syntactic trees; Functional syntactic approaches<br><br>
Historical Linguistics: Language families, with emphasis on India language map,
Comparative Method, Language evolution<br><br>
Language and cognition: Language and the brain, language and metaphor<br><br>
Socio-linguistics: Language and Class, caste, and gender; Code-switching and code-
mixing; Professional language<br><br>
Doing field-work in linguistics: Linguistic ethnography; Language documentation;
Ethics of fieldwork                                                                </p>

                                                </div>

                            </div>

                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="HS">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/hs.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Classics of World Literature, Politics and Economics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Classics of World Literature, Politics and Economics [HS 422]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   In India, few understand the basis of the world we live in. Indian culture is deeply rooted not only in its
ancient past but also influences from the Arab World, Iran, Europe and, now the US. Yet we rarely
understand the fundamental ideas that underpin our society such as Montesquieu’s idea of separation of
powers that emerged from the Enlightenment, the age of Voltaire. Similarly, few delve deep into what
lies beneath the idea of the zero that is a product of the uniquely speculative genius of India. This will
be a course that will introduce the great minds from literature and philosophy in the context of the
society and politics of their time.
<br><br>
This is a writing heavy class in which students will spend all their time reading original texts and
penning essays on them. The topics that will be covered during the course are as follows:<br><br>
1. Historical context of the texts<br>
2. Cultural beliefs underpinning the classics<br>
3. Classics defining culture thereafter<br>
4. Philosophical assumptions of the various authors<br>
5. Economic circumstances in which these works were created<br>
6. Critical examination of the texts<br>
7. Great books as bedrock of collective imagination                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#2F4F4F">HS 422</span>
						
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="HS">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/hs.png" /></i>
						
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Writing Lab </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>1 | 0 | 1.5 | 2							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Writing Lab [HS 423]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1 | 0 | 1.5 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Critical thinking; intelligent reading; effective note taking; organizing thoughts; drawing up outlines writing practice; review and editing<br><br>

Critical Thinking<br>
Critical thinking means thinking for oneself and asking pertinent questions. Before penning anything,
writers could begin by asking themselves three simple questions: Why am I writing? Who will read
me? What impact do I want to have on my readers? Apart from questioning, critical thinking involves
exploring multiple perspectives and engaging with divergent points of view. It involves understanding
context, logic and argument. Only after deep, clear and hard thinking can writers pen something
worthwhile. Therefore, the first session of the workshop is an initiation into critical thinking.
<br><br>
Intelligent Reading and Effective Note Taking<br>
Reading involves not only deciphering the words on the page or screen, but also understanding what
the writer is trying to say. This includes a feeling for the rhythm of the language that is inextricably

interwoven with the writer’s ideas. Intelligent readers learn to pose a single question at every
paragraph and page: What is the point that the writer is trying to make? Obviously, we comprehend
not only speakers but also writers better when we make notes. There are a plethora of techniques to
choose from, but jotting down relevant information concisely, precisely and clearly is the essence of
good note taking. Therefore, the second session is a deep dive into techniques of reading and note
taking.
<br><br>
Organizing Thoughts, Drawing up Outline and Writing Clearly<br>
Following the note-taking exercises, participants choose issues, themes and topics to write on. This
session involves deciding the subject matter of the writing exercise, developing ideas and then
drawing up an outline. Participants will learn to develop clarity about the intent behind their writing,
to know their audience, and to identify what they wish to communicate to relevant readers. This
necessitates drawing up an outline, developing pertinent arguments and adducing key facts. In brief,
participants in the third session will draw up a de facto communication strategy to attain clarity of
purpose in their writing.
<br><br>

Writing Exercise<br>
Once participants have validated their communication strategies with the facilitators, they will attack
their writing exercise. This involves understanding the anatomy of writing and its components,
including logic, drama, rhythm, rhetoric and structure. In the fourth session, participants will produce
a first draft, fleshing out their skeletal outline.
<br><br>
Proofreading, Citing Properly and Revision<br>
Nearly all first drafts are imperfect. They require reading, rereading, corrections and clarifications.
Participants will learn that good writing includes proofreading and even rewriting. Critically
evaluating one’s own work is both a science and an art. At its essence, it involves an appreciation of
conveyed meaning. It also includes the ability to spot the divergence between intended and actual
meaning. Along with proofreading, participants will focus on the importance of citing their sources.
That is essential not only for getting the facts right, but also for developing credibility with readers.
Publications judge writers on the quality of their sources and accuracy with which they cite them.
This fifth and final session will demonstrate to participants that revising their initial drafts is like
polishing diamonds. It is the final stage of a process that produces a gem.
<br><br>

Writing Exercises followed by Reviews<br>
Finally, principles have to be applied and the proof of the pudding is in the eating. So, students will
have one writing exercise after another in subsequent sessions followed by detailed reviews. Emails,
cover letters, essays et al will feature one after another in these sessions.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

backgrounds<br><br>
Other texts: Arthashastra, Panchatantra, Tirukural, Subhashitas, Bhakti literature, and their treatment of ethical conflicts<br><br>

Specificities of Indian ethics vis-à-vis ethical systems in other cultures<br><br>

Ethics for a fulfilled life.                                                                </p>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Archaeology [HS 425]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Overview and Introduction<br>
&bull; Why study or practice Archaeology?<br>
&bull; Defining the discipline: its nature, scope and key concepts<br>
&bull; Its space among other disciplines (Anthropology, History, Prehistory) and in the contemporary world<br><br>

Theoretical development<br>
&bull; Antiquarianism to Culture-Historical approach<br>
&bull; Paradigms of Processual and Post-Processual Archaeology<br>
&bull; Controversies, current and future trends<br>
&bull; Archaeology in India<br><br>

Archaeological methods through case studies<br>
&bull; Pre-excavation strategy, excavation and post- excavation techniques and analyses<br>
&bull; Data and contexts<br>
&bull; Contribution of Sciences (chemistry, physics, geophysics, earth sciences, biological
sciences and palaeoclimatology) in Archaeology<br>
&bull; Contribution of Technology (mapping, remote sensing, viewing/imaging and computer
science) in Archaeology<br><br>

Cultures and Chronologies<br>
&bull; Human origins and Evolution (Africa, Europe and Asia)<br>
&bull; Culture (Palaeolithic, Mesolithic, Neolithic, Chalcolithic, Iron age, Early Historical, Medieval and Modern periods) vs material culture (artifacts and ecofacts)<br>
&bull; Simple to complex societies to civilizations<br>
&bull; Explaining cultural change/adaptation through case studies<br>
&bull; Cultural heritage (tangible and intangible), cultural resource management<br>
&bull; Laws and legislations<br>
<br>

Branches/Types of Archaeology<br>
&bull; Social archaeology<br>
&bull; Environmental archaeology<br>
&bull; Public archaeology<br>
&bull; Underwater archaeology etc.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Creative Writing in Practice </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>2 | 0 | 2 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>

                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Creative Writing in Practice [HS 426]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 0 | 2 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Topic 1: Types of creative writing: description v/s reflection, narrative, poetry, genre,
medium.<br>
Topic 2: Techniques: plotting a story, character formation and development, style,
dialogue.<br>
Topic 3: Writing Practice and Method: constrained writing, writing games, free-
association, collaborative writing, building a text from a given premise.<br>
Topic 4: Writers on Writing: Interviews on writing habits, processes, writers’ insights.<br><br>

Some of the activities will include:<br><br>
1. Writing descriptions<br>
2. Writing reflective/observational pieces on a color, a scene, a photograph or a painting<br>
3. Collaborative writing where one sentence is given to all and together they write a
story/poem/text<br>
4. Constrained writing where writers would be asked to use a rule such as, write a story
where no character can speak<br>
5. Writing based on free-association<br>
6. Writing based on thought experiment<br>
7. Writing dialogue with a given situation<br>
8. Giving the same premise to everyone and asking them to write different genre pieces
(crime, horror, romance, comedy) with that<br>
9. Writing the same content in different forms and styles                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Performance Studies </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>2 | 0 | 2 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Performance Studies [HS 427]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 0 | 2 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Theme #1: Representation vs. Performance: Distinguish between representation and
performance by engaging with theories of Plato, Richard Schechner, Richard Bauman,
Paulla Ebron, and Michael Taussig. Performance text: Dharamvir Bharati’s Andha Yug.<br><br>

Theme #2: Performativity: Learn about the theoretical paradigm of performativity which
informs and organizes the discipline of performance studies. Theorists include JL Austin,
Judith Butler, Uma Chakravarti, and James Baldwin. Performance text: Mahesh Dattani’s
Dance like a Man.
<br><br>
Theme #3: Ephemerality vs. Endurance: Investigate the ways in which performance is
capable of revealing and restaging ephemeral histories by engaging with the debate
between Peggy Phelan, Jose Munoz, Rebecca Schneider, Joseph Roach, and Diana
Taylor. Also, Dwight Conquergood on performance ethnography. Performance text: Maya
Rao’s Walk.
<br><br><br><br>

Pedagogical strategy emphasizes active learning methods including inquiry-based,
experiential, and embodied modes of engaging students in combination with orienting
lectures. Specific instructional techniques include:<br><br>
1. Orienting lectures followed by two-way Q&amp;A (clarifying questions from students for
the instructor, and assessment questions from instructor to students)<br>
2. Staggered discussions in pairs, followed by small groups, and finally at class-level
(combination of think-pair-share and jigsaw methods of discussion)<br>
3. Seminar discussion at class-level of performance texts and theoretical readings
(preparatory readings pre-class followed by in-class discussion using an inquiry-
based learning style)<br>
4. Workshop productions of performance texts (experiential and embodied mode of
engagement in self-directed groups)                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Pride and Prejudice: A Critical Introduction to Sociology </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit

								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>

                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Pride and Prejudice: A Critical Introduction to Sociology [HS 428]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p><strong>1. What is Sociology?</strong><br>
  &bull; Developing a Sociological Outlook.<br>
  &bull; How can Sociology help us in our lives?<br>
&bull; The development of Sociological thinking.</p>
<p><strong>2. Society and Culture</strong><br>
  &bull; The concept of culture.<br>
  &bull; Types of Societies.<br>
&bull; Socialisation and Social Change.</p>
<p><strong>3. Social Stratification</strong><br>
  &bull; Theories of Class and Stratification.<br>
  &bull; Measuring Class.<br>
  &bull; Social class divisions in Western societies.<br>
  &bull; Social class divisions in Indian society.<br>
  &bull; Gender and Stratification.<br>
&bull; Social Mobility</p>
<p><strong>4. Prejudice based on Race, Ethnicity, Migration</strong><br>
  &bull; Understanding Race and Ethnicity.<br>
  &bull; Prejudice.<br>
  &bull; Ethnic integration and Ethnic Discrimination.<br>
&bull; Globalisation and Global Migration.</p>
<p>  <strong>5. Cities and Urban Spaces</strong><br>
&bull; The development of contemporary cities.<br>
&bull; Theories of urbanism.<br>
&bull; Urbanisation in the developing world.<br>
&bull; Cities and global governance.</p>
<p><strong>6. Mass Media and Communications</strong><br>
  &bull; Newspapers and Television.<br>
  &bull; Theories of the media<br>
  &bull; The internet.<br>
&bull; Globalisation and Media Imperialism.</p>
<p><strong>7. Education </strong><br>
  &bull; Industrialisation and Education.<br>
  &bull; New Communications Technology.<br>
  &bull; Privitisation.<br>
&bull; Schooling and Inequality.</p>
<p><strong>8. Social Interaction and Everyday Life</strong><br>
  &bull; The study of everyday life.<br>
  &bull; Non-verbal communication.<br>
  &bull; Social rules and talk.<br>
&bull; Face, body and speech in interaction.</p>                                                                </p>

                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/hs.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Research Methods in Humanities and Social Sciences </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Research Methods in Humanities and Social Sciences [HS 505]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This is designed as a foundational course for those interested in in research in the Humanities and Social Sciences. It meant to familiarize students with a broad range of methods and analytical tools commonly employed in research in these fields.<br>
The course has been divided in to two broad  components ; Quantitative and qualitative Methods. In Quantitative, method students will be taught how to conduct quantitative studies with focus on the understanding research questions, conceptual models, counter factual causal theory, confounding, mediation, moderation, measurement, scale development study designs and thread to validity of causal inferences.<br>
The quantitative segment the focus will be primarily on Ethnographic and interpretive methods and Historical research and Cultural Analysis. In the first pat ie, Ethnographic and interpretive research students will learn how to conduct interactive and Focus Group Discussions and analyze the data obtained from field surveys. In historical Research and cultural analysis, on the other hand students taught some basic conceptual and methodological issues pertaining to the historical discipline . the primary focus on the themes such as the ways and means of dealing with the object from the past. The manner of conducting archival research, various orders of evidence, the usefulness and method of studying orality and memory and the cultural significances of objects and images as well as the methods of using material for historical enquiry.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Humanism, Anti-humanism, and Posthumanism </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Humanism, Anti-humanism, and Posthumanism [HS 507]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This course focuses on the emergence and legacies of Humanism into what is now called
Posthumanism. We will discuss the origins of humanism, the various forms its takes, and its
contemporary manifestations; various critiques of humanism (Anti-humanism); and new forms of
humanism and ways in which it is being transformed, discarded or renewed (Posthumanism). The
primary focus is on the question of what is the human, and what are its relations to others. To
achieve this we approach a broad range of material concerning the human’s relation to animality,
exceptionalism, knowledge, identity, subjectivity, technology, and gender, and how each of these
relations shift as Humanism, Anti-humanism, and Posthumanism, draw and re-draw the boundaries
between them. <br><br>
Keywords: Animality, Exceptionalism, Gender, Humanness and the Human, Knowledge, Ideology,
Identity, Subjectivity, and Technology.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Indian Knowledge Systems </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Indian Knowledge Systems [HS 508]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Indian knowledge systems, darshana, jñāna and vidyā, their continuous and
cumulative nature; Traditions of argumentation (tarka), text interpretation, commentary and
knowledge transmission; Social organisation of knowledge; Philosophical systems; Traditions of
grammar and language; Systems of aesthetics, Nātyashāstra and the rasa theory; Systems of
economy and polity (arthashāstra); Systems of ethics (nītishāstra) and values; Ayurveda; Systems of
mathematics and astronomy, their nature, methods and accomplishments; Systems of chemistry and
alchemy; Agriculture as an interplay of shāstra and loka paramparā; Systems of architecture
(shilpashāstra) in principle and application; Systems of water harvesting and management; Systems
of military science and martial arts.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Social History of Dissent </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Social History of Dissent [HS 509]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This course will introduce students to conceptual issues concerning protest history or the history of dissent in India and other parts of the world in the eighteenth, nineteenth and twentieth centuries. The purpose of the course is to understand the constitutive factors determining different modes of protest. The course will pick and choose from a wide variety of movements such as the French Revolution, the Revolt of 1857, some of the nationalist movements that took place in Europe during this period, the nationalist movements in India led by the Indian National Congress and the numerous peasant and 'tribal' rebellions of the colonial period in India, The course will also discuss other modes of protest which have been equally and perhaps more effective at times than direct action in resisting subjection and bringing about change. Students will be exposed to conceptual issues such as the socio-political factors determining specific modes of protests, the role of the mob in a movement, the question of 'agency' or autonomy of marginalized groups in interpreting and changing their conditions and so on. The focus of the course may vary from year to year. However the emphasis will be mostly on protests by peasants, forest dwellers, factory workers, migrants and indigenous and colonized peoples.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>

                                        </section>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Perspectives on Indian Civilization </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Perspectives on Indian Civilization [HS 510]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Indus civilization: A description of the first civilization on the Indian subcontinent, its main features, achievements and legacy.<br>
Post-Harappan transition and the Aryan debate: Will explore the transition of the 2 nd millennium BCE, the grey area between India's two urbanizations.<br>
Gangetic civilization: Emerging in the early 1 st millennium BCE, it will lead to India's classical civilization; its treatment here will include several recent findings.<br>
Sacred geography and the making of India: The cultural unity of India was an important contribution of the Gangetic civilization, with the concept of sacred geography playing a major part. Early Indian society: A look at some of the dominant features of early Indian society, with inputs from literary, archaeological and epigraphic sources.<br>
Woman and child in ancient India: Will examine the place of woman and child in early Indian society as far as the sources allow us to reconstruct it.<br>
Knowledge systems and knowledge transmission: India early on attempted to organize and codify all knowledge in well-defined systems; this will explore the architecture of knowledge as well as the principal systems and their interaction.<br>
Main schools of thought and belief systems: Among the above knowledge systems, India's various philosophical and belief systems have pride of place and will be outlined here.<br>
Architecture and sacred geometry in classical India: This will explore the fundamental principles and practices of classical Indian architecture, as one example from the world of art.<br>
India's ecological traditions: A rarely highlighted but culturally important area, with increasing relevance to today's context.<br>
An overview of Science and Technology in ancient India: India's contributions to the world of science and technologies are either under-represented or misrepresented (positively or negatively); this will offer a fair portrayal of the high points in those fields.<br>
India's interface with other civilizations: Civilizations never live in isolation: India richly interacted with the rest of Asia and the Greek and Arab worlds, and later with Europe. This will explore those exchanges as well as the channels and methods of influence.<br>
Ethics and values: India developed its own systems of ethics and values; after studying their basic principles and some of their manifestations in Indian history, case studies will show how they survive to this day in diverse sections of Indian society.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Political Thought </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Political Thought [HS 512]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   State and the social contract: Understanding the modern state and various theories about it; human nature
and state of nature and the relationship between these, social contract theories; Justice: Modern conceptions
of justice, basic tenets of John Rawls’s Theory of Justice, critiques and responses to Rawlsian theory by
Amartya Sen, idea of fairness and justice, rejecting binary understanding of justice in favor of degrees;
Liberalism and Communitarianism: Liberalism and its basic tenets, key ideals of communitarianism;
communitarian critiques of liberalism, politics of Indian communitarianism, specifically Indian criticisms of
liberalism in the communitarian tradition; Multiculturalism and Cosmopolitanism: Essential claims of
multiculturalism in the West; key debates on citizenship in the context of immigrants, counterclaims of
cosmopolitanism, toleration versus accommodation, contemporary cosmopolitanism debates; Civil Society:
How is civil society theorized in the West and in India? What is its relation to democracy? ; Political Economy:
Investigating the relationship between the state and market, theories of wealth creation and distribution,
capitalism and its contradictions, the embedded nature of economics in society and culture; Critical theory
approaches to democracy: Democracy and justice, Democracy and difference, deliberative democracy, and
agonistic democracy. Secularism: meaning and origins of secularism in the West, Indian debates on
secularism.                                                                </p>

							<hr>

                                                               <h1>Perspectives in History [HS 513]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Idea and philosophy of history; Nature and limitations of the historical narrative; Relations between power
and representation and its relevance for history writing; Modernity and its Impact on History writing; The call
for a scientific history: What is ‘scientific history’? How did attempts to discover laws of history affect the
craft of the historian?; History and theory: Can we identify universal patterns in history? What is the role of
theory building in history? How does history compare with the other social sciences?; History from below:
What was wrong with conventional history writing? What is so special about the call to write a ‘history from
below’? ; The Postmodern Turn in History Writing: What is the validity of the historical narrative? How did
the literary turn in the Social Sciences affect the way historians perceive the past?; Nationalism and Its
Impact on History Writing.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Tourist and the leisure class: Modernity and middle class, high and low culture, postindustrial age.
Tourist gaze: construction of tourist experience, ways of seeing. Sight and photography: visual
depictions of destinations. Souvenirs and material culture: consumption: media, branding,
globalization, Performing and staging tourism: regulation, commodification of people and places.
Sustainable and ethical tourism: ecotourism, responsible tourism, social inequality, protecting nature
and culture.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">The Politics of the Environment </h4>
							<hr>

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>The Politics of the Environment [HS 515]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Environment and Society: changing notions of nature/environment, human impacts on environments
and vice versa. Colonialism and environment: mis/use of natural resources and green imperialism.
Conservation and development: contradictory relations with environment, ecological equity and
justice. Human-animal relations: trust and domination, animal icons and animals in urban society.
Environmental movements: local struggles, resource control, social inequity and injustice.
Green capitalism and neoliberal environmentalism: markets and commodification of nature.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Literary Experiment in European Modernism </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Literary Experiment in European Modernism [HS 517]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The notion of experiment; European Modernist literature; Linguistics; Meaning making process; Literary forms; The notion of Truth in Literature; Thought Experiments; Experimental compositional methods; avant-garde; Theory and Practice; Modernist Literary Movements; Technology and Literary Humanities; Mathematical thinking and Literature.                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Philosophy, Cognition and Psychoanalysis [HS 518]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Philosophy of cognition: definition of cognition. epistentolog.<br>
Neuro-psychoanalysis: the combination of neuro-cognitivism and psychoanalysis. the neuronal idea of the unconscious. the unconscious and the non-conscious.<br><br>
Inter-disciplinary traffic among philosophy, psychoanalysis and cognitivism: affect in neuro-psyehology and philosophy, debate between cognitive and psychoanalytic therapies. examples from literature. theatre. the use of neural network to understand socio-political netssorks. cognitixe linguistics •conceptual metaphors and their function in different disciplines.<br>
Cultures of mind and body: definitions of mind and body in philosophy and psychoanalysis, embodied mind and extended mind debates. plasticity in neuro-science and philosophy.                                                                </p>
