							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Linguistic Anthropology </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Linguistic Anthropology [HS 519]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Topic 1) Speech and language communities: <br><br>
 --Difference between “speech” communities comprised of speakers with diverse linguistic repertoires, and ideological projection of ‘language’ community, or affiliation to one language<br><br>
Subtopic A) linguistic differentiation –how diverse speech practices crystallize into languages through ideological and semiotic social processes<br><br>
B) language socialization—how language becomes socialized through both linguistic and cultural activity<br><br>
C) language hegemonies—how certain varieties become to be seen as “prestigious” (language standard), their institutional circulation, and the resistance by certain communities to standardization processes 
<br><br>
Topic 2) Semiotics—examining language as a sign-producing activity integrated with the social, economic, and material world<br><br>
Subtopic A) political economy of language—how language is part of a political economic system, mediated through its sign-functions conceptions of value<br><br>
B) linguistic reflexivity—looking at how the metalinguistic function allows us to reflect on language use and make semiotic connections between language and social practice and ideologies<br><br>
 C) scalar processes –the way language is semiotically scaled from micro to macro- and involved in large-scale processes like globalization<br><br>
D) voice and heteroglossia—the study of language as it indexes certain characters, stereotypes or “voices” [in a Bakhtinian sense) and the interplay of these voices in different genres<br><br>

Topic 3) Conversation analysis: <br><br>
The method used to describe face-to-face interaction<br><br>
A) multi-modal analysis –analysis of conversation beyond the utterance of words but also including gesture, gaze, etc.<br><br>
 B) transcription methods—different ways of transcribing face-to-face interaction<br><br>
C) Face to face interaction—analysis of real time talk and assessing how this sheds light on social, cultural, cognitive aspects of language<br><br>
D) Performance—how to analyze performative uses of language and its implication for linguistic and cultural theory<br><br>

Topic 4) Language and thought<br><br>
The study of the way language may condition thought or vice-versa<br><br>
Subtopic A) Grammatical categories and cognition—Examination of the Sapir-Whorf Hypothesis and its criticisms; looking at experiments that relate how grammatical categories may influence perception of shape, colour, tacticity, etc.<br><br>
B) agency and intentionality—examining how agency and intentionality are grammatically embedded in language and what implication this may have on social construction 
<br><br>
Topic 5) Writing and literacy<br><br>
The study of language in its written mode, including attention to reading, writing, script, and materiality of language<br><br>
 A) Visual language<br><br>
Examination of how the graphic aspects of language may offer different semiotic affordances than spoken language<br><br>
B) Textuality<br><br>
The way in which the idea of “text” is understood cross-culturally, and the intersection of oral and written speech in textual production<br><br>
C) Language and media<br><br>
The mediatization of language and its importance for changing literacy practice
<br><br>
Topic 6) Language death and revitalization<br><br>
Investigation into the social conditions that cause languages to become endangered or die; whether languages should be revitalized or not; community politics around dead or dying langauges; and critical analyses of programmes of language revitalization.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Ancient Indian Architecture </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 1 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Ancient Indian Architecture [HS 520]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 1 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction: Tracing the beginning of architectural activities since prehistoric times, elements of
Harappan Architecture and other contemporary Indian cultures.
<br><br>
Concepts and Architectural Typologies: Textual references (e.g. Brhatsamhita and
Samaranganasutradhara) and structural forms; broad characteristics of northern and southern
styles (nagara, dravida and vesara); other styles like Bhumija, Kalinga and Varata; principles of
composition; alignments and axes in temple forms<br><br>

Beginnings of Architectural forms: Evidences from the historical period onwards, early
elements and forms; archaeological evidences; early architectural features like rock-cut, brick-built, wooden and other structural forms
<br><br>

Rock-cut architectural forms: Tracing the beginnings of rock-cut architecture of Buddhist, Hindu
and Jaina faiths, their evolution and elaboration during the early medieval period; critical analysis
of significant examples of all faiths; the role of monasteries, royal patronages and ordinary
worshippers in shaping the establishments; spread of architectural forms in various parts of India,
influences and regional styles.
<br><br>

Structural temples of the past: Architecture of the ancient temples of Buddhist, Hindu and Jaina
faiths, their evolution and different styles; tracing their essential elements in the rock-cut
architecture and transformation into structural forms; critical analysis of important architectural
styles (nagara, dravida and vesara); the role of masons, sculptors and royal patronage; the
influence of architectural styles in India and abroad, e.g. Kailasa of Ellora and Angkor Vat,
Cambodia.<br><br>

Indo-Islamic Architectural forms: Introduction of a new style of architecture involving arches
and domes in India; architecture of tombs, mosques; analysis of regional styles of Indo-Islamic
architecture like Mughal, Jaunpur, Sharqi, Deccan, Qutb Shahi styles.
<br><br>

Role of architectural heritage in establishing universal cultural values: Whose heritage?
Need for preservation and dissemination; role of UNESCO in establishing norms for World
Heritage Properties, symbols of universal cultural heritage.

<br><br>
Field Trips and onsite Explanations: At the end of the course, field trips to important
architectural centres in Gujarat, e.g. Sun Temple, Modhera and Rani-ki-Vav, Patan (both can be covered in a single day), will be arranged for the students to have onsite discussions and explanations. Apart from these architectural centres, a trip to Dholavira in the Kachchh district is
also planned to expose the students to site management principles and practical approaches;
discussions with the Site Managers at all these places will add further to their knowledge.

<br><br>
The field trips will be mandatory and the students will submit individual detailed report of the field
trip, which will be graded.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="HS">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/hs.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Development Economics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Development Economics [HS 521]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>I. I. INTRODUCTION TO DEVELOPMENT ECONOMICS &amp; SOME BASIC CONCEPTS<br>
  • Nature of poverty across the world. Lives of the poor.<br>
  • Meaning of poverty [GDP, utility, Sen’s Capabilities approach]<br>
  • Meaning of Development, its indicators and determinants.<br>
  II. II. FUNDAMENTALS OF THE EMPIRICAL METHODS OF DEVELOPMENT ECONOMICS – CAUSALITY VERSUS CORRELATION <br>
  • Causation versus correlation. The idea of selection bias and the ‘Counterfactual’.<br>
  • Introduction to the methods of Impact Evaluation: <br>
  o Non-experimental (Before-After and Cross-sectional comparison)<br>
  o Experimental (Randomized Control Trial - RCT)<br>
  o Quasi-experimental (Instrumental Variable, Regression Discontinuity, Difference-in-Differences)<br>
  III. TOPICS IN DEVELOPMENT ECONOMICS:<br>
  1. Household Economics and intra-household decision-making: Considering households as independent economic units, focus will be on their decision-making processes given their resources – labour and capital.<br>
  2. Population growth and Economic Development: After looking at population trends, the focus will be on its relations to economic development and on the policy prescriptions to control population.<br>
  3. Labour and Unemployment: The definitions of employment, unemployment and labour force. Basic theories of formal and informal employment, and the labour markets in developing countries. <br>
  4. Migration: Why do people migrate despite high urban unemployment [Harris-Todaro model]? What incentivizes migrants to send remittances? Role of policies in guiding migration.<br>
  5. Education: Education as the first component of human capital. Role of human capital in economic development. Demand-side and supply-side determinants of schooling.<br>
  6. Health and Nutrition: Health and nutrition as the second component of human capital. Demand-side and supply-side determinants of health achievements, and the related policies. <br>
  7. Agricultural transformation and rural development: Role of agriculture vis-à-vis industry in economic development [The Lewis Model]. Microeconomics of farmer behaviour and agriculture.<br>
  8. Sustainable development and Environment: Managing resource use while ensuring environmental sustainability. Role of negative externalities (market failures), property rights (private, open-access, CPR), environmental activities as public good. Kuznets Environmental curve, GDP and pollution.</p>
<p>III. IV. POLITICAL ECONOMY OF DEVELOPMENT<br>
  9. Institutions, colonialism and path dependence: Meaning of institutions. New Institutional Economics and market failures. Examples of historical institutions (colonialism), and present institution (sharecropping). <br>
  10. Policymaking, Governance, and the role of the state: Functionalist theory of the state – the social planner that maximizes social welfare. The pluralist theory of the state – special interest groups and electoral outcomes. Double cost of rent-seeking. Democracy and governance.<br>
</p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">History, Concepts and Theories of Development </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>History, Concepts and Theories of Development [HS 522]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p><strong>1. Philosophical Doctrines of Development</strong><br>
  &bull; The European setting of development.<br>
  &bull; The design of development.<br>
  &bull; The Indian reiteration.</p>
<p><strong>2. Theories and strategies of Development</strong><br>
&bull; The nineteenth century theological debate.<br>
&bull; Underdevelopment in a Historical Perspective &ndash; Theory and Critics.<br>
&bull; Post-war &lsquo;underdevelopment theory.&rsquo;<br>
&bull; Theories, strategies and ideologies of development.<br>
&bull; Dualistic and unilinear concepts of development.</p>
<p><strong>3. Industrialisation</strong><br>
&bull; Industry and Empire: From 1750 to the 21st century.<br>
&bull; Global shift in labour and employment: Industrialisation and development. <br>
&bull; The emergence of the industrial labour force in India.</p>
<p><strong>4. Urbanisation</strong><br>
&bull; Urbanisation in developing countries.<br>
&bull; World cities and development.<br>
&bull; Urbanisation in the Third World.</p>
<p><strong>5. Environment</strong><br>
&bull; Climate, environment and development.<br>
&bull; Local Agenda 21 and The Third World.<br>
&bull; Sustainable development in the Indigenous areas of India.</p>
<p><strong>6. Gender and Development</strong><br>
&bull; The birth of gender.<br>
&bull; From equality to empowerment.<br>
&bull; Women and the state.<br>
&bull; Women in the global economy. </p>
<p><strong>7. Why Development? Critical Reflections from Latin America, Africa and Asia </strong><br>
&bull; The crises of development theory and the problem of dependence in Latin America.<br>
&bull; The industrial elite in Latin America.<br>
&bull; The development trajectories of India and South-East Asia.<br>
&bull; How Europe Underdeveloped Africa.<br>
&bull; Land in the Central Province of Africa.<br>
&bull; Development and Tribes in India.<br>
</p>                                                                </p>

&#61623; Descriptive Statistics<br>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Qualitative Research Methods </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Qualitative Research Methods [HS 524]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p><strong>Module 1&ndash; What is Qualitative Social Science Research?</strong><br>
  Introduction, qualitative modes of inquiry, philosophical foundations and perspectives of interpretive qualitative research</p>
<p><strong> Module 2&ndash; Interpretivist Tradition</strong><br>
  Thick description, Participant observation, interpretivism vs positivism of natural science, methodological pluralism. </p>
<p><strong> Module 3&ndash; Interviews and Focus groups</strong><br>
  Formulating Questions, role of interviewer and interviewee, kind of interviews, online interviews, tools used, strengths and weakness of these methods.</p>
<p><strong>  Module 4- Transcription and field notes</strong><br>
  Processing Field notes, coding and memoing, types of coding, how to make field notes, type of field notes, use of technology, including digital media<br>
  <br>
  <strong>Module 4&ndash; Historiography </strong><br>
  Archival research, what are archives, where to find them, how to analyze the records, interpretation of past events, Archives-power dynamics.</p>
<p><strong> Module 5&ndash; Researcher&rsquo;s Positionality</strong><br>
  Self-reflexivity, auto-ethnography, researcher status (with respect to class, caste, gender, discipline, religion), insider-outsider dilemma</p>
<p><strong> Module 6&ndash; Oral history</strong><br>
  Histories of marginalized societies, spoken memories, personal memories, oral literature, folk lore, people&rsquo;s testimonies, historical context of oral traditions, knowledge without written texts</p>
<p><strong> Module 7&ndash; Research Ethics</strong><br>
  Why ethics is crucial in qualitative research, ethical problems, institutional ethics clearance, research and academic integrity.</p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">South Asia: History, Culture and Society </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>South Asia: History, Culture and Society [HS 612]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This course provides a broad survey of fundamental developments in South Asian history, culture
and society. The geographic focus of the class will be the Indian subcontinent and will include the
peoples and histories of India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal and Sri Lanka. Critical ideas relating
to social structures, religious practices, modes of polity and the production of art and literature will
be explored from a historical perspective so as to highlight how these multi-dimensional cultural
practices have both changed and remained constant over time and space. The course will
examine events and ideas from ancient, medieval, early modern, colonial, post-colonial and
contemporary South Asia. Themes of cultural adaptation, rejection, assimilation and
transformation will provide a critical framework to understand how this culturally rich, complex
and diverse region of the world has evolved over many millennia.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Digital Cultures and New Media </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">

                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Digital Cultures and New Media [HS 631]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The core focus of the course is to understand the significance of digital cultures and the role of new media in shaping public life and opinion. The discussion will revolve around creative dissent, strife, and the role of new media in the concerns for ‘peace’ and ‘justice’. The course will be conducted through a series of case-studies, field projects, and seminars.
<br><br>

Some of the modules proposed to be covered in the course are as follows: (a) The story of
the print media (case-studies of The Statesman, The Young India, The Harijan, The
Telegraph); (b) The role of television (case-study of Doordarshan, case-study of exit polls and television ad-campaigns, the national debates, and the media trials); (c) Films of peace and strife (Issues of Censorship; case-studies of Judgment at Nuremberg, Rang De Basanti, Mr. and Mrs. Iyer, Firaaq); (d) Digital media: (Hyper-text and Blogging; the role of blogs and freedom of expression; photo-journalism and documentaries); (e) Social networks and micro- blogging (case-studies of Jan Lokpal movement, creating a tribe called IIT through social networks and blogs)                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">A Multilingual Nation </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                               <h1>A Multilingual Nation [HS 632]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Language and statehood; colonial scholarship on language; linguistic reorganization of states;
Language : hegemony, culture, economics; language and “dialect” debates; the Eighth Schedule,
Linguistic nationalisms in India (Konkani, Bodo, Oriya, Rajasthani, Hindi, Urdu etc); the cultural
politics of English; purity vs hybridity debates (“Hinglish,” “Tamlish” etc) and the role of translation
in a multilingual society.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Scarred Nations : Partition in the Indian subcontinent </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Scarred Nations : Partition in the Indian subcontinent [HS 633]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The ramifications of the 1947 Partition in Bengal, Punjab and Sindh have been far-reaching.
Recent scholarship interrogates the temporal marker of 1947 and suggests the persistence of
fragmentation of memories and identities in postcolonial India. Topics covered include but are not
limited to : Indian nationalism; moments of revivalism; economies of regions; from high-politics to
testimonies; narrating the ‘other’; beyond the Hindu-Muslim binaries; divisions of the word, space,
music, and histories; Partition in the East; displacement and memory in the West; the cinematic
Partition; Partitions of contemporary India.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#2F4F4F">HS 633</span>
						
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="HS">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">

																	<br>

The special emphasis will be on an overlapping and intersecting body of texts written in the two languages. It will engage in critical and analytical thinking, and will examine the different elements that comprise fictional writings, It will also examine the various styles, trends and critical approaches to literature.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Theories of social epidemiology </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>

							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Theories of social epidemiology [HS 636]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This course will introduce students to theoretical perspectives that explain the distribution and determinants of disease in society. The focus will be on the application of these theories to understand the impact of social determinants of health.<br>
History of public health; development of theories of disease distribution; historical and political influences on development of theoretical perspectives; ancient theories of health', traditional epidemiological models', individual-level health behaviour theories and models; social-ecological model; psychosocial theories; social production of disease (political economy of health); ecosocial theory; importance of theory; application of social epidemiological theories to disease distribution; implications for health policy.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Disease, Health & Inequality </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Disease, Health & Inequality [HS 638]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This course draws on medical anthropology, public health and development literature to examine the
relationship between disease, health and inequality. The course will encourage students’ critical engagement
in understanding the embodiment of inequality and the larger historical, social and economic forces that shape
people’s health experiences globally. In particular, the course will begin with a discussion on the links
between science and colonialism and subsequently move on to more contemporary debates on the
inequalities of disease, suffering and infections (e.g. HIV/AIDS, Ebola and cancer), social determinants of
health and illness, organ trafficking and commodification of human bodies, bioethics of global health practices
and a critique of humanitarian aid and market-based medicine in the context of global health. The course will
conclude with an examination of medicalized resistance to power and health as a human right.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->

                                                               <h1>Structures and Hydrology in Ancient India [HS 642]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to emergence of structures in Archaeology: Cave shelters, Making of
temporary structures, Emergence of habitational structure and Emergence of Religious structures;
Emergence and Development of Structural Stupa Architecture: Origin of Stupa Architecture -
Theoretical aspects, Stupa Architecture - Pre-Mauryan and Mauryan periods, North and Central India-
Sanchi, Bharhut, Sanghol, Deccan-Pauni, Amravati, Nagarjunkonda, Gandhar-Taxila, Structural
monasteries and Chaityas; Emergence and Development of Rock-cut Architecture: Theoretical
aspects. Eastern India- Udaygiri, Khandagiri, Western Deccan- Bhaje, Pitalkhora, Kondivate,
Kondane, Bedasa, Karle, Nasik, Kanheri, Junnar, Ajanta and Aurangabad, Eastern Deccan-Guntapalle.
Central India-Bagh, Udayagiri. Western India-Junagrh, Talaja.
Hydrology and hydrologic cycle, Rain gauges, Evaporation, Flood, Water supply, water Conservation,
Water quality, Water harvesting                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>History of India, 1930-1964 [HS 645]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The nature of the organized national movement of the Indian National Congress and other political
organizations; Negotiations between the British government and nationalist leaders and other
interest groups; Growth of the left in the thirties and forties; Nature and scope of the final phase of
the Gandhian mass movement; Growth of communal forces in the country and the consolidation of
regional and cultural identities; Circumstances leading to partition and its myriad consequences;
Intellectual debates concerning the making of a constitution for the new state; Survey of the early
years in the life of the new nation state; The philosophy of political and economic policies pursued
by the government; Issues of pluralism and national integration; Foreign policy initiatives of the
Government of India; Challenges encountered by the new nation state, policies followed and their
long term impact on the people of the country.                                                                </p>

this?; Who is an Author?; What do Indian traditions and Western philosophy have to say about this
pleasure and the threat it might pose to a “moral” society?; What are the social conditions that
produce specific kind of literature? Deconstructive analysis and literature                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Public Policy and its Impact Evaluation </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Public Policy and its Impact Evaluation [HS 649]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   1. Introduction to Public Policy concepts: 
a. Markets and efficiency
b. When and why do markets fail?
c. Alternatives, when markets fail.
d. Role of public policy 
2. Basic concepts for quantitative research
a. Mean, Standard Deviation and correlation
b. Sampling distribution and t-test
c. Regressions: estimation, interpretation of coefficients and standard errors.
3. Statistical Quantitative analytical tool will be used for understanding data, producing descriptive tables and figures, and for running regressions and interpreting them
4. Health/Nutrition policy
a. Health and Nutrition, their measurements, and the role of public policy
b. POLICY: India’s Integrated Child Development Scheme (ICDS)
c. Impact Evaluation of the ICDS, using the following method:
• METHOD: Use of Controls and Fixed Effects in Regressions
5. Employment policy
a. Unemployment, labor markets in developing countries, and the role of public policy
b. POLICY: The Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA)
c. Impact Evaluation of MGNREGA in India, using the following method:
• METHOD: Difference-in-differences 
6. Environmental policy
a. Economics of Environmental Policies. Negative Externalities.
b. POLICY: India’s Environmental Policy
c. Impact Evaluation of the Environmental Clearances in India, using the following method:
• METHOD: Regression Discontinuity Design (RDD)
7. Gender policy
a. Women empowerment, agency and voice.
b. POLICY: Political reservation for women in India
c. Impact Evaluation of the 73rd Amendment to the Indian Constitution reserving one-third of Panchayat seats for women
• METHOD: Instrumental Variable (IV)                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Critical Perspectives in Anthropology </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Critical Perspectives in Anthropology [HS 650]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p><strong>Module 1&ndash;Theory and History of Anthropology</strong><br>
  History of Anthropology in India and Europe; Social Evolutionism; Structural-Functionalism;  Structuralism; Practice Theory; Semiotics</p>
<p><strong>Module 2&ndash;Kinship</strong><br>
  Classical Studies of Kinship and the Family; House societies; Substance exchange; Nature-culture divide; Queer kinship; Kinship and caste; Love and Intimacy; Kinship beyond the human</p>
<p><strong>Module 3&ndash;Exchange and gift economies</strong><br>
  Difference between commodity and gift; social bases of economic exchange; cross-cultural ideas of scarcity, leisure, transaction; kinship and economic systems</p>
<p><strong>Module 4-Anthropology of the State</strong><br>
  State as a social and cultural construction, vertical vs. horizontal state structures; anthropology of bureaucracy,  state policy as everyday practice</p>
<p><strong>Module 4&ndash;Multisensorial Anthropology</strong><br>
  Anthropology and Phenomenology; socio-biological bases of sense perception; sense perception and social hierarchy; multimodal ethnography</p>
<p><strong>Module 5&ndash;Anthropology of Time</strong><br>
  Anthropology and History; temporality as a social process; non-linear history; incorporating oral historiy into ethnographic research; historical events as ongoing cultural practice</p>
<p><strong>Module 6&ndash;Posthuman Ethnography</strong><br>
  Multispecies anthropology; social ecology; social relations between humans and non-humans including animals, plants, and spirits; perspectivalism, communication between humans and non-human others.</p>
<p><strong>Module 7&ndash;Decolonizing Anthropology</strong><br>
  Anthropology as a colonial enterprise, examples of collaborative anthropology and ethnography, indigenous anthropology; anthropological theory from the Global South</p>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Academic Communication: Argumentation and Reasoning </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 2							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Academic Communication: Argumentation and Reasoning [HS 655]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This course is designed for scholars in Social Sciences whose academic work crucially requires reasoning with data or previous literature.
1.	Academic reasoning: Abstract reasoning for academic achievement; Basic structure of logical reasoning; Commonalities and differences between plagiarism and well-thought-out writing.
2.	Message and goal of argumentation: Narrowing down the focus; Thesis statement; Selection by exclusion; Connecting reading to writing; Systematic Literature Research; Reading notes, annotated bibliography, and research log; Critical reading and extrapolating research ideas from reading materials.  
3.	Target audience assessment: Target audience’s current opinion on the topic,  common ground, and point of opposition; Target journals and conferences; Identification of scholars relevant to the topic of students’ choice.
4.	Rhetorics, strategies, and heuristics: Genre-specific argumentations and rhetorics; Logical fallacies; Metacognitive work; Common heuristics and strategies for academic persuasion; Probing, listing, freewriting, and outlining.
5.	Stylistic cues of argumentation: Different effects from keeping an objective perspective versus expressing one’s own voice; Explanation versus argument; Displaying originality while explaining; Structuring claims surrounded by facts, data, and other scholars’ work
6.	Data selection and alignment: Types of evidence; Source analysis and appraisal; Evidence-based writing; Goal-driven breaking down and reformulation of message and argumentation; Meta-analytic approaches from primary sources.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>

                                                               <h1>The Discovery of Mathematics [IN 201]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Early history of mathematics - Origin of numbers, zero, counting, computation, in various civilizations; A
chronological treatment of about 20 mathematical personalities and covering their life stories, highlighting
famous and/or elegant results, contributions to fields of mathematics and their applications (if any);
Famous conjectures and famous paradoxes; Periodic discussions on framing of a problem
mathematically, the need for rigor, and various approaches to proofs                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Engineering and Democracy: An Indian Imperative </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                               <h1>Engineering and Democracy: An Indian Imperative [IN 202]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Facets of Engineering: What is the purpose of engineering?; multifaceted academic discourse on science, technology and engineering; engineering as a captive discourse of market forces; military-industrial complex; the history of the discipline of engineering in the West and India. 
<br>
Paradox of Democracy: What is the purpose of democracy?; requirements for a flourishing democracy; technocrats vs. democrats; the vulnerabilities of democracy. 
<br>
Portrait of a virtuous engineer: common grounds between Democracy and Engineering for technical progress and social justice; human use of engineering and democracy; the challenges of the Indian context.<br> 

Intellectual independence: Reorganization of knowledge in Information Age; opportunity for newly independent countries like India; capturing the intellectual vibrancy of Indian freedom struggle; inspiration from the makers of modern India, orienting engineering for Social Minimum.                                                                </p>

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Ancient Indian Technology [IN 304]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This course will cover a whole range of issues from Archaeology to Ethnography of use of
stone, ceramic and glass in pre-modern India. This course is going to alert students to the
contextual nature of technologies and how different societies respond to different needs
within the environmental, material and cultural constraints. This course will also involve a
number of experimental studies and visit to the surviving traditional industries. History of
Ancient Indian technology; Surviving traditional technologies in India; Raw materials used in
Palaeolithic Technology: Rocks; Palaeolithic Technology: Evolution of stone tools through
time; Stone working: Stone objects (grinding stone, beads etc.); Shell, Bone and Antler
Working; Ceramic Production: Pottery and Terracotta; Glass Making and Working;
Furnaces/Kilns; The carriers of our heritage                                                                </p>

Business: Business models, Company characteristics, R&amp;D Stages, Project Selection, and
Outsourcing.<br><br>
Ethics: Research involving animals and human subjects, Animal cloning, Privacy issues.                                                                </p>

                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="IN">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/in.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Human Evolution </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Human Evolution [IN 402]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   ‘We’, ‘Us’ or ‘Modern Humans’ are a consequence of our evolutionary past, traversing both
biological and cultural interconnected developments. The course aims to inculcate in
students an appreciation and understanding of how we became who we are today and
recognize the current diversity in humankind through factual knowledge. This course will
introduce students to some of the fundamental theories, debates, concepts, methods and
nomenclatures that contribute to the study of human evolution. Students will be able to
reconstruct our bio-cultural history through multidisciplinary and scientific perspectives
including evolutionary biology, palaeoanthropology, prehistoric archaeology, and genetics.<br>
<br>
Topic 1: Brief introduction to subjects, theories, methods and concepts which are
employed in the study of human evolution.<br><br>

&bull; Scope of subjects such as physical anthropology, palaeoanthropology and
prehistoric archaeology.<br>
&bull; History of evolutionary thought – Cuvier, Hutton, Lyell, Linnaeus, Lamarck, Darwin,
Mendel, Huxley. Concepts of adaptation, natural selection, speciation, mutation,
heredity and variation. Micro and macro evolution.<br>
&bull; Linnaean system of classification.<br>
&bull; Geological time scale, prehistoric timeline, importance of context and material
culture in Archaeology, relative and absolute dating techniques and methods.<br>
&bull; Miocene, Pliocene and Pleistocene epoch environmental settings.<br>
&bull; Human osteology and morphometrics, process of fossilization and taphonomy.<br><br>

Topic 2: Assessment of the human fossil record: tracing the major stages of human
evolution during the Miocene- Pliocene – Early Pleistocene epochs (between 23 and 5
million years ago to 1.2 million years ago).<br><br>

&bull; Primate evolution.<br>
&bull; Hominoidea superfamily; divergences between- Hominidae and Hylobatidae
families, Homininae and Ponginae sub-family, Hominini and Gorillini tribe, Hominina
and Panina sub-tribe.<br>
&bull; The earliest hominins- Sahelanthropus, Orrorin and Ardipithecus.<br>
&bull; Genus Australopithecus, Kenyanthropus and Paranthropus.<br>
&bull; Key fossil finds (discovery, prehistoric sites, context and dating); their origins;
description and comparative assessment of their biological, behavioural and
adaptive characteristics.<br><br>

Topic 3: Assessment of the human fossil record: tracing the major stages of evolution of
our own genus during the Pleistocene (2.5 million years ago to 12,000 years before
present) epoch.<br><br>

&bull; The earliest Homo and the earliest archaeological record<br>
&bull; From Homo ergaster to Modern Homo sapiens.<br>
&bull; Recent Homo species discoveries from South Africa and South East Asia<br>
&bull; Key fossil finds (discovery, prehistoric sites, context and dating); their origins;
description and comparative assessment of their biological, behavioural, adaptive
and cultural characteristics.<br><br>

Topic 4: Special lectures on<br><br>

&bull; Out of Africa theory and the extant human diversity (one lecture by Sharmistha
Majumdar)<br>
&bull; Using DNA to trace the most recent common ancestor (one lecture by Umashankar
Singh)<br>
<br>
The course will be taught through lectures based on published and online sources along
with documentary viewing followed by discussions, human osteology laboratory sessions,
museum/field visits and workshops for experimental replication of stone, bone, shell and
ceramic objects.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

                                                               <h1>Independent Research Seminar [IN 792]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit

Institute or outside during that period. Students may be encouraged to choose some of those
topics from outside their field of expertise; Students can seek guidance from a faculty member
in selecting the topic and developing the framework of the presentation.                                                                </p>

                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="MA">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/ma.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Mathematics I </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Mathematics I [MA 101]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of limits, continuity, differentiability; Mean value theorem, Taylor’s Theorem, Maxima
and Minima; Riemann integrals, Fundamental theorem of Calculus, Improper integrals,
applications to area, volume; Convergence of sequences and series, Newton’s method,
Picard’s method; Multi-variable functions, Partial Derivatives, gradient and directional
derivatives, chain rule, maxima and minima, Lagrange multipliers; Double and Triple
integration, Jacobians and change of variables formula; Parametrization of curves and
surfaces, vector fields, Line and surface integrals; Divergence and curl, Theorems of Green,
Gauss, and Stokes.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#808000">MA 101</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="MA">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/ma.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Mathematics II </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4							</h4>

of variation of parameters; Laplace transforms, Shifting theorems, Convolution theorem.                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Calculus of Single Variable and Linear Algebra [MA 103]</h1>

                                                                   <p><strong>Linear Algebra:</strong><br>
Systems of linear equations, matrices and Gauss elimination, determinant of a matrix, Invertible matrices, transpose of a matrix, special matrices (diagonal, triangular, symmetric etc); Vector space, linear independence, basis and dimension, linear transformations, matrix of a linear transformation, change of basis and similarity, rank of a matrix, Rank-Nullity theorem, eigenvalues and eigenvectors, characteristic polynomials, multiplicity, diagonalization; Inner product spaces, Gram- Schmidt process, orthonormal bases, projections and least-squares approximation, Spectral theorem for real symmetric matrices, Singular value decomposition (overview). </p>
<p><strong>Calculus of a Single Variable: </strong><br>
Limits and Continuity - definition and examples, Intermediate Value Property (IVP), Derivative of a function, Differentiation rules, L'H&ocirc;pital's Rule, Chain rule, Implicit differentiation, Local maxima and Local minima, Intermediate Value theorem, Rolle&rsquo;s Theorem and Mean Value Theorem, Boundedness, monotonicity, Functions and their Geometric properties; Convexity, Concavity and curve sketching; Antiderivatives, Definite integrals (definition and examples), Riemann integration, Fundamental theorem of calculus, Area between curves, Techniques of integration - transcendental functions, integration by parts, Improper integrals. </p>
<p><strong>Sequences and Series: </strong><br>
  Convergence of a sequence, Subsequences and Cauchy Sequences, Cauchy Criteria, Infinite series, Convergence of series, Tests for convergence, Sandwich Theorem, Monotone sequence, Comparison test, Ratio test, Root test, Alternating series, Power series, Radius of convergence, Taylor series and Maclaurin series, Taylor&rsquo;s Theorem.<br>
</p>                                                                </p>

																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Groups: Review of groups, subgroups, homomorphisms, finite and discrete groups of
motions, group actions, class equation, Sylow theorems, classification of groups of small
order, the structure theorem for finitely generated abelian groups, generators and
relations, SL(R), SU(2), simplicity of alternating groups and PSL(2), Nilpotent and
Solvable Groups, Normal and Subnormal Series.<br><br>
Rings: Rings, ideals, quotient rings, Euclidean domains, principal ideal domains, unique
factorization domains, primes in Z[i] and Fermat&#39;s 2-square theorem, ideal classes in
imaginary quadratic fields.<br><br>
Representations of Groups: Definitions, Irreducible representations, Unitary
Representations, Characters.                                                                </p>

  functions and properties of the determinant function, linear functionals and

  Diagonalization, Primary decomposition, Cyclic decomposition, Generalized 
Eigenvectors and The Jordan canonical form.</p>

  operators, normal, unitary, and self-adjoint operators, Schur's theorem, spectral theorem for
self-adjoint and normal operators, Symmetric bilinear forms, Sylvester&rsquo;s law of Inertia.</p>                                                                </p>
