							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Applied Linear Algebra, Probability and Statistics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Applied Linear Algebra, Probability and Statistics [MA 601]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <ul>
  <li><strong>Linear  Algebra </strong></li>
</ul>
<p><strong>I-A. Syllabus</strong><br>
  <strong>            </strong><u>Linear  Vector Spaces </u><br>
  Definition,  Basic properties, Examples of vector spaces.<br>
  <u>All  about Matrices I  </u><br>
  Review  of basics (eigenvalue/eigenvectors), Characteristic equation, Geometric and  Algebraic multiplicity of Eigenvalues, Conditions for diagonalizability of a  matrix and examples of non-diagonalizable matrices, Cayley-Hamilton theorem,  Exponentiating a matrix<br>
  <u>All  about Matrices II  </u><br>
  Matrices  as operators on linear vector spaces, Important classes of matrices: Hermitian,  Unitary, and Positive Definite/Semidefinite matrices (Specific Examples of  important classes for engg: Toeplitz, Circulant, Rotation Matrix etc.) Rank and  Nullity.<br>
  <u>Inner  Products  </u><br>
  Definition  of Inner products, orthonormal basis sets, Gram-Schmidt orthonormalization,  Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, Dual spaces (if time permits)<br>
  <u>Numerical  Linear Algebra: Matrix Reduction/Elimination/Decomposition </u><br>
  Gaussian  Elimination, Cholesky Decomposition for positive definite matrices, Least  Squares Approximation, Singular Value Decomposition, Condition Number, Sparse  Matrices.</p>
<ul>
  <li><strong>Probability  and Statistics </strong></li>
</ul>
<p><strong>II-A. Syllabus</strong><br>
  <u>Engineering  method and statistical thinking </u><br>
  Engineering  method and statistical thinking; collecting engineering data; Mechanistic and  Empirical models; Probability and Probability models<br>
  <u>Probability</u><br>
  Sample  space and events; axioms of probability; additional rules; conditional  probability; multiplication and total probability rules; Bayes theorem<br>
  <u>Discrete  and Continuous Random variables and their distributions</u><br>Random  variables, Distribution Functions, Discrete random variables, probability mass  functions (p.m.f.), Continuous random variables, probability density functions  (p.d.f.), Expectations, mean, variance, moments, examples<br>
  <u>Point  estimation of parameters and sampling distributions</u><br>
  Method  of moments, maximum likelihood estimators<br>
  <u>Statistical  Intervals</u><br>
  Confidence  intervals on mean and variance; Guidelines for constructing confidence  intervals. <br>
  <u>Hypothesis  testing</u><br>
  Tests  on Mean, variance and standard deviation of normal distributions, Inference <br>
  procedures  for a single sample<br>
  <u>Linear  Regression:</u><br>
  Empirical  Models, Simple Linear Regression, Least Square Estimators, Confidence intervals  in regression, prediction of new observations<br>
  <u>Design  and analysis of single-factor experiments:</u> <br>
  Analysis  of variance<strong>&nbsp;</strong></p>
<ul>
  <li><strong>Optimization</strong></li>
</ul>
<p><strong>III-A. Syllabus</strong><br>
  <u>Introduction to engineering optimization </u><br>
  Introduction to optimisation problems in engineering, convex sets,  convex functions, and their properties<u> </u><br>
  <u>Multivariate  optimization</u><br>
  No  constraints, equality constraints, and inequality constraints; Kuhn Tucker Conditions<br>
  <u>Linear programming</u> <br>
  Simplex method, basic feasible solution, optimal solution, duality and  complementary slackness, primal-dual simplex algorithm, Linear integer  programming problem<br>
  <u> </u><strong> </strong></p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Applied Vector Calculus and Differential Equations </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Applied Vector Calculus and Differential Equations [MA 602]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <ul>
  <li><strong>Vector  Calculus</strong></li>
</ul>
<p><strong>I-A. Syllabus</strong><br>
  <strong>            </strong><u>Review:  Some Preliminaries  </u><br>
  Vectors,  Vectors in 3-d, Scalar Product, Triple Product, Einstein summation  convention  for manipulation of vectors.<br>
  <u>Differential  Operator </u><br>
  Gradient,  Divergence, and Curl operators and their interpretation<br>
  <u>Integration </u><br>
  Line  Integrals, Surface Integrals, and Volume Integrals, Numerical Integration: Newton-Coates <br>
  <u>Vector Integral Theorems </u> <br>
  Gauss&rsquo;  theorem, Green&rsquo;s Theorem, and Stokes&rsquo; theorem</p>
<ul>
  <li><strong>Integral  Transforms </strong></li>
</ul>
<p><strong>II-A. Syllabus</strong><br>
  <u>Basics  of Fourier series </u><br>
  Expressing  periodic functions as a Fourier series, Operations (Computing and manipulating  Fourier series. Use of shifts, differentiation and integration, examples and  applications<br>
  <u>Delta  functions, impulse response and Convolution</u><br>
  Impulse  or delta function as inputs to DEs, Convolution and its use in Green's formula<br>
  <u>Discrete  Fourier Transform and Fast Fourier Transforms</u><br>
  <u>Laplace  Transforms</u><br>
  Laplace  and inverse Laplace transforms, partial fractions, transfer functions (system  functions), applications</p>
<ul>
  <li><strong>Differential  Equations </strong></li>
</ul>
<p><strong>III-A. Syllabus</strong><br>
  <u>Review:  Linear ODEs with constant coefficients </u><br>
  Geometric  meaning and Direction Fields, Solution techniques for linear ODEs with constant  coefficients<br>
  <u>Linear  ODEs with variable coefficients </u><br>
  Power  series method, Legendre&rsquo;s equation and the Legendre polynomials, Frobenius&rsquo; method,  the Bessel&rsquo;s functions, Solutions using Laplace transforms  <br>
  <u>Systems  of ODEs </u><br>
  Phase  Plane Method, Criteria for Critical Points. Stability, Qualitative Methods for  Nonlinear Systems, Nonhomogeneous Linear Systems of ODEs<br>
  <u>Numerical  solution of ODEs</u><br>
  Euler  method, Runge-Kutta method, Shooting method, and Finite difference method <br>
  <u>Sturm-Liouville  problems</u> <br>
  Eigenvalues  and eigenfunctions with properties  <br>
  <u>Partial  Differential Equations</u><br>
  Solution  of elliptic, parabolic, and hyperbolic PDEs, Green's functions for  nonhomogeneous PDEs<br>
  <u>Numerical  solution of PDEs</u><br>
  Finite  difference methods for PDEs, Elliptic equations, Parabolic PDEs:  Explicit and implicit methods,  Crank-Nicholson method, ADI method, Hyperbolic PDEs: Upwind schemes and Lax  methods <strong></strong></p>                                                                </p>

problems and integral equations.Variational Analysis with Optimization and Control: Bilinear and
semilinear forms, Convex functionals and minimization, Controllability in
finite and infinite dimensional spaces.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The course aims at developing the fundamentals of Differential Topology. The following is an outline of the topics that will be covered:<br>
&#9679;	Manifolds, Partition of Unity, Differentiable manifolds, Orientation of a manifold, Smooth Maps: Immersion and  Submersion, Germs of smooth maps, Tangent vectors and Derivations<br>
&#9679;	Jacobian criterion, Sard's theorem, Tangent Bundle, Vector Fields, Exterior Algebra, Differential Forms, Integration on Manifolds, complexes of groups and their cohomology, de Rham Cohomology<br>
&#9679;	If time permits, we will look into more advanced topics such as de Rham isomorphism theorem.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">

																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   First order quasi-linear equations: method of characteristics, Monge cone, Nonlinear
equations, Cauchy-Kowalewski’s theorem, Higher order equations and characteristics,
Classification of second order equations, Riemann’s method and applications, Wave
equation and D’ Alembert’s method, Method of decent and Duhamel’s principle, Solutions
of equations in bounded domains and uniqueness of solutions.<br><br>
Laplace equations: mean value property, maximum principle, Poisson&#39;s formula, BVPs for
Laplace’s and Poisson’s equations, Green’s functions and properties, Existence theorem
by Perron’s method, Heat equation, Uniqueness of solutions via energy method,
Uniqueness of solutions of IVPs for heat conduction equation, Stability theory, Finite
difference solutions of partial differential equations.                                                                </p>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                               <h1>Stochastic Differential Equations [MA 625]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Overview of measure-theoretic probability; Stochastic processes, filtrations,
stopping times, martingales; Brownian motion, construction and properties,
Kolmogorov&#39;s extension and continuity theorems; Stochastic integrals,
construction and properties, Ito versus Stratonovich, Ito’s formula, Levy&#39;s
characterisation of Brownian motion, Girsanov’s theorem; Stochastic
differential equations, existence and uniqueness of solutions, strong and
weak solutions, Markov property, infinitesimal generator, probabilistic
representation of solutions to certain linear partial differential equations; the
filtering problem, Kalman-Bucy filter.                                                                </p>

																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>1. Normed linear spaces and Banach spaces. Bounded linear operators. Dual of a 
  normed linear space. Hahn-Banach theorem, uniform boundedness principle, open 
  mapping theorem, closed graph theorem. Computing the dual of some well known 
  Banach spaces.</p>
<p>  2. Hilbert spaces, adjoint operators, self-adjoint and normal operators, spectrum, 
  spectral radius, analysis of the spectrum of a compact operator on a Hilbert space, 
  spectral theorem for compact self-adjoint operators on Hilbert spaces.
  (If time permits) Weak and weak-star topologies, Banach-Alaoglu Theorem</p>                                                                </p>

  <li>    Integration on Chains: Algebraic preliminaries, Fields and forms, Geometric 
    preliminaries, The fundamental theorem of Calculus (Stokes&rsquo; theorem). </li>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Measure Theory & Probability </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Measure Theory & Probability [MA 630]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <h4>Basic theory of measure and integration:</h4>
<ul>
  <li>Sigma algebras, measures, basic properties of measures, construction of measures (sketch), examples (Lebesgue measure on the real line).</li>
  <li>Measurable functions and integration. Simple functions, properties of measurable functions, (Lebesgue) integration, limit theorems for integration (Fatou’s Lemma, Monotone Convergence Theorem, Dominated Convergence Theorem), comparison with Riemann integrals.</li>
  <li>Absolute continuity, Radon-Nikodym theorem (statement), functions of bounded variation.</li>
  <li>Convergence of sequences of measurable functions.</li>
  <li>Product measures and the Fubini-Tonelli theorems (statements only).</li>
</ul>

<h4>Basic theory of probability:</h4>
<ul>
  <li>Motivating examples from the discrete setting.</li>
  <li>Probability spaces, events, probability measures, basic properties.</li>
  <li>Random variables. Distributions functions and properties, densities and mass functions, examples.</li>
  <li>Expectation. Mean, variance, moments, moment generating functions and properties.</li>
  <li>Independence and conditioning. Borel-Cantelli Lemmas, conditional expectations and probabilities given sigma algebras, properties.</li>
  <li>Convergence of random variables. Weak law of large numbers, Strong law of large numbers (statement only), Central limit theorem (statement only).</li>
</ul>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Theory of Partitions </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit

							<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Theory of Partitions [MA 633]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Fundamental theorems in q-series and theta functions – q-binomial theorem,
Jacobi triple product identity, Euler’s pentagonal number theorem, Ramanujan’s
1Ψ1 summation formula, Quintuple product identity and its special cases.
<br><br>
Applications of the elementary series-product identities to partitions -
combinatorial techniques in partition theory – through generating functions, and
through bijective proofs, Gaussian polynomials and their properties.
<br><br>
Congruences for p(n) and the associated partition Statistics - congruences
for the partition function p(n) modulo 5, 7, and 11, ranks, cranks – their
properties and their generalizations,
<br><br>
Restricted partition functions - connections with q-series, modular forms and
mock modular forms
<br><br>
Rogers-Ramanujan identities - analytic and combinatorial versions,
applications in various areas.
<br><br>

The Hardy-Ramanujan-Rademacher formula for p(n) – proof and applications
<br><br>Miscellaneous topics in partitions – Macmahon’s partition analysis, plane
partitions, recent advances in partition theory.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Perturbation Methods </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Perturbation Methods [MA 635]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Asymptotic Approximations: Asymptotic expansions; Regular perturbation problems (including the domain perturbation problems, e.g.,  and singular perturbation problems; Asymptotic solution of algebraic, transcendental, and differential equations. <br>
Matched Asymptotic Expansion: Outer and inner solutions, matching, composite expansion; Boundary layers; Interior layers; Corner layers; Partial differential equations – elliptic problem, parabolic boundary layer. <br>
Multiple Scales: Two and three-time scales; Forced motion near resonance; Weakly coupled oscillators; Slowly varying coefficients; Boundary layers; Linear and nonlinear waves and their applications, application to porous media flow. <br>
The Wentzel-Kramers-Brillouin (WKB) theory: Turning points – multiple turning points; Wave propagation and energy methods; Wave propagation and slender-body approximations; Ray methods – WKB expansion, surfaces, and wavefronts, Eikonal equation, transport equation, ray equation; Discrete WKB method, application to potential barrier problems. <br>
Applications: There will be significant emphasis on highlighting applications of these topics in fluid mechanics, electrodynamics, quantum mechanics. Some of these examples can also be discussed at the discretion of the instructor, if time permits.                                                                </p>

																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to manifolds: Locally Euclidean spaces, Topological manifolds, Differential manifolds, differentiable functions and mappings; Tangent spaces: Tangent space, Vector fields, Lie groups, immersions and embeddings, action of Lie groups on Manifolds, One-Parameter and Local One-Parameter Groups Acting on a Manifold,  One-Parameter Subgroups of Lie Groups, the Lie Algebra of Vector Fields on a Manifold, (if time permits, the existence theorem for ordinary differential equations); Differential forms: Differential forms on manifolds, Tensors,  Integration on manifolds, Stokes theorem, Orientation and volume forms; Riemannian Manifolds: Riemannian metrics, Levi-Cevita connection, differentiation on Riemannian manifolds, minimizing properties of Geodesics, Curvature, Gauss-Bonnet theorem, Tangent Bundle and Exponential Mapping; Applications: Computer imaging, Statistics and data analysis, General relativity                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Matrix Lie Groups </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Matrix Lie Groups [MA 638]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Definition of a matrix Lie group and its examples;
Topological properties; connectedness, compactness, and simply connectedness of certain matrix Lie groups;
Lie algebra of a matrix Lie group, exponential mapping, complexification of a Lie algebra;
The Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff formula, the connection between Lie group homomorphism and Lie algebra homomorphism;
Basic representation theory, direct sum and tensor product of representations, Dual representation, Complete reducibility, Hopf algebra of matrix elements of finite dimensional representations;
The representation of the Lie algebra su(2);
Semisimple Lie algebra, Roots and Root spaces, Weyl Group, Root system;
Highest weight Representation of a semisimple Lie algebra, Construction of representations through Verma module;
Classification of simple Lie algebras, a glimpse into Kac-Moody algebras.
Applications of matrix Lie groups, for example, SU(2), SU(3), SO(3), SO(3,1), to physics.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                               <h1>Matrix Analysis [MA 639]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review: Spectral theorem, singular value decomposition, Moore-Penrose inverse, least squares solutions.<br>
Variational principles of spectrum: The Minmax principle for spectrum of Hermitian matrices, Rayleigh&ndash;Ritz theorem, Courant&ndash;Fischer theorem, Weyl&rsquo;s monotonicity theorem, Cauchy interlacing theorem, inertia and congruence, Sylvester&rsquo;s law of inertia.<br>
Matrix norms: Definition and examples of matrix norms, Gelfand formula, location of eigenvalues, Greschogorin theorem. Applications to sensitivity of linear systems.<br>
Positive semidefinite and Positive definite matrices: Properties and characterizations, the Loewner partial order relation, operator monotone and operator convex functions, Hadamard&rsquo;s inequality, Fischer&rsquo;s inequality. Applications to dynamical systems.<br>
Perron&ndash;Frobenius theory: Matrices with positive and non-negative entries. Applications to Page rank algorithm and steady state of a stationary finite Markov chain.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Integral Geometry in Imaging Sciences </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Integral Geometry in Imaging Sciences [MA 640]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to integral transforms: A basic model for tomography,&nbsp;Tomography, Beer&rsquo;s<br>
law, and X-ray tomography.<br>
&#61623; The Radon transform: The Radon transform, Inversion of the Radon transform, the<br>
Central slice theorem, the Radon Inversion Formula, Backprojection, Filtered<br>
Backprojection, an alternate formula for the Radon inverse, the Hilbert transform, mapping<br>
properties of the Hilbert transform,&nbsp;Approximate inverses for the Radon transform, the<br>
range of the Radon transform, data with bounded support, Continuity of the Radon<br>
transform and it&rsquo;s inverse, estimates for the inverse transform, the higher dimensional<br>
Radon transform.<br>
&#61623; X-ray transform: Definition and elementary properties of X-ray transform, Inversion<br>
formulas, the interplay between X-ray transform, Fourier transform, and convolution,<br>
Uniqueness, the ranges, stability estimates.<br>
&#61623; The Doppler transform and its generalizations: Definition and some properties of<br>
Doppler transform, kernel description and injectivity modulo the natural kernel, invertibility,<br>
and discussion of the range of the Doppler transform.<br>
&#61623; Incomplete data and support theorem: Support theorems for the Radon and X-ray<br>
transform. The limited angle problem, the exterior problem, the interior problem, and the<br>
restricted source problem.<br>
&#61623; Current state of the art: A brief survey of generalization of these transforms to tensor<br>
fields and some ongoing research problems.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Calculus in Banach spaces, Inverse and Implicit function theorem.</p>
<p>Topological degree: Brouwer degree and its properties, Applications: the Brouwer fixed point theorem, An analytic definition of the degree, Degree for continuous maps. The Leray-Schauder degree, The Schauder fixed point theorem, Applications of the Leray-Schauder degree, Borsuk&rsquo;s theorem, Genus, Index and applications to differential equations, Schauder and Tychonoff fixed point theorems for nonexpensive and set valued maps.</p>
<p>Bifurcation theory: the Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction method, bifurcation from the simple eigenvalues, Applications to nonlinear eigenvalue problems, Morse&rsquo;s lemma, A perturbation methods, Krasnoselski&rsquo;s bifurcation theorem, Rabinowitz theorem, Applications.</p>
<p>Variational methods: minimization of functionals, Deformations of sublevels, Deformations and compactness, The Palais-Smale condition, Existence of constrained minima, The mountain pass theorem, Linking theorems, Lusternik-Schnirelman theorems, Applications.</p>                                                                </p>

																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Heat equation, Convection-Diffusion Equation, Navier-Stokes equation, classification of PDEs; Basics of finite difference, finite difference approximation of Laplacian in two-dimension, stability and convergence of the finite difference approximation of the Poisson problem, higher-order discretisation; Weak derivatives and distributions, Lebesque spaces and Sobolev spaces, Sobolev spaces with non-integer and negative exponents, Gaussian theorem, Sobolev imbedding theorems; Riesz theorem, Lax-Milgram lemma, weak formulation, Ritz and Galerkin method, finite element spaces, finite element on simplices and quadrilaterals, interpolation in Sobolev spaces, general convergence theorem, error estimates; Mesh handling, numerical integration, sparse matrix storage, assembling of matrices and vectors, inclusion of boundary conditions, solution of algebraic system.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Advanced Numerical Analysis for Partial Differential Equations </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content

                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Advanced Numerical Analysis for Partial Differential Equations [MA 644]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Basics of variational formulation, review of finite element method for elliptic problems; Existence & Uniqueness of saddle point problems, continuous and discrete inf-sup stability condition, examples of finite element spaces violating and satisfying the discrete inf-sup condition, application to Stokes problem; Hyperbolic & Parabolic Problems, notion of stability, time-discretisation using theta-schemes and strong stability preserving Runge-Kutta schemes, space-time FEM, method of lumped mass, FEM formulation for wave equation; Estimator strategies including residual-based, hierarchical, and duality-based, marking strategies, adaptive-mesh refinement; Basic concepts of neurons and neural networks, supervised and unsupervised learning algorithms, deep neural networks with application to differential equations, physics-informed neural networks.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

                                            </div>

																 <hr class="short">

																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Integral extensions: Lying over, going up and going down theorems of Cohen-Seidenberg, Noether normalisation and its application to weak and strong nullstellensatz, Serre’s normality criterion, finiteness of rings of integers; Dedekind domains: Valuation rings, fractional ideals, Dedekind domains, divisor class groups of rings of integers; Completions of local rings: I-adic topologies and completions, inverse limits, graded rings and modules, Artin-Rees Lemma, Krull intersection theorem, flatness of completions; Dimension theory: Dimension of finitely generated ring extensions, dimension inequality, dimension and integral extensions, flat extensions and dimension, Hilbert-Serre theorem, dimension theorem for local rings and graded rings, applications to face rings of simplicial complexes; Cohen-Macaulay local rings: Depth and dimension, depth lemma, Cohen-Macaulay modules and rings, catenary property of Cohen-Macaulay rings, examples of Cohen-Macaulay rings in invariant theory, determinantal rings and face rings of simplicial complexes.                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Statics and Dynamics [ME 206]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 1 | 1.5 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Static equilibrium, free body diagrams; Structures, method of joints, method of
sections; Centroid, area moment of inertia, parallel axis theorem; Kinematics and (Newtonian)
dynamics of a particle and system of particles; Two- and three-dimensional rigid-body kinematics
and dynamics; motion relative to a translating and rotating frame of reference; Work-energy

principle, impulse-momentum principle; angular momentum, gyrodynamics, Euler equations, Euler-
angles; Analytical mechanics, Euler-Lagrange’s equations; Introduction to multi body dynamics

through MSC Adams (or an equivalent multi-body-dynamics software);Measurement techniques,
error analysis, calibration, structured and unstructured experiments                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Fluid Dynamics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 5							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Fluid Dynamics [ME 207]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 5<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction, history, and applications of fluid dynamics: Properties: continuum approximation,
Knudsen number, density, specific gravity, pressure, vapor pressure, compressibility, speed of
sound, viscosity, surface tension; Fluid kinematics: Lagrangian vs Eulerian descriptions, flow patterns
and visualization, linear and volumetric strains, shear strain, and vorticity; Reynolds transport
theorem, integral and differential forms of conservation of mass, momentum (linear and angular), and
energy; Navier-Stokes equations and exact solutions; Bernoulli’s equations, applications and its
relation to conservation of energy; Dimensional analysis and modeling: Dimensional analysis and
similarity, method of repeating variables and the Buckingham Pi theorem, experimental testing and
modeling; Reynolds number and qualitative description of different flow regimes: laminar vs turbulent
flow, creeping flow, inviscid flow, irrotational flow, and boundary layers; Internal flow: Boundary layer
development, laminar and turbulent flow in pipes, major and minor losses, piping networks and pump
rating; External flows: concept of drag and lift, flow separation, friction and pressure drag, drag
coefficients; Introduction to compressible flows: Definition of compressible flow, Mach number and
flow regimes, Quasi-1D isentropic flows, area-velocity relation, flow in nozzles and diffusers, choking,
qualitative description of shock and expansion waves; Solution of flow problems using CFD
softwares.
Laboratory:

The laboratory component will complement the theory component of the
course. The students will learn to do the following measurements and
visualizations: pressure measurement; velocity and flow rate measurement;
flow visualization
The acquired skills and techniques will in turn be used to critically examine and
understand the concepts covered in the theory component of the course                                                                </p>

                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>

                                                               <h1>Vibrations [ME 208]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to vibration theory, applications and relevance. Governing
equations, equilibrium points and linearization about an equilibrium point. Free analysis of
undamped and damped systems with viscous, Coulomb and hysteretic damping. Forced response
due to arbitrary excitations (Duhamel/convolution integral) and time-periodic excitations, modal
analysis, decoupling of EOMs of multi-degree of freedom systems, proportionally and classically
damped systems. Vibration isolation systems, tuned mass absorbers/dampers; Introduction to
vibration testing and sensors                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 1 | 1.5 | 3<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Manufacturing - Historical perspective; Importance of manufacturing; Classification of manufacturing processes; Introduction to Engineering Materials- Fundamentals of the mechanical behavior of materials; Structure and manufacturing properties of metals, polymers, ceramics, glass and composites; Principles of heat treating; annealing, normalizing, hardening and tempering; Casting Processes-Basics of casting; Analysis of melting, pouring and solidification phenomena; Design of pattern, core, feeder, and gating system; Deformation Processes- Mechanics of Forming Processes; Basic concepts of plastic deformation; Hot & cold working; Common bulk deformation processes; Common sheet metal forming processes; Joining Processes- Fundamentals & classification of Joining processes, Principles of solid phase and fusion welding, Principles of solid/liquid state joining (brazing and soldering, adhesive bonding); Subtractive Manufacturing- Mechanism and mechanics chip formation; Tools - geometry, materials, Common machining operations (turning, milling, drilling, etc); Material removal rates and surface finish; Economics of machining; Abrasive machining operations; Additive Manufacturing- Principles of rapid prototyping and digital manufacturing; Layer wise manufacturing, liquid-based processes, powder-based processes, deposition-based processes, economic considerations; Manufacturing of Polymers- Principles of extrusion, injection molding, blow molding, compression, and transfer molding; Non-Traditional Manufacturing Processes- Energy beam (lasers, electron beam, ion beam) based processes, Electric discharge Machining, Electrochemical machining, ultrasonic machining and abrasive jet machining.                                                                </p>

							<hr>

                                                               <h1>Mechanics of Deformable Bodies [ME 321]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Stress at a point, equilibrium equations, 3-D state of stress, principal stresses and maximum shear
stress, stress invariants; Strain at a point, strain-displacement relations in Cartesian coordinates,
compatibility equations; stress-strain relations; Yield criteria: von Mises (maximum distortion energy)
and Tresca (maximum shear stress); Stresses beyond yielding (for elastic perfectly plastic materials)
in axial members, bending and torsion. Un-symmetrical bending, shear flow and shear center;
Symmetric bending of curved bars; Torsion of non-circular shafts; Formulation of elasticity problem,
boundary conditions, plane stress and plane strain problems; 2-D problems in polar cylindrical
coordinates, equilibrium equations and strain-displacement relations, thick cylinders, composite
tubes, rotating discs; Airy’s stress function, solution by polynomials, end effects – St. venant
principle; Stress distribution around a circular hole, stress concentration; Fracture mechanics
concepts; Fatigue failure.                                                                </p>

Grashof condition for Fourbar linkages. Kinematic (Position, Velocity and Acceleration) Analysis and Synthesis of Mechanical Linkages. Cam Follower Mechanisms. CAM Design. Gears and Gear Trains. Belts, Chains and Sprockets. Static and Dynamic Analysis of Mechanisms.

Lab Work: A few structured experiments on four bar linkages, QR mechanism, CAMs and Gears (plus new experiments) and computer modelling in support of the theory and perform group mechanism design projects targeted at Mechanism Design Contests.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Manufacturing Processes and Systems </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Manufacturing Processes and Systems [ME 331]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Fundamentals of Machine Tools: General-purpose, semi-automatic and Automatic machine tools, Set-
ups nd operations such as lathe, drilling, milling, grinding, broaching; Machining processes for
production: Gear cutting (Hobbing and Shaping), Thread cutting; Finishing operations: honing, lapping,
electroplating, painting etc. Metal forming machines and processes. Introduction to Jigs and Fixture
Design. Principles of location and clamping. Basics of Computer Numerical Control Technology.
Computer Integrated Manufacturing Engineering (CIME). Non-conventional Machining Processes:
Electric discharge Machining (EDM), Electrochemical Machining, Laser and Abrasive Flow Machining,
etc.Dimensional Metrology: Limits, Fits and dimensional tolerancing; Design of limit gauges, Taylor’s
principle, Gage tolerancing; Geometrical tolerancing of form, orientation, position, location, run-out; Basic
definitions and measurement principles, MMC/RFS conditions. Comparators and Metrological
Instruments: Principles of optical, pneumatic, electric/electronic instruments; Inspection of gears and
screw threads; Surface finish and its measurement, Coordinate Dimensional metrology. Non-contact
vision based inspection systems. Introduction to assembly engineering.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Industrial Engineering, Work-time study and productivity; Capacity planning; Location and
layout models for factories and warehouses; Manufacturing planning concepts based on forecasting,
push and pull models of planning, and aggregate planning; Basic inventory models; Introduction to
concepts of operations research and optimization; Linear Programming: problem formulation, simplex
method, concept of primal-dual, duality and sensitivity analysis, Interior point methods Network flow
model &amp; methods including transportation and assignment models, min-cost flow, shortest path problem;
Integer programming models, branch and bound method; Introduction to applied probability models for
decision making; Random variables and their distributions; Independence and conditional
probabilities/expectations; Expectations, variances and probabilistic notions of performance.                                                                </p>

Metrology
•	Dimensional Metrology: Limits, Fits and dimensional tolerancing; Taylor’s principle, Gage tolerancing; Geometrical tolerancing of form, orientation, position, location, run out; Basic definitions and measurement principles, MMC/RFS conditions.

																 <hr class="short">

																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Fluid Dynamics; Introduction to basic tensor analysis and the frame-invariant
form of governing transport equations in fluid dynamics (Navier-Stokes equations) and heat
transfer (steady and unsteady heat conduction equations); Classification of partial
differential equations; The finite difference method, discretization schemes, truncation error,
stability and convergence, and boundary conditions; Review of direct and iterative solution
methods to a system of linear algebraic equations; Temporal discretization schemes –
explicit and implicit schemes, treatment of convection and diffusion terms, one-dimensional
and two-dimensional unsteady advection equation and advection-diffusion equation;
Numerical solution of parabolic, elliptical, and hyperbolic governing equations; The finite
volume method for one-dimensional and multi-dimensional domains, boundary conditions
in the finite volume method, and the discretization of flux terms for non-orthogonal meshes;
Numerical Solution of the Navier-Stokes equations (including the SIMPLE Method)                                                                </p>

                                                                   Governing equations of fluid mechanics; Equation of continuity, Momentum balance, stress
and strain tensor, constitutive equations, Flow Equations in Moving Reference Frames.
Lagrangian and Eulerian Formulations. steady and unsteady flows; vorticty vector and
rotation tensor, vorticity equation, two-dimensional flows: stream function. creeping flow,
Inviscid flow Introduction to stability, Kelvin-Helmholtz instability, Squire&#39;s theorem, Orr-
Sommerfeld equation, stability characteristics of simple flows, transition, Turbulent Spots.
Hairpin Vortices.<br><br>
Turbulent Flows. Classical experiments/observations, Kolgomorov’s scale. closure problem,
correlations and spectra. RANS, Reynolds stress. Wall bounded and free shear flows.
vorticity in turbulent flows, jet flows. kinetic energy budget, turbulent kinetic energy
production and cascading, Eddy viscosity, mixing length, turbulent dispersion. Some insight
into current activities for turbulence modeling.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Compressible Flow </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Compressible Flow [ME 635]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of the governing equations of compressible flow and Thermodynamic concepts. Forms of energy equation for
compressible flow. Wave propagation speed in ideal gases. Isentropic flow. Stagnation Flow Properties. Critical Flow
Properties. Compressible frictionless flow in a shock tube and variable area ducts based on one-dimensional Euler
Equations. Normal Shocks and Rankine-Hugoniot conditions. Oblique shock waves. Expansion Waves. Prandtl-Meyer
Function. Shock-Expansion Theory for external compressible flow past bodies. Conical Flows. Internal compressible
flow in constant area and variable area ducts with heat transfer and with friction leading to notions of Rayleigh and Fanno Flows. Combustion Waves. Condensation Shocks. Compressible Potential Flow Theory. Vorticity considerations.
Notions of compressible velocity potential. Development of low-order compressible flow models for high speed flows
based on linearized small-disturbance potential flow theory for subsonic, transonic and supersonic flows. Similarity
Rules. Brief insight into hypersonic flow and rarified gas dynamics concepts. Disturbance behavior in unsteady
compressible flow. Notions of Compressible Boundary Layers. Crocco’s Theorem. Shock wave boundary layer
interaction. High temperature flows. Aerodynamic Heating.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Fracture Mechanics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">

                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Fracture Mechanics [ME 640]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of theory of elasticity, indicial notations, balance equations, thermodynamics;
Fundamentals of fracture mechanics and failure analysis, Griffith theory, solutions to linear
elastic crack problems, stress fields at crack tip, energy release rates, compliance analysis and
relations to stress-intensity factors, energy methods for estimating G and K; Small scale
yielding, crack growth and stability; J-integral; Dugdale-Barenblatt model; Large scale yielding;
Crack propagation; Micromechanics for failure analysis, Eshelby inclusion analysis, self-
consistent methodology; Introduction to modeling mechanisms of ductile and brittle fracture;
Case studies on computational modeling of fracture.                                                                </p>

High-order moments. Probability concepts. Random variables and estimators: Description of averaged equations: Reynolds equations, Closure problem, Eddy viscosity, Reynolds stress equations: Turbulence kinetic energy: Governing equations, Dissipation rate, Production, Transport: Homogeneous turbulence: Relevance, Turbulence decay: Free shear flows: Averaged equations, Free shear layer equations, analysis of ets, wakes: Wall-bounded turbulence: Boundary layers, Sublayers, Pressure gradient effects: Statistical concepts, part II: Autocorrelation, Integral length scale, Taylor microscale: Random processes and homogeneity: Multipoint correlations, Symmetries, Isotropy: Decomposition: Scales of turbulence, Homogeneous and inhomogeneous fields: Equilibrium: Signal processing an Spectral analysis: Analog to digital conversion. Modal decomposition, Window and filters.                                                                </p>

operations; Rank and determinant, Representation of linear systems of equations; Gaussian elimination; Matrix inversion; Vector spaces,<br>
inner product of vectors and Linear dependence, Singular Value Decomposition; Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors: Applications of eigenvalues and eigenvectors; Eigenvalue decomposition; Change of basis and similarity transformations; Diagonalization.<br>
<strong>Ordinary Differential Equations:</strong> Applications of ODEs in ME; First-order differential equations: Methods of solution: separable equations, linear equations, integrating factors, exact solutions; Initial Value Problems, Boundary Value Problems and Set of ODEs; Second and higher-order linear differential equations: Homogeneous and non-homogeneous equations, Complementary and Particular Solutions, Method of undetermined coefficients and variation of parameters; Systems of ODEs: Matrix methods for linear systems, Eigenvalue method for systems of differential equations; Stability and phase plane analysis; Solution by Laplace transforms<br>
<strong>Vector Calculus:</strong> Applications in ME; Vector and scalar fields: Vector-valued functions, Gradient, divergence, and curl, Physical interpretation; Parametric representation of curves, Line integrals of scalar and vector fields; <strong>Surface Integrals:</strong> Parametric representation of surfaces, Surface integrals of scalar and vector fields; Green's Theorem, Stokes' Theorem, Gauss' Divergence Theorem; their<br>

                                                               <h1>Mathematical Tools for Mechanical Engineers-II [ME 649]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Partial Differential Equations (PDEs): Introduction to PDEs; Applications of PDEs in Engineering and Natural Sciences; First Order PDEs – Classification (linear, quasi-linear and non-linear); Solution to 1st order linear and quasi-linear PDEs using method of characteristics; Second order linear PDEs and their classification (Hyperbolic, Parabolic, Elliptic); Canonical Forms of Second order PDEs; Some useful PDEs in Mech. Engg. – Wave Equation, Heat Equation, Laplace and Helmholtz Equation; Introduction to Fourier Series, Eigenfunctions and their orthogonality; Solutions to Second order PDEs using Separation of variables and Eigenfunction Expansions; Initial and Boundary Value Problems in Second Order PDEs; Complex Variables: Complex Numbers, Argand Plane, Polar representation; Functions of complex variables; Limit and continuity of functions of complex variables; Cauchy-Riemann condition; Analytic Functions; Differentiation of functions of complex variables; Harmonic functions; Examples of some elementary analytic complex functions (Exponential, Hyperbolic, trigonometric, etc.); Taylor series representation of complex functions; Laurent Series representation; Convergence of series representation; Poles – definition and their orders; Introduction to complex integration – path integral, line integral and contour integrals; Cauchy Residue Theorem – Proof; Jordan’s Lemma; Cauchy Integral Formula with applications; Introduction to Branch points and Branch cuts; Introduction to Conformal Mapping; Integral Transforms: History and applications of various integral transforms; Review of Fourier Series (Part-I); Fourier Transform and Inverse Transform – definition; Some useful properties of Fourier Transforms (e.g., transforms of derivatives and integrals, translation, scaling, exponentiation, convolutions); Solutions to linear ODEs and PDEs using Fourier Transform – with application of Complex Contour Integrals; Engineering Applications of Fourier Transform (e.g., Data and Image Processing); Introduction to Laplace Transform – definition; Inverse Laplace Transform using Complex Contour Integrals; Useful properties of Laplace Transforms (transforms of derivatives and integrals, translation, scaling, exponentiation, convolutions, etc.); Solutions to linear ODEs and PDEs using Laplace Transform – with application of Complex Integration in selected cases; Brief overview of Hankel and Mellin Transform.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">

statements – Ratio Analysis; Introduction to costing – cost behavior - transfer pricing; Marginal
costing including break even analysis, standard costing and variance analysis and budgetary
control; Introduction to financial engineering; Sources of long- term funds- stocks and bonds
and their valuation; Methods of project evaluation – payback period, accounting rate of return,
net present value, benefit cost ratio, internal rate of return; Risk – Return Analysis : portfolio
theory and capital assets pricing model; Introduction to interest rates, exchange rates and
derivative instruments.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>

                                                               <h1>Principles of Business Management [MS 306]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   What exactly is “business management”? What does a manager do? Can management principles be
applied to non-business situations? Is management an “art’ or a ‘science”? This introductory course aims
to address some of these questions. It provides a broad overview of management and its functions. The
topics covered are as below:<br><br>
Evolution of Business Management as a discipline; Introduction to basic concepts in Management;
Primary functions of management: Planning; Organising/Staffing; Directing/leading; Controlling;
Management in context: Small v/s large organizations, management in different cultural contexts;
introduction to some “popular” management tools/concepts – strategy, core competence, re-engineering,
centralization/decentralization.                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Business Skills for Entrepreneurs [MS 406]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Field: This is an advanced entrepreneurship course for those who have already completed MS403. It is
intended for students to develop an in-depth understanding of how to manage a start-up.<br><br>
It will be taught as a case study based instruction method. After students are exposed to the cases, the
successes and failures will be analyzed in class in the context of the skills being discussed and students would
be required to answer questions as class-work or homework. The cases may be presentations by
entrepreneurs or written cases.<br><br>
In this first half of the semester, case studies will primarily focus on corporate culture, customer development,
team building and negotiations, four essential elements of entrepreneurship. The second half of the semester
will primary focus on idea generation, product management and financial planning &amp; control.<br><br>
The course also allows students to start a new project or continue to develop their concept started in MS403.
In addition to the instructors, visit by successful entrepreneurs, guest lectures and visits to start-ups will be
organized to enhance learning.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Survey of analytical methods for planning and execution of operations. Topics include introduction to Linear
Programming, operations strategy, capacity analysis, forecasting, queuing, inventory planning, supply and
demand planning, total quality management, statistical quality control, and aggregate planning, scheduling and
simulation techniques.<br><br>

service/flexibility.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Financial Considerations in Engineering Decisions </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 2							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Financial Considerations in Engineering Decisions [MS 408]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   This course is designed to train students to develop an in-depth perception of major
considerations in decision making involving large engineering projects. Students will be
presented with tools of finance and decision making and interactively lead through practical
applications. The course concludes with a case study involving a real world example. A primer
will be provided on the technology relevant to the keystone case.<br><br>
Interpretation of Financial Statements: Concise review of corporate financial statements with
practical applications from start-ups to mature corporations. Special emphasis on the
interpretation of financial statements and the application of such interpretations to corporate
decision making with emphasis on technology projects.<br><br>
Applied Decision Making: Introduction to decision making models with interactive exercises on
canonical applications.<br><br>
Case study methodology: methodology review and in-class interactive case study by
example.<br><br>
Review of Relevant Technology: The keystone case involves in its core, a technology
milestone. A primer with examples and illustrations of that technology will be presented so
that the students may perform an informed in-depth case analysis.<br><br>
The Case: Case study involving a major real life technology breakthrough and various
decisions that lead to the accomplishment of the goal will be presented through a case
summary. Students will be assigned different topics of analysis and would be guided through
their independent analyses of the corporate decision making process taking into detailed
consideration, the financial as well as technology aspects. As a bonus, the students will be
given the opportunity to make predictions on alternate outcomes and their potential impact on
the companies as well as society.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->

                                    </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Medical Products Industry:Quality and Regulations </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 2							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
