                                                               <h1>Microstructural Engineering<br><br> [MSE 201]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 3 | 5<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Definition of a phase, thermodynamic criterion for phase stability, equilibrium between phases,
Gibb’s phase rule, introduction to phase diagrams, potential phase diagram (e.g. temperature-
pressure diagram of H 2 O), composition phase diagram and concepts of solidus, liquidus, solvus
curves, tie line, lever rule, Introduction to various types of transformations such as eutectic,
eutectoid etc.; Solidification: homogenous and heterogeneous nucleation, rate of nucleation,
growth, isomorphous phase diagram (e.g. Cu-Ni) and solidification of alloys, Scheil equation,
constitutional and thermal super-cooling, dendritic solidification principles, Eutectic phase
diagrams (Al-Si, Ag-Cu, Fe-C) and eutectic solidification. Introduction to other phase
transformations involving liquid phase such as peritectic and monotectic transformations; Solid-
Solid Phase Transformations: Diffusional phase transformation; classical nucleation theory,
growth, role of interfaces, spinodal decomposition. Partitionless phase transformations;
massive and displacive/martensitic phase transformation; Thermo-mechanical treatments:
Annealing, Normalizing, Quenching treatments, CCT/TTT diagrams, hardening and
introduction to surface treatments, tempering, deformation induced phase transformation.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Transport Phenomena in Materials Engineering </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Transport Phenomena in Materials Engineering [MSE 204]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to transport phenomena<br>
Concepts of momentum transport: Newton&rsquo;s law of viscosity and non-Newtonian fluids. Mathematical framework for Continuity equation, Navier-Stokes equations, Hagen-Poiseullie&rsquo;s equation, Stokes law etc.  Flows: laminar flow, turbulence, flow in porous beds. <br>
Applications of momentum transport in ceramic suspensions and polymer solutions, oil refineries, flow in porous membranes/electrodes. <br>
Concepts of heat transport: Fundamentals of conduction heat transfer, convection heat transfer, radiative heat transfer. Fourier&rsquo;s law, steady and unsteady state conduction laws and governing equations. Heat transfer coefficient in convection. Radiation, Fick&rsquo;s law of diffusion, mass transfer coefficient<br>
Applications of heat transport in materials processing namely heat treatment, solidification, casting, cooling of slabs, crystal growth, heat losses from the furnace, alloy homogenization, segregation, oxidation, reduction, microfluidics, cooling in electronic devices, flow and vacuum production.<br>
Concepts of mass transport: Fundamentals of diffusion; Fick&rsquo;s first law and second law for steady and unsteady diffusion, uphill diffusion and Kirkendal's effect. <br>
Fundamentals of convective mass transfer; free and forced convective mass transfer, Convective mass transfer, rate laws and mass transfer coefficient.<br>
Application of mass transfer in case hardening of metals, doping of semi-conductors, homogenization, oxidation, absorption/desorption of gases in liquid metals.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Atomic-Pair potential and its relation to Elastic modulus, Hooke's law, Intro to Stress as tensor, Strain as a tensor, Tensor calculus and stress tensor in different coordinate system, Mohrs circle, Elastic Stress-strain relation for isotropic material, Elastic modulus as a tensor, Crystal anisotropy, Iso-stress, Iso-strain problems, Plane strain, Plane stress, 
Defects in Materials - Point defects, Dislocations - Structure, Burgers vector, Dislocations- Peierls stress, Stress field of Dislocation, Line energy, Frank's Partial, sessile dislocations, Lommer cottrell Locks, Interactions, locks, Pile-up, 
Strengthening mechanism - Grain Boundaries, twin boundaries, HP strengthening mechanisms, Solute interaction, Yield point drop, Luders band, Portevin-Le Chatelier effect, Dislocation/Taylor hardening, Precipitation hardening, 
Creep - Constant Load, Effect of Stress, temperature, Constitutive equation, Diffusion mechanisms, Dislocation mechanisms, Deformation mechanism Maps, 
Fracture - Stress concentration, Griffith's theory, K1 Fracture toughness, Fatigue - Low cycle, high cycle fatigue, Microstructure                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction, review of crystal lattices and bonding, Reciprocal lattice, Wigner Seitz cells, Brillouin zones.
Metals: Electronic and Thermal Properties: The Drude (free electron) model, The relaxation-time approximation, Ohm’s law, Classical derivation of resistivity, Different methods of measuring resistivity, Classification of crystalline solids based on electrical measurement, Failure of Drude model, Electronic heat capacity, Thermal conductivity, and The Weidemann-Franz law, Hall effect; Sommerfeld model: The introduction of quantum mechanics, Fermi energy, The Fermi-Dirac function, Electronic density of states, The electronic heat capacity, Success and failure of the Sommerfeld model. Concept of phonons and specific heat, Einstein and Debye Models, Debye temperature, Applications.
Schrödinger equation for a free electron (with no potential), Equation for bound electrons in a solid and its relevance to periodic potential, Nearly free electron model, Energy bands, Effective mass, Distinction between metals, semiconductors, and insulators, Applications.
Optical properties, direct and indirect bandgap semiconductors, Applications.
Superconductivity, Type I, and Type II, Meissner effect, Critical current and Critical magnetic field, Applications.                                                                </p>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Material Thermodynamics and Kinetics [MSE 209]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of basic concepts and combined first and second law of thermodynamics; condition
and criterion for equilibrium; single component systems and Clausius-Clapeyron equation;
solution thermodynamics, partial molal properties and chemical potential, Gibbs-Duhem
equation, reference states, Gibbs free-energy vs composition diagrams, composition-
temperature diagrams, tie line, lever rule, Gibbs phase rule; thermodynamics of defects;
Concept of diffusion, self-diffusion, Fick’s first and second law, solutions to diffusion
equation; atomic model for diffusion, Kirkendall effect, Darken’s analysis; concentration
dependent diffusivity and diffusion in multiphase system, diffusion in crystalline solids,
diffusion and crystal imperfections                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Microstructural Engineering </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 1.5 | 4							</h4>

                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Microstructural Engineering [MSE 210]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 1.5 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of structures and defects; Concepts of grain recovery, recrystallization
and grain growth. Differences between crystal, grains, sub-grains. Types of grain boundaries: low
angle and high angle grain boundaries. Methods to measure grain sizes and lattice parameters;
Definition of a phase, thermodynamic criterion for phase stability, equilibrium between phases,

Gibb’s phase rule, introduction to phase diagrams, potential phase diagram (e.g., temperature-
pressure diagram of H2O), composition phase diagrams (ex: iron-carbon) and concepts of solidus,

liquidus, solvus curves, tie line, lever rule, introduction to various types of transformations such as
eutectic, eutectoid, peritectic, peritectoid etc.; Solidification – homogenous and heterogeneous
nucleation, rate of nucleation, growth, isomorphous phase diagram and solidification of alloys,
Scheil equation, constitutional and thermal super-cooling, dendritic solidification principles,
eutectic phase diagrams and eutectic solidification. Introduction to other phase transformations
involving liquid phase such as peritectic and monotectic transformations, concept of undercooling;
Solid-Solid phase transformations – diffusional phase transformation, classical nucleation theory,
critical nucleus size, growth, role of interfaces, displacive/martensitic phase transformation;
Thermo-mechanical treatments – annealing, normalizing, quenching. CCT/TTT diagrams,
hardening and introduction to surface treatments, tempering, deformation induced phase
transformation.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->

																<br>1.5 | 0 | 1.5 | 3<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Theory: Overview of the material characterization techniques and relevance; X-ray-based techniques (interaction of X-ray with matter, Electromagnetic radiation, X-ray production, Bragg&rsquo;s law, order of diffraction, concept of absorption and scattering, elastic and inelastic scattering, form and structure factor, concept of allowed and forbidden reflections, multiplicity factor, Lorenz-polarization factor, temperature factor, fluorescence, and photoelectron generation, principle of operations and applications in XRD, XRF and XPS); Electron-based techniques (Interaction of electron with matter, secondary electron, backscattered electron, SEM, EDX, working principles using TEM, concept of diffraction, dark and bright field imaging, applications); Thermal analysis techniques (concepts of melting, crystallization, glass transition, oxidation, decomposition, effect of heating rate, sample size, environment on the DSC, TGA curves, applications); Surface analysis techniques (overview, surface morphology, roughness, wettability, surface energy, applications). </p>
<p>Lab: X-ray based techniques; Electron-based techniques; Thermal characterization techniques; Surface analysis techniques</p>                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">

																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Definition of corrosion – type of corrosion, economic impact of corrosion, corrosion science and
corrosion engineering; Principles of degradation – environmental factors controlling corrosion,
electrochemical reactions (half-cell, Anodic/Cathodic), atmospheric corrosion, Faraday’s law, Corrosion
rates; Thermodynamic of corrosion – Chemical potential and standard states, Free energy and electrode
potentials, Nernst Equation, Electrochemical cells, metallurgical characteristics important to corrosion,
Galvanic corrosion, Crevice corrosion, Differential concentration cells, Pourbaix diagrams; Kinetics of
Corrosion – Methods to determine corrosion rates, Electrochemical polarization, Electrode Kinetics for
Activation Polarization, Mixed Potential theory; Mechanisms of Corrosion – Crevice and Pitting
corrosion, Mechanically assisted corrosion, high temperature corrosion, Degradation of polymers and
ceramics, Environmental factors affecting corrosion/degradation; Passivity – Electrochemical theory,
properties and characterization of passive layer, passive layer formation for different surfaces (steel,
iron, aluminum, alloys)                                                                </p>

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Mechanical Behaviour of Materials [MSE 303]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 3 | 5<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Plastic deformation of single crystals – Crystal geometry/defect, deformation by slip, critical resolved
shear stress for slip, deformation of single crystals; Dislocation theory – Burgers vector and dislocation
loop, dislocation observation, Dislocations in FCC/HCP/BCC, stress fields and energy of dislocations,
dislocation slide and climb, jogs, dislocation sources; Strengthening mechanisms – Grain boundary
strengthening, yield point phenomena, strain aging, solid solution strengthening, strengthening from
particle/fibers, strengthening due to point defects, martensite strengthening, strain hardening, cold-
working, annealing, recovery/recrystallization; Fracture – Types of fracture, theoretical cohesive
strength, Griffith theory of brittle fracture, fractography, dislocation theories of brittle fracture, ductile
fracture, notch effect, fracture curve; Fracture mechanics – Strain energy release rate, stress intensity
factor, fracture toughness, plane-strain toughness (K Ic ), plasticity corrections, crack opening
displacement, J integral, R Curve; Brittle fracture and impact failure, notched-bar impact test, transition-
temperature curve, fracture analysis, temper embrittlement, environment sensitive fracture; Fatigue of
metals – Stress cycle, S-N curve, cyclic stress-strain curve, low cycle fatigue, strain-life equation, fatigue
crack propagation, stress concentration, surface effects, cumulative fatigue damage and sequence
effect, effect of metallurgical variables, machine design, corrosion fatigue; Creep and stress rupture –
Time dependent mechanical behavior, creep curve, stress-rupture test, creep deformation mechanism,
deformation mechanism maps, activation energy for steady-state creep, superplasticity, fracture at
elevated temperature                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Design, selection and processing of engineering alloys [MSE 306]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">

																	Credit
																	<br>

																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 2<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction: Evolution of engineering materials, typical properties, microstructure control, structure-
property correlation, life cycle analysis, recycling; Basic steps in alloy selection: Translation, screening,
ranking and supporting information, material property charts (Ashby diagrams), including Young’s modulus-
density, strength-density, modulus-strength, fracture toughness-modulus, fracture toughness – strength,
strength-maximum service temperature and thermal conductivity-electrical resistivity relationship, relevance
in design, material index;<br><br>
Ferrous alloys: Classification of carbon, low alloy, stainless and tool steels and cast irons, effect of trace
(minor) elements, thermal processing - isothermal and continuous cooling diagrams and effect of annealing,
normalizing, quenching, martempering, and austempering on microstructure and properties, surface
hardening, HSLA, TRIP and TWIP steels; Nonferrous alloys: Classification of aluminum, magnesium
titanium and copper alloys, effect of trace elements, thermal processing and resulting microstructure, step
and interrupted ageing treatments, nickel and cobalt-based super alloys, recycling; Evolution of metallic
glasses, composites and foams, high entropy alloys.                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Transport Phenomena in Materials Engineering [MSE 311]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to transport phenomena, concepts of momentum, energy and mass transport, Newton’s
law of viscosity, Non-Newtonian fluids, continuity equation, Navier-Stokes equations, laminar flow,
Turbulence, concept of friction factor, fluidized bed, heat conduction, Fourier’s law, steady and
unsteady state conduction, convection, heat transfer coefficient, radiation, Fick’s law of diffusion,
mass transfer coefficient, Applications in macro and micro scale devices and materials processing;
Solidification, casting, crystal growth, heat losses from the furnace, alloy homogenization,
segregation, oxidation, reduction, microfluidics, cooling in electronic devices, flow and vacuum
production.                                                                </p>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

							<br>2 | 1 | 1.5 | 4							</h4>

                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Computational Materials Engineering [MSE 315]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 1 | 1.5 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Introduction: Role of computational techniques; Materials design and selection and computational databases; a simplistic materials model: the Random-walk diffusion model; Ab-initio methods and electronic structure; Molecular dynamics simulations; Monte Carlo methods; Phase Field methods; Continuum Methods: Finite difference and Finite Element Methods; Materials Informatics and data-driven approaches</p>
<p>The course will have a dedicated lab (computational lab) component which will cover hands-on simulations to understand materials structure, design and phenomena at each length and time scale detailed above. Students will also get an experience to run simulations on the HPC facility; compile open-source tools and to use programming to post-process data. </p>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Corrosion and Degradation of Materials </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>2 | 1 | 1.5 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Corrosion and Degradation of Materials [MSE 316]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>2 | 1 | 1.5 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Introduction<br>
  Definition of corrosion; type of corrosion; economic impact of corrosion<br>
  Thermodynamics of corrosion<br>
  Electrochemical reactions (half-cell, Anodic/Cathodic); Free energy and electrode potentials, EMF Series; Nernst Equation; Galvanic Series<br>
  Pourbaix diagrams; Electrochemical cells; <br>
  Kinetics of Corrosion<br>
  Electrochemical polarization; Electrode Kinetics for Activation Polarization; Mixed Potential theory; Examples <br>
  Forms of Corrosion <br>
  Uniform corrosion; Galvanic corrosion; Crevice corrosion; Pitting; Intergranular corrosion; Selective leaching<br>
  Mechanically assisted corrosion &ndash; Erosion corrosion, Stress corrosion cracking, Corrosion fatigue<br>
  Environmentally assisted corrosion- Hydrogen damage; Biologically influenced corrosion; Atmospheric corrosion; Soil corrosion<br>
  Corrosion Environments and Corrosion Control<br>
  Corrosion in acids<br>
  Corrosion control &ndash; Design, Material selection, Coatings and inhibitors, Cathodic/Anodic protection<br>
  Corrosion of Non-Metallic Materials<br>
  Degradation of polymers and ceramics; High temperature corrosion</p>
<p>The course will have a dedicated lab component which will cover the following topics: Electrochemical corrosion analysis &ndash; potentiodynamic polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, Corrosion rate using weight loss, Intergranular corrosion, High temperature corrosion, salt spray testing of coatings.<br>
</p>                                                                </p>

																<br>2 | 1 | 1.5 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Theory: Overview of various materials processing techniques; mechanical processing techniques (rolling, forging, extrusion, forming, joining); Powder processing techniques (powder mixing, compaction, and sintering); chemical processing techniques; electrochemical processing techniques, vacuum processing techniques.</p>
<p>Lab: Milling and sintering; mechanical processing techniques; Powder processing techniques (powder mixing, compaction, and sintering); chemical processing techniques (choice of chemicals, mixing, preparation steps); electrochemical processing techniques, vacuum processing techniques (various semiconductor processing units).</p>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Quantum Materials and Devices </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 3							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Quantum Materials and Devices [MSE 318]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 3<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Overview and Relevance: Relevance of Quantum materials and devices, transition of Classical to Quantum realm, roadmap of IBM, Intel, Microsoft, Google, and various quantum industries to develop commercial quantum devices, NQM objectives, and government initiatives; Introduction to Quantum materials: Crystal structure and symmetry: periodic lattice and defects, Band theory of solids: review of basic concepts in quantum mechanics, Bloch wave, band structure, density of states, and quasiparticles, difference between quantum and classical materials. Introduction to qubits, superposition, and entanglement and various types of qubits: trapped ion, superconducting, photonic, electron spin, Quantum dots, Rydberg (neutral) atom, topological, and nuclear magnetic resonance. Quantum confinement, Van der Waals materials and their optoelectronic properties, Color centers in solids, Quantum dots and single-photon sources and detectors. Transition Metal Dichalcogenides, Topological quantum materials, Topology and Berry phase in solids, Topological insulators, Dirac and Weyl semimetals, on-demand topological band engineering, Correlated quantum materials arising from many-body interactions, Band theory limitation and electron correlation, Metal-insulator transitions, Mott insulator, Mottronics, Magnetism, Light-driven electronic phase transitions; Quantum Devices: Quantum optics, communication, and computation, Quantum effects visible at macroscale, Superconductivity, Josephson junction, superconducting hybrid systems, Topological quantum devices, single electron transistor, single photon emitter and detector photonic devices, Majorana fermion devices, quantum random number generator, Spintronics, photonic integrated circuits, atomic clocks, quantum well lasers, sensors, merger of classical and quantum devices, and brief introduction to the growth and characterization techniques of quantum materials and devices.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#4B0082">MSE 318</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="MSE">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/mse.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Material characterization techniques </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Material characterization techniques [MSE 352]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>1.5 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to material characterization techniques; Structural Characterisation: X-Ray diffraction
(principle, phase identification and quantification), Electron Microscopy (principle, morphology,
crystallite size, elemental detection); Surface characterization: Atomic Force Microscopy (for
determining topography) Contact angle (surface energy, hydrophilicity); Electrical characterization:
Four probe measurement (for measuring sheet resistance), Hall measurement (for measuring sheet
resistance, carrier concentration and mobility); Thermal characterization: Differential Scanning
calorimetry , Differential Thermal Analyzer (to understand phase transition); Mechanical (Dynamic
mechanical analyser for soft materials); Compositional characterization: (energy-dispersive
spectroscopy, Inorganic content evaluation)                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Surface Engineering </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Surface Engineering [MSE 355]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   1. Fundamentals on surface energy, surface tension, polar and apolar materials, viscosity, vapor pressure,
enthalpy of vaporization<br><br>
2. Surface properties: roughness, flatness, topography, chemical composition/surface chemistry, bond
structure<br><br>
3. Introduction to wetting, tribology, capillarity and biofouling<br><br>
4. Types of fluids: pure liquids, emulsions, suspensions, Newtonian &amp; non-Newtonian, biological and
classification of real-life fluids such as blood, paints, ketchup, oil, yogurt etc.<br><br>
5. Theories: Young’s equation, Wenzel theory, Cassie-Baxter theory, Zisman plot, van Oss-Chaudary-
Good theory, Gibbs free energy minimization, van der Waals forces namely Debye, Keesom, London
forces as well as hydrogen bonding<br><br>
6. Surface modifications by chemical ways: dip coating, spraying, sputtering, silanization treatments,
carburizing, nitriding, physical vaporization deposition, chemical vaporization method etc.<br><br>
7. Engineering surface topography: micromachining, e- beam lithography, photolithography, laser ablation,
chemical etching, curing, replication, hot embossing etc.<br><br>
8. Surface characterization techniques: SEM, CA goniometer, AFM, optical profilometer, frictional
measurements-oscillating tribometer, sphere on disc and pin on disc, Nanoindentation<br><br>
9. Bioinspired strategies for tuning surface adhesion: Lotus leaf effect, butterfly surfaces, rose petals,
Nepenthes Pitcher plant, gecko-lizard, underwater oleophobicity by fish scales<br><br>
10. Tribology: Stribeck curve, lubrication, influence of viscosity, load, sliding speed on dry and lubricated
friction and wear of materials. Archard’s wear principle and wear mechanisms<br><br>
11. Applications and emerging challenges                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->

failure, low cycle fatigue, structural features of fatigue, effect of temperature on fatigue, fatigue
crack growth, thermal fatigue and corrosion fatigue, certain practical aspect of fatigue failure,                                                                </p>

energy balances; equipment selection and specifications; Detailed plant flow-
sheet;Siteselection;Economic evaluation of project; environment impact analysis;Planning tools
– CPM &amp;PERT;Case studies                                                                </p>

control rate equation, reaction rate constant, reaction order, non-elementary reactions; Solid State
Diffusion -Fick’s Law, mechanisms of diffusion, uphill diffusion, Kirkendall effect, steady and
transient diffusion; External mass transfer -fluid flow and its relevance to mass transfer, general
mass transport equation, concept of mass transfer coefficient, models of mass transfer -film theory
and Higbie’s penetration theory; Internal mass transfer-ordinary and Knudsen diffusion, mass
transfer with reaction; Adsorption –physical adsorption vs. chemisorption, adsorption isotherms -
Langmuir, BET; Adsorption as the rate limiting step examples - gasification of C by CO2, dissolution
of N2 in molten steel; Porous solids - specific surface area and pore size distribution; Reactor design
-batch vs continuous reactors, ideal stirred tank and plug flow reactors; Mass balance in ideal
reactors, residence time distribution; Models of industrial reactors; Electrochemical kinetics-concept
of polarization, activation over potential, Butler-Volmer and Tafel’s equation, applications in electro-
deposition and corrosion.                                                                </p>

																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 3 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review and relevance of characterization of materials; Structural Characterisation: Optical microscopy
(determination of microstructure and grain size ), Scanning Electron Microscopy (determining
morphology, crystallite size, elemental detection, thickness), Transmission Electron Microscopy
(microstructure, lattice parameter, substrate orientation relationship, thickness); Electron Probe Micro
Analysis, Energy-dispersive spectroscopy and wavelength-dispersive spectroscopy (chemical
analysis); Surface characterization: Atomic Force Microscopy (for determining topography), X-ray
Photoelectron Spectroscopy (chemical characterization), Auger Electron Spectroscopy (chemical
characterization, grain boundary segregation); Electrical characterization: Four probe measurement
(for measuring sheet resistance), Hall measurement (for measuring sheet resistance, carrier
concentration and mobility), Scanning Tunelling Microscopy (to understand local surface electronic
structure); Magnetic properties; Thermal characterization: Differential Scanning calorimetry ,
Differential Thermal analyzer (to understand phase transition), Dilatometer (to measure thermal
expansion coefficient); Mechanical and Thermomechanical characterization                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#4B0082">MSE 632</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="MSE">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Transmission Electron Microscopy </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 3 | 5							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Transmission Electron Microscopy [MSE 633]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 3 | 5<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to TEM: Ray diagrams and review of the basic optics; Modes of operation of
a TEM; Electron Guns; Thermionic source; vs Schottky vs cold-FEG; Gun Brightness and
coherency of the source; Illumination lens system; convergent vs parallel beam
illumination; apertures; Imaging lens system; Lens defects; spherical aberration; chromatic
aberration; diffraction; astigmatism; Resolution of a microscope <br><br>

Scattering: Introduction to coherence vs incoherence; concepts of phase, energy,
amplitude and cross-section; X-ray scattering; Electron scattering; elastic and inelastic
scattering; kinematical and dynamical scattering; Born approximation for electrons; atomic
form factor; comparison with nuclear scattering<br><br>

Inelastic Scattering: Introduction to electron energy loss spectroscopy; Instrumentation;
Plasmon principles; General features of EELS spectra; Core Excitations; Energy filtered
TEM imaging; energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS); electron-material interaction;
instrumentation; artifacts; quantitative EDS and ZAF corrections; Thin film approximation;
energy filters and chemical mapping; Chemical analysis with high spatial resolution
<br><br>

Diffraction: Electron diffraction from a material; wave diffraction from a material;
Reciprocal Lattice; Laue condition, Bragg’s law; structure factor and shape factor
calculations; structure factor rules; Symmetry operations and forbidden diffractions;
Superlattice diffractions; deviation parameter; Ewald sphere construction, Laue Zones;
Tilting specimen vs tilting electron beam; Indexing of diffraction patterns; stereographic
projections; Kikuchi lines and maps; double diffraction and forbidden diffractions;
convergent beam electron diffraction (CBED)
<br><br>

Imaging (diffraction contrast): Contrast in TEM; diffraction from crystal with defects;
extinction distance; phase-amplitude diagram; Thickness fringes; bend contours;
diffraction contrast from strain fields; Dislocation and Burgers vector determination; Weak-
Beam Dark-Field (WBDF) imaging; Fringes at the interface; Moire fringes; Imaging of 1D,
2D and 3D defects; Introduction to Phase contrast (High resolution) imaging; Introduction
to scanning TEM (STEM) mode operation; Various modes of STEM operation including
bright filed (BF), dark filed (DF), annular bright filed (ABF), angular dark filed (ADF) and
 high-angle angular dark filed (HAADF); comparison of diffraction vs phase vs Z-contrast
imaging in TEM                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Semiconductors and Fabrication: Basics of semiconductors and transistors, Moore’s law, an overview of the semiconductor fabrication process: from sand to device, future of chip technology with emphasis on the Angstrom era of semiconductors. Semiconductor fabrication: Front end of line process (FEOL): Oxidation, diffusion and ion implantation, lithography, etching, and thin film deposition. Semiconductor fabrication: Back end of line process (BEOL): Interconnects, chemical mechanical polishing, and packaging of semiconductor chips. Statistical and software tools used in semiconductor fabs: Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts, Process Control Statistical Analysis (PCSA), Klarity wafer map for defects and critical dimensions, Automated Process Control (APC), Structured Query Language (SQL), ScriptHost, LabVIEW, and JMP.                                                                </p>

                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Advanced Alloy Design and Processing [MSE 635]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Alloy Design: Evolution of alloy design. Significance of alloy design in various sectors. Structure-property correlation, material property charts. Factor of safety and material index.<br>
Strengthening and Plasticity Mechanisms in Alloys: Mechanisms of strengthening, Strain transfer mechanisms.<br>
Steels: Fe-C phase diagram; Alloying elements in Fe-Alloys. Low-alloy steels, high-strength low-alloy steels, advanced high-strength steels, maraging steel, stainless steels, TRIP and TWIP steels. <br>
Alloys for High-Temperature Applications: Superalloys (Ni-, Co-, and Fe-based). Strengthening phases in superalloys. Eutectic Alloys and microstructure control in eutectic alloys. Cu alloys for high heat-flux applications.<br>
High Entropy Alloys (HEAs): Stability and properties, &ldquo;Cocktail effect,&rdquo; sluggish diffusion, and lattice distortion effects. Microstructural design and phase stability. Mechanical properties, corrosion resistance, and thermal stability.<br>
Lightweight Alloys: Al and Mg-based alloys-alloying strategies. Processing methods and their influence on properties. Ti-based alloys- Importance in aerospace and biomedical applications.<br>
Alloy Processing and Non-equilibrium Techniques: Advanced processing techniques- directional solidification, rapid solidification, powder metallurgy, additive manufacturing. Non-equilibrium processing and its effects on alloy stability and properties. Examples of advanced alloys developed through novel processing methods.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Marine Oceanography </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Marine Oceanography [MTE 605]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to Oceanography: Basics, ocean basins origin (plate tectonics, seafloor spreading), Nature and scope, history, branches of oceanography, Ocean floor, continental shelf, trenches, abyssal plains, Marine sediments, Coral reefs, seawater composition and characteristics; Characteristics of the Ocean Waves, tsunamis, tides, ocean currents, Storm surges, and cyclones: cause and effects, characteristics, environmental consequences, and management; Phenomenon observed in the Ocean ,Ocean Salinity: Salinity change, causes, distribution, and implications. Ocean temperature: Temperature change, causes, vertical and horizontal distribution, and implications. Marine Environment: Marine climatic Zone, El Niño; Ocean and global environment: Man-Ocean relation, Sea-level change: Definition, causes, past, present, and future trends of S.L. changes, Consequences of S. L. changes, Oceanic regions & Marine resources, Marine pollution.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

                                                               <h1>Physics<br><br> [PH 101]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction of vector analysis and calculus. Grad, div and curl. Line, surface and volume integrals.
Gauss and Stokes’ theorem. Curvilinear co-ordinate system, Dirac delta function. Concept of
electric and magnetic fields. Electrostatic potential, Poisson and Laplace’s equation. Multipole
expansion. Biot-Savart Law, Ampere and Faraday’s law. Electromagnetic induction. Conservation
equation, introduction to Maxwell’s equations. Electromagnetic waves, Waveguides. Stern Gerlach
experiment. Limitations of classical concepts. Kets, bras, and operators, Hilbert space, postulates
of quantum mechanics, measurements and observables, expectation value, Ehrenfest theorems.
Schrodinger equation, position and momentum representation. Wave-function, particle in a box,
potential well, tunneling and it’s applications, periodic potential. Electron band structure in solids.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Dawn of Quantum Mechanics (QM): Black Body Radiation, Photoelectric Effect, Atomic Structure, deBroglie Hypothesis; Fundamental Postulates of QM via The Electron Double Slit Experiment; First Look at the Wavefunction: Schrödinger Equation, Probability, Statistical Interpretation, Momentum, Uncertainty Principle; Time-Independent Schrödinger Equation and One-Dimensional Problems: Particle in a box, Harmonic Oscillator, Free Particle, Finite Square Well; Quantum Tunnelling with examples, Kronig-Penny Model: Particle in a periodic potential; Quantum Mechanics in 3-dimension: Hydrogen Atom and Angular Momentum; Formalism: Hilbert Space, Observables, Eigenfunctions, Statistical Interpretation and  Uncertainty Principle revisited, Dirac Notation; Introduction to Quantum Computing: Brief ideas and perspectives regarding quantum computing, classical gates, single qubit gates, multiple qubit gates.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Solid State Physics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Solid State Physics [PH 203]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Module 1: Introduction to Quantum Mechanics and Thermal Physics. (with the perspective to introduce electronic and thermal properties of materials) Wavefunction, Schrodinger&rsquo;s equation and its solution to simple potentials; Equipartition theorem, Boltzmann distribution; Quantum and classical distribution functions.</p>
<p>Module 2: Electronic properties of materials. Classical models and their limitations; Origin of bands, Fermi energy, Fermi surface, density of states, conductivity; Metals and insulators; Magnetic moment, Exchange energy and Hund&rsquo;s rules; Origin of magnetism, Para- Dia- and Ferro-magnetism.</p>
<p>Module 3: Thermal properties of materials. Vibration of one-dimensional monoatomic and diatomic chain, Group velocity, Normal modes, Phonons (optical and acoustic); Phonon heat capacity, Planck distribution, Debye T3 law, Einstein model; Thermal conductivity of phonon gas.</p>
<p>Module 4: Physics of Semiconductors. Energy bands in semiconductors, band gap; Electrons, Holes, Effective mass; Silicon and Germanium; Conductivity and Mobility of carriers.</p>
<p>Module 5: Introduction to Superconductivity. Electrodynamics of superconductor, London equations, Meissner effect; Type I and type II superconductors; Cooper pairs, energy gap, coherence length; Josephson effect.</p>                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#008080">PH 203</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="PH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/phy.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Quantum Technologies – Applications </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Quantum Technologies – Applications [PH 204]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Overview and Motivation – Introduction to the landscape of quantum technologies, including quantum computing, sensing, communication, and quantum materials; historical development and motivation, with emphasis on identifying the core physical principles that enable quantum technological advantage and how material platforms underpin later developments; Quantum States, Control, and Open Systems – Physical realization of qubits as two-level systems; control and manipulation; density matrix formalism; decoherence, relaxation, and dephasing (T₁ and T₂ processes); introduction to open quantum systems and noise channels, with illustrations from superconducting qubits, trapped ions, and other platforms; Measurement and Quantum Resources – Quantum measurement theory including projective measurement, backaction, and basic ideas of weak measurement; quantum resources such as coherence, superposition, entanglement, and squeezing, and their operational roles across quantum technologies; Quantum Limits and Metrology – Fundamental limits to measurement precision including the standard quantum limit and Heisenberg scaling; role of quantum correlations and squeezing in enhancing sensitivity; applications to atomic clocks, interferometry, and gravimetry; Quantum Information and Noise – Classical and quantum information concepts including Shannon entropy and von Neumann entropy; qualitative understanding of quantum channels, noise, and information flow; implications for communication and computation; Quantum Computation – Principles and Realizations – Quantum gates and circuits; many-body Hilbert space and the origin of computational advantage; overview of algorithms such as Shor’s algorithm; realistic considerations including NISQ devices, noise, and error correction; critical assessment of quantum advantage; Quantum Communication – Information and Implementations – Principles of quantum communication including no-cloning, security, and eavesdropping; basic ideas of quantum key distribution; physical implementations in fibre, free-space, and satellite platforms; Platforms, Materials, and Scalability – Comparative overview of leading hardware platforms including superconducting systems, semiconductor and spin-based systems, atomic and photonic platforms, and defect-based systems; emphasis on how quantum materials and device physics enable control while limiting scalability; Limits, Challenges, and Outlook – Fundamental and practical limitations of quantum technologies including decoherence, scalability, and control; realistic assessment of near-term and long-term prospects across computing, sensing, and communication.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#008080">PH 204</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="PH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Quantum Sensing </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Quantum Sensing [PH 301]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1.5 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Classical sensing and measurement—physical signals, sensors, data acquisition, and noise sources (thermal, shot, and 1/f), along with signal processing techniques including filtering and lock-in detection, parameter estimation, Fisher information and the Cramér–Rao bound; accompanied by laboratory sessions using Arduino/Raspberry Pi platforms for sensor interfacing, noise characterization, signal detection, and estimation; Quantum theory recap—quantum postulates, projective measurements, positive operator-valued measures (POVMs), quantum noise, measurement  backaction, weak and continuous measurements, and quantum non-demolition measurements; Limits to precision — standard quantum limit, its origin in the trade-off between measurement imprecision and backaction, and illustrative examples from interferometry (Ramsey protocol) and position measurements (Optomechanics); Quantum estimation theory—quantum Fisher information, quantum Cramér–Rao bound, and optimal measurement strategies; Quantum resources for sensing— coherent, Fock, and squeezed states, entanglement-enhanced sensing and Heisenberg scaling; Platforms for quantum sensing—atomic systems, interferometric techniques, ultracold atoms, cavity QED systems, solid-state platforms such as NV centers, and quantum detection methods.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->

                                                               <h1>Molecular and Crystal Physics [PH 404]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction: an overview of fundamental of crystallography and the module introduces to the
geometry of crystalline state, scattering of x-rays,Diffraction from a crystal and experimental
methods to collect diffraction data and analyze. Symmetry in crystal lattice, Chemical bonds,
Diffraction from periodic structures and methods of structure solving and solutions.
<br><br>
Crystal growth and crystal defects - It deals with the theory and kinetics of crystal growth, the
various techniques such as melt growth, solution growth, vapour growth and the study of
defects and crystals.
<br><br>
Lab Experimental demonstration: Powder X-ray diffraction analysis of solids in polycrystalline
form and to find out the crystal systems by indexing the data sets.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to the Special Theory of Relativity </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">

                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to the Special Theory of Relativity [PH 406]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Overview of Newtonian Mechanics: Galilean Relativity, Incompatibility with Electromagnetism, Michelson-Morley Experiment and the Aether Hypothesis.
Postulates of Special Relativity: Lorentz transformations, Relativity of Simultaneity, Time dilation and Length contraction, Doppler Effect; Understanding Relativity through paradoxes: Pole and Barn Paradox, Twin Paradox.<br>
Mathematics of Special Relativity: Notion of four-vectors; Covariant and contravariant four-vectors; notion of invariant distance; four-vector calculus; Tensors and their transformation properties; Tensor calculus; Causality and the Causal structure of space-time, space-time diagrams.<br>
Relativistic Dynamics: Mass-energy equivalence; Relativistic treatment of collisions and decays; Relativistic electrodynamics and covariant formulation of Maxwell equations.<br>
Experimental verifications of Special Relativity: Kennedy-Thorndike experiment, Ives-Stilwell experiment. High energy collision of elementary particles (LHC, LEP) and fundamental tests of Special Relativity.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Waves and Oscillations </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Waves and Oscillations [PH 408]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   ● Periodic Motions: Simple harmonic oscillator (SHO), Superposition of
vibrations: Lissajous Figures, Parallel and perpendicular superposition,
examples of free vibrations in physical system: mass-spring system,
Young Modulus, Pendulum etc.<br><br>
● Forced Vibrations: Undammed oscillator with force term, Forced
oscillation with damping, Transient phenomena, Examples of resonance:
Electrical, optical, nuclear resonance, Anharmonic oscillator.<br><br>
● Coupled Oscillators: Introduction of the concept of normal modes,
natural frequency, forced vibrations and resonance of two coupled
oscillator, generalization of N coupled oscillator: finding normal modes for
N coupled oscillator, Longitudinal oscillations, normal modes for crystal
lattice, stretched string, Longitudinal vibrations of a rod, vibrations of air
columns, elasticity of gas, normal modes of two and three dimensional
systems, Fourier analysis of normal modes problem, quasi normal modes
and dissipation.<br><br>
● Progressive Waves: Travelling waves, progressive waves in specific
media, superposition of wave pulses, Dispersion; phase and group
velocities, transport of energy by the wave, momentum flow and
mechanical radiation pressure, Polarization, solutions of the wave
equation in even and odd dimensions.<br><br>
● Interference: The Huygens-Fresnel Principle, Reflection and refraction of
plane waves, Doppler effect, Double slit interference, Diffraction by single
slit, Diffraction grating, Fraunhofer vs Fresnel diffraction.                                                                </p>
