							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Introduction to Astronomy and Space Science </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Introduction to Astronomy and Space Science [PH 409]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   The planetary system: Central force and the two-body problem, Kepler problem, orbit equation, conserved quantities. Perturbation of the two-body problem, perihelion of precession of planets. The basic introduction to the three-body problem. Restricted three-body problem. Gravitational slingshot.
Sky coordinates, Moon's orbit and eclipses, time measurement - synodic, sidereal. Solar eclipse, Saros cycles
Distance Ladder in Astronomy: How to measure distances of celestial objects, standard candles in astronomy, luminosity and magnitude, parallax method, cepheid variable stars. Supernova.
Physics of the stars: The Hertzsprung-Russell (H-R) diagram; star clusters; Stellar evolution, classification of stars from their position in the H-R diagram, UBV photometry, The dependence of stellar properties and timescales on mass, The determination of the ages of clusters, Physical conditions within stars, nuclear fusion. 
Theory of white dwarfs, electron degenerate pressure, elementary derivation of the Chandrashekar limit, neutron degeneracy and neutron star.
Galaxies: Classification of galaxies by their appearance, Galaxy interactions, The Milky Way, Active galaxies, and Radio jets. Supermassive black hole.
Cosmology: The expansion of the Universe, The Cosmic Microwave Background, The Big Bang model, Measurement of the age and mass, and the future evolution of the Universe.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Advanced Condensed Matter Physics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Advanced Condensed Matter Physics [PH 410]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Second Quantization: System of identical particles; Fock space, creation and annihilation operators for fermions, bosons, and their algebra; Many-body operators.</p>
<p>Interacting Hamiltonians and Hartree-Fock Theory: Review of free Fermi gas; Jellium model, Hartree-Fock correction to ground state energy; Tight-binding model; Fermi Hubbard model, Stoner ferromagnetism; Heisenberg model, superexchange.</p>
<p>Landau Fermi Liquid (FL) Theory: Quasiparticles, FL correction to thermodynamic quantities; Deformation of Fermi surface, Pomeranchuk instability; Collective excitations, zero sound; Examples of non-FL phases.</p>
<p>Superconductivity: Phenomenology, Meissner effect, Ginzburg-Landau theory; Cooper pairs, BCS theory.</p>
<p>Interacting Bosons and Superfluidity: Bose-Einstein condensation; Weakly repulsive Bose gas, Bogoliubov theory; Landau criterion and superfluidity; Broken symmetry, Goldstone modes; Superfluid-Mott transition in Bose Hubbard model.</p>
<p>Introduction to Quantum Hall Effect: Aharonov-Bohm effect, Berry phase and Berry connection, Chern number; Graphene; Landau levels, spectral flow, TKNN invariant, integer quantum Hall effect, role of disorder.<br>
</p>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Mathematical Methods of Physics - I </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content

                                </a>

                                                               <h1>Mathematical Methods of Physics - I [PH 502]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Finite and infinite dimensional vector spaces, Hilbert space, operators in infinite dimensional spaces, Matrix
algebra, Cayley-Hamilton theorem; Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization, commuting matrices with degenerate
eigenvalues.<br><br>
Algebra of complex numbers, Schwarz inequality, function of a complex variable, Cauchy-
Riemann equations and their applications, harmonic functions, complex integrals,
Cauchy&#39;s theorem and its corollaries, Taylor and Laurent expansion, classification of singularities, branch point
and branch cut, residue theorem and evaluation of integrals.<br><br>
Theory of second order linear homogeneous differential equations, Frobenius method, Fuch&#39;s theorem, Sturm-
Liouville theory, Hermitian operators, orthogonal expansion and completeness. Inhomogeneous differential
equations, Green&#39;s functions, special functions (Bessel, Legendre, Hermite and Laguerre functions) and
properties.<br><br>
Integral transforms: Fourier and Laplace transforms and their inverse transforms, solution of differential
equations using integral transform.<br><br>
Elementary group theory, point symmetry groups, group representations reducible and irreducible
representations, Lie groups and Lie algebra with SU(2) as an example.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Quantum Mechanics I </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Quantum Mechanics I [PH 503]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Wave functions, superposition principle, wave packets, Schrodinger equation, probability and current
densities, expectation values and Ehrenfest&quot;s theorem. <br><br>
Linear vectors and operators in Hillbert space, observables, commuting operators, momentum representation
and uncertainty principle, unitary transformations, Schrodinger and Heisenberg representations, equations of
motion.<br><br>
Applications: one-dimensional potential problems, linear harmonic oscillator with polynomial solutions, and
creation and annihilation operators.<br><br>
Central forces, free and bound states in a Coulomb potential, angular momentum, spherical harmonics, Stern-
Gerlach experiment for spin ½ system, spin, addition of angular momenta, Clebsch-Gordan coefficients.
Time independent perturbation theory, first and second order corrections to the energy eigenvalues,
degenerate perturbation theory, application to one-electron system, Zeeman effect and Stark effect.
Variational methods: Helium atom as example, Ritz principle for excited states.<br><br>
Special topics like Quantum dots, coherent and squeezed states, lasers, Aharonov-Bohm effect, Berry
phases, quantum entanglement and EPR paradox.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>

                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#008080">PH 503</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="PH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/phy.png" /></i>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Quantum Mechanics II </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Quantum Mechanics II [PH 504]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Time dependent perturbation theory, interaction picture, Fermi’s Golden rule, sudden and adiabatic
approximations. WKB approximation, tunneling through a barrier.<br><br>
Scattering theory, diﬀerential and total scattering cross-sections, Scattering by spherically symmetric
potentials, Partial wave analysis and phase shifts, Coulomb scattering, Green’s function in scattering
theory, Born approximation.<br><br>
Symmetries in quantum mechanics, Conservation laws and symmetries: continuous and discrete,
Rotation group, SO(3) and SU(2) representation, Lorentz group, SL(2,C) representation, matrix
representation of generators , Irreducible spherical tensor operators, parity and time reversal.
Identical Particles, symmetric and antisymmetric wavefunctions, slater determinant, Symmetric and
antisymmetric spin wavefunctions of two identical particles, algebra of bosonic and fermionic creation
an annihilation operators, continuous one particle spectrum and quantum field operators, dynamics of
identical particles.<br><br>
Relativistic quantum mechanics, Klein-Gordon equation, negative energy states and concept of
antiparticles, Dirac equation, plane wave solution and momentum space spinors, Helicity and
chirality, charge conjugation.<br><br>
Special Topics: Many body physics, Gross-Pitaevskii equation, Bose-Einstein Condenstation,
Superfluidty, Quantum well lasers, Nuclear magnetic resonance, Electron Spin resonance, Raman
Effect, fractional braiding statistics in quantum Hall systems, qubits and quantum computing.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>

						</div>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                               <h1>Classical Electrodynamics [PH 505]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of electrostatics and magnetostatics: Laplace and Poisson equations, uniqueness theorem, boundary-
value problems, Lorentz force.<br><br>
Maxwell’s equations, electromagnetic waves, Poynting theorem. Gauge transformations and gauge invariance,
electromagnetic potentials, wave propagation in conductors and dielectrics, Lorentz theory of dispersion,
complex refractive index.<br><br>
Special relativity, Minkowski space and four vectors, concept of four-velocity, four acceleration and higher rank
tensors, relativistic formulation of electrodynamics, Maxwell equations in covariant form, gauge invariance and
four-potential, the action principle and electromagnetic energy momentum tensor.<br><br>
Liénard-Weichert potentials, radiation from an accelerated charge, Larmor formula, Bremsstrahlung and
synchrotron radiation, multipole radiation, dispersion theory, radiative reaction, radiative damping, scattering by
free charges; applications to wave-guides, fibres and plasmas.<br><br>
Radiation reaction from energy conservation; Problem with Abraham-Lorentz formula; Limitations of
Classical electrodynamics.<br><br>
Special Lecture Topics: Plasma physics , Plasma and its occurrence in nature, uniform but time-dependent
magnetic field : Magnetic pumping; Magnetic bottle and loss cone; MHD equations, Magnetic Reynold&#39;s
number, Pinched plasma; Bennett&#39;s relation.                                                                </p>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                               <h1>Statistical Mechanics [PH 507]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Postulates of Thermodynamics; Conditions of thermal, mechanical and chemical equilibrium, examples;
Maxwell relations, Thermodynamics stability; Statistical basis of thermodynamics, microscopic and macroscopic
states. Classical ideal gas, Boltzman H theorem and irreversibility. Ergodic process; Micro canonical ensemble,
counting of states and phase space volume; Canonical Ensemble, equilibrium between system and heat
reservoir, canonical partition function, Helmholtz free energy, Grand canonical Ensemble, partition function,
particle number and energy fluctuations; Quantum statistical ensemble theory: density matrix formulation;
system of identical particles, manybody wavefunctions for non-interacting fermions and bosons; ideal quantum
gases: Bose-Einstein statistics, Fermi-Dirac statistics; Bose systems, Bose Einstein Condensation (BEC ) in
non-interacting gases. BEC in Interacting systems- experimental observation in Rb atoms; Photon gas, and
thermodynamics of Blackbody radiation. Elementary excitations of liquid Helium –II; Ideal Fermi gas description,
Paramagnetism and Landau diamagnetism, electron gas in metals, Specific heat of metals; Phase transitions,
Condensation in Van der Waals gas, Ising model and Ferromagnetism. Landau Phenomenological theory;
Non-Equilibrium statistical mechanics, Brownian motion, random walks, Langevin equation, Markov process.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of elementary Newtonian mechanics: Mechanics of a particle, system of particles. Constrained motions,
Virtual Work and D’Alembert’s Principle.<br><br>
Lagrangian formulation of mechanics: Variational calculus, Hamilton’s principle, Generalized coordinates,
Lagrange’s equations of motion, Symmetry and conservation theorems, Rotating frame of reference, illustrative
examples and applications.<br><br>
Central forces : Two body central force, equations of motion, first integrals, condition for closed orbits, Kepler’s
problem, virial theorem, orbit of satellites, physics of tides.<br><br>
Rigid body motion : Kinematics of rigid body motion: Euler Angles, Rotation matrices, Orthogonal
transformations, Degrees of freedom of rigid body, Euler’s theorem, Inertial tensor, Energy and Angular
momentum, Equations of motion, solution in torque free system, Gyroscopes, Precession of Satellite orbits,
Precession of charged bodies in EM fields.<br><br>
Hamilton’s equation of motions: Legendre Transformation, Hamilton function, Hamilton’s equations of motion,
Liouville’s theorem, cyclic coordinates and conservation theorems, illustrative examples.<br><br>
Lagrangian formulation for continuous systems: Transition from discrete to continuous systems, Lagrangian
formulation of continuous systems, the stress-energy tensor and conservation theorems. Noether’s theorem.                                                                </p>

                                                                   Condensed matter physics as study of many-body systems. Spatial correlations. Limitations of single particle
quantum mechanics, role of interactions, effective approaches. Hartree and Hartree-Fock approximations;
Crystalline solids, lattices, symmetries, reciprocal lattice, Bravais lattice, space groups. Liquid crystals and quasi
crystals. Spatial correlations through electron, neutron, electromagnetic field scattering. Theory of scattering
from crystals. X-Ray diffraction, correlation functions, structure factor; Crystal Vibrations. Phonons. Specific
heat of solids, Debye and Einstein models. Anharmonic effects in crystals. Mössbauer effect; Electrons in
solids, free electron theory of metals: free electron Fermi gas, semi-classical theory of conduction in metals,
Hall effect, Electrons in a periodic potential, Bloch’s theorem, band structure, Brillouin zones, tight-binding
model, effective mass, holes, insulators and semiconductors. Homogeneous semiconductors, semiconductor
band structures, inhomogeneous semiconductors; Theory of nanostructures, electron in a one-dimensional
array of potential wells, Quantum wires, wells and dots. Optical properties of nano structures; Quantum theory
of diamagnetism and paramagnetism. Ferromagnetism. Order-disorder transitions. Curie point and exchange
field. Concepts of Anti-ferromagnetism and frustration.                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Electronic Devices and Digital Systems for Physicists [PH 513]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Module 1: Semiconductor Basics Review of the basics of semiconductor physics, electronic structure, energy bands, carrier transport, doping</p>
<p>Module 2: Electronic Devices Diodes, junctions, transistors, field effect devices, homo and heterojunction devices, device structure and characteristics, frequency dependence and applications, Operational amplifiers, difference amplifiers, filters</p>
<p>Module 3: Digital Electronics Logic gates, flip-flops, Boolean Algebra, Karnaugh maps, registers and counters, A/D and D/A converters. </p>
<p>Module 4: Introduction to Nanoelectronics device scaling, Moore&rsquo;s law, Opto-electronic devices (solar cells, photodetectors, LEDs), two terminal quantum dot devices, graphene electronics, memory devices, spintronic devices</p>
<p>Module 5: Microprocessor and microcontroller basics Basic Functions and Applications of microprocessor and microcontrollers, Arduino programming</p>                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">AI in Physics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 1.5 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>AI in Physics [PH 514]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 1.5 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   AI applications in Physics research - examples from condensed matter, astronomy, optics, and materials science; Linear Algebra and Statistics - Vectors, matrices, eigenvalues, gradients, covariance, Gaussian distribution, regression; Supervised Learning (Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Support Vector Machines, K-nearest neighbours, Naive Bayes) and Unsupervised Learning (K-means clustering, Principal Component Analysis); Perceptrons, concept of weights and activation functions, Deep Learning and Artificial Neural Networks, learning via backpropagation; Types of Neural Network Architectures - Fully Connected Feed-Forward, CNN, RNN, GAN and Transformers); Hebbian learning and Ising models, Gradient descent and backpropagation viewed as energy minimization; Energy-based learning - Hopfield networks, Boltzmann machines; Physics-inspired neural networks - incorporating symmetries, conservation laws, and constraints in Neural networks; solving differential equations with embedded physical loss terms; Limitations of digital AI hardware, alternative physical implementations of neural networks (Optical, spintronic, and memristive systems), Reservoir computing and analog computing concepts; Applications and Mini Projects - Data fitting and noise reduction, Pattern recognition in microscopy or spectroscopy                                                                </p>

                                                </div>

                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="PH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">

                                                               <h1>Topics in Classical Mechanics and Electrodynamics [PH 605]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Lagrangian formulation of Classical mechanics; Principle of least action; Conservation laws and symmetries;
Phase space formulation; Hamiltonian mechanics; Poisson brackets; Canonical transformations; Hamilton-
Jacobi equation; Adiabatic invariants; Lagrangian and Hamiltonian formulation of Continuous systems and
fields; Noether theorems; Gauge transformations; Local and Global symmetries; Review of Special relativity;
Relativistic Mechanics of Charged Particle; Action principle for Electromagnetic field, Maxwell equations in
covariant form; Electromagnetic energy momentum tensor; Propagation of Electromagnetic waves; Field due to
a moving charge; Radiation reaction; Problem with Abraham-Lorentz formula; Limitations of Classical
electrodynamics.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Postulates of QM in bra-ket notation, entangled states, local hidden variable theory and classical and non-
classical states; QM of composite systems, density matrix; Harmonic oscillator in operator representation,
coherent states of harmonic oscillator; Addition of angular momenta; Symmetry in QM, concept of spin,
SU(m) and SO(m) groups, geometric and dynamic symmetries, Runge-Lenz vector for Coulomb potential;
Schrödinger equation in a periodic potential, energy spectrum of bulk and nano materials; Elements of the
theory of scattering; Shannon and von Neumann entropies, statistical mechanical ensembles as the result of
maximization of entropy with constraints; Spin and statistics, Maxwell-Boltzmann, Bose-Einstein, and Fermi
distributions; Partition function, thermodynamic potentials in statistical mechanics, relationship between
statistical and thermodynamic entropies; Bose-Einstein condensation.                                                                </p>

Unitary and Orthogonal groups in physics                                                                </p>

                                                               <h1>Introduction to Einstein’s Theory of General Relativity [PH 609]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Review of special relativity, uniformly accelerated observer, equivalence principle, gravitational redshift, gravity
as the manifestation of space time curvature; Concept of differential manifold: Covariant derivative and
connection, Lie derivative, space time metric, Christoffel symbols, Riemann curvature tensor, Ricci and Einstein
tensors, Weyl tensor; Electrodynamics in curved space time; Hilbert action and Einstein’s field equation,
Newtonian limit, energy conditions; Space time symmetries and Killing vector. Conserved quantities; Vacuum
solution of general relativity, Schwarzschild metric, Birkhoff’s theorem, geodesics of Schwarzschild space time,
Newtonian vs. relativistic orbits; Experimental test of general relativity: Bending of light, perihelion precession of
Mercury, Shapiro time-delay; Weak field limit and linearised field equations, gravitational radiation, radiation by
sources, energy loss. Introduction to post-Newtonian formulation.                                                                </p>

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit

																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Lorentz Invariance, Lorentz Group, Introduction to Spinors, Klein Gordan equation and Dirac equation, Limitations
of relativistic quantum mechanics.<br><br>
Scalar field theory, quantization and construction of the Fock space, Propagators. Complex scalar field,
Antiparticles. Quantization of Dirac field, Spin Statistics theorem.<br><br>
Interacting Field Theory, \lambda \phi^4 theory, Two point correlation function, Wick&#39;s Theorem, Interaction
picture and Dyson series, Construction of Feynman Diagram.<br><br>
Quantization of electromagnetic field, Gauge fixing, Introduction of QED, Feynman Rules and scattering cross
sections of tree level processes.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">X-ray Scattering: Concepts and Applications </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">

                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>X-ray Scattering: Concepts and Applications [PH 611]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   X-rays and their interaction with matter, scattering and absorption cross section, refraction and
reflection; Sources: X-ray tubes, advent of synchrotron radiation; Refraction and reflection from
surfaces and interfaces: refractive index including absorption, Snell’s law and the Fresnel
equations in the X-ray region; Kinematical scattering I: non-crystalline materials: scattering from
an atom, molecule, liquids and glasses, small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS); Kinematical
scattering II: crystalline order, scattering from a crystal, quasiperiodic structures, crystal
truncation rods, lattice vibrations, Debye-Waller factor and Thermal Diffuse Scattering,
applications of kinematical diffraction; Diffraction by perfect crystals: kinematical reflection from
a few layers, Darwin theory and dynamical diffraction; Diffuse scattering: correlation functions,
Born approximation; Applications of X-ray scattering: surface X-ray scattering techniques like X-
ray reflectivity (XRR), grazing incidence X-ray diffraction (GIXD), grazing incidence small angle
X-ray scattering (GISAXS), photoelectric absorption, resonant scattering.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Quantum Field Theory II </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Quantum Field Theory II [PH 612]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 1 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   I. Renormalization: Systematics, Degree of divergence. phi^4 theory at one-loop: Dimensional regularization, Counter terms, Renormalized perturbation theory.
<br>
II. QED at one-loop: Vacuum polarization and running of the coupling constant, Vertex graph, Electron self-energy, Counter terms and renormalization conditions in QED, Anomalous magnetic moment of the electron, Ward-Takahashi identity, Infrared divergences. Renormalizable and Non-renormalizable theories, Remarks on Effective field theories.
<br>
III. Renormalization Group: Dimensional Transmutation, Renormalization Group Equation (RGE), Solutions of RGE, Beta function of QED, RGE for Green’s functions: Callan-Symanzik equations, Anomalous dimensions. Brief introduction to the Wilsonian approach to the Renormalization Group.
<br>
IV. Path integral quantization: Path integrals in Quantum Mechanics, Functional quantization of scalar fields, Quantization of the EM field, Spinor field quantization using path integrals.                                                                </p>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                                               <h1>Atomic and Molecular Physics [PH 614]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Schrödinger equation for one-electron atoms, energy levels, eigenfunctions of the bound state,
expectation values. The electromagnetic field and its interaction with one electron systems, The
dipole approximation. Einstein’s coefficients. Selection rules, lifetimes of excited states. Line
shapes and widths. Fine structure of hydrogenic atoms. Spectral terms. The Zeeman effect. The
Stark effect. The lamb shift. Hyperfine structure and isotope shifts.<br><br>
Two-electron atoms. Spin wave functions and the Pauli exclusion principle. Level scheme of
Helium atom. The ground and excited states of two-electron atoms. Approximation methods.
Auger effect.<br><br>
Many-electron atoms. The central field approximation. Thomas-Fermi model. Slater
determinant. The Hartree-Fock method and the self-consistent field. Corrections to the central
field approximation. L-S and j-j coupling. X-ray spectra.<br><br>
Molecular structures and spectra. Born-Oppenheimer approximation, Rotational and vibrational
spectra of diatomic molecules. Structure of polyatomic molecules. Raman Scattering. Electronic
spectra. The Frank Condon principle. Raman Spectroscopy.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Physics of two-dimensional materials </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Physics of two-dimensional materials [PH 615]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction: Classification of materials based on dimensions, 2D material family, History,
Hexagonal crystal structure, Structural stability, Superlative properties.<br><br>
Synthesis of 2D materials: Micromechanical exfoliation, Chemical vapor deposition, Liquid
exfoliation, van der Waals epitaxy.<br><br>
Characterization methods: Microscopy techniques (AFM, TEM), Raman spectroscopy, PL
spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction.<br><br>
Electronic properties and devices: Band structure of graphene, Tight-binding approach,
Effective mass, Dirac Fermions, Density of states, Band structure of bilayer graphene,
MoS 2 , WS 2 , Field effect transistor (FET) with 2D materials, High frequency transistors,
Klein tunneling, Schottky interface, p-n junctions, Heterostructures, Quantum Hall effect.
Optical properties: Light-matter interactions, Fine structure constant, Optical conductivity,
Thickness dependent optical properties, van Hove singularity, Plasmons, Excitons and
Trions<br><br>
Chemical properties: Hybridization, Functionalization, Hydrogenation, Graphane,
Flourographene, Adsorption characteristics<br><br>
Mechanical properties: Tensile strength, Young’s modulus, Bendability, Mermin-Wagner
Theorem, Mechanical resonators<br><br>
Thermal properties: Thermal conductivity, Weidman-Franz law, Thickness dependency,
Thermoelectric power-Seebeck effect, Nernst effect<br><br>
Membrane science: 2D laminates, Nanopores, Nanochannels, Desalination, Ion sieving,
Blue energy, DNA sequencing<br><br>
Future Perspective and Applications: Lightweight transparent coatings, Flexible displays,
Solar cells, Heterostructure based devices, Tunneling transistors, Light emitting diodes,
Super capacitors, Sensors, Composites, Future prospects                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Quantum Optics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Quantum Optics [PH 616]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Hamiltonian of free classical electromagnetic (e.m.) field. The concept of field mode. The e.m. field
quantization, Coherent and squeezed states of the field. Wigner Functions, the Glauber-Sudarshan P-
function and the concept of classical and non-classical states of the e.m. field.
<br><br>
Classical atomic dipole – e.m. field interaction, Polarizability, Damping, Dipole Radiation, Mechanical
effects of light on atoms – dipole force/optical tweezers/laser cooling
<br><br>
Hamiltonian of a free atom. Atomic dipole moment in terms of atomic eigenstates.
<br><br>
Classical e.m. field, Two and three-level atoms, interaction Hamiltonian in dipole approximation and
Rotating Wave Approximation (RWA). Atomic dynamics in single mode classical field.
<br><br>
Optical Bloch equations for damped atomic dynamics. The concept of Density Matrix. Two-time
correlation functions; absorption and emission spectrum (resonance fluorescence) of scattered
radiation from atoms. Mechanical effects of light on the two-level atom, dressed states.
<br><br>
Two level interacting with single mode quantized field: Jaynes-Cummings model.
<br><br>
Quantum Theory of Damping: Atoms in a thermal field: master equation for two-level atoms and the
origin of the spontaneous decay of an excited state and the Einstein-A coefficient.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Quantum Computing and Information </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Quantum Computing and Information [PH 643]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Why quantum computing. Review of postulates of quantum mechanics.
Concept of a qubit and its practical realization. Bloch sphere representation of a qubit.
Algebra of one and two qubit states and operators. Schmidt decomposition of pure states of
two qubits. Quantum gates and circuits. Hamiltonians for implementing gates and their
physical realization. Universal quantum gates. Implementing arbitrary n-qubit gates in terms
of universal gates. <br><br>
Quantum Fourier transform. Quantum computing algorithms: Deutsch algorithm, Deutsch-
Jozsa algorithm, refined Deutsch-Jozsa algorithm, Bernstein-Vazirani algorithm, Grover’s
search algorithm, algorithm for finding period of a function, and Shor’s factorization
algorithm.<br><br>
Error correction in quantum computing.<br><br>
Fundamental differences between classical and quantum mechanics based on the
concepts of entanglement and separability, and highlighted by Schrödinger’s Cat paradox,
and Einstein, Podolosky, Rosen (EPR) paradox. Local hidden variable theory. Concept of
classical and non-classical states and exclusive quantum effects. Bell like inequalities and
other approaches to identify non-classical states.<br><br>
Why quantum information. Dense coding, quantum teleportation, quantum cryptography.<br><br>
Classical information and Shannon entropy as a measure of information. Shannon’s
noiseless channel coding theorem. Classical joint, conditional, and mutual information.<br><br>

Density matrix. Quantum information and von Neumann entropy. Problem of
communicating quantum states and fidelity. Schumacher’s quantum noiseless coding
theorem. Mutual information in quantum theory. Measures of quantumness of mutual information: quantum discord, measurement induced disturbance, symmetric discord, and
local hidden variables theoretic measure of quantumness of mutual information.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">

                                                               <h1>Tools of Experimental Physics [PH 644]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 1 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   ● Essentials of data analysis: Uncertainties in measurement, propagation
of errors, probability distributions, rejection of data, least-squares fitting, the
chi-squared test.<br><br>
● Structure analysis techniques: Overview of X-ray, neutron and low
energy electron diffraction methods. Demonstration of XRD and data
analysis.<br><br>
● Electrical and magnetic characterization: Resistivity studies, Classical &amp;
Quantum Hall effect; principle &amp; experimental measurement, Magnetic
Susceptibility, low Current &amp; Voltage measurements, Demonstration of Hall
Effect and resistivity measurement set up, Principles of vibrating-sample
magnetometer and superconducting quantum interference device.<br><br>
● Vacuum technology and deposition methods: Generation and
measurement of low pressure, evaporation and sputtering techniques.
Demonstration of relevant experimental set up.<br><br>
● Spectroscopy and Microscopy techniques: Raman spectroscopy, X-ray
photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS/UPS). Overview of Nuclear Magnetic
Resonance Spectroscopy and Electron Spin Resonance Spectroscopy.
Basic principles &amp; Measurements. scanning electron microscopy,
Rutherford backscattering, scanning probe microscopy, transmission
electron microscopy and atomic force microscopy. Demonstration of NMR.
AFM, and TEM.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Topics in Soft and Active Matter Physics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Topics in Soft and Active Matter Physics [PH 645]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   ● Introduction and Overview:  Definition and classification of soft matter.
Energies and timescales.<br><br>
● Intermolecular and Surface Forces: Thermodynamic and statistical
aspects of intermolecular forces, Interactions involving polar molecules,
Van der Waals forces, Repulsive steric forces, Forces between particles
and surfaces, Thermodynamic principles of self-assembly, Interactions of
Biological Membranes and Structures.<br><br>
● Dynamics of Molecules: Diffusion, permeation and flow, Smoluchoski’s
relation, Onsager reciprocal relations.<br><br>
● Essentials of Polymer: Microstructures, fractal nature of polymer
conformations, Ideal chain models, free energy, excluded volume and self-
avoiding walks, Flory theory, structure and functions of proteins.<br><br>
● Colloidal Assemblies: Electrostatics, Poisson-Boltzmann equation,
Debye-Huckel theory, Derjaguin approximation, hydrodynamics, Diffusion
limited aggregation, Derjaguin-Landau-Verwey-Overbeek (DLVO) theory,
Depletion forces, electrokinetic processes.<br><br>
● Active Matter: Definition and examples. Physics at low Reynolds number,
Biomolecular motors, dynamics of bacterial suspensions, chemically
powered systems, behavior of single and multiple swimmers, emergent
dynamics, activity induced propulsion and processes under cytosolic
conditions.                                                                </p>

							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>

                                       Course Content

                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Advanced Statistical Physics [PH 646]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   <p>Critical Phenomena and Phase transition: Some important questions and developments.      Scaling theory, Thermodynamic Limit, Phase boundaries, Different types of phase transitions, Finite-size effect and correlation lengths. </p>
<p>Role of models in Phase Transitions:  The Ising Model and existence of phase transition in it (at zero temperature, non-zero temperature in d=1, d=2 etc), Importance of boundary conditions. Transfer matrix, Special correlation using Transfer matrix, low temperature and high-temperature expansion. <br>
  <br>
  Mean Field Theory: Mean Field Theory of the Ising Model, Bragg-Williams Approximation, Critical Behavior of Mean Field Theories, Ising Chain: Exact Solution, Landau Theory of Phase Transitions, Symmetry Considerations, Potts model, Landau-Ginzburg Theory for Fluctuations. Applications of Mean Field Theory e.g. Order-Disorder Transition. </p>
<p>Renormalization Group (RG) Flow:  Fixed Points, Position Space Renormalization: Cumulant Method, First-order approximation, Second-order approximation, Phenomenological Renormalization Group, Epsilon-Expansion, Gaussian model</p>
<p>Linear Response Theory: Some exact results: Generalized susceptibility and the structure factor, Sum rules, Entropy Production, the response of observable quantities due to time-dependent perturbations: the Kubo Formula, and the Onsager Relations for Transport Coefficients, The Boltzmann Equation</p>                                                                </p>

							<hr>

                                                                   Review of basic concepts in differential geometry, intrinsic and extrinsic curvatures, Gauss Codazzi   equations. Variation Principle in General Relativity, Einstein Hilbert Action, Diffeomorphism Invariance, and Bianchi  Identity. Hamiltonian Formulation of General Relativity, ADM decomposition, Foliation of the boundary, Lapse function and Shift vector, Gravitational Hamiltonian, Hamiltonian &amp; Momentum constraints. ADM and  Bondi Masses, Komar Mass. <br>
Stationary Black hole solutions of Vacuum Einstein&rsquo;s equation, The Kerr Solution: stationary observers, Ergophere &amp; Event Horizon, Penrose process, Kerr-Neumann Solution<br>
Application to Cosmology: Homogeneity and Isotropy, Friedmann&ndash;Lema&icirc;tre&ndash;Robertson&ndash;Walker  metrics, Open, Flat and Closed universes, Cosmological constant, Drawbacks of the Big Bang  Model. Horizon &amp; Flatness Problem, Cosmic inflation, Inflation Field, Slow roll models. Puzzles of the late universe, Dark energy &amp; Dark Matter, Cosmic Tension.                                                                </p>

							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Group Theory for Physics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Group Theory for Physics [PH 650]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Groups: Basic Definitions,  Subgroups and Cosets, Conjugates, Normal Subgroups,  Cyclic, Dihedral and Permutation Groups, Matrix groups. Representations, General Group Actions, Representations, Unitarity and Reducibility. Schur&rsquo;s Lemma, Orthogonality Theorem.<br>
Classification of crystalline structure, Bravis lattices and Miller indices, Point groups, space groups and site groups.<br>
Group theory and Symmetries, Wigner Theorem,<br>
Rotations and Angular Momentum, Isospin invariance, SO(3) and SU(2)/Z_2, Representation of Angular Momentum Commutation relations. SO(2n) and Sp(2 n) and their relation to n number of bosonic and fermionic creation/annihilation alegbra. <br>
Relativistic Symmetries, Lorentz and Poincare Groups, SO(3,1) and SL(2,C). Spinor Representations, Irreducible representation of the Poincare group, Little group for massive and massless particles, Euclidean Group in 2 dimensions (E(2)). <br>
General Lie Groups and Lie Algebra, Matrix Lie group. Constructing an Irreducible Representation.  Roots and Weights. Casimir Operators, the group SU(3) and the eightfold way.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#008080">PH 650</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="PH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/phy.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Nanomagnetism and Spintronics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Nanomagnetism and Spintronics [PH 651]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   &bull;	Magnetic moment, magnetism of an isolated spin, Diamagnetism and Paramagnetism<br>
&bull;	Itinerent and localized magnetic moments, exchange interactions, magnetic ordering, Ferromagnetism, Antiferromagnetism, Ferrimagnetism<br>
&bull;	Magnetic anisotropy, Domains, Magnetic Hysteresis, The Stoner-Wohlfarth model<br>
&bull;	Basics of magnetization dynamics, Landau-Lifshitz Gilbert equation, concept of effective field and its components, magnetic resonance, micromagnetic and macrospin models<br>
&bull;	Magnetoresistive effects: Anisotropic Magnetoresistance, Giant Magnetoresistance, Tunnel Magnetoresistance<br>
&bull;	Spintronic Phenomena and devices: Spin current creation, injection and detection, non-local spin transport, Spin Transfer Torque (STT), Spin Orbit torque (SOT), Spin Hall effect (SHE), Spin Pumping, Spin Torque Diode Effect, Spin Valves, Spin Torque Nano Oscillators (STNOs), Magnetic Tunnel Junctions (MTJs), Spin Hall Nano Oscillators (SHNOs) <br>
&bull;	Device Applications: Magnetization Switching, MTJ auto-oscillators, microwave sensors, magnetic field sensors, energy harvesting<br>
&bull;	Topological textures in magnetism: Skyrmions- nucleation, annihilation and motion, antiskyrmions, skyrmionium, skyrmion based devices                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#008080">PH 651</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="PH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/phy.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Gravitational Wave Astronomy </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Gravitational Wave Astronomy [PH 652]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Introduction to gravitational waves and detection overview, Gravitational Wave Detectors: Michelson interferometers and Fabry-Perot cavities, Power recycling, readout techniques, and interferometer design, Noise sources and noise power spectral density; Resonant mass detectors
Review of Generation of Gravitational Waves: Linearized gravity and the weak-field approximation GW solutions in vacuum; TT gauge and stress-energy pseudotensor Energy and momentum carried by GWs; Detector response and antenna patterns. Order of magnitude estimation of GW emission from binary neutron stars – strain and power.
Gravitational Wave Sources: Compact binary coalescences (CBCs): BBH and BNS systems Waveform morphology: inspiral, merger, ringdown; Chirp signals; Inspiral due to GW emission. Continuous wave sources: spinning neutron stars and emission mechanisms Search strategies: targeted, directed, and all-sky; Stochastic GW background: astrophysical and cosmological origins.
Detection Theory and Signal Processing: Probability review: univariate/multivariate distributions Noise types: white, colored, and wide-sense stationarity Matched filtering and hypothesis testing Neyman-Pearson criteria and decision surfaces Composite hypothesis, maximum likelihood detection Ambiguity function, parameter-space metric, template placement, and signal-consistency tests and noise mitigation.
Parameter Estimation and Tests of General Relativity: Fisher information matrix and parameter estimation fundamentals, Bayesian inference and MCMC methods, Parametrized tests of general relativity using GW signals, Synergy with space detectors.
Practical Applications and Capstone: Introduction to GWOSC and public LIGO data Hands-on analysis: identifying GW signals Parameter estimation in real datasets, Capstone discussion: recent discoveries, challenges, and future directions.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>
							
                        </div>
                        
												<span class="label" style="background:#008080">PH 652</span>
												
						</div>

                    					
                        <div class="col-md-4 masonry__item"  data-masonry-filter="PH">
					
                        <div class="feature feature-4 boxed boxed--lg boxed--border">
							
                            
													<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/phy.png" /></i>
					
												<!--<i class=""><img style='margin-bottom:0;' src="https://iitgn.ac.in/assets/img/discipline_icon/new/es.png" /></i>-->
												
						
							
							
							<h4 style="min-height:78px;">Advanced Condensed Matter Physics </h4>
							<hr>
							<h4 style="margin-bottom: 3em;">
								Credit
								<br>
								L | T | P | C
							<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4							</h4>
							
							
							<div class="modal-instance block">
                                <a class="btn btn--primary modal-trigger" href="#">
                                    <span class="btn__text">
                                       Course Content
                                    </span>
                                </a>
                                <div class="modal-container">
                                    <div class="modal-content">
                                        <section class=" feature-large bg--white border--round ">
                                            <div class="container" style="margin-top:-50px;">
                                                <div class="row justify-content-end">
                                                    <div class="col-md-12">
                                                               <h1>Advanced Condensed Matter Physics [PH 653]</h1>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                <p >
																	Credit
																	<br>
																	L | T | P | C
																<br>3 | 0 | 0 | 4<br>
																 <hr class="short">
                                                                   Second Quantization: System of identical particles; Fock space, creation and annihilation operators for fermions, bosons, and their algebra; Many-body operators; Interacting Hamiltonians and Hartree-Fock Theory: Review of free Fermi gas; Jellium model, Hartree-Fock correction to ground state energy; Tight-binding model; Fermi Hubbard model, Stoner ferromagnetism; Heisenberg model, superexchange; Landau Fermi Liquid (FL) Theory: Quasiparticles, FL correction to thermodynamic quantities; Deformation of Fermi surface, Pomeranchuk instability; Collective excitations, zero sound; Examples of non-FL phases; Superconductivity: Phenomenology, Meissner effect, Ginzburg-Landau theory; Cooper pairs, BCS theory; Interacting Bosons and Superfluidity: Bose-Einstein condensation; Weakly repulsive Bose gas, Bogoliubov theory; Landau criterion and superfluidity; Broken symmetry, Goldstone modes; Superfluid-Mott transition in Bose Hubbard model; Introduction to Quantum Hall Effect: Aharonov-Bohm effect, Berry phase and Berry connection, Chern number; Graphene; Landau levels, spectral flow, TKNN invariant, integer quantum Hall effect, role of disorder.                                                                </p>
                                                               
                                                               
                                                          
                                                        <!--end of row-->
                                                    </div>
                                                </div>
                                                <!--end of row-->
                                            </div>
                                            <!--end of container-->
                                        </section>
                                    </div>
                                </div>
                            </div>

<script>
        let fontSize = 14;
        let isGrayscale = false;
        let isHighContrast = false;
        let isLightBackground = false;
        let isDarkMode = false;

        function increaseText() {
            fontSize += 2;
            applyTextSettings();
        }

        function decreaseText() {
            fontSize -= 2;
            applyTextSettings();
        }

        function toggleGrayscale() {
            // isGrayscale = !isGrayscale;
            isGrayscale = true;
            applyTextSettings();
        }

        function toggleHighContrast() {
            isHighContrast = !isHighContrast;
                 applyTextSettings();
        }

        function toggleLightBackground() {
            // isLightBackground = false;
            isGrayscale = !isGrayscale;
            applyTextSettings();
        }

        function toggleDarkMode() {
            isDarkMode = !isDarkMode;
            applyTextSettings();
        }

        function resetToNormalSize() {
            fontSize = 14;
            applyTextSettings();
        }
        
        function resetSettings() {
            fontSize = 14;
            isGrayscale = false;
            isHighContrast = false;
            isLightBackground = false;
            isDarkMode = false;
            applyTextSettings();
        }

        function applyTextSettings() {
            document.body.style.fontSize = `${fontSize}px`;
            document.body.classList.toggle('grayscale', isGrayscale);
            document.body.classList.toggle('high-contrast', isHighContrast);
            // document.body.classList.toggle('dropdown dropdown__trigger', isHighContrast);
            // document.body.classList.toggle('grayscale', isLightBackground);
            document.body.classList.toggle('dark-background', isDarkMode);
        }

        function toggleSettings() {
            const settingsContainer = document.getElementById('settings-container');
            settingsContainer.style.display = (settingsContainer.style.display == 'none') ? 'block' : 'none';
        }
    </script>
